2. **Lack of trained personnel:** Small firms typically employ semi-skilled workers who may not be trained in statistical techniques, leading to misinterpretation of control charts and sampling plans.

3. **Small batch sizes:** Small-scale production often involves short runs of diverse products, making statistical sampling unreliable due to insufficient sample sizes.

4. **Resistance to change:** Family-run small businesses may resist formal quality systems, preferring traditional, experience-based quality checks.

5. **Limited data infrastructure:** Small firms often lack ERP/MIS systems to record, store, and analyze quality data over time.

**Conclusion:** SQC yields maximum benefit in **repetitive, large-scale production** environments; in small-scale or job-shop contexts, its application is **constrained** by economic and operational limitations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing "SQC has limitations" without **explaining** them. *(Always pair each limitation with a one-line justification.)*
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **Quality Control (QC)** with **Quality Assurance (QA)** in definitions.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Listing only benefits or only limitations in a "Discuss both" question — KTU awards marks for **both** sides.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting units or justification in the **mathematical derivation** (Step-by-step working carries 5 of 7 marks).
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Not concluding with an **engineering interpretation** — KTU's OBE framework rewards applied thinking.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Board Prep)**

- ✅ **SQC = Statistics + Engineering + Economics** — never treat it as pure math.
- ✅ **Benefits are classified** into Economic, Quality, Managerial, and Strategic — *use these four heads in answers.*
- ✅ **Limitations arise from** technical, economic, and operational sources.
- ✅ **Sampling risks** ($\alpha$ and $\beta$) are *inherent* to SQC, not flaws.
- ✅ **SQC minimizes, not eliminates, variation** — the Juran cost curve justifies this.
- ✅ **SQC complements, not replaces, engineering judgment.**
- ✅ **Most effective in repetitive mass production**, least in job-shop or one-off production.
- ✅ **Cost of Quality:** $C_T = C_P + C_A + C_F$ — minimum occurs at $Q^* = (b/2a)^{1/3}$.
- ✅ **Process capability indices** $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ are the bridge between SQC and engineering specifications.
- ✅ **Six Sigma, TQM, ISO 9001** are all modern extensions of SQC philosophy.
- ✅ **Key buzzwords for full marks:** *assignable cause, chance cause, control limits, process capability, quality consciousness, prevention cost, appraisal cost, failure cost.*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Quality Objectives

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quality Objectives** are specific, measurable, and time-bound goals that an organization establishes to achieve the desired level of quality in its products, services, and processes. They are the operational translation of the **Quality Policy** into actionable targets and serve as the foundation upon which **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** systems are designed, monitored, and improved.

According to **ISO 9001:2015 (Clause 6.2)**, quality objectives must be:
- Consistent with the quality policy
- Measurable
- Monitored
- Communicated
- Updated as appropriate
- Available as documented information

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Policy** states the *intent* of an organization regarding quality. **Quality Objectives** translate that intent into *specific, measurable outcomes* at every level of the organization.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS Trip Planner"

Imagine you are driving from Kottayam to Bengaluru (a ~700 km journey). Your *Quality Policy* is the decision: *"We will reach Bengaluru safely and economically."* This is too vague to act upon. So you set **Quality Objectives** (waypoints):

| Waypoint (Objective) | Metric (Measurement) | Deadline |
|---|---|---|
| Reach Kochi by 9 AM | Distance remaining ≤ 50 km | Today 9:00 AM |
| Maintain fuel efficiency | Avg. mileage ≥ 18 km/l | Throughout trip |
| Zero phone-use violations | Count = 0 | End of trip |
| Arrive Bengaluru safely | Damage count = 0 | Tomorrow 8:00 PM |

Just as these waypoints **transform a vague goal into a navigable journey**, Quality Objectives transform the abstract Quality Policy into concrete, trackable, statistical targets (e.g., *Cpk* ≥ 1.33, DPMO ≤ 3.4, defect rate ≤ 0.1%).

## 1.3 Characteristics of Good Quality Objectives — The SMART Framework

A well-formulated quality objective must satisfy the **SMART** criteria:

> [!NOTE]
> **SMART Quality Objectives**
> - **S** — Specific: Clearly defined scope and deliverable
> - **M** — Measurable: Quantifiable metric (defects, %, ppm, sigma level)
> - **A** — Achievable: Realistic given resources and constraints
> - **R** — Relevant: Aligned with customer needs and the quality policy
> - **T** — Time-bound: A specific deadline or review period

## 1.4 The "M" in SMART — Why Measurement is the Backbone of SQC

Statistical Quality Control **cannot exist** without quantifiable objectives. Every control chart, every process capability index, every hypothesis test presupposes a numerical target. The measurable metrics commonly tied to Quality Objectives are:

- **Defect rate** (in **%** or **PPM — Parts Per Million**)
- **Process Capability Indices** (*Cp*, *Cpk*, *Pp*, *Ppk*)
- **Sigma Level** (typically 3σ to 6σ)
- **Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)** in **%**
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** in **%**
- **Cost of Quality (COQ)** in monetary units (₹/unit or % of revenue)

## 1.5 Classification of Quality Objectives

Quality Objectives are not monolithic. They fall into **five interlinked categories**:

1. **Product Quality Objectives** — conformance to specifications, reliability, durability
2. **Process Quality Objectives** — process stability, capability, throughput yield
3. **Customer-Related Objectives** — satisfaction, complaint reduction, on-time delivery
4. **Supplier-Related Objectives** — incoming quality levels, supplier rating
5. **Financial Objectives** — cost of poor quality (COPQ), warranty cost reduction
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchy of Quality Objectives — Cascading

A defining feature of an effective quality system is the **vertical and horizontal deployment** of objectives. This is known as **Hoshin Kanri** (Policy Deployment) in Japanese management literature and is closely tied to Juran's *Top-Down / Bottom-Up* planning approach.

### 2.1.1 Three-Tier Cascade

| Level | Tier | Time Horizon | Typical Objective | SQC Tool Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Strategic** | Top Management / Board | 3–5 years | "Achieve Six Sigma by 2027" | Strategic scorecard, long-term Cpk targets |
| **Tactical** | Middle Management / Engineers | 6–12 months | "Reduce process variation by 20%" | Process mapping, FMEA, Pareto analysis |
| **Operational** | Shop-floor / Workforce | Daily / Weekly | "Zero rework on Line 3 this shift" | Control charts (X̄-R, p, c), check sheets |

### 2.1.2 The Logic of Cascading

The deployment follows a strict **SMART translation chain**:

1. The strategic goal (e.g., "Achieve 4σ quality level") is decomposed.
2. Each tactical function receives a sub-goal (e.g., Manufacturing → "Cpk ≥ 1.33"; Procurement → "Incoming defect ≤ 0.5%").
3. Each operator/workstation receives an operational micro-goal (e.g., "Keep all points within control limits on the X̄ chart").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Whenever the question asks *"How are quality objectives cascaded?"* the expected answer must mention **Strategic → Tactical → Operational** hierarchy with at least **one numerical example** at each level.

## 2.2 Juran's Quality Trilogy — The Theoretical Backbone of Quality Objectives

Joseph M. Juran proposed that quality management is composed of **three universal processes**. Quality objectives are the *output specification* of the **Quality Planning** stage, the *benchmark* for the **Quality Control** stage, and the *target* of the **Quality Improvement** stage.

1. **Quality Planning (QP):** Identify customers, determine needs, develop product/process, establish quality objectives.
2. **Quality Control (QC):** Evaluate actual performance vs. objectives, take corrective action.
3. **Quality Improvement (QI):** Establish infrastructure, identify improvement projects, establish controls.

## 2.3 Cost of Quality (COQ) — Linking Objectives to Money

The **Cost of Quality** (also called *Cost of Poor Quality*, COPQ) is the financial mirror of the quality objective system. It is the single most powerful argument to convince management to invest in quality improvement.

| COQ Category | Type | Examples | Behaviour vs. Quality Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Prevention Costs (P)** | Cost of *doing it right* | Training, quality planning, supplier capability audits | Decreases as quality improves |
| **Appraisal Costs (A)** | Cost of *detecting* defects | Inspection, testing, calibration of gauges | Decreases as quality improves |
| **Internal Failure Costs (IF)** | Cost of *catching* defects before delivery | Rework, scrap, re-inspection, downtime | Decreases sharply as quality improves |
| **External Failure Costs (EF)** | Cost of *escaping* defects to customer | Warranty, returns, recalls, lawsuits, lost goodwill | Decreases dramatically as quality improves |

**Total COQ** is the sum of all four:

$$\text{Total COQ} = P + A + IF + EF$$

**Optimal Quality Level:** As prevention spending increases, failure costs decrease. The **economic optimum** is at the point where the marginal cost of prevention equals the marginal savings in failure costs. This point is also the *optimum quality objective* from a financial standpoint.

## 2.4 Process Capability Indices — The Quantitative Language of Quality Objectives

For any **measurable quality characteristic** (e.g., shaft diameter, fill volume, tensile strength), two numerical targets dominate SQC:

### 2.4.1 Cp — Process Capability Index (Centered Process)

Measures whether the **process spread** (6σ) fits within the **specification width** (USL − LSL).

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

A **higher Cp means a more capable process**. The classical *Six-Sigma objective* requires **Cp ≥ 2.0** (since the 6σ spread fits exactly into ±3σ of the spec, leaving half a sigma on each side after the 1.5σ shift).

### 2.4.2 Cpk — Process Capability Index (Off-Center Correction)

Cp assumes the process mean is exactly at the center of the specification. In reality, processes drift. Cpk penalizes this off-center positioning.

$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

The two terms are also written individually as $Cpu = (USL - \mu) / (3\sigma)$ (upper) and $Cpl = (\mu - LSL) / (3\sigma)$ (lower). The minimum of the two becomes the official Cpk value.

### 2.4.3 Interpretation of Capability Indices

| Cpk Value | Process Capability | Typical DPMO (1.5σ shift) | Six-Sigma Quality Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **< 1.00** | Incapable (high defect rate) | > 66,807 ppm | < 3σ |
| **1.00 – 1.33** | Marginal | 6,210 – 66,807 ppm | 3σ – 4σ |
| **1.33 – 1.67** | Capable | 233 – 6,210 ppm | 4σ – 5σ |
| **1.67 – 2.00** | Excellent | 3.4 – 233 ppm | 5σ – 6σ |
| **≥ 2.00** | World-class / Six Sigma | ≤ 3.4 ppm | ≥ 6σ |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Sheet — Quality Objectives (Module 1)**

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Process Capability | $C_p = (USL - LSL) / (6\sigma)$ | Centred process potential |
| 2 | Process Capability Index | $C_{pk} = \min \big[ (USL - \mu)/(3\sigma),\ (\mu - LSL)/(3\sigma) \big]$ | Off-centre penalty |
| 3 | Process Performance | $P_p = (USL - LSL) / (6s)$ | Overall (long-term) capability |
| 4 | DPMO (Defects/Million Opps) | $DPMO = (D / (U \times O)) \times 10^6$ | Six-Sigma metric |
| 5 | Rolled Throughput Yield | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Multi-stage yield |
| 6 | First Pass Yield | $FPY = (Good\ Units\ First\ Time) / (Total\ Units)$ | Single-stage yield |
| 7 | Total Cost of Quality | $COQ = P + A + IF + EF$ | Financial measure |
| 8 | Sigma Level | $\sigma = (USL - \mu) / Z$ | Defect probability |
| 9 | Z-Score (Short-term) | $Z_{ST} = (USL - \mu) / \sigma$ | Capability |
| 10 | Z-Score (Long-term) | $Z_{LT} = Z_{ST} + 1.5$ | Six-Sigma convention |
| 11 | Yield from DPMO | $Yield = 1 - (DPMO / 10^6)$ | Approximation |
| 12 | Cpk lower-bound CPU CPL | $CPU = (USL - \mu) / (3\sigma);\ CPL = (\mu - LSL) / (3\sigma)$ | One-sided capability |

*(Note: The vertical pipe $\vert$ symbol has been intentionally replaced with $\min$ and \big[ \big] syntax to preserve markdown table integrity.)*

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

Quality Objectives drive virtually every modern quality initiative:

- **Automotive (Toyota, Tata Motors):** Objective — Zero defects in vehicle assembly; metric — JPH (jobs per hour) defect rate; tool — SPC.
- **Software (Infosys, TCS):** Objective — ≤ 1 defect per KLOC; metric — DPMO, escaped-defect count; tool — Six-Sigma DMAIC.
- **Pharma (Sun Pharma, Cipla):** Objective — 100% compliance to US-FDA; metric — OOS (Out-of-Specification) rate; tool — process validation, capability studies.
- **Semiconductor (Intel, TSMC):** Objective — 6σ cleanliness in cleanroom; metric — particles/m³; tool — control charts, Cpk ≥ 1.5.

## 2.7 Common Pitfalls When Framing Quality Objectives

> [!WARNING]
> - **Vague targets** ("improve quality") are not objectives — they are aspirations.
> - **Over-ambitious** targets destroy morale and produce fudged data.
> - **Over-conservative** targets indicate no real improvement programme.
> - Objectives **without owners** are not objectives — they are wishes.
> - Objectives **without measurement systems** cannot be tracked.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Process Capability Index $C_p$

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Suppose a critical dimension of a bearing has the following specifications:

- **Lower Specification Limit (LSL)** = 9.95 mm
- **Upper Specification Limit (USL)** = 10.05 mm
- **Process standard deviation (σ)** = 0.015 mm
- **Process mean (μ)** = 10.00 mm (centred)

The specification width is the difference between USL and LSL:

$$W_{spec} = USL - LSL = 10.05 - 9.95 = 0.10\ \text{mm}$$

The natural process spread covers **99.73%** of all parts (±3σ under a normal distribution), and is:

$$W_{proc} = 6\sigma = 6 \times 0.015 = 0.090\ \text{mm}$$

### 3.1.2 Definition of Capability

A natural measure of capability is the **ratio of specification width to process spread**:

$$C_p = \frac{W_{spec}}{W_{proc}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Evaluation

Substituting the values:

$$C_p = \frac{0.10}{6 \times 0.015} = \frac{0.10}{0.090} \approx 1.111$$

**Interpretation:** The process is *marginally capable* (Cpk between 1.00 and 1.33). It can meet specifications *only when centred*. Any drift will push parts outside the limits.

## 3.2 Derivation of $C_{pk}$ for an Off-Centre Process

### 3.2.1 New Data

Now consider that the same process drifts. The new mean is μ = 10.025 mm. The standard deviation is unchanged at σ = 0.015 mm.

The two one-sided capability values are:

$$C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{10.05 - 10.025}{3 \times 0.015} = \frac{0.025}{0.045} \approx 0.556$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{10.025 - 9.95}{3 \times 0.015} = \frac{0.075}{0.045} \approx 1.667$$

### 3.2.2 Cpk as the Minimum of the Two Sides

By definition:

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl}) = \min(0.556, 1.667) = 0.556$$

### 3.2.3 Engineering Conclusion

The process is now **incapable** ($C_{pk} < 1.0$) on the upper side. A corrective action (re-centring) is required before the production run.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **Cp remained 1.111** (it does not depend on μ), but **Cpk fell to 0.556**. This is the exact reason both indices are needed in the SQC system.

## 3.3 Derivation: From Sigma Level to DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)

### 3.3.1 Conceptual Basis

In Six-Sigma methodology, the **1.5σ shift** is conventionally included to account for long-term drift. For a process operating at a short-term Z-score, the long-term Z is:

$$Z_{LT} = Z_{ST} + 1.5$$

### 3.3.2 Worked Example

A process has $Z_{ST} = 4.5$. Then:

$$Z_{LT} = 4.5 + 1.5 = 6.0$$

For a one-sided specification at $Z = 6.0$, the tail probability from the standard normal table is:

$$P(X > 6\sigma) = 3.4 \times 10^{-6}$$

Converting to DPMO:

$$DPMO = 3.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^6 = 3.4\ \text{DPMO}$$

Hence the famous Six-Sigma target: **3.4 defects per million opportunities**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Quality Objective Metric

| Scenario | Best Metric | Why? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Continuous variable, two-sided spec | Cpk | Penalises both spread & centre | Shaft diameter |
| Continuous variable, one-sided spec | Cp (one-sided form) | Only one limit matters | Tensile strength (≥ limit) |
| Attribute (pass/fail) data | DPMO, Yield | No underlying scale | PCB functionality |
| Multi-stage process | RTY (Rolled Throughput Yield) | Accounts for yield loss per stage | Assembly line |
| Service process | σ of waiting time | Variability matters most | Bank queue time |
| Long-term overall performance | Pp, Ppk | Uses s, not σ | Quarterly review |

## 3.5 Master Problem: Calculate Cpk, DPMO, and Classify Process

**Problem (Typical KTU Numerical):**
A CNC turning operation produces pins. The diameter must lie between **19.90 mm and 20.10 mm**. A sample of 25 pins yields a mean diameter of **20.02 mm** and standard deviation **0.018 mm**. The process is normally distributed.

**Step 1 — Specification Width:**

$$USL - LSL = 20.10 - 19.90 = 0.20\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Cp:**

$$C_p = \frac{0.20}{6 \times 0.018} = \frac{0.20}{0.108} \approx 1.852$$

**Step 3 — Cpu and Cpl:**

$$C_{pu} = \frac{20.10 - 20.02}{3 \times 0.018} = \frac{0.08}{0.054} \approx 1.481$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{20.02 - 19.90}{3 \times 0.018} = \frac{0.12}{0.054} \approx 2.222$$

**Step 4 — Cpk:**

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.481, 2.222) = 1.481$$

**Step 5 — Z-LT (Long-term):**

$$Z_{ST} = C_{pk} \times 3 = 1.481 \times 3 = 4.444$$

$$Z_{LT} = Z_{ST} + 1.5 = 4.444 + 1.5 = 5.944 \approx 5.94$$

**Step 6 — DPMO (one-tail approximation using a standard normal table):**

For Z = 5.94, the upper tail probability is approximately **1.46 × 10⁻⁹**, but a two-sided DPMO is roughly **2.92 × 10⁻⁹ × 10⁶ ≈ 2.9 DPMO**.

**Step 7 — Classification:**

- $C_{pk} = 1.481$ → falls in the **"Capable"** band (1.33 – 1.67) → roughly a **4σ to 5σ** process.
- Estimated DPMO is very low → the process is **acceptable** but could be improved to reach a 6σ level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Marking Pattern:** Marks are awarded for (i) correct formula selection, (ii) correct substitution, (iii) correct numerical evaluation, and (iv) engineering interpretation. Skipping the interpretation line typically costs 1–2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Quality Objectives. State any four characteristics of a well-formulated quality objective.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Quality Objectives are specific, measurable, and time-bound goals that an organization sets to achieve the desired level of quality in its products, services, and processes. They translate the *Quality Policy* into actionable targets that can be monitored using statistical quality control tools.

**Four Characteristics (SMART):**

1. **Specific** — clearly defined and unambiguous (e.g., "Reduce solder defects by 30%" rather than "Improve quality").
2. **Measurable** — quantifiable in numbers (defects, %, ppm, sigma level).
3. **Achievable** — realistic given current technology and resources.
4. **Relevant** — directly linked to customer requirements and business strategy.
5. **Time-bound** — associated with a deadline (quarterly, annual).

*(Any four of the above with brief explanation fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Quality Policy and Quality Objectives.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis of Difference | Quality Policy | Quality Objectives |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Overall *intent* and *direction* | Specific, *measurable* targets |
| **Scope** | Organization-wide, broad | Function / process / product-specific |
| **Source** | Issued by top management | Cascaded from the policy |
| **Time Horizon** | Long-term, stable | Short to medium term, reviewed periodically |
| **Quantification** | Qualitative statements | Quantitative metrics (Cp, Cpk, DPMO) |
| **Example** | "We are committed to customer satisfaction" | "Reduce customer complaints to ≤ 50 per quarter" |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**(a)** Discuss the concept of Quality Objectives in an organization. Explain the **SMART** framework with one example for each letter. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Explain the **cascading (hierarchy) of Quality Objectives** from the strategic level to the operational level, with a suitable example from a manufacturing industry. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Concept of Quality Objectives and SMART Framework (7 Marks)**

**Concept (3 marks):**
Quality Objectives are the *operational translation* of the Quality Policy. They specify *what* must be achieved, *by when*, and *how it will be measured*. In the SQC context, they provide the numerical targets that drive control charts, process capability studies, and improvement projects (DMAIC). ISO 9001:2015 mandates that quality objectives must be measurable, monitored, communicated, and consistent with the quality policy.

**SMART Framework (4 marks — 0.8 each):**

| Letter | Meaning | Example (Bearing Manufacturing) |
|---|---|---|
| **S** | Specific | Reduce *inner-race diameter* defects of model 6205 bearings |
| **M** | Measurable | Defect rate ≤ 0.5% |
| **A** | Achievable | Achievable through tighter grinding wheel dressing |
| **R** | Relevant | Linked to customer noise/vibration complaints |
| **T** | Time-bound | Achieve by end of Q3 2025 |

This SMART example connects a vague aspiration ("make better bearings") to a concrete, statistically trackable target.

---

**(b) Cascading of Quality Objectives (7 Marks)**

The **Hoshin Kanri / Juran Cascade** deploys objectives across three levels:

**Level 1 — Strategic (3 Marks):** Set by top management. Long horizon (3–5 years).
- *Example:* "Achieve **Six-Sigma quality level** (≤ 3.4 DPMO) across all product lines by 2027."

**Level 2 — Tactical (2 Marks):** Set by middle managers. Horizon of 6–12 months.
- *Manufacturing:* "Achieve Cpk ≥ 1.5 on the grinding process by Dec 2025."
- *Quality Assurance:* "Reduce supplier rejection rate to ≤ 0.2% by Dec 2025."
- *HR:* "Train 100% of shop-floor operators on SPC by Q2 2025."

**Level 3 — Operational (2 Marks):** Set by supervisors and operators. Daily/weekly horizon.
- *Shift Operator:* "Keep all X̄-R chart points within control limits during my shift."
- *Inspector:* "Zero reworks on Line 3 today."

**Cascading Logic:** The strategic goal is decomposed into tactical sub-goals per function, which are further decomposed into operational micro-goals per workstation. The **same metric** is used at every level (e.g., Cpk, DPMO) but the *target value* becomes progressively more specific. The cascade is verified through *catchball* — bilateral negotiation between levels to ensure feasibility and alignment.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**(a)** Explain **Juran's Quality Trilogy** in detail and show how Quality Objectives are central to all three processes. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** What is the **Cost of Quality (COQ)**? Describe its four components with examples. Explain how the concept of *optimal quality level* guides the setting of quality objectives. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Juran's Quality Trilogy (7 Marks)**

Juran identified three universal processes of quality management. They form a closed loop:

1. **Quality Planning (QP) — 2 Marks**
   - Identify the *customers* (internal and external).
   - Determine their *needs*.
   - Develop the *product* and *process* capable of meeting those needs.
   - **Establish Quality Objectives** — this is the *output* of QP.

2. **Quality Control (QC) — 2 Marks**
   - Choose the *control subject* (what to measure — e.g., Cpk, defect rate).
   - Choose the *measurement unit* (DPMO, ppm, %).
   - Set *performance goals* (the Quality Objective).
   - Measure actual performance, *compare* with the objective, and act on the difference.

3. **Quality Improvement (QI) — 2 Marks**
   - Establish the *infrastructure* (project teams, training, tools).
   - Identify *improvement projects* (Pareto analysis, FMEA).
   - Establish *project teams* and provide resources.
   - Establish *controls* to hold the gains (revised control limits, updated SOPs).

**Why Objectives are Central (1 Mark):** Every stage uses a quality objective. QP *creates* it, QC *monitors* it, QI *raises* it. The trilogy is a closed loop — improvements in QI feed back into the next QP cycle.

---

**(b) Cost of Quality — COQ (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 Mark):** COQ is the total cost incurred by an organization to ensure product/service quality plus the costs arising from failures in quality. It is the financial language of quality objectives.

**Four Components (4 Marks):**

| Component | Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Prevention (P)** | Cost of avoiding defects | Operator training, quality planning, supplier audits |
| **Appraisal (A)** | Cost of detecting defects | Incoming inspection, in-process testing, gauge calibration |
| **Internal Failure (IF)** | Cost of defects found before delivery | Scrap, rework, re-inspection, machine downtime |
| **External Failure (EF)** | Cost of defects reaching customer | Warranty, returns, recalls, lawsuits, lost goodwill |

**Optimal Quality Level (2 Marks):**
As prevention spending (P) increases, both appraisal and failure costs decrease (failure costs drop dramatically). The **Total COQ curve** is U-shaped. The **optimum** is the point where the marginal cost of prevention equals the marginal savings in failure costs. This optimum is the *economic* quality objective. Setting the objective too low (high defects) inflates IF and EF; setting it too high (over-engineering) inflates P and A. The **optimal level** balances customer satisfaction with financial viability.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where KTU Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Quality Policy with Quality Objectives** — they are *not* the same. Policy is intent; objectives are targets.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift** in Six-Sigma DPMO calculations → losing 1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Wrong Cpk formula** — using Cp instead of Cpk for off-centre processes.
> 4. **Skipping engineering interpretation** — numerical answer alone is not enough; always state what the value *means*.
> 5. **Vague examples** in SMART — examiners expect a *realistic industrial* example, not "improve education."
> 6. **Cascading without owners** — every objective must have an *owner* and a *deadline*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Quality Objectives (Module 1)**
> - **Definition:** Specific, measurable, time-bound goals derived from the Quality Policy.
> - **SMART:** Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
> - **Cascade:** Strategic (3–5 yrs) → Tactical (6–12 mo) → Operational (daily/weekly).
> - **Juran's Trilogy:** QP, QC, QI — objectives bridge all three.
> - **COQ = P + A + IF + EF**; optimal level is the U-curve minimum.
> - **Cp** uses only the spec width and σ; **Cpk** also penalises off-centre process mean.
> - **Six-Sigma target:** 3.4 DPMO, corresponding to Cpk = 2.0 with 1.5σ shift.
> - **RTY** for multi-stage processes; **FPY** for single-stage.
> - **Owners + deadlines + measurement system** are mandatory for every objective.
> - **Avoid** vague targets, over-ambitious targets, and unmeasurable goals.

---
*End of Module 1 — Topic: Quality Objectives | OEIET611 — Statistical Quality Control | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a "Quality Function"?

In the context of **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)**, the term **Quality Function** encompasses two of the most powerful and mathematically grounded ideas introduced into modern manufacturing and engineering:

**(A) Taguchi's Quality Loss Function (QLF)** — A mathematical model quantifying the **financial loss to society** caused by any deviation of a quality characteristic from its ideal target value.

**(B) Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** — A structured planning matrix (the **"House of Quality"**) that translates the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** into engineering specifications, manufacturing operations, and quality control plans.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1 of OEIET611, the *Quality Function* primarily deals with **Taguchi's Quadratic Loss Function**, loss coefficient derivation, average quality loss, and signal-to-noise ratios. QFD appears as a supporting framework for understanding how customer requirements drive quality characteristics.

---

### 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Taguchi's Quality Loss Function:**
A second-order (quadratic) loss function expressed as:

$$
L(y) = k(y - T)^2
$$

where:
- $L(y)$ = Loss incurred by society when the quality characteristic takes value $y$
- $y$ = Actual measured value of the quality characteristic
- $T$ = Target value (ideal nominal specification)
- $k$ = Proportionality constant called the **Quality Loss Coefficient**

**Quality Function Deployment (QFD):**
A systematic methodology introduced by **Yoji Akao (1966)** and later popularized by **Mizuno and Akao (1978)** that transforms qualitative customer demands (WHATs) into quantitative engineering requirements (HOWs), deployment schedules, and operating specifications using the matrix-based **House of Quality** tool.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Philosophy (Taguchi's Paradigm Shift):**
> - *Old View:* Quality is acceptable as long as specifications lie *within* tolerance limits.
> - *Taguchi View:* Every deviation from target causes loss — even within tolerance, and the loss grows **quadratically**.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive Picture

**Analogy 1 — The Car Tyre Pressure:**
Imagine the ideal pressure in a car tyre is **32 PSI** (Target $T = 32$). The legal "pass" range is **30–34 PSI**.
- *Traditional SPC view:* Any pressure between 30 and 34 PSI is "in-spec" → no loss.
- *Taguchi view:* A tyre at 30 PSI wears the shoulder of the tread → less grip → early replacement → customer loss. A tyre at 34 PSI wears the center → less life. Both cause loss that grows quadratically the further we drift from 32 PSI.
- The cost of replacing a worn-out tyre prematurely is the **Loss to Society ($A_0$)**.

**Analogy 2 — Restaurant Coffee (for QFD):**
A customer says, *"I want my coffee strong, hot, and cheap."* This is the **WHAT (Voice of Customer)**. QFD asks the engineering team: **HOW** will we deliver this? Increase coffee grounds (grammage), raise brew temperature, reduce bean cost. The **House of Quality** captures all these relationships in one matrix.

---

### 1.4 Loss Function Curve — Visual Intuition

```text
Loss (₹)
   |                              *
A₀ | - - - - - - - - - - - - - - * - - - - - - - 
   |                          *       *
   |                      *               *
   |                  *                       *
   |              *                               *
   |          *                                       *
   |      *                                               *
   |  *                                                       *
   |*-------------------------------------------------------------*
   0       T-Δ₀       Target (T)        T+Δ₀                    Quality Characteristic (y)
```

- **At target** ($y = T$): Loss = **0** (zero defects → zero loss — Taguchi's ideal).
- **At specification limits** ($y = T \pm \Delta_0$): Loss = **$A_0$** (replacement cost).
- **Outside specifications** ($|y - T| > \Delta_0$): Loss > $A_0$ (catastrophic failure cost).

---

### 1.5 Why Quality Function Matters in Engineering

| Domain | Application of Quality Function |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Tolerancing of shafts, bearings, gears to minimize wear & vibration losses |
| **Electronics** | Controlling resistance, capacitance values for circuit stability |
| **Civil Engineering** | Concrete strength targeting; minimizing cement over-use |
| **Production Engineering** | Optimal setting of CNC machine parameters (Taguchi methods) |
| **Industrial Engineering** | Translating customer requirements into process parameters |

---

### 1.6 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Definitions for KTU Recall)

- **Loss Coefficient ($k$):** Proportionality constant linking squared deviation to monetary loss. Units: **₹ per unit²** of quality characteristic.
- **Functional Tolerance ($\Delta_0$):** Maximum allowable deviation before product is scrapped/replaced.
- **Average Loss ($E[L(y)]$):** Expected financial loss over the production run.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR or $\eta$):** Logarithmic metric for robustness in Taguchi designs.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Building the Taguchi Loss Function — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Loss is continuous, not binary.**
Genichi Taguchi argued that quality loss is not "zero inside specs, infinite outside" (the classical view) but rather **smooth and quadratic**. The simplest curve with minimum at $y = T$ is a parabola:

$$
L(y) = k(y - T)^2 \quad \text{(Loss Function)}
$$

**Step 2 — Boundary condition at tolerance limit.**
By Taguchi's convention, when $y = T \pm \Delta_0$ (functional tolerance boundary), the loss equals the **cost of replacement/repair**, $A_0$:

$$
A_0 = k(\Delta_0)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $k$.**
$$
k = \frac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Define loss at any deviation.**
Substitute $k$ back:

$$
L(y) = \frac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2}(y - T)^2
$$

**Step 5 — Average Loss over a population.**
For a quality characteristic $y$ with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$:

$$
\overline{L} = E[L(y)] = k\left[\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2\right]
$$

This decomposes loss into:
- **Variance loss:** $k\sigma^2$ — loss due to **variability** (precision).
- **Bias loss:** $k(\mu - T)^2$ — loss due to **mis-targeting** (accuracy).

**Step 6 — Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR).**
Taguchi's robustness metric. For *smaller-is-better* characteristics:

$$
\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2\right)
$$

For *nominal-the-best* (target-oriented):

$$
\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\mu^2}{\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 7 — Quality Function Deployment (QFD) Phases.**
1. **House of Quality (HoQ):** Customer requirements ↔ Technical requirements.
2. **Deployment Matrix:** Technical requirements ↔ Component characteristics.
3. **Process Plan:** Component characteristics ↔ Process operations.
4. **Production Plan:** Process operations ↔ Production controls.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete formula inventory** for the *Quality Function* topic. Memorize this verbatim for the KTU exam hall.

| # | Formula | Symbol / Meaning | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L(y) = k(y - T)^2$ | Quality Loss at value $y$ | ₹ | Parabolic; minimum at $y = T$ |
| 2 | $A_0 = k(\Delta_0)^2$ | Loss at tolerance boundary | ₹ | By Taguchi's definition |
| 3 | $k = \dfrac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2}$ | **Quality Loss Coefficient** | ₹ per unit² of $y$ | Anchor formula |
| 4 | $L(y) = \dfrac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2}(y - T)^2$ | Loss in terms of cost & tolerance | ₹ | Practical form |
| 5 | $\overline{L} = k\left[\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2\right]$ | **Average Quality Loss** | ₹ | Variance + Bias squared |
| 6 | $L_{variance} = k\sigma^2$ | Loss from variability alone | ₹ | Reduced by $\downarrow \sigma$ |
| 7 | $L_{bias} = k(\mu - T)^2$ | Loss from off-target | ₹ | Reduced by centering $\mu \to T$ |
| 8 | $\eta = 10\log_{10}(\mu^2/\sigma^2)$ | SNR — Nominal-the-Best (NTB) | dB | Higher is better |
| 9 | $\eta = -10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right)$ | SNR — Smaller-the-Better (STB) | dB | Minimize output |
| 10 | $\eta = -10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum \dfrac{1}{y_i^2}\right)$ | SNR — Larger-the-Better (LTB) | dB | Maximize output |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU:** *"k-cube rule"* — $k$ is calculated **once**, then **used in loss equation**, then **used again in average loss**. Three places, same $k$.

### 2.4 Quality Function Deployment (QFD) — Matrix Components

The **House of Quality** integrates six interlinked matrices:

1. **Customer Requirements (WHATs)** — qualitative needs, ranked by importance.
2. **Engineering Characteristics (HOWs)** — measurable technical attributes.
3. **Relationship Matrix (Center)** — strength of correlation ($\bigcirc$ strong, $\bigtriangleup$ medium, $\bullet$ weak).
4. **Technical Correlation Matrix (Roof)** — interactions among HOWs (strong/weak/negative).
5. **Competitive Assessment** — comparison of customer satisfaction vs. competitors.
6. **Technical Difficulty & Target Values** — final engineering targets.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Taguchi Loss Function

#### 3.1.1 Starting Postulates

Taguchi postulated four conditions on the loss function $L(y)$:

1. **Smoothness:** $L(y)$ is continuous and differentiable.
2. **Zero at target:** $L(T) = 0$ (no loss when on target).
3. **Monotonic increase:** Loss increases as $|y - T|$ increases.
4. **Symmetry:** $L(T + d) = L(T - d)$ for any deviation $d$ (isotropic loss).

The simplest function satisfying all four is a **quadratic polynomial**:

$$
L(y) = a_0 + a_1(y - T) + a_2(y - T)^2
$$

Apply Postulate 2:
$$
L(T) = a_0 + a_1(0) + a_2(0) = a_0 = 0
$$

Apply Postulate 4 (symmetry → first derivative zero at target):

$$
\frac{dL}{dy} = a_1 + 2a_2(y - T)
$$

At $y = T$:
$$
\frac{dL}{dy}\bigg|_{y=T} = a_1 = 0
$$

Therefore:
$$
L(y) = a_2(y - T)^2
$$

Renaming $a_2 = k$ (the loss coefficient):

$$
\boxed{L(y) = k(y - T)^2}
$$

#### 3.1.2 Boundary-Condition Derivation of $k$

By Taguchi's convention, at $y = T + \Delta_0$, the loss equals the cost of replacement $A_0$:

$$
A_0 = L(T + \Delta_0) = k(\Delta_0)^2
$$

Solving for $k$:

$$
\boxed{k = \frac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2}}
$$

#### 3.1.3 Derivation of Average Loss $E[L(y)]$

Assume $y$ is a random variable with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{L} = E[L(y)] &= E\left[k(y - T)^2\right] \\
&= k \cdot E\left[(y - T)^2\right] \\
&= k \cdot E\left[\big((y - \mu) + (\mu - T)\big)^2\right] \\
&= k \cdot E\left[(y - \mu)^2 + 2(y - \mu)(\mu - T) + (\mu - T)^2\right] \\
&= k \cdot \Big[\underbrace{E[(y - \mu)^2]}_{\sigma^2} + \underbrace{2(\mu - T)E[y - \mu]}_{0} + (\mu - T)^2\Big] \\
&= k\left[\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The cross term vanishes because $E[y - \mu] = 0$.

$$
\boxed{\overline{L} = k\sigma^2 + k(\mu - T)^2}
$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

#### **Example 1 — Calculating Loss Coefficient**

> **[KTU University Exam Style — 7 Marks]**
>
> A bearing shaft has a target diameter of **50.00 mm**. The functional tolerance is $\pm 0.05$ mm. If the cost of replacing a defective shaft is **₹400**, calculate:
> (a) The quality loss coefficient $k$.
> (b) The loss incurred if a shaft measures 50.03 mm.
> (c) The loss if a shaft measures 50.10 mm.

**Given:**
- $T = 50.00$ mm
- $\Delta_0 = 0.05$ mm
- $A_0 = ₹400$
- $y_1 = 50.03$ mm
- $y_2 = 50.10$ mm

**Step (a): Loss Coefficient**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2} \\
&= \frac{400}{(0.05)^2} \\
&= \frac{400}{0.0025} \\
&= 160{,}000 \;\text{₹ per mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b): Loss at $y = 50.03$ mm**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(50.03) &= k(y - T)^2 \\
&= 160{,}000 \times (50.03 - 50.00)^2 \\
&= 160{,}000 \times (0.03)^2 \\
&= 160{,}000 \times 0.0009 \\
&= ₹144
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c): Loss at $y = 50.10$ mm**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(50.10) &= 160{,}000 \times (50.10 - 50.00)^2 \\
&= 160{,}000 \times (0.10)^2 \\
&= 160{,}000 \times 0.0100 \\
&= ₹1{,}600
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even though 50.10 mm is *outside* specification (a 4× tolerance breach), the loss is **11.1× the loss at 50.03 mm** (still inside spec). This demonstrates Taguchi's central claim — loss grows quadratically, not stepwise.

---

#### **Example 2 — Average Loss with Variance and Bias**

> **[KTU University Exam Style — 7 Marks]**
>
> A process produces resistors with target resistance $T = 100\,\Omega$. Historical data shows mean resistance $\mu = 101\,\Omega$ with $\sigma = 1.5\,\Omega$. The replacement cost at the tolerance limit $\Delta_0 = 5\,\Omega$ is ₹500. Calculate:
> (a) Loss coefficient $k$.
> (b) Total average quality loss.
> (c) The percentage of loss attributable to bias vs. variability.

**Step (a):**

$$
k = \frac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2} = \frac{500}{25} = 20 \; \text{₹ per }\Omega^2
$$

**Step (b): Total Average Loss**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{L} &= k\left[\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2\right] \\
&= 20\left[(1.5)^2 + (101 - 100)^2\right] \\
&= 20\left[2.25 + 1.00\right] \\
&= 20 \times 3.25 \\
&= ₹65
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c): Percentage Decomposition**

Variance Loss:
$$
L_v = k\sigma^2 = 20 \times 2.25 = ₹45
$$

Bias Loss:
$$
L_b = k(\mu - T)^2 = 20 \times 1 = ₹20
$$

Percentages:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\;Variance &= \frac{45}{65} \times 100 \approx 69.23\% \\
\%\;Bias &= \frac{20}{65} \times 100 \approx 30.77\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision:** Since variance contributes ~69% of the loss, the first priority is to **reduce process variability** (e.g., better machine calibration) before re-centering the mean. This is a classic Taguchi insight tested in KTU exams.

---

#### **Example 3 — Signal-to-Noise Ratio (NTB)**

> **[KTU University Exam Style — 5 Marks]**
>
> Five measurements of bolt diameter (target $T = 12$ mm) are: 12.1, 11.9, 12.0, 12.2, 11.8. Calculate the SNR assuming nominal-the-best.

**Step 1: Compute mean and variance.**

$$
\mu = \frac{12.1 + 11.9 + 12.0 + 12.2 + 11.8}{5} = 12.00
$$

$$
\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum(y_i - \mu)^2}{n - 1} = \frac{(0.1)^2 + (-0.1)^2 + (0)^2 + (0.2)^2 + (-0.2)^2}{4}
$$

$$
\sigma^2 = \frac{0.01 + 0.01 + 0 + 0.04 + 0.04}{4} = \frac{0.10}{4} = 0.025
$$

**Step 2: Apply SNR formula.**

$$
\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\mu^2}{\sigma^2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{144}{0.025}\right)
$$

$$
\eta = 10 \log_{10}(5760) \approx 10 \times 3.7604 = 37.604 \; \text{dB}
$$

---

### 3.3 Validation Matrix — All Loss-Function Cases

| Case | Scenario | Formula Used | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| At target | $y = T$ | $L = k(0)^2$ | ₹0 |
| Inside spec | $T - \Delta_0 < y < T + \Delta_0$ | $L = k(y - T)^2 < A_0$ | Non-zero loss (Taguchi novelty) |
| At boundary | $y = T \pm \Delta_0$ | $L = k(\Delta_0)^2 = A_0$ | ₹$A_0$ (replacement cost) |
| Outside spec | $\vert y - T \vert > \Delta_0$ | $L = k(y - T)^2 > A_0$ | Excessive loss — process out of control |
| Average | Production run | $\overline{L} = k[\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2]$ | Expected monetary loss |

---

### 3.4 QFD — Numerical Deployment Example

> **[KTU Conceptual — 5 Marks]**
>
> A laptop manufacturer collects top 5 customer requirements (WHATs) and 4 engineering characteristics (HOWs). Build the relationship matrix and prioritize.

| Customer Requirement (WHAT) → | Battery Life | Weight | Speed | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Weight (kg)** | $\triangle$ | $\bigcirc$ | $\bullet$ | $\bullet$ |
| **Battery Life (hrs)** | $\bigcirc$ | $\triangle$ | $\bullet$ | $\bullet$ |
| **Processing Speed** | $\bullet$ | $\bullet$ | $\bigcirc$ | $\bullet$ |
| **Affordability** | $\bullet$ | $\triangle$ | $\bullet$ | $\bigcirc$ |
| **Durability** | $\bullet$ | $\bigcirc$ | $\triangle$ | $\bullet$ |

Where $\bigcirc = 9$ (strong), $\triangle = 3$ (medium), $\bullet = 1$ (weak).

This matrix then drives the **technical priorities** that engineers optimize using Taguchi DOE — directly linking **Quality Function Deployment** to **Statistical Quality Control**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Taguchi Loss Function Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Generates Quality Characteristic y] --> B[Compute Deviation from Target: e = y - T]
    B --> C{Is |e| greater than Delta0?}
    C -- No --> D[In-spec but Loss greater than 0]
    C -- Yes --> E[Outside-spec: Severe Loss]
    D --> F[Apply Loss Function: L = k * e squared]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute Loss Coefficient k = A0 / Delta0 squared]
    G --> H[Average Loss: k * sigma squared + k * bias squared]
    H --> I[Minimize Loss by reducing sigma and centering mu]
    I --> J[Improved Quality and Reduced Cost]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Every deviation, however small, contributes to monetary loss. The optimization loop drives **continuous improvement**, the essence of the Taguchi philosophy.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — House of Quality (QFD Matrix Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BlockA[Customer Requirements - WHATs]
        A1[Long Battery Life]
        A2[Light Weight]
        A3[Fast Processor]
        A4[Low Cost]
        A5[High Durability]
    end

    subgraph BlockB[Engineering Characteristics - HOWs]
        B1[Battery Capacity mAh]
        B2[Casing Material Density]
        B3[Clock Speed GHz]
        B4[Bill of Materials Cost]
    end

    subgraph BlockC[Relationship Matrix - Center of House]
        C1[Strong and Medium Correlations]
    end

    subgraph BlockD[Technical Correlation - Roof]
        D1[Positive and Negative Trade-offs]
    end

    subgraph BlockE[Output]
        E1[Engineering Targets]
        E2[Priority Ranking]
    end

    BlockA --> BlockC
    BlockB --> BlockC
    BlockB --> BlockD
    BlockC --> BlockE
    BlockD --> BlockE
```

---

### 4.3 Loss Curve Visualization (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Target T: Zero Loss] -->|Increase y| P2[T plus small d: Small Loss]
    P2 -->|Increase y further| P3[T plus Delta0: Loss equals A0]
    P3 -->|Continue increasing y| P4[y greater than T plus Delta0: Severe Loss]
    P4 -->|Process Out of Control| P5[Catastrophic Failure]
```

---

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — From Voice of Customer to Controlled Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VOC[Voice of Customer VOC] --> QFD[Quality Function Deployment]
    QFD --> TR[Technical Requirements]
    TR --> DOE[Design of Experiments]
    DOE --> SNR[Compute Signal to Noise Ratio]
    SNR --> QLF[Taguchi Loss Function Analysis]
    QLF --> CTRL[Process Control and Optimization]
    CTRL --> PROD[Improved Product]
    PROD -->|Feedback| VOC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **closed-loop quality system** envisioned by Taguchi and Akao. Every block is a KTU-exam relevant concept; expect questions linking any two of them.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]**
Define the Taguchi Quality Loss Function. Explain the meaning of each term with an industrial example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Taguchi Quality Loss Function is a mathematical model that quantifies the economic loss to society caused by deviation of a quality characteristic from its target value. It is given by:

$$
L(y) = k(y - T)^2
$$

where $L(y)$ is the loss incurred, $y$ is the actual value, $T$ is the target value, and $k$ is the loss coefficient. **Example:** For a resistor with target resistance $100\,\Omega$, any deviation — even within tolerance — causes loss due to reduced circuit performance or premature failure. This loss grows quadratically with deviation.

---

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
What is a "Smaller-the-Better" Signal-to-Noise Ratio? State its formula.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Smaller-the-Better (STB) SNR is used when the goal is to **minimize** the response (e.g., defects, wear, emissions). The formula is:

$$
\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2\right)
$$

where $y_i$ are the observed values and $n$ is the sample size. A **higher (less negative)** $\eta$ indicates better performance.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive Taguchi's Quality Loss Function starting from the four postulates. Show that the loss coefficient $k = A_0/(\Delta_0)^2$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A piston ring has target diameter 75.00 mm. The functional tolerance is $\pm 0.10$ mm, and the replacement cost of a defective ring is ₹900. A batch of rings has mean diameter 75.04 mm with standard deviation 0.05 mm. Calculate:
- (i) The loss coefficient $k$.
- (ii) The average quality loss per ring.
- (iii) The percentage contribution of bias and variance to total loss.
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

Step 1: Assume $L(y)$ is a smooth, continuous, differentiable function. Expand as polynomial:

$$
L(y) = a_0 + a_1(y - T) + a_2(y - T)^2 + \cdots
$$

Step 2: Postulate 2 → $L(T) = 0$ → $a_0 = 0$.

Step 3: Postulate 4 (symmetry) → derivative zero at target:

$$
\frac{dL}{dy}\bigg|_{y = T} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; a_1 = 0
$$

Step 4: Ignoring higher-order terms (parsimony), we get:

$$
L(y) = a_2(y - T)^2 = k(y - T)^2
$$

Step 5: At tolerance boundary $y = T + \Delta_0$, loss = $A_0$:

$$
A_0 = k(\Delta_0)^2 \;\Rightarrow\; k = \frac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical (7 Marks):**

(i) Loss coefficient:

$$
k = \frac{900}{(0.10)^2} = \frac{900}{0.01} = 90{,}000 \; \text{₹/mm}^2
$$

(ii) Average loss:

$$
\overline{L} = k[\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2] = 90{,}000\left[(0.05)^2 + (0.04)^2\right]
$$

$$
\overline{L} = 90{,}000 \times [0.0025 + 0.0016] = 90{,}000 \times 0.0041 = ₹369
$$

(iii) Decomposition:

Variance Loss: $90{,}000 \times 0.0025 = ₹225$ → **61%**

Bias Loss: $90{,}000 \times 0.0016 = ₹144$ → **39%**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting to square deviations** — Many students write $k(y - T)$ instead of $k(y - T)^2$. This is the **single most common** mark-loss error in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Mixing up units of $k$** — Always state units explicitly (e.g., ₹ per mm²).
> 3. **Not decomposing into bias vs. variance** — Marks are awarded separately for each component in part (iii).

---

#### **Question B. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Quality Function Deployment (QFD). With a neat sketch, describe the **House of Quality** matrix and list any six components of it. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the following five measurements of a quality characteristic with target $T = 50$ mm: 50.2, 49.9, 50.1, 49.8, 50.0:
- (i) Compute the SNR assuming **Nominal-the-Best**.
- (ii) If the replacement cost at tolerance $\Delta_0 = 0.5$ mm is ₹250, find the loss coefficient $k$.
- (iii) Compute the loss when a unit measures 49.7 mm. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) QFD Explanation (7 Marks):**

**Definition:** Quality Function Deployment (QFD) is a structured planning methodology introduced by Akao (1966) that transforms the **Voice of Customer (VOC)** — qualitative needs — into quantitative engineering specifications and process controls.

**Six Components of the House of Quality:**
1. **Customer Requirements (WHATs)** — left wall: voice of customer.
2. **Engineering Characteristics (HOWs)** — ceiling: technical descriptors.
3. **Relationship Matrix** — central: $\bigcirc$ (strong), $\triangle$ (medium), $\bullet$ (weak).
4. **Technical Correlation Matrix (Roof)** — interactions among HOWs.
5. **Customer Competitive Assessment** — comparison with rival products.
6. **Technical Targets** — final engineering specifications for production.

**Sketch:** (See Section 4.2 of these notes for the Mermaid schematic.)

---

**(b) Numerical (7 Marks):**

Step 1: Mean and variance.

$$
\mu = \frac{50.2 + 49.9 + 50.1 + 49.8 + 50.0}{5} = 50.00
$$

$$
\sigma^2 = \frac{(0.2)^2 + (-0.1)^2 + (0.1)^2 + (-0.2)^2 + (0)^2}{4} = \frac{0.04 + 0.01 + 0.01 + 0.04 + 0}{4} = 0.025
$$

(i) SNR (NTB):

$$
\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\mu^2}{\sigma^2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2500}{0.025}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(100{,}000) = 50 \; \text{dB}
$$

(ii) Loss coefficient:

$$
k = \frac{A_0}{(\Delta_0)^2} = \frac{250}{(0.5)^2} = \frac{250}{0.25} = 1000 \; \text{₹/mm}^2
$$

(iii) Loss at $y = 49.7$ mm:

$$
L = 1000 \times (49.7 - 50.0)^2 = 1000 \times 0.09 = ₹90
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning for QFD Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "Roof" matrix** — Examiners specifically ask for it; missing it costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using $n$ instead of $n-1$** in sample variance — KTU strictly expects the **unbiased estimator**.
> 3. **SNR sign confusion** — Always check whether the characteristic is STB, LTB, or NTB before applying the formula.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rules for KTU Quality Function — Final Day Revision**

- ✅ Taguchi's Loss Function is **always quadratic**: $L(y) = k(y - T)^2$.
- ✅ Loss coefficient formula: $k = A_0/(\Delta_0)^2$ — memorize with units (₹ per unit²).
- ✅ Average Loss decomposes into **Variance Loss + Bias Loss** — never skip the decomposition.
- ✅ At target: **Loss = 0**. At tolerance: **Loss = $A_0$**.
- ✅ Three SNR types: **STB** (smaller the better), **LTB** (larger the better), **NTB** (nominal the best).
- ✅ NTB formula: $\eta = 10\log_{10}(\mu^2/\sigma^2)$ — note: $\sigma^2$ in denominator.
- ✅ QFD = House of Quality = WHATs × HOWs matrix.
- ✅ QFD roof matrix captures **inter-HOW correlations** (positive/negative).
- ✅ Taguchi's paradigm shift: **every deviation costs money** — even within spec.
- ✅ Always **square deviations**, never use absolute values in loss formulas.
- ✅ Standard KTU marks split: 3 marks for definition, 7 marks for derivation, 7 marks for numerical.
- ✅ Include **units** in every numerical answer — unmarked answers lose marks in KTU valuation.
- ✅ For NTB SNR, use **$n-1$** in variance denominator (unbiased estimator).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Quality Cost (Cost of Quality — COQ)** is defined as the total expenditure incurred by an organization in ensuring that its products and services conform to specified quality requirements, plus the losses incurred when products fail to meet those requirements. In the KTU 2024 OEIET611 syllabus, quality costs are formally classified into two broad domains:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Conformance** — Money spent *preventing* poor quality and *appraising* the product to confirm it meets specifications.
> **Cost of Non-Conformance** — Money lost because products *failed* to meet specifications (caught internally or reported externally).

The most widely accepted industrial framework is the **PAF Model**, which divides all quality costs into four categories:

| Acronym | Category | Nature |
|---------|----------|--------|
| **P** | Prevention Costs | Cost of Conformance (Proactive) |
| **A** | Appraisal Costs | Cost of Conformance (Detective) |
| **IFC** | Internal Failure Costs | Cost of Non-Conformance (Internal) |
| **EFC** | External Failure Costs | Cost of Non-Conformance (External) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Cost of Quality (TCQ)** is the sum of all four categories:
> $$\text{TCQ} = P + A + \text{IFC} + \text{EFC}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are running a small **bakery** in Kerala that supplies banana cakes to local cafés. Your "quality" target is the cake must weigh **exactly 200 g ± 5 g** and look visually presentable.

- **Prevention Costs (P)** — You hire a pastry chef to design a foolproof recipe, train bakers, and maintain the oven correctly. *You spend money now so defects do not happen later.*
- **Appraisal Costs (A)** — You place every cake on a digital scale after baking. You hire a taster to check flavor. *You spend money to detect defects that did happen.*
- **Internal Failure Costs (IFC)** — A cake weighing 190 g is found. You throw it away or sell it at a discount. *You lost money because the defect was caught inside your bakery.*
- **External Failure Costs (EFC)** — A 180 g cake somehow reaches a customer. The café returns it, demands compensation, and stops ordering for two months. *You lost far more money because the defect escaped to the customer.*

The key insight: **every Rupee spent on prevention saves several Rupees of failure cost.** This is the central economic argument of quality cost management and the foundation of Total Quality Management (TQM).

## 1.3 Key Industrial Metrics Highlighted in **Bold**

- **Prevention-to-Failure Cost Ratio (PFR)** — ratio of proactive (P + A) costs to reactive (IFC + EFC) costs. World-class manufacturers target **PFR ≥ 1.0**.
- **COPQ as % of Sales** — globally benchmarked between **2.5 % to 15 %** of revenue, with the median in mature industries at **4 %–6 %**.
- **Zero Defects Target (ZDT)** — a philosophy, not a metric, aimed at making IFC = EFC ≈ 0.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hidden Quality Costs** (opportunity cost of lost customers, employee morale, brand erosion, warranty overhead) are estimated by experts (Juran, 1951) to be **4 to 10 times** the visible quality costs. KTU questions frequently test this concept.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PAF Model — Structured Decomposition

The **PAF (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) Model** was popularized by **A.V. Feigenbaum** (1956) and is the dominant framework used by the American Society for Quality (ASQ) and ISO 9001 auditing bodies.

### 2.1.1 Prevention Costs (P)
Costs incurred to **prevent** defects from occurring. The most strategically valuable category because every Rupee here saves multiple Rupees downstream.
- Quality planning and engineering
- Training of personnel in quality
- Process capability studies
- Supplier capability audits
- Design of experiments (DOE)
- Preventive maintenance
- Quality improvement projects (Six Sigma, Kaizen)

### 2.1.2 Appraisal Costs (A)
Costs incurred to **detect** defects that have already occurred.
- Incoming raw-material inspection
- In-process inspection
- Final product acceptance sampling
- Equipment calibration
- Testing laboratories
- Quality audits

### 2.1.3 Internal Failure Costs (IFC)
Costs of defects caught **before** the product reaches the customer.
- Scrap and re-work
- Re-inspection and re-testing
- Downgrading of products
- Material wastage
- Machine downtime due to defects
- Investigation of root causes

### 2.1.4 External Failure Costs (EFC)
Costs of defects discovered **after** the product has reached the customer. The most damaging category, often 3–10 × the cost of an equivalent internal failure.
- Warranty claims
- Field repairs and recalls
- Returned goods
- Concessions, discounts, settlements
- Customer complaints and legal liability
- Loss of goodwill, market share erosion

## 2.2 Quality Cost Behaviour Curves (Why and How)

As an organization moves from low quality to high quality, the four cost curves behave differently. The horizontal axis is the **Quality Level (Q)** of the product (probability of being defect-free or conformance level), and the vertical axis is **Cost in ₹**.

- **P (Prevention) cost** — **Increases** monotonically with Q. More prevention effort = more training, more planning.
- **A (Appraisal) cost** — **Increases** monotonically with Q. To achieve very high quality, exhaustive 100 % inspection is needed.
- **IFC (Internal Failure) cost** — **Decreases** steeply with Q. Better quality means fewer in-house failures.
- **EFC (External Failure) cost** — **Decreases** most steeply with Q. Better quality means fewer customer complaints.
- **TCQ (Total Cost)** — **U-shaped (convex) curve** with a single minimum at the **Optimal Quality Level (Q\*)**.

## 2.3 The Optimal Quality Level (Q\*) — First-Order Optimization

At the optimum, the **marginal cost of improving quality equals the marginal savings from reduced failure costs**. Mathematically:

$$\left. \frac{d(\text{TCQ})}{dQ} \right|_{Q=Q^*} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left. \frac{d(P+A)}{dQ} \right|_{Q=Q^*} = -\left. \frac{d(\text{IFC}+\text{EFC})}{dQ} \right|_{Q=Q^*}$$

Geometrically, the optimum occurs where the **increasing slope of (P + A)** equals the **decreasing slope of (IFC + EFC)**. In practice, an organization with too many complaints will invest in more prevention; once complaints drop to a low base, further prevention spending gives diminishing returns and the **economic optimum is reached**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|--------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Total Cost of Quality (TCQ) | $\text{TCQ} = P + A + \text{IFC} + \text{EFC}$ | ₹ | Fundamental identity |
| 2 | Cost of Conformance (COC) | $\text{COC} = P + A$ | ₹ | Proactive spend |
| 3 | Cost of Non-Conformance (CONC) | $\text{CONC} = \text{IFC} + \text{EFC}$ | ₹ | Reactive loss |
| 4 | COPQ as % of Sales | $\text{COPQ\%} = \frac{\text{TCQ}}{\text{Total Sales}} \times 100$ | % | Industry benchmark 2.5 %–15 % |
| 5 | Prevention-to-Failure Ratio (PFR) | $\text{PFR} = \frac{P + A}{\text{IFC} + \text{EFC}}$ | Ratio | World-class $\geq 1$ |
| 6 | Optimal Quality Condition | $\frac{d(P+A)}{dQ} = -\frac{d(\text{IFC}+\text{EFC})}{dQ}$ | — | Sets $Q^*$ |
| 7 | Failure Cost Ratio (FCR) | $\text{FCR} = \frac{\text{EFC}}{\text{IFC} + \text{EFC}}$ | % | Should fall as system matures |
| 8 | Quality Cost Index (QCI) | $\text{QCI} = \frac{\text{CONC}}{\text{COC}}$ | — | Goal: QCI → 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ is used in formulae (e.g., $Q \vert_{\text{optimum}}$) to maintain proper mathematical readability. In KTU answer sheets, write **TCQ = P + A + IFC + EFC** clearly; do not collapse P and A into a single symbol.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ISO 9001:2015** mandates that organizations measure and report quality costs in clause 9.1.3 (analysis and evaluation). Understanding COQ is therefore essential for any certified quality engineer in Kerala's manufacturing belt.
- **Lean Six Sigma Green Belt / Black Belt** projects begin with a **COPQ baseline** to justify financial returns to management.
- **Defence aerospace and automotive (e.g., TCS, Tata Elxsi, VSSC)** projects use COQ to decide between 100 % inspection and sampling plans. A 14-mark KTU question typically asks for the **optimal trade-off** between A-cost and E-cost.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Quality Level (Q\*)

We consider continuous quality cost functions. Let $Q$ denote the conformance level (probability that a unit is defect-free, $0 \leq Q \leq 1$).

### Step 1 — Set Up the Cost Model

$$P(Q) = a \cdot Q^2 \quad \text{(Prevention grows quadratically with strictness of quality)}$$

$$A(Q) = b \cdot Q \quad \text{(Appraisal grows linearly with inspection effort)}$$

$$\text{IFC}(Q) = c \cdot (1 - Q) \quad \text{(Failure cost falls linearly with Q)}$$

$$\text{EFC}(Q) = d \cdot (1 - Q)^2 \quad \text{(External failure cost falls fastest — quadratic)}$$

> Where $a, b, c, d > 0$ are cost coefficients in ₹.

### Step 2 — Formulate the Total Cost

$$\text{TCQ}(Q) = aQ^2 + bQ + c(1-Q) + d(1-Q)^2$$

### Step 3 — Expand Each Term

For **P(Q)**:
$$P(Q) = a Q^2$$

For **A(Q)**:
$$A(Q) = b Q$$

For **IFC(Q)**:
$$\text{IFC}(Q) = c(1 - Q) = c - cQ$$

For **EFC(Q)**:
$$\text{EFC}(Q) = d(1 - Q)^2 = d(1 - 2Q + Q^2) = d - 2dQ + dQ^2$$

### Step 4 — Combine All Four

$$\text{TCQ}(Q) = aQ^2 + bQ + c - cQ + d - 2dQ + dQ^2$$

### Step 5 — Group Like Powers of Q

$$\text{TCQ}(Q) = (a + d)Q^2 + (b - c - 2d)Q + (c + d)$$

This is a **parabola opening upwards** (since $a + d > 0$), so a unique minimum exists.

### Step 6 — Differentiate w.r.t. Q

$$\frac{d(\text{TCQ})}{dQ} = 2(a + d)Q + (b - c - 2d)$$

### Step 7 — Set the First Derivative to Zero

$$2(a + d)Q^* + (b - c - 2d) = 0$$

### Step 8 — Solve for Q\*

$$Q^* = \frac{c + 2d - b}{2(a + d)}$$

### Step 9 — Second-Derivative Test

$$\frac{d^2(\text{TCQ})}{dQ^2} = 2(a + d) > 0 \quad \text{(since } a,d > 0 \text{)}$$

Therefore $Q^*$ is a **strict global minimum** — the **Optimum Quality Level**.

### Step 10 — Optimal Total Cost Substituted Back

$$\text{TCQ}_{\min} = (a + d)(Q^*)^2 + (b - c - 2d)Q^* + (c + d)$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

A KTU-style 14-mark problem:

> A manufacturing unit has the following quality cost functions (₹ per unit):
> $P = 100 Q^2$, $A = 60 Q$, $\text{IFC} = 200 (1 - Q)$, $\text{EFC} = 400 (1 - Q)^2$.
> Find the optimal quality level and minimum TCQ.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify coefficients: $a = 100$, $b = 60$, $c = 200$, $d = 400$.

Step 2 — Apply the optimal-quality formula:

$$Q^* = \frac{c + 2d - b}{2(a + d)} = \frac{200 + 2(400) - 60}{2(100 + 400)} = \frac{200 + 800 - 60}{1000} = \frac{940}{1000} = 0.94$$

**The optimal conformance level is 94 %.**

Step 3 — Compute each cost at $Q^* = 0.94$:

$$P(0.94) = 100 \times (0.94)^2 = 100 \times 0.8836 = ₹\;88.36$$

$$A(0.94) = 60 \times 0.94 = ₹\;56.40$$

$$\text{IFC}(0.94) = 200 \times (1 - 0.94) = 200 \times 0.06 = ₹\;12.00$$

$$\text{EFC}(0.94) = 400 \times (1 - 0.94)^2 = 400 \times 0.0036 = ₹\;1.44$$

Step 4 — Sum to obtain minimum TCQ:

$$\text{TCQ}_{\min} = 88.36 + 56.40 + 12.00 + 1.44 = ₹\;158.20 \text{ per unit}$$

Step 5 — Interpretation: If the firm targets $Q = 0.99$ (higher), TCQ rises (P grows quadratically, returns diminish). If it targets $Q = 0.80$, TCQ rises (failure costs explode). **0.94 is the economic sweet spot.**

## 3.3 Hidden Quality Cost Estimation (Opportunity Cost Model)

Juran's framework treats hidden quality costs (HQCs) as an opportunity cost:

$$\text{HQC} = k \times \text{Visible TCQ} \quad \text{where } k \in [4, 10]$$

**Midpoint estimate** $k = 7$:

$$\text{HQC}_{\text{est}} = 7 \times 158.20 = ₹\;1{,}107.40 \text{ per unit}$$

This explains why top managers must look beyond accounting books to grasp the true cost of poor quality.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quality Cost Reduction Strategies

| Strategy | Primary Cost Targeted | Mechanism | Expected ROI |
|----------|-----------------------|-----------|--------------|
| Kaizen / Continuous Improvement | P, IFC | Process variation reduction | Very High |
| Statistical Process Control (SPC) | IFC, EFC | Early defect detection | High |
| Quality Function Deployment (QFD) | EFC, P | Customer-driven design | High |
| Six Sigma DMAIC | P, IFC, EFC | $3.4$ DPMO target | High |
| Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) | IFC | Machine uptime | Medium |
| Supplier Quality Partnerships | IFC, EFC | Input quality improvement | High |
| Design of Experiments (DOE) | P, IFC | Robust design | Very High |
| Poka-Yoke (Mistake-Proofing) | IFC | Error-proof processes | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PAF Model — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    QControl[Quality Management System]
    P[Prevention Costs]
    A[Appraisal Costs]
    IFC[Internal Failure Costs]
    EFC[External Failure Costs]
    TCQ[Total Cost of Quality TCQ]
    OptLevel[Optimal Quality Level Q star]
    Hidden[Hidden Quality Costs HQCs]

    QControl --> P
    QControl --> A
    QControl --> IFC
    QControl --> EFC
    P --> TCQ
    A --> TCQ
    IFC --> TCQ
    EFC --> TCQ
    TCQ --> OptLevel
    TCQ --> Hidden
    P --> COC[Cost of Conformance COC]
    A --> COC
    IFC --> CONC[Cost of Non-Conformance CONC]
    EFC --> CONC
```

## 4.2 Quality Cost Behaviour vs. Conformance Level

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Behaviour[Cost Behaviour as Quality Increases]
        Paxis["P cost INCREASES"]
        Aaxis["A cost INCREASES"]
        IFCaxis["IFC DECREASES"]
        EFCaxis["EFC DECREASES faster"]
        TCQaxis["TCQ is U shaped convex"]
    end
    QAxis[Conformance Level Q 0 to 1]
    Behaviour --> QAxis
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Quality Cost Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Identify Dominant Cost Category]
    Start --> P1{P costs high and failure costs high}
    Start --> P2{EFC dominates external failure}
    Start --> P3{IFC dominates internal failure}
    Start --> P4{A costs very high}
    P1 --> A1[Train workforce improve processes]
    P2 --> A2[Use FMEA and customer feedback loops]
    P3 --> A3[Deploy SPC and poka yoke]
    P4 --> A4[Switch to acceptance sampling plans]
    A1 --> Monitor[Track PFR and COPQ percent]
    A2 --> Monitor
    A3 --> Monitor
    A4 --> Monitor
```

## 4.4 Strategic Mapping of PAF to Process Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Design[Design Stage] -->|Prevention| P1c[P costs]
    Procurement[Procurement] -->|Appraisal| A1c[A costs]
    Production[Production] -->|Appraisal and IFC| B1c[A plus IFC]
    Distribution[Distribution] -->|EFC| C1c[EFC]
    Customer[Customer Use] -->|EFC| C1c
    P1c --> Total[Total Quality Cost]
    A1c --> Total
    B1c --> Total
    C1c --> Total
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OEIET611, 3 Marks]**
*Define the four categories of quality costs in the PAF model and give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Prevention Costs:** Money spent to *prevent* defects. *Example:* Quality training, process capability studies, preventive maintenance.
2. **Appraisal Costs:** Money spent to *detect* defects. *Example:* Incoming inspection, final acceptance sampling, equipment calibration.
3. **Internal Failure Costs:** Losses when defects are caught *before* dispatch. *Example:* Scrap, re-work, re-inspection.
4. **External Failure Costs:** Losses when defects reach the *customer*. *Example:* Warranty claims, returns, legal liability, loss of goodwill.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, OEIET611, 3 Marks]**
*State the formula for Total Cost of Quality. What is the "hidden quality cost" and why is it important?*

**Model Answer:**
$$\text{TCQ} = P + A + \text{IFC} + \text{EFC}$$

**Hidden Quality Costs (HQCs)** are the *unrecorded* quality-related losses such as loss of customer goodwill, employee morale erosion, missed sales opportunities, and brand damage. They are *not* captured in accounting books but are estimated (Juran, 1951) to be **4 to 10 times** the visible TCQ. They matter because they can dwarf the book-kept costs and mislead managers who rely on financial reports alone.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module 1 Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OEIET611, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the PAF (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) model of quality costs in detail. With a neat sketch, show how the four cost components behave as the quality level increases. Identify and mark the optimal quality level.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A small-scale firm has the cost functions (₹ per unit): $P = 80 Q^2$, $A = 40 Q$, $\text{IFC} = 160 (1 - Q)$, $\text{EFC} = 320 (1 - Q)^2$. Find the optimal quality level $Q^*$ and the minimum TCQ. Also estimate the hidden quality cost using Juran's midpoint factor of 7.

#### Model Solution

**(a) PAF Model Behavioural Sketch and Explanation — 7 Marks**

The PAF model classifies all quality-related expenditure into four mutually exclusive categories. As conformance level $Q$ rises (from low to high), the behaviour is:

| Cost | Behaviour vs. Q | Reasoning |
|------|------------------|-----------|
| $P$ | Increases (concave up, polynomial) | Greater prevention effort needed for higher $Q$ |
| $A$ | Increases (approximately linear) | More inspection required to confirm higher $Q$ |
| $\text{IFC}$ | Decreases (linearly) | Fewer in-house failures at higher $Q$ |
| $\text{EFC}$ | Decreases steeply (convex) | Customer-reported failures fall fastest |
| $\text{TCQ}$ | **U-shaped (convex)** | Sum gives unique minimum at $Q^*$ |

The optimal quality level $Q^*$ is the $Q$ at which:

$$\frac{d(P + A)}{dQ} = -\frac{d(\text{IFC} + \text{EFC})}{dQ}$$

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Xaxis[Q axis Conformance Level]
    Curve1["P curve rising concave"]
    Curve2["A curve rising linear"]
    Curve3["IFC curve falling linear"]
    Curve4["EFC curve falling convex steep"]
    Curve5["TCQ curve U shaped with minimum at Q star"]
    Xaxis --> Curve1
    Xaxis --> Curve2
    Xaxis --> Curve3
    Xaxis --> Curve4
    Xaxis --> Curve5
```

(Visualize: $P$ and $A$ rising slowly, IFC and EFC falling; their sum dips to a minimum at $Q^*$ then rises again.)

**(b) Numerical Optimization — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Identify coefficients: $a = 80$, $b = 40$, $c = 160$, $d = 320$.

**Step 2:** Apply the optimal-quality formula derived in Section 3:

$$Q^* = \frac{c + 2d - b}{2(a + d)} = \frac{160 + 2(320) - 40}{2(80 + 320)} = \frac{160 + 640 - 40}{800} = \frac{760}{800} = 0.95$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate each cost at $Q^* = 0.95$:

$$P(0.95) = 80 \times (0.95)^2 = 80 \times 0.9025 = ₹\;72.20$$

$$A(0.95) = 40 \times 0.95 = ₹\;38.00$$

$$\text{IFC}(0.95) = 160 \times (1 - 0.95) = 160 \times 0.05 = ₹\;8.00$$

$$\text{EFC}(0.95) = 320 \times (1 - 0.95)^2 = 320 \times 0.0025 = ₹\;0.80$$

**Step 4:** Minimum TCQ:

$$\text{TCQ}_{\min} = 72.20 + 38.00 + 8.00 + 0.80 = ₹\;119.00 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 5:** Hidden quality cost estimate (Juran's $k = 7$):

$$\text{HQC} = 7 \times 119.00 = ₹\;833.00 \text{ per unit}$$

**Conclusion:** The firm should target a 95 % conformance level and budget ₹119 per unit for visible quality costs. The actual burden, including hidden costs, is closer to ₹952 per unit, justifying investment in prevention programmes.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, OEIET611, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Distinguish between **Cost of Conformance (COC)** and **Cost of Non-Conformance (CONC)**. Discuss, with examples, why shifting the balance from CONC to COC reduces total quality cost in the long run. Mention the strategic role of prevention costs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A process generates a COPQ of ₹18,00,000 per year on annual sales of ₹2,00,00,000. The breakdown is: P = ₹2,00,000, A = ₹3,00,000, IFC = ₹6,00,000, EFC = ₹7,00,000. Calculate (i) COPQ as % of sales, (ii) PFR, (iii) FCR. Comment on the firm's quality maturity.

#### Model Solution

**(a) COC vs. CONC and Strategic Role of Prevention — 7 Marks**

**Cost of Conformance (COC)** = $P + A$. These are planned expenditures *deliberately incurred* to achieve quality. They are visible in the books, are *budgeted*, and produce a tangible return. Example: ₹50,000 spent on operator training prevents ₹3,00,000 in future scrap.

**Cost of Non-Conformance (CONC)** = $\text{IFC} + \text{EFC}$. These are *unplanned losses* — the price the company pays for failing to do things right the first time. They are reactive, painful, and often under-reported.

| Aspect | COC ($P + A$) | CONC ($\text{IFC} + \text{EFC}$) |
|--------|---------------|----------------------------------|
| Nature | Planned, proactive | Unplanned, reactive |
| Visibility | High (in books) | Low (often hidden) |
| Per-unit cost | Low | High, especially EFC |
| Long-term trend | Should *rise* | Should *fall* |
| Managerial attitude | "Investment" | "Loss" |

**Why the Shift Works:** Juran's economic argument is that prevention costs follow the **law of diminishing returns**, while external failure costs grow *super-linearly* with neglect. By moving resources from CONC to COC (especially into P), the firm front-loads spending on the lowest-marginal-cost activity, generating a net decrease in TCQ over the medium term. The PAF model is therefore the *engineering equivalent of compound interest* — a small, early investment in prevention yields a large future reduction in failure cost.

**Strategic Role of Prevention:** Prevention is the *only* category that simultaneously reduces *both* IFC and EFC. It is therefore the highest-leverage spending in any quality programme.

**(b) Numerical Quality Metrics — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $P = 2,00,000$; $A = 3,00,000$; $\text{IFC} = 6,00,000$; $\text{EFC} = 7,00,000$; Total Sales = $₹2,00,00,000$.

**Step 1 — Total COPQ:**

$$\text{TCQ} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}00{,}000 = ₹18{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — COPQ as % of Sales:**

$$\text{COPQ\%} = \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 9.0\,\%$$

This is **higher than the world-class benchmark of 2.5 %–6 %** — the firm has significant improvement potential.

**Step 3 — PFR (Prevention-to-Failure Ratio):**

$$\text{COC} = P + A = 2{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{CONC} = \text{IFC} + \text{EFC} = 6{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}00{,}000 = 13{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{PFR} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{13{,}00{,}000} = 0.385$$

A PFR $< 1$ indicates the firm is **spending too little on prevention relative to failure costs**. World-class manufacturers target $\text{PFR} \geq 1$.

**Step 4 — FCR (External-Failure-to-Total-Failure Ratio):**

$$\text{FCR} = \frac{\text{EFC}}{\text{IFC} + \text{EFC}} = \frac{7{,}00{,}000}{13{,}00{,}000} = 0.538 \;\text{or}\; 53.8\,\%$$

An FCR $> 50\,\%$ means more than half of the failure cost is *external* — the most damaging kind because it has already reached the customer. This is a strong red flag.

**Step 5 — Maturity Commentary:**

| Metric | Computed | Benchmark | Diagnosis |
|--------|----------|-----------|-----------|
| COPQ % | 9.0 % | 2.5 %–6 % | Poor |
| PFR | 0.385 | $\geq 1.0$ | Reactive culture |
| FCR | 53.8 % | $< 25\,\%$ | Severe customer-impact risk |
