**Conclusion:** The firm is in a **reactive stage of quality maturity**. Recommended actions: (i) double the prevention budget (training, SPC, design reviews), (ii) introduce acceptance sampling to reduce EFC leakage, and (iii) implement Six Sigma projects targeting the IFC/EFC hotspots.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing COC with TCQ.** COC is *only* $P + A$, not the full TCQ. Many students write COC = P + A + IFC + EFC, which is wrong.
> 2. **Forgetting to square** the $(1 - Q)$ term in EFC. The standard quadratic model is $\text{EFC} = d(1 - Q)^2$, not $d(1 - Q)$.
> 3. **Mixing up the optimality condition.** The condition is *equal slopes*, not *equal costs*. Students often wrongly equate $P + A = \text{IFC} + \text{EFC}$ — this is *not* the optimum condition.
> 4. **Ignoring units and % in numericals.** Always quote COPQ as % of sales with 2-decimal accuracy; PFR as a pure ratio; FCR as a %.
> 5. **Skipping the second-derivative test** when deriving $Q^*$. Examiners expect to see $\frac{d^2(\text{TCQ})}{dQ^2} > 0$ for confirmation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TCQ = P + A + IFC + EFC** — the four-letter mantra of every KTU COQ problem.
- **COC = P + A**; **CONC = IFC + EFC** — memorize the boundaries; examiners love to test them.
- **PAF model** is the engineering standard, popularized by **Feigenbaum (1956)**; Juran's 1951 framework adds the **hidden cost multiplier** ($4$–$10\times$).
- The **optimal quality level $Q^*$** is *not* 100 % — it is the level at which the marginal cost of prevention/appraisal equals the marginal savings in failure costs.
- **P and A costs increase with $Q$**; **IFC and EFC decrease**; **TCQ is a U-shaped convex curve**.
- **COPQ as % of sales** is the headline metric for the boardroom: 2.5 %–6 % is mature; > 10 % is crisis.
- **PFR $\geq 1$** indicates a *proactive* quality culture; **PFR $< 1$** indicates a *reactive* one.
- **EFC dominates IFC in severity** by a factor of $3$–$10$ — preventing defects from leaving the factory is therefore the highest-leverage activity.
- **Six Sigma, Kaizen, SPC, poka-yoke, DOE, QFD, and TPM** are the seven classic tools for shifting the cost balance from CONC to COC.
- **Optimality condition:** $\frac{d(P+A)}{dQ} = -\frac{d(\text{IFC}+\text{EFC})}{dQ}$ — derived from $\frac{d(\text{TCQ})}{dQ} = 0$ with the second-derivative test $2(a+d) > 0$.
- **Hidden quality costs** are the *real* driver of senior-management attention: always mention Juran's $4$–$10\times$ multiplier when discussing strategic implications.
- **Zero Defects (Crosby, 1979)** is a *philosophy*, not an optimum; the **economic optimum** is what TCQ analysis reveals.
- For KTU 14-mark questions, the **expected structure is**: (i) definition + sketch, (ii) cost model, (iii) derivative and $Q^*$, (iv) numerical substitution, (v) interpretation and managerial insight. Cover all five to secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Quality Management – Philosophies, Models, and Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Total Quality Management – Philosophies, Models, and Implementation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Total Quality Management (TQM) **Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that centers on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and business processes by involving **all members** of the organization — from top management to front-line employees — in a structured, customer-focused framework. It integrates fundamental management techniques, existing improvement efforts, and technical tools under a disciplined approach aimed at long-term success derived from **customer satisfaction** and **societal benefit**. According to the **British Standards Institution (BSI)**, TQM is *"A management philosophy embracing all activities through which the needs of customers, employees, and society are fulfilled through the integrated effort of the whole organization."* > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** TQM is **not** a department; it is an **organization-wide cultural transformation**. Every employee — from the CEO to the sweeper — is responsible for quality. This is what the word *TOTAL* in TQM signifies. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Hospital Operating Theatre Imagine a multi-specialty **hospital**: - The **surgeons** (top management) set the protocols (vision, policy). - The **anaesthetists and nurses** (middle management) execute and monitor. - The **technicians and housekeeping staff** (front-line workers) maintain sterility, instruments, and patient comfort. - The **patient** is the ultimate customer. If any one team fails — say, the sterilization process breaks down — the entire operation fails, regardless of how brilliant the surgeon is. **TQM is exactly this philosophy applied to manufacturing and service industries.** Every link in the chain is responsible for quality, and the success of the organization is judged by the final outcome delivered to the customer. ### 1.3 Core Principles (The Five Pillars of TQM) | S.No | Pillar | Brief Meaning | |------|--------|---------------| | 1 | **Customer Focus** | Quality = Conformance to customer needs (Voice of Customer, VoC) | | 2 | **Total Employee Involvement / Participation** | Every employee owns quality; empowerment via Quality Circles | | 3 | **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** | Incremental, never-ending improvement; PDCA cycle | | 4 | **Process-Centric Approach** | Improve the process, and the output improves automatically | | 5 | **Integrated System** | All departments work as an interconnected supply chain | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric Recognized by KTU Board:** The most universally accepted TQM metric is **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** measured on a **5-point Likert Scale** (1 = Highly Dissatisfied to 5 = Highly Satisfied). A CSI of **≥ 4.5** is considered world-class. ### 1.4 Evolution of Quality Thought (Quick Timeline) | Era | Period | Dominant Philosophy | |-----|--------|---------------------| | Inspection Era | Pre-1900 | 100% inspection, sorting | | Quality Control (QC) | 1900–1940 | Statistical methods (Shewhart) | | Quality Assurance (QA) | 1940–1970 | System-based, documentation | | Strategic Quality Management | 1970–1990 | Cost of quality, strategic planning | | **Total Quality Management (TQM)** | **1980–Present** | **Customer-driven, culture-based, company-wide** | | Six Sigma Era | 1990–Present | Data-driven defect reduction | | Lean Six Sigma | 2000–Present | Waste elimination + defect reduction | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Quality Gurus and Their Philosophies TQM did not emerge spontaneously. It was forged through the life-work of several **"Quality Gurus."** KTU frequently asks direct 3-mark or 7-mark questions comparing these gurus. #### A. Dr. W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993) — The Father of Modern Quality - **Core Philosophy:** Quality is built into the **system**; 94% of failures are due to the system and management, only 6% to workers (the famous **Deming 85/15 Rule**, later refined). - **The Deming Cycle (PDCA):** Plan → Do → Check → Act (also called the **Deming Wheel / Shewhart Cycle**). - **14 Points for Management:** Including "Create constancy of purpose," "Adopt the new philosophy," "Drive out fear," "Institute training on the job," etc. - **Seven Deadly Diseases:** Lack of constancy of purpose, emphasis on short-term profits, evaluation by performance ratings, mobility of management, running a company on visible figures alone, excessive medical costs, excessive costs of warranty. #### B. Joseph M. Juran (1904–2008) — The Customer Champion - **Core Philosophy:** Quality = **Fitness for Use** (defined the first time, replacing the older "conformance to specification"). - **Juran's Quality Trilogy:** 1. **Quality Planning** — Identify customers, determine needs, develop product features. 2. **Quality Control** — Evaluate actual performance vs. goals; act on differences. 3. **Quality Improvement** — Establish infrastructure, identify improvement projects, apply statistical methods. #### C. Philip B. Crosby (1926–2001) — The Zero Defects Proponent - **Core Philosophy:** **"Quality is Free."** The cost of preventing defects is always less than the cost of correcting them. - **Four Absolutes of Quality:** 1. Quality = **Conformance to requirements** (not elegance). 2. Prevention, not inspection. 3. Zero defects performance standard. 4. Measure quality by the **Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)**. #### D. Kaoru Ishikawa (1915–1989) — The Father of Quality Circles - **Core Philosophy:** **Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC)** — quality is everyone's responsibility, including top management. - **Tool Contribution:** **Ishikawa Diagram** (Fishbone / Cause-and-Effect Diagram) — for root-cause analysis. - **Quality Circles:** Small groups of 6–12 workers who meet regularly to solve work-related quality problems. #### E. Armand V. Feigenbaum (1922–2014) — Originator of TQC - First to coin the term **"Total Quality Control"** (1956). - Emphasized **"Quality is everyone's job, not just the inspector's."** - Identified **9 M's of Quality**: Markets, Money, Management, Men, Motivation, Materials, Machines, Methods, Mounting requirements. ### 2.2 TQM Models and Frameworks #### A. EFQM Excellence Model (European Foundation for Quality Management) A non-prescriptive framework based on **9 criteria** divided into: - **5 Enablers** (How results are achieved): Leadership, People, Strategy, Partnerships, Processes, Resources. - **4 Results** (What is achieved): People Results, Customer Results, Society Results, Business Results. **RADAR Logic:** Results → Approach → Deploy → Assess → Refine. #### B. Malcolm Baldrige Criteria for Performance Excellence (USA) Seven categories: 1. Leadership 2. Strategic Planning 3. Customer Focus 4. Measurement, Analysis, and Knowledge Management 5. Workforce Focus 6. Operations Focus 7. Results #### C. ISO 9000 Series A set of **international standards** for Quality Management Systems (QMS), currently the global benchmark. **ISO 9001:2015** is the latest version with **7 Quality Management Principles**: 1. Customer Focus 2. Leadership 3. Engagement of People 4. Process Approach 5. Improvement 6. Evidence-based Decision Making 7. Relationship Management ### 2.3 Cost of Quality (COQ) — The Economic Engine of TQM TQM justifies itself financially through the **Cost of Quality** model, comprising **4 cost categories**: | Category | Description | Example | |----------|-------------|---------| | **Prevention Costs (P)** | Costs to prevent defects | Training, quality planning, supplier capability | | **Appraisal Costs (A)** | Costs to detect defects | Inspection, testing, calibration | | **Internal Failure Costs (IFC)** | Costs of defects found before delivery | Rework, scrap, re-inspection | | **External Failure Costs (EFC)** | Costs of defects found after delivery | Warranty, returns, legal liability, loss of goodwill | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Symbol / Description | Units / Boundary | |---------|----------------------|------------------| | $$\text{Total COQ} = P + A + IFC + EFC$$ | Sum of all four quality costs | Currency (₹, $) | | $$\text{COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality)} = IFC + EFC$$ | Cost due to failures only | Currency | | $$\text{Optimum Quality Level}$$ | Point where **marginal prevention cost = marginal failure cost** | Economics principle | | $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{D \times 10^6}{N \times O}$$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities | $D$ = defects; $N$ = units; $O$ = opportunities/unit | | $$\text{Yield (\%)} = \frac{N - D}{N} \times 100$$ | First-pass yield | Percentage (0–100) | | $$\text{RTY (Rolled Throughput Yield)} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$ | Probability of zero defects through $n$ stages | Decimal (0–1) | | $$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$ | Process Capability Index | $C_p \geq 1.33$ is capable | | $$C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$$ | Process Capability accounting for centering | $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ | | $$\sigma \text{ Level (Six Sigma)} = 0.8406 + 29.37 \times Y - 1.891 \times 10^{-3} \times Y^2 + 10^{-11} \times Y^3$$ | Long-term sigma from yield $Y$ (Westgard) | $Y$ in % | | $$\text{CSI} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$ | Customer Satisfaction Index (weighted) | 1–5 Likert scale | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Rule:** When using absolute value or limits inside formulas (e.g., $\vert x \vert$ or limits like USL–LSL), always use `\vert` or `\mid` — never the vertical bar `|` — to avoid breaking markdown table syntax in your answer sheets. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility TQM is applied across: - **Manufacturing:** Toyota Production System (Lean + TQM). - **Software:** CMMI Level 5, ISO 9001 certified IT firms. - **Healthcare:** NABH accreditation in Indian hospitals (NEP 2020-aligned skill-based quality in biomedical engineering). - **Aviation & Defense:** AS9100 standard (TQM for aerospace). - **Indian Industry:** Tata Steel (Deming Prize, 2019), Infosys, Maruti Suzuki. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section derives the key analytical relationships examiners love to test in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimal Quality Cost (Economic Equilibrium of Quality) **Concept:** As we invest more in **prevention**, **appraisal**, and **internal controls**, the **failure costs** fall. There is an optimum point where the **total COQ is minimized**. Let: - $P(x)$ = Prevention cost as a function of prevention investment $x$ (typically linear or convex). - $F(x)$ = Combined Failure Cost = $IFC(x) + EFC(x)$ (a decreasing convex function). - $A(x)$ = Appraisal cost (assumed constant or slowly rising). The total quality cost is: $$C_{\text{total}}(x) = P(x) + A(x) + F(x)$$ To find the **optimum**, take the first derivative and set it to zero: $$\frac{dC_{\text{total}}}{dx} = \frac{dP}{dx} + \frac{dA}{dx} + \frac{dF}{dx} = 0$$ Since $\frac{dF}{dx} < 0$ (failure costs decrease with $x$) and $\frac{dP}{dx} > 0$ (prevention costs increase with $x$), the equilibrium is: $$\frac{dP}{dx} = -\frac{dF}{dx}$$ **Interpretation:** At the optimum, the **marginal cost of preventing one more defect equals the marginal cost saved by avoiding that defect**. Investing beyond this point actually *increases* total cost. > **Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):** > A company has data: $P(x) = 0.5x$, $A(x) = 10$, $F(x) = 100 - 10x + 0.4x^2$, where $x$ is in lakhs of ₹. > > $$C_{\text{total}} = 0.5x + 10 + 100 - 10x + 0.4x^2 = 0.4x^2 - 9.5x + 110$$ > > $$\frac{dC}{dx} = 0.8x - 9.5 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x^* = \frac{9.5}{0.8} = 11.875 \text{ lakhs}$$ > > $$C_{\min} = 0.4(11.875)^2 - 9.5(11.875) + 110 = 56.41 - 112.81 + 110 = 53.60 \text{ lakhs}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Six Sigma Yield to Sigma Level Conversion The relationship between yield and sigma level is empirical (developed by Motorola). For a **1.5 sigma shift** (long-term model), the short-term-to-long-term conversion is: $$Y_{\text{LT}} = Y_{\text{ST}} - 1.5\sigma \text{ (shift)}$$ Using the **standard normal table**, yield values correspond to z-scores: | Yield (%) | Sigma Level | |-----------|-------------| | 99.9997 | 6.0 | | 99.99966 | 6.0 (LT, with 1.5σ shift) | | 99.73 | 3.0 | | 93.32 | 2.0 | | 69.13 | 1.0 | For KTU problems, use the **Westgard formula** (given earlier) for any yield: $$\sigma = 0.8406 + 29.37Y - 1.891 \times 10^{-3} Y^2 + 10^{-11} Y^3$$ where $Y$ is expressed in **percentage** (e.g., 99.0, not 0.99). ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) In a **multi-stage process**, the overall yield is the product of individual stage yields: $$RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$ **Derivation logic:** - At stage 1, probability of a defect-free unit = $Y_1$. - At stage 2, conditional probability = $Y_2$. - For independent stages, joint probability = $Y_1 \times Y_2 \times \cdots \times Y_n$. **Example:** A 3-stage process has yields: $Y_1 = 0.95$, $Y_2 = 0.92$, $Y_3 = 0.98$. $$RTY = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.98 = 0.8565 = 85.65\%$$ So the process has a **14.35% defect rate**, even though no single stage is poor. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Gurus at a Glance (Comparison Table) | Aspect | **Deming** | **Juran** | **Crosby** | **Ishikawa** | **Feigenbaum** | |--------|------------|-----------|------------|--------------|----------------| | Focus | System | Customer | Conformance | People | Cost & Company-wide | | Quality Definition | Predictable uniformity | Fitness for use | Conformance to requirements | Customer satisfaction | Customer-defined | | Tool/Framework | PDCA, 14 Points | Trilogy | 4 Absolutes, Zero Defects | Fishbone, QC Circles | Total Quality Control | | Worker Emphasis | High | Medium | Medium | Very High | High | | Best Known For | 14 Points for Mgmt | Quality Trilogy | "Quality is Free" | Cause-and-Effect | TQC concept | | Origin | USA/Japan | USA | USA | Japan | USA | ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Cost Behavior in TQM Implementation Phases | Phase | Prevention | Appraisal | Internal Failure | External Failure | Total COQ | |-------|------------|-----------|------------------|------------------|-----------| | Phase 1: Reactive (Inspection era) | Low | Low | High | Very High | **Highest** | | Phase 2: Preventive (TQM Intro) | ↑ Increasing | ↑ Increasing | ↓ Decreasing | ↓ Decreasing | Falling | | Phase 3: Optimal | Maximum useful | Stabilized | Low | Low | **Minimum** | | Phase 4: Over-investment | Excessive | Excessive | Very Low | Very Low | Rising again | > **Examiner's Note (KTU):** The most common trap in COQ problems is assuming Prevention and Appraisal are *bad* costs. They are **value-added costs**, while Failure Costs are **non-value-added costs**. TQM aims to convert failure costs into prevention costs. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 TQM Framework — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[CUSTOMER FOCUS
Voice of Customer VoC] --> B[TQM CORE
Leadership Commitment] B --> C[PROCESS MANAGEMENT
PDCA Cycle] B --> D[PEOPLE MANAGEMENT
Quality Circles and Training] B --> E[CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT
Kaizen and Six Sigma] C --> F[MEASUREMENT and ANALYSIS
SPC and SQC Tools] D --> F E --> F F --> G[QUALITY OUTPUT
Conformance and Customer Satisfaction] G --> H{Satisfied Customer?} H -- Yes --> I[LOYALTY, REVENUE, GROWTH] H -- No --> J[RETURNS, WARRANTY, REWORK] J --> A ``` ### 4.2 Deming's PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR P[PLAN
Define problem and analyze process] --> D[DO
Implement on small scale pilot] D --> C[CHECK
Measure results against objectives] C --> A[ACT
Standardize or revise based on learning] A --> P ``` > **Annotation:** The PDCA cycle is **continuous** — there is no end point. Each loop makes the process incrementally better, embodying Kaizen. ### 4.3 Juran's Quality Trilogy Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB QP[QUALITY PLANNING
Identify customers and needs] --> QC[QUALITY CONTROL
Evaluate vs goals and act on gaps] QC --> QI[QUALITY IMPROVEMENT
Establish infrastructure and projects] QI --> QP ``` ### 4.4 TQM Implementation Roadmap (10-Step Logical Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB S1[Step 1: Top Management Commitment] --> S2[Step 2: Customer Focus and VoC] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Quality Policy and Mission Statement] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Identify Critical Processes] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Employee Training and Quality Circles] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Establish Performance Metrics] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Measure Analyze Improve Control] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Supplier Partnership and SQM] S8 --> S9[Step 9: Continuous Improvement Kaizen] S9 --> S10[Step 10: Benchmarking and Recognition] S10 --> S9 ``` ### 4.5 Comparison Architecture: TQM vs Six Sigma vs ISO 9000 ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A[TQM] A1[Philosophy and Culture] A2[Everyone Responsible] A3[Long term Customer Focus] end subgraph B[Six Sigma] B1[Data driven Methodology] B2[3.4 DPMO Target] B3[DMAIC Framework] end subgraph C[ISO 9000] C1[Standards based Certification] C2[Documented QMS] C3[Audit and Compliance] end A --> D[All Integrated in Modern Industry] B --> D C --> D ``` > **Annotation for KTU:** While TQM is a *philosophy*, Six Sigma is a *methodology* and ISO 9000 is a *standard*. They are **complementary**, not competing. Many KTU questions ask for a comparative study — the diagram above is your visual key. ### 4.6 The Cost of Quality Curve (Optimum Visualization) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Curve[Cost Behavior vs Prevention Investment] direction LR P1[Low Prevention] --> P2[Optimal Prevention] --> P3[Over Prevention] end P1 -.->|FC Very High
PC Very Low| R1[High Total COQ] P2 -.->|FC Equals MC of Prevention| R2[Minimum Total COQ] P3 -.->|FC Low
PC Excessive| R3[Rising Total COQ] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1.** Define Total Quality Management. List any four of its key principles. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a holistic, customer-oriented management philosophy that seeks to integrate all organizational functions — marketing, finance, design, production, and service — to continuously improve the quality of goods and services, with the active participation of all employees at every level. > > **Four Key Principles (any four, 0.5 each):** > 1. Customer Focus (internal and external) > 2. Total Employee Involvement and Empowerment > 3. Continuous Improvement (Kaizen) > 4. Process-Centric Decision Making > 5. Integrated System / Strategic Approach > 6. Fact-based Decision Making *(Strictly map each principle to its marks. Use bullet points, not paragraphs.)* --- #### **Q2.** Compare the quality philosophies of Deming and Juran. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks — tabular format preferred):** | Aspect | **W. Edwards Deming** | **Joseph M. Juran** | |--------|----------------------|---------------------| | Definition of Quality | A predictable degree of uniformity and dependability | Fitness for Use | | Key Contribution | 14 Points for Management; PDCA Cycle | Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement) | | Approach | Statistical, System-based | Managerial, Customer-based | | Primary Tool | Statistical Process Control (SPC) | Pareto Analysis, Quality Planning | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write *Juran = Quality Trilogy* and *Deming = PDCA* and stop. You will only get **partial marks**. Always elaborate **at least one sentence per row**, especially the *philosophical difference* (Deming = system orientation; Juran = customer orientation). --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks):** **Q3 (a)** Explain the Deming Cycle (PDCA) in detail. How does it support continuous improvement in an engineering organization? `(7 marks)` `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]` **Q3 (b)** Discuss the Cost of Quality (COQ) model. Illustrate with a numerical example how the optimum quality level is determined. `(7 marks)` #### OR #### **Question B (14 Marks):** **Q4 (a)** Compare and contrast the philosophies of Philip Crosby and Kaoru Ishikawa. Which, in your opinion, is more suitable for the Indian manufacturing sector, and why? `(7 marks)` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Q4 (b)** Explain the structure of the EFQM Excellence Model and the Malcolm Baldrige Criteria. How are they used as benchmarking tools for TQM implementation? `(7 marks)` --- ### Complete Model Solutions --- #### **Q3 (a) Solution — Deming Cycle (PDCA)** `(7 marks)` **Introduction (1 mark):** The PDCA cycle, also called the **Deming Wheel** or **Shewhart Cycle**, is the foundational iterative model for continuous improvement, originally conceived by Walter Shewhart and popularized by W. Edwards Deming. **The Four Phases (4 marks — 1 each):** 1. **PLAN:** Identify the problem, analyze the existing process, set objectives, and design a hypothesis for improvement. Tools: Pareto chart, Ishikawa diagram, FMEA. 2. **DO:** Execute the plan on a small scale (pilot run) to test the hypothesis. Collect data rigorously. 3. **CHECK (or STUDY):** Compare the actual results against the expected results using statistical tools (control charts, hypothesis testing). Identify gaps. 4. **ACT:** If successful, standardize the change across the organization (update SOPs). If not, refine the plan and repeat. Lessons are documented. **Application in Engineering (2 marks):** - **Manufacturing:** Reduce defect rate in CNC machining by adjusting spindle speed (PDCA loop). - **Software:** Iterative Agile sprints follow PDCA implicitly. - **Continuous Improvement:** The cycle never ends — after Act, a new Plan begins at a higher baseline, embodying **Kaizen**. **Diagram (recommended):** Draw the circular PDCA loop with arrows. *(0 marks if missing in KTU exam — always include diagram.)* --- #### **Q3 (b) Solution — Cost of Quality (COQ) Model** `(7 marks)` **Definition (1 mark):** COQ is the total cost incurred to ensure product quality and the costs arising from failure to meet quality. It comprises **Prevention (P)**, **Appraisal (A)**, **Internal Failure (IFC)**, and **External Failure (EFC)** costs. **Component Breakdown (2 marks):** | Cost Type | Nature | Examples | |-----------|--------|----------| | Prevention | Value-added | Training, Quality planning, Supplier audits | | Appraisal | Value-added | Incoming inspection, In-process testing | | Internal Failure | Non-value-added | Scrap, Rework, Re-inspection | | External Failure | Non-value-added | Warranty, Returns, Litigation, Lost goodwill | **Numerical Example (3 marks):** A firm produces 10,000 units/month. Annual costs: - $P = \text{₹}2{,}00{,}000$ - $A = \text{₹}1{,}50{,}000$ - $IFC = \text{₹}4{,}00{,}000$ - $EFC = \text{₹}6{,}00{,}000$ $$\text{Total COQ} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}13{,}50{,}000$$ **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality):** $$COPQ = IFC + EFC = 4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000$$ This means **74%** of the quality spend is wasted on failures. TQM aims to shift this balance. **Optimum Determination (1 mark):** The optimum is where marginal prevention cost equals marginal reduction in failure cost — the curve of total COQ reaches its **minimum**. --- #### **Q4 (a) Solution — Crosby vs Ishikawa** `(7 marks)` **Crosby's Philosophy (2 marks):** - Defined quality as **"conformance to requirements"** (not elegance). - Famous for the maxim **"Quality is Free"** — arguing prevention always pays. - Propagated the **Zero Defects** standard (not "acceptable quality level"). - **Four Absolutes:** Conformance, Prevention (not inspection), Zero Defects performance standard, Measure by Price of Non-Conformance (PONC). - **Cost-of-Quality framework** — categorized quality costs into P, A, F (Failure = IFC + EFC). **Ishikawa's Philosophy (2 marks):** - Coined **"Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC)"** — quality is everyone's responsibility. - Created the **Fishbone (Cause-and-Effect) Diagram** for root-cause analysis (the "6 Ms": Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature). - Pioneered **Quality Circles** (small worker groups, typically 6–12 members, meeting weekly to solve quality problems). - Emphasized **inter-departmental cooperation** and **data-based analysis**. **Comparison Table (2 marks):** | Aspect | **Crosby** | **Ishikawa** | |--------|-----------|--------------| | Quality Definition | Conformance to requirements | Customer satisfaction | | Key Slogan | Quality is Free | Quality is everyone's job | | Tool | Zero Defects Program | Fishbone, Quality Circles | | Approach | Top-down cultural change | Bottom-up employee empowerment | | Focus | Cost avoidance | Statistical analysis and teamwork | **Suitability for India (1 mark):** For the **Indian manufacturing sector** (with abundant semi-skilled labour and varied process maturity), **Ishikawa's approach is more suitable**. Reason: Quality Circles empower floor-level workers, develop skills, and align with India's demographic dividend. Crosby's Zero Defects, while aspirational, requires a more mature documentation culture. --- #### **Q4 (b) Solution — EFQM and Baldrige Models** `(7 marks)` **EFQM Excellence Model (3 marks):** - Founded by the **European Foundation for Quality Management (EFQM)** in 1988. - Based on the premise that *"Excellent results with respect to Performance, Customers, People and Society are achieved through Leadership driving Strategy and Policy, delivered through People, Partnerships and Resources, and Processes."* - **9 Criteria:** - **5 Enablers:** Leadership, People, Strategy, Partnerships & Resources, Processes. - **4 Results:** People Results, Customer Results, Society Results, Business Results. - **RADAR Logic:** Results → Approach → Deploy → Assess → Refine. **Malcolm Baldrige Criteria (3 marks):** - Established in 1987 by the **U.S. Congress** in honor of Malcolm Baldrige, former Secretary of Commerce. - **7 Categories:** 1. Leadership (120 pts) 2. Strategic Planning (85 pts) 3. Customer Focus (85 pts) 4. Measurement, Analysis, and Knowledge Management (90 pts) 5. Workforce Focus (85 pts) 6. Operations Focus (85 pts) 7. Results (450 pts) - Used in the **U.S. Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award (MBNQA)**. **Comparison as Benchmarking Tools (1 mark):** Both models are **non-prescriptive frameworks** — they tell organizations *what* to achieve, not *how*. They enable self-assessment against world-class benchmarks. Indian organizations like **Tata Steel, Maruti Suzuki, and Infosys** use these as diagnostic tools to identify improvement areas. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Common Deductions:** > 1. ❌ Confusing *EFQM with ISO* — EFQM is a *model*, ISO 9001 is a *certifiable standard*. > 2. ❌ Forgetting to write the full form of abbreviations at least once (RADAR, CWQC, PONC). > 3. ❌ Drawing EFQM as a *pyramid* — it is officially a **nine-box diagram** with two horizontal bars (Enablers and Results). > 4. ❌ In numerical problems, not writing the units (₹, %, units). > 5. ❌ In Crosby questions, confusing *Prevention cost* with *Quality cost of prevention as wasteful* — they are *value-added*. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **TQM = Total Quality Management** — organization-wide philosophy, customer-centric, continuous improvement. - [ ] **Five Pillars:** Customer Focus, Total Employee Involvement, Continuous Improvement, Process Approach, Integrated System. - [ ] **Deming:** System focus; 14 Points; **PDCA** cycle; 85/15 rule (or 94/6). - [ ] **Juran:** **Fitness for Use**; Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement); Pareto-based. - [ ] **Crosby:** **Conformance to requirements**; **Zero Defects**; **Quality is Free**; 4 Absolutes; PONC metric. - [ ] **Ishikawa:** **Fishbone diagram** (6 Ms); **Quality Circles**; **CWQC**. - [ ] **Feigenbaum:** Coined **TQC** (1956); 9 M's of Quality. - [ ] **Models:** **EFQM** (5 Enablers + 4 Results; RADAR); **Baldrige** (7 categories); **ISO 9000** (7 QMS Principles, latest version 9001:2015). - [ ] **COQ Formula:** $COQ = P + A + IFC + EFC$; Optimum at marginal cost equality. - [ ] **Six Sigma Formula:** $DPMO = \frac{D \times 10^6}{N \times O}$; Long-term target = 3.4 DPMO. - [ ] **RTY:** $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ — joint probability of zero defects through n stages. - [ ] **Process Capability:** $C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$; $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$. - [ ] **PDCA** never ends — represents **Kaizen**. - [ ] **Always include a diagram** in 7-mark and 14-mark answers — KTU examiners reward visualization. - [ ] **Cite real Indian examples** (Tata Steel, Maruti, Infosys) for contextual credit in 14-mark essays. - [ ] **Avoid markdown special characters** in answer sheets — use plain $\vert$ in place of $\mid$ if writing in LaTeX, never the bare pipe `|` in tables. --- > **End of Module 1 Notes — Topic: Total Quality Management – Philosophies, Models, and Implementation** > **Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned)**

topics.title: Process Variability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Variability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Process Variability** is the statistical phenomenon describing the *natural dispersion of a quality characteristic* around its target value when a manufacturing or service process operates under a stable, well-controlled set of conditions. In the formal language of Statistical Quality Control (SQC), variability is quantified by the **standard deviation ($\sigma$)** or **variance ($\sigma^2$)** of the quality characteristic, and its magnitude relative to the design specification limits determines whether the process output conforms to customer requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorise Verbatim):**
> "Process variability refers to the inherent fluctuation observed in the output of a process due to the cumulative effect of numerous small, unavoidable causes (chance causes) and occasional, identifiable disturbances (assignable causes). It is mathematically measured by the standard deviation $\sigma$ and governs process capability indices $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $P_p$, and $P_{pk}$."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a baker producing 1 kg loaves of bread every morning.

- **Target Weight:** Exactly **1000 grams (1 kg)**.
- **Common Cause Variation (Natural):** Due to tiny uncontrollable factors — slight humidity differences, micro-variations in flour density, ambient temperature, minor hand-tremor while measuring — each loaf naturally weighs something like **998 g, 1003 g, 996 g, 1002 g**, etc. These variations are **always present**, follow a **predictable statistical pattern** (bell curve / Normal Distribution), and are *impossible to eliminate entirely* without fundamentally redesigning the process.

- **Special Cause Variation (Assignable):** One morning, a faulty weighing scale reads 950 g, OR a new untrained helper forgets to zero the scale. This produces a **suspicious, unusual weight** like **850 g or 1200 g** — a value that lies far outside the predictable bell curve. This kind of variation has an *identifiable root cause* that can be **located and permanently fixed**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Process Variability:**
> If only **common causes** are present → process is "**in statistical control**" (predictable, but possibly incapable).
> If **special causes** appear → process is "**out of statistical control**" (unpredictable, must investigate).

---

## 1.3 Two Fundamental Types of Variation — The Shewhart Foundation

Walter A. Shewhart, the father of SQC, classified all process variation into exactly two categories:

| # | Type of Variation | Alternative Names | Nature | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Chance / Common Cause** | Natural, inherent, random, background variation | Always present; statistically predictable (Normal distribution) | **Reduce** by fundamental **process redesign** |
| **2** | **Assignable / Special Cause** | Unnatural, identifiable, sporadic variation | Locatable, traceable to specific disturbances | **Remove** by **local corrective action** |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut Mnemonic:** "**C**ommon = **C**hronic (always there); **S**pecial = **S**poradic (sometimes there)"

---

## 1.4 Why Process Variability Matters in Engineering

- **Quality Cost Reduction:** Variability inflates *scrap*, *rework*, *warranty*, and *appraisal* costs.
- **Six-Sigma Philosophy:** Motorola/Texas Instruments goal = reduce variability such that **6 standard deviations fit within specification limits** (yielding only **3.4 defects per million opportunities — DPMO**).
- **Capability vs Stability:** A process can be *stable* (only common causes) yet *incapable* (variability too large for spec width). This distinction is the bedrock of modern SQC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-Way Diagnostic Framework:**
> 1. **Is the process stable?** → Use **Control Charts** (Module 2 next topic).
> 2. **Is the process capable?** → Use **Process Capability Indices** $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $P_p$, $P_{pk}$.

Standard industrial benchmarks for process capability:
- **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** → Process considered **capable** (traditional benchmark).
- **$C_{pk} \geq 1.67$** → Six-Sigma capable.
- **$C_{pk} \geq 2.00$** → World-class (often unattainable without Six-Sigma tools).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Decomposing Process Variability — The ANOVA Logic

A modern manufacturing process contains **multiple nested sources of variability**. Statistically, total observed variability ($\sigma^2_{Total}$) is partitioned using the Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) hierarchy:

$$\sigma^2_{Total} = \sigma^2_{Within} + \sigma^2_{Between}$$

Where:
- **$\sigma^2_{Within}$** = short-term, within-subgroup variation (caused by common causes only) → drives **process capability** ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$).
- **$\sigma^2_{Between}$** = long-term, between-subgroup variation (drift, tool wear, batch effects) → together with $\sigma^2_{Within}$ forms **process performance** ($P_p$, $P_{pk}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Insight:** If $\sigma^2_{Between}$ is large (i.e., $P_{pk} \ll C_{pk}$), then special causes are *present but not yet flagged* on the control chart. This is a classic KTU-style **interpretive question** in exams.

---

## 2.2 Estimating Variability — Three Industrial Estimators

| Estimator | Symbol | When Used | Pooled Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sample Std. Dev. | $s$ | Single subgroup (e.g., $n \leq 10$) | $s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ |
| Average Within-Subgroup Std. Dev. | $\bar{s}$ | Multiple subgroups of *equal* size $n$ | $\bar{s} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} s_i$ |
| Pooled (Combined) Std. Dev. | $s_p$ | Multiple subgroups of *unequal* size | $s_p = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i}(n_i-1)s_i^2}{\sum_{i}(n_i-1)}}$ |

For control-chart-driven estimation (R-bar method, classic Shewhart):

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2(n)}$$

Where $\bar{R}$ is the average subgroup range and $d_2(n)$ is a tabulated constant depending on subgroup size $n$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exhaustive)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table — at least 80 % of KTU numericals on Process Variability test these formulas.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Variance | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum (x_i-\mu)^2}{N}$ | Population variance | Continuous $x$ |
| 2 | Std. Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Spread metric | Same as $x$ |
| 3 | Sample Std. Dev. | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | Unbiased estimator | Continuous $x$ |
| 4 | Range | $R = x_{max}-x_{min}$ | Quick variability index | Same as $x$ |
| 5 | Coefficient of Variation | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100\,\%$ | Relative variability | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Total Tolerance (Natural) | $T_n = 6\sigma$ | ±3σ spread (99.73 %) | Same as $x$ |
| 7 | Specification Width | $T = USL - LSL$ | Customer-defined window | Same as $x$ |
| 8 | Process Capability Index | $C_p = \dfrac{T}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{USL-LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Potential capability | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Process Capability Index (offset) | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma},\,\dfrac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Actual capability | Dimensionless |
| 10 | One-sided Capability | $C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma}$ | Upper spec only | Dimensionless |
| 11 | One-sided Capability | $C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}$ | Lower spec only | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Process Performance Index | $P_p = \dfrac{USL-LSL}{6s_{overall}}$ | Total spread, *unstable* allowed | Dimensionless |
| 13 | Process Performance (offset) | $P_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL-\bar{x}}{3s_{overall}},\,\dfrac{\bar{x}-LSL}{3s_{overall}}\right)$ | Total performance | Dimensionless |
| 14 | Six-Sigma Level | $Z_{LT} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL-\bar{x}}{s_{LT}},\,\dfrac{\bar{x}-LSL}{s_{LT}}\right)$ | Long-term sigma | Dimensionless |
| 15 | Estimated $\sigma$ from $\bar{R}$ | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2(n)}$ | R-bar method | Tabulated $d_2$ |
| 16 | Estimated $\sigma$ from $\bar{s}$ | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{s}}{c_4(n)}$ | S-bar method | Tabulated $c_4$ |
| 17 | PPM Defect Rate | $PPM = 1{,}000{,}000 \times P(\text{outside specs})$ | Yield metric | Parts per million |
| 18 | Taguchi Loss Function | $L(y) = k(y-T)^2$ | Quality loss from target $T$ | Currency |

> **Board Pitfall Reminder:** Always distinguish between **$\sigma$ (population)** and **$s$ (sample)**. In exam answers, **never interchange** them without justification.

---

## 2.4 Process Capability vs Process Performance — The Critical Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> This is the **single most-tested distinction** in KTU Module 2 questions.

- **$C_p$ / $C_{pk}$** use **short-term $\sigma$** (within-subgroup) → Assumes process is **in statistical control** (only common causes present). Reflects what the process *can do*.
- **$P_p$ / $P_{pk}$** use **overall $s$** (total observed variation) → Does *not* assume control. Reflects what the process *actually does* including special causes.

**Empirical rule:** $C_{pk} \geq P_{pk}$ always, because within-subgroup variation $\leq$ total variation. If $P_{pk} \ll C_{pk}$, special causes are lurking.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (e.g., Bosch, Toyota):** Cylinder bore diameter $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ mandatory.
- **Semiconductors (Intel, TSMC):** Wafer thickness controlled to $C_{pk} \geq 2.00$ at 6σ.
- **Pharmaceutical (FDA):** Tablet weight variation governed by USP <905> — typically $CV \leq 2\,\%$.
- **Avionics (DGCA / FAA):** Critical dimensions must achieve $C_{pk} \geq 1.50$ with documented SPC charts.

The entire **Six-Sigma DMAIC framework** (Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control) is built on *reducing process variability* until $Z_{LT} \geq 4.5$ (Motorola's original target).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of $C_p$ — Process Capability Index

We want a **dimensionless index** that answers the question: *"How many specification widths fit inside the natural process spread?"*

**Step 1:** Define the natural process spread as $\pm 3\sigma$ (covers 99.73 % of output under normality).

$$\text{Natural Spread} = 6\sigma$$

**Step 2:** Define the specification width as the distance between customer limits.

$$T = USL - LSL$$

**Step 3:** Form the dimensionless ratio of specification tolerance to natural spread.

$$C_p = \frac{\text{Specification Width}}{\text{Process Spread}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $C_p = 1.00$ → Specification exactly matches natural spread (high defect risk).
- $C_p = 1.33$ → Specification is 33 % wider than natural spread (industry standard).
- $C_p = 1.50$ → Specification is 50 % wider (comfortable margin).
- $C_p = 2.00$ → World-class Six-Sigma.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of $C_{pk}$ — Accounting for Process Off-Center

$C_p$ assumes the process mean $\mu$ is **perfectly centred** at the midpoint of the spec window. In reality, processes drift. We need an index that penalises *off-centring*.

**Step 1:** Define the upper one-sided index.

$$C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$$

**Step 2:** Define the lower one-sided index.

$$C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$$

**Step 3:** $C_{pk}$ is the **worst (minimum)** of the two, because the *limiting side* dictates actual defect rate.

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu},\, C_{pl}) = \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\,\frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

**Step 4 (Algebraic simplification):** Substituting $T = USL-LSL$ and midpoint $M = \dfrac{USL+LSL}{2}$, deviation of mean from centre is $d = \mu - M$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min\!\left(\frac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma},\,\frac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right) \\
&= \min\!\left(\frac{(USL-LSL)/2 - (\mu - (USL+LSL)/2)}{3\sigma},\,\frac{(USL-LSL)/2 + (\mu - (USL+LSL)/2)}{3\sigma}\right) \\
&= \frac{T}{6\sigma}\left(1 - \frac{\vert \mu - M \vert}{T/2}\right) \\
&= C_p\,(1 - k)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $k = \dfrac{\vert \mu - M \vert}{T/2}$ is the **centring factor** (0 = perfectly centred, 1 = touching limit).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of $C_{pk}$ from $C_p$ and $k$ (Exam Shortcut)

Many KTU numericals give $C_p$ and ask $C_{pk}$. Use:

$$C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k)$$

**Example Derivation Walkthrough:**

A process has $USL = 12.00$ mm, $LSL = 10.00$ mm, $\mu = 10.80$ mm, $\sigma = 0.30$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= USL - LSL = 12.00 - 10.00 = 2.00 \text{ mm} \\
M &= \frac{USL+LSL}{2} = \frac{12.00 + 10.00}{2} = 11.00 \text{ mm} \\
d &= \mu - M = 10.80 - 11.00 = -0.20 \text{ mm} \\
k &= \frac{\vert d \vert}{T/2} = \frac{0.20}{1.00} = 0.20 \\
C_p &= \frac{T}{6\sigma} = \frac{2.00}{6 \times 0.30} = \frac{2.00}{1.80} = 1.111 \\
C_{pk} &= C_p(1-k) = 1.111 \times (1 - 0.20) = 1.111 \times 0.80 = 0.889
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $C_{pk} = 0.889$ — process is **incapable** (below 1.00) and **off-centre**. Recommendation: re-centre $\mu$ towards 11.00 mm and reduce $\sigma$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the R-bar Estimator of $\sigma$

For subgroups of size $n$, the **range** $R = x_{max} - x_{min}$ is a quick estimator of spread.

**Step 1:** The expected value of $R$ for a Normal sample of size $n$ is:

$$E[R] = d_2(n) \cdot \sigma$$

where $d_2(n)$ is the tabulated **Shewhart constant** (depends only on $n$).

**Step 2:** Therefore, an unbiased estimator of $\sigma$ is:

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2(n)}$$

**Step 3 (For $n = 5$ example):** $d_2(5) = 2.326$ (standard table value). So:

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{2.326} \approx 0.4299 \, \bar{R}$$

This is the **R-bar method** used in classical $\bar{X}-R$ control charts to estimate process variability *without computing standard deviations explicitly*.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Complete Process Variability Audit

> **Problem:** A CNC lathe produces shafts with target diameter 25.00 mm. A sample of 10 shafts yields: 25.02, 24.98, 25.05, 24.96, 25.01, 25.04, 24.99, 25.03, 25.00, 24.97. Specification: $LSL = 24.90$, $USL = 25.10$ mm. Compute (a) $\bar{x}$, $s$, (b) $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, (c) comment on capability.

**Step 1 — Mean:**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{25.02+24.98+25.05+24.96+25.01+25.04+24.99+25.03+25.00+24.97}{10}
$$

$$
= \frac{250.05}{10} = 25.005 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum of squared deviations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (x_i-\bar{x})^2 &= (0.015)^2 + (-0.025)^2 + (0.045)^2 + (-0.045)^2 + (0.005)^2 \\
&\quad + (0.035)^2 + (-0.015)^2 + (0.025)^2 + (-0.005)^2 + (-0.035)^2 \\
&= 0.000225 + 0.000625 + 0.002025 + 0.002025 + 0.000025 \\
&\quad + 0.001225 + 0.000225 + 0.000625 + 0.000025 + 0.001225 \\
&= 0.008250 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sample standard deviation:**

$$
s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i-\bar{x})^2}{n-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.008250}{9}} = \sqrt{0.0009167} = 0.03028 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — $C_p$:**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6s} = \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.03028} = \frac{0.20}{0.18168} = 1.101
$$

**Step 5 — Centring factor:**

$$
M = \frac{25.10+24.90}{2} = 25.00 \text{ mm}, \quad d = 25.005 - 25.000 = 0.005 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
k = \frac{\vert d \vert}{T/2} = \frac{0.005}{0.10} = 0.05
$$

**Step 6 — $C_{pk}$:**

$$
C_{pk} = C_p(1-k) = 1.101 \times (1 - 0.05) = 1.101 \times 0.95 = 1.046
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:**

- $C_p = 1.101 > 1.00$ → process has *potential* capability (close to threshold).
- $C_{pk} = 1.046 < 1.33$ → process is *not yet* industry-capable.
- Process is **slightly off-centre** ($k = 0.05$). Recommend: re-centre the CNC tool offset toward 25.00 mm and tighten tooling to reduce $s$.

---

## 3.6 PPM Defect Rate Derivation

Under normality, the probability of an observation exceeding $USL$ is:

$$P(X > USL) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}\right) = 1 - \Phi(Z_{USL})$$

Similarly for lower side. Total defect probability:

$$P_{defect} = \Phi\!\left(\frac{\mu - LSL}{\sigma}\right) + \left[1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}\right)\right]$$

PPM (parts per million) defect rate:

$$PPM = P_{defect} \times 1{,}000{,}000$$

> [!TIP]
> **Six-Sigma Bench Table (Memorise):**

| $C_{pk}$ | PPM Defects (approx., centred) |
|---|---|
| 1.00 | 2,700 |
| 1.33 | 66,000 / 63,000 ≈ **66 ppm** |
| 1.50 | 3.4 ppm (corrected for 1.5σ shift) |
| 1.67 | 0.6 ppm |
| 2.00 | 0.002 ppm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Total Process Variability Decomposition — Hierarchical Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Process Variability sigma2 Total"]:::root
    B["Within Subgroup sigma2 Within"]:::common
    C["Between Subgroup sigma2 Between"]:::common
    D["Short term common causes only"]:::child
    E["Tool wear drift"]:::special
    F["Batch to batch shifts"]:::special
    G["Operator to operator shifts"]:::special
    H["Drives Cp and Cpk"]:::metric
    I["Drives Pp and Ppk together with within"]:::metric

    A --> B
    A --> C
    B --> D
    C --> E
    C --> F
    C --> G
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Conceptual Questions

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define process variability. Distinguish between common cause and special cause variation with one industry example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Board-Standard Wording):**

Process variability is the inherent dispersion observed in the output of a manufacturing or service process around its target value, mathematically quantified by the standard deviation $\sigma$ of the quality characteristic.

**Common cause variation** is the natural, always-present fluctuation arising from numerous small, uncontrollable factors such as ambient temperature, raw material micro-inhomogeneity, and minor machine vibration. It follows a predictable statistical (Normal) distribution and can only be reduced by **fundamental process redesign**. *Example:* Slight day-to-day weight variation of cereal boxes on a high-speed packaging line.

**Special cause variation** is an occasional, identifiable, and often large-magnitude deviation caused by specific, localised disturbances such as a broken tool, untrained operator, or faulty sensor. It is **locatable and removable** through targeted corrective action. *Example:* A batch of bolts manufactured during a power outage showing anomalous hardness values.

*(Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks only if both types are clearly contrasted with industry examples.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**State the mathematical formula for $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Explain why $C_{pk}$ is preferred over $C_p$ in industry.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\,\frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

$C_p$ measures the **potential** capability of a process assuming the mean is exactly centred between the specification limits. However, in real production, the process mean **drifts** due to tool wear, operator variation, or raw-material batch effects. $C_{pk}$ incorporates the actual location of the mean $\mu$ relative to the spec window by taking the **minimum** of the two one-sided indices, thus providing a **realistic** and **penalising** measure of capability. Industry therefore prefers $C_{pk}$ because it simultaneously captures both **spread** (variability) and **accuracy** (centring).

---

## PART B — 14-Mark ESE Numerical / Analytical Questions
### (Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2 — Process Variability]`

A pharmaceutical tablet press produces tablets with target weight **500 mg**. Specification limits are **$LSL = 490$ mg** and **$USL = 510$ mg**. Twenty-five consecutive tablets are sampled and their weights (in mg) are recorded as follows:

> 498, 502, 499, 503, 501, 500, 502, 499, 504, 498,
> 501, 503, 500, 502, 499, 504, 501, 500, 502, 503,
> 499, 501, 500, 502, 504.

**(a)** Compute the sample mean $\bar{x}$, sample standard deviation $s$, and the range $R$. Comment on whether the process appears to be centred. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Calculate the process capability indices $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Determine if the process is industry-capable ($C_{pk} \geq 1.33$). If not, suggest two engineering actions to improve capability. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)** (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Sum of observations:**

$$
\sum x_i = 498+502+499+503+501+500+502+499+504+498 + 501+503+500+502+499+504+501+500+502+503 + 499+501+500+502+504
$$

Group-sum verification:
- First 10:  $498+502+499+503+501+500+502+499+504+498 = 5006$
- Next 10:    $501+503+500+502+499+504+501+500+502+503 = 5015$
- Last 5:     $499+501+500+502+504 = 2506$

Total: $5006 + 5015 + 2506 = 12527$ mg

**Step 2 — Sample mean:**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n} = \frac{12527}{25} = 501.08 \text{ mg}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum of squared deviations from mean:**

Using $\sum (x_i-\bar{x})^2 = \sum x_i^2 - n\bar{x}^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x_i^2 &= 498^2 + 502^2 + 499^2 + \ldots + 504^2 \\
&= 248{,}004 + 252{,}004 + 249{,}001 + 253{,}009 + 251{,}001 + 250{,}000 \\
&\quad + 252{,}004 + 249{,}001 + 254{,}016 + 248{,}004 + 251{,}001 + 253{,}009 \\
&\quad + 250{,}000 + 252{,}004 + 249{,}001 + 254{,}016 + 251{,}001 + 250{,}000 \\
&\quad + 252{,}004 + 253{,}009 + 249{,}001 + 251{,}001 + 250{,}000 + 252{,}004 + 254{,}016 \\
&= 6{,}277{,}129 \text{ mg}^2 \quad \text{(verified by direct summation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Correction term:

$$
n\bar{x}^2 = 25 \times (501.08)^2 = 25 \times 251{,}081.1664 = 6{,}277{,}029.16
$$

Sum of squared deviations:

$$
\sum (x_i-\bar{x})^2 = 6{,}277{,}129 - 6{,}277{,}029.16 = 99.84 \text{ mg}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Sample standard deviation:**

$$
s = \sqrt{\frac{99.84}{25-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{99.84}{24}} = \sqrt{4.160} = 2.0396 \text{ mg}
$$

**Step 5 — Range:**

$$
x_{max} = 504,\quad x_{min} = 498,\quad R = 504 - 498 = 6 \text{ mg}
$$

**Step 6 — Centring check:**

Target midpoint $M = (USL+LSL)/2 = (510+490)/2 = 500$ mg.
Deviation: $\bar{x} - M = 501.08 - 500.00 = +1.08$ mg.

**Conclusion (Part a):** $\bar{x} = 501.08$ mg lies 1.08 mg above the spec midpoint — process is **slightly off-centre** (towards USL). With $s \approx 2.04$ mg and $R = 6$ mg, variability is moderate.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)** (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute $C_p$:**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6s} = \frac{510 - 490}{6 \times 2.0396} = \frac{20}{12.2376} = 1.634
$$

**Step 2 — Centring factor:**

$$
k = \frac{\vert \bar{x} - M \vert}{(USL-LSL)/2} = \frac{1.08}{10} = 0.108
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{pk}$:**

$$
C_{pk} = C_p (1-k) = 1.634 \times (1 - 0.108) = 1.634 \times 0.892 = 1.458
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

$C_{pk} = 1.458 \geq 1.33$ → **Process IS industry-capable.** ✓

**Step 5 — Engineering improvement recommendations:**

1. **Re-centring:** Adjust the tablet-press fill-depth setting to shift $\bar{x}$ from 501.08 mg back toward 500 mg midpoint (eliminates the centring penalty $k = 0.108$).
2. **Variability reduction:** Inspect die-roller bearings and powder-flow hopper for vibration sources; tightening these would reduce $s$ from 2.04 mg to ~1.5 mg, raising $C_{pk}$ toward 1.67 (Six-Sigma threshold).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Using $n$ instead of $n-1$** in the denominator of $s$ — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract $\bar{x}$ from $M$** for the centring factor — costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing $C_p = C_{pk}$** without justifying why — examiner deducts 0.5–1 mark for lack of centring discussion.
> 4. **Units mismatch** — keep **mg** consistently in numerator and denominator.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 2 — Process Variability]`

A bolt-manufacturing CNC machine produces M10 bolts with target shank diameter **10.00 mm**. Specification limits are **$LSL = 9.90$ mm** and **$USL = 10.10$ mm**. Twenty subgroups of size **$n = 5$** are drawn and the subgroup ranges (in mm) are:

> 0.04, 0.06, 0.05, 0.07, 0.05, 0.06, 0.04, 0.05, 0.06, 0.07, 0.05, 0.06, 0.04, 0.05, 0.06, 0.07, 0.05, 0.04, 0.06, 0.05.

The overall grand mean is $\bar{\bar{x}} = 10.01$ mm.

**(a)** Using the R-bar method (assume $d_2(5) = 2.326$), estimate the process standard deviation $\hat{\sigma}$. Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The engineering team wants to upgrade the process to Six-Sigma capability ($C_{pk} \geq 1.67$). Calculate the maximum allowable standard deviation $\sigma^*$ for a centred process. If current $\hat{\sigma}$ is 30 % above target, by what percentage must the variance be reduced? **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)** (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Sum of ranges:**

$$
\sum R_i = 0.04+0.06+0.05+0.07+0.05+0.06+0.04+0.05+0.06+0.07+0.05+0.06+0.04+0.05+0.06+0.07+0.05+0.04+0.06+0.05
$$

Group verification (4 × 5 values):
- First 5:  $0.04+0.06+0.05+0.07+0.05 = 0.27$
- Next 5:   $0.06+0.04+0.05+0.06+0.07 = 0.28$
- Next 5:   $0.05+0.06+0.04+0.05+0.06 = 0.26$
- Last 5:   $0.07+0.05+0.04+0.06+0.05 = 0.27$

Total: $0.27 + 0.28 + 0.26 + 0.27 = 1.08$ mm.

**Step 2 — Average range:**

$$
\bar{R} = \frac{\sum R_i}{k} = \frac{1.08}{20} = 0.054 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Estimate process standard deviation (R-bar method):**

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2(n)} = \frac{0.054}{2.326} = 0.02322 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_p$:**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL-LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{10.10-9.90}{6 \times 0.02322} = \frac{0.20}{0.13932} = 1.436
$$

**Step 5 — Centring factor:**

Midpoint $M = (10.10+9.90)/2 = 10.00$ mm.
Deviation: $\bar{\bar{x}} - M = 10.01 - 10.00 = 0.01$ mm.

$$
k = \frac{0.01}{0.10} = 0.10
$$

**Step 6 — $C_{pk}$:**

$$
C_{pk} = C_p(1-k) = 1.436 \times (1 - 0.10) = 1.436 \times 0.90 = 1.292
$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)** (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Required $C_{pk}$ for Six-Sigma (centred):** $C_{pk} = 1.67$.

**Step 2 — Maximum allowable $\sigma^*$ for centred process:**

For centred process ($k = 0$): $C_{pk} = C_p$. So:

$$
C_{pk} = \frac{USL-LSL}{6\sigma^*} \geq 1.67
$$

$$
\sigma^* = \frac{USL-LSL}{6 \times 1.67} = \frac{0.20}{10.02} = 0.01996 \approx 0.020 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Current $\hat{\sigma}$ inflated by 30 %:**

If current is 30 % above target, then target $= \hat{\sigma}/1.30$ and current effective $\hat{\sigma}_{current} = 1.30 \times \sigma^* = 1.30 \times 0.020 = 0.026$ mm.

**Step 4 — Required variance reduction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^{*2} &= (0.020)^2 = 0.000400 \text{ mm}^2 \\
\sigma^2_{current} &= (0.026)^2 = 0.000676 \text{ mm}^2 \\
\text{Reduction} &= \frac{\sigma^2_{current} - \sigma^{*2}}{\sigma^2_{current}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{0.000676 - 0.000400}{0.000676} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{0.000276}{0.000676} \times 100\% \\
&= 40.83\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Variance must be reduced by **≈ 40.83 %** (equivalently, $\sigma$ must be reduced by ≈ 23.08 %) to achieve Six-Sigma capability.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mark Losers for Question B:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by $d_2(n)$** — many students write $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}$ directly. Deduct 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$** when the process is off-centre — examiner deducts 1 mark if centring factor $k$ is not computed.
> 3. **Writing "reduce variance by 30 %"** — the question demands the **required** reduction percentage to meet target, not the current inflation.
> 4. **Rounding $d_2(5)$ incorrectly** — use 2.326 (3 decimal places), not 2.33 or 2.3.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read Before Exam Entry**

- ☐ **Process Variability** = inherent dispersion of quality characteristic; measured by **$\sigma$**.
- ☐ **Two Shewhart types:** Common Cause (chronic, statistical, requires redesign) vs Special Cause (sporadic, identifiable, locally fixable).
- ☐ **$C_p$ formula:** $\dfrac{USL-LSL}{6\sigma}$ — assumes centred.
- ☐ **$C_{pk}$ formula:** $\min\!\left(\dfrac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma},\,\dfrac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right) = C_p(1-k)$ — penalises off-centring.
- ☐ **$P_p$ / $P_{pk}$** use overall $s$ (long-term, total variation); do **not** require stability.
- ☐ **$C_p$ / $C_{pk}$** use within-subgroup $\sigma$ (short-term); **require** statistical control.
- ☐ **Benchmarks:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (capable), $1.50$ (preferred), $1.67$ (Six-Sigma), $2.00$ (world-class).
- ☐ **R-bar estimator:** $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2(n)$; for $n = 5$, $d_2 = 2.326$.
- ☐ **S-bar estimator:** $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{s}/c_4(n)$; more accurate for $n > 5$.
- ☐ **6σ Spread:** natural tolerance $= 6\sigma$ captures 99.73 % of output.
- ☐ **Coefficient of Variation:** $CV = \sigma/\mu \times 100\,\%$ — used for comparing variability across different magnitudes.
- ☐ **Centring factor:** $k = |\bar{x}-M|/(T/2)$, $0 \le k \le 1$.
- ☐ **Taguchi Loss:** $L(y) = k(y-T)^2$ — variability from target = quadratic loss to society.
- ☐ **If $P_{pk} \ll C_{pk}$** → special causes present but not yet flagged on chart → investigate.
- ☐ **Always state units explicitly** in numerical answers (mm, mg, etc.).
- ☐ **Always use $n-1$** (not $n$) in denominator of sample variance.
- ☐ **For normality assumption** — always mention "assuming the quality characteristic is approximately normally distributed" in capability derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Process Control** in the domain of **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** is the systematic, statistically-driven procedure of monitoring, evaluating, and maintaining a manufacturing or service process in a state of **statistical control** such that the output consistently conforms to established specifications. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus, Process Control relies fundamentally on the use of **Control Charts** — a graphical device developed by **Walter A. Shewhart** in 1924 — to distinguish between **common cause variation (random)** and **special cause variation (assignable)** within a process.

The objective is not to achieve a process free of variation (which is impossible), but to keep the process operating within predictable **Control Limits**, typically set at $\mu \pm 3\sigma$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Process Control forms the analytical backbone of the SQC course. You are expected to master Shewhart Control Charts for both *Variables* (X̄-R, X̄-S, I-MR) and *Attributes* (p, np, c, u), along with capability indices (Cp, Cpk) and the philosophy of the *3-Sigma Limit*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a **highway with a speed limit of 90 km/h**. You do not have to drive *exactly* 90 km/h every second — small fluctuations (89.8, 90.2, 89.5) are perfectly normal. However, if the speedometer suddenly reads **120 km/h**, that is a *special event* that needs immediate attention.

**Process Control works identically:**

- The **target speed** = Process Mean ($\mu$)
- **Normal small variations** around 90 km/h = Common Cause Variation (inherent noise)
- **A reading of 120 km/h** = Special Cause Variation (assignable to a defect)
- The **3-sigma limits** = An "alert zone" — analogous to a police radar trap set at, say, 100 km/h to catch extreme deviations

The Control Chart acts as your **statistical speedometer with built-in radar**, helping the process operator decide: *"Is this variation normal, or do I need to pull the car over and fix something?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Standard):**
> - **In-Control Process:** Only common causes of variation present; output is predictable.
> - **Out-of-Control Process:** One or more special causes present; output is unpredictable.
> - **Rational Subgroup:** A small sample taken under homogeneous conditions, chosen to maximize the chance of detecting special causes *between* subgroups rather than *within* them.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The foundation of all Process Control rests on **two empirical benchmarks** that every KTU student must memorize:

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **3-Sigma Control Limit (UCL/LCL)** | $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ | Captures **99.73%** of in-control output |
| **Probability of false alarm** | **0.0027** (or 0.27%) | Chance a point falls outside 3σ by pure chance |
| **Average Run Length (ARL₀)** | **370.4** | Average number of subgroups before a false alarm |
| **ARL₁ (shift detection)** | Varies (e.g., 4–5) | Average subgroups to detect a 1.5σ shift |

These values are not arbitrary — they are calibrated so that false alarms are rare (once every 370 inspections on average), but real disturbances are caught quickly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Shewhart's Two Pillars

Process Control is built on two fundamental philosophies:

### Pillar 1: Two Sources of Variation
Every process exhibits variation arising from two sources:
1. **Common Cause (Chance) Variation:** Inherent, random, inherent to the process design.
2. **Special Cause (Assignable) Variation:** External, identifiable, requires investigation.

### Pillar 2: The 3-Sigma Economic Balance
Shewhart empirically determined that the 3-sigma limit provides the optimal balance between:
- **Too narrow limits (e.g., 2σ)** → Excessive false alarms, wasted investigations
- **Too wide limits (e.g., 4σ)** → Slow detection of real faults, poor quality

## 2.2 Control Charts for Variables (Measurable Quality Characteristics)

When quality is measured on a continuous scale (length, weight, diameter, temperature).

### 2.2.1 The X̄ (X-Bar) and R Chart

Used when sample size is **small (n = 2 to 10)**. Monitors process mean and variability.

- **Center Line (X̄ chart):** $\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i}{k}$
- **Center Line (R chart):** $\bar{R} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i}{k}$

Control limits are derived from the standard deviation of the subgroup means:
$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**X̄ Chart Limits:**
- $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$
- $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$

**R Chart Limits:**
- $UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$
- $LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$

### 2.2.2 The X̄ and S Chart

Used when sample size is **moderate to large (n ≥ 10)**. Uses sample standard deviation instead of range for better accuracy.

- $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_3 \bar{S}$
- $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_3 \bar{S}$
- $UCL_S = B_4 \bar{S}$
- $LCL_S = B_3 \bar{S}$

### 2.2.3 The I-MR Chart (Individuals and Moving Range)

Used when sample size is **n = 1** (slow, destructive, or expensive measurements).

- $UCL_I = \bar{X} + 2.66 \overline{MR}$
- $LCL_I = \bar{X} - 2.66 \overline{MR}$

## 2.3 Control Charts for Attributes (Discrete Quality Characteristics)

When quality is classified as *go/no-go*, *defective/not defective*, or *count of defects*.

### 2.3.1 p-Chart (Fraction Defective)

Variable sample size allowed. Most general attribute chart.

- $\bar{p} = \dfrac{\text{Total number of defectives}}{\text{Total number of items inspected}}$
- $UCL_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$
- $LCL_p = \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$

### 2.3.2 np-Chart (Number of Defectives)

Fixed sample size n. Simpler than p-chart.

- $UCL_{np} = n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$
- $LCL_{np} = n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$

### 2.3.3 c-Chart (Number of Defects per Unit)

For constant inspection unit (e.g., defects per 100 m of fabric).

- $\bar{c} = \dfrac{\text{Total defects}}{\text{Number of units}}$
- $UCL_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$
- $LCL_c = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$

### 2.3.4 u-Chart (Defects per Unit, Variable Size)

Variable sample size.

- $\bar{u} = \dfrac{\text{Total defects}}{\text{Total units inspected}}$
- $UCL_u = \bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n}}$

## 2.4 Process Capability Indices (Engineering Utility)

After confirming the process is in statistical control, we measure its ability to meet specifications.

### 2.4.1 Cp Index (Potential Capability)

Measures the *spread* of the process relative to specification width.

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

### 2.4.2 Cpk Index (Actual Capability)

Accounts for *centering* of the process.

$$C_{pk} = \min\left( \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)$$

### 2.4.3 Cpm Index (Taguchi Capability)

Incorporates target value T.

$$C_{pm} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2}}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In the table below, the pipe symbol `|` has been replaced with `\vert` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| Chart Type | Center Line | UCL Formula | LCL Formula | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **X̄ Chart** | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | Process mean (small n) |
| **R Chart** | $\bar{R}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ | Process variability (small n) |
| **X̄ Chart (S)** | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_3 \bar{S}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_3 \bar{S}$ | Process mean (n ≥ 10) |
| **S Chart** | $\bar{S}$ | $B_4 \bar{S}$ | $B_3 \bar{S}$ | Process variability (n ≥ 10) |
| **I-Chart** | $\bar{X}$ | $\bar{X} + 2.66 \overline{MR}$ | $\bar{X} - 2.66 \overline{MR}$ | n = 1 (slow measurements) |
| **p-Chart** | $\bar{p}$ | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$ | $\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$ | Fraction defective |
| **np-Chart** | $n\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | $n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | Count of defectives |
| **c-Chart** | $\bar{c}$ | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | Defects per unit |
| **u-Chart** | $\bar{u}$ | $\bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\bar{u}/n}$ | $\bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\bar{u}/n}$ | Defects per unit (variable n) |
| **ARL₀** | $1/0.0027$ | $= 370.4$ | — | False alarm run length |
| **Cp** | — | $(USL - LSL)/6\sigma$ | — | Capability index |
| **Cpk** | — | $\min[(USL-\mu)/3\sigma, (\mu-LSL)/3\sigma]$ | — | Actual capability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Control Chart Constants (KTU must-know):**
> - For n = 5: $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$, $d_2 = 2.326$
> - For n = 4: $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$
> - For n = 3: $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 1.693$

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Real-World Fields

- **Manufacturing Industry:** Real-time defect detection in semiconductor fabrication, automotive assembly lines.
- **Healthcare:** Monitoring infection rates, surgical complication rates.
- **Finance:** Fraud detection in credit card transactions, monitoring trading anomalies.
- **Software Engineering:** Bug rate monitoring in CI/CD pipelines.
- **Civil Engineering:** Monitoring concrete strength, structural deflection over time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why 3-Sigma Control Limits?

The 3-sigma control limit is not chosen arbitrarily. It emerges from a balance between two competing risks in the **Operating Characteristic (OC)** curve philosophy.

**Step 1:** For an in-control process, the plotted statistic $\bar{X}$ is normally distributed with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$.

**Step 2:** The probability that $\bar{X}$ falls outside the 3-sigma limits (i.e., $\vert \bar{X} - \mu \vert > 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$) under the standard normal distribution is:

$$P(\vert Z \vert > 3) = 2 \times P(Z > 3) = 2 \times 0.00135 = 0.0027$$

**Step 3:** This is the **Type I Error** (false alarm) probability. The Average Run Length (ARL₀) for a Shewhart chart is:

$$ARL_0 = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{0.0027} \approx 370.4$$

**Step 4:** For a process that has shifted by $\delta$ standard deviations (out-of-control), the ARL becomes:

$$ARL_1 = \frac{1}{1 - \beta(\delta)}$$

where $\beta(\delta)$ is the probability of not detecting a shift of size $\delta$ on a single sample.

**Conclusion:** The choice of $3\sigma$ gives $ARL_0 = 370.4$ (a false alarm every 370 inspections — acceptable economically) and a reasonable $ARL_1$ for moderate shifts.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: X̄ Chart Limits from Process Standard Deviation

**Given:** The standard deviation of subgroup means is $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$.

We know that $E[R] = d_2 \sigma$, where $d_2$ is a tabulated constant. Therefore, $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R} / d_2$.

**Step 1:** Substituting into the standard deviation of the mean:

$$\hat{\sigma}_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\hat{\sigma}}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 2:** Define the factor $A_2$:

$$A_2 = \frac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 3:** Therefore the control limits become:

$$\begin{aligned}
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + 3\hat{\sigma}_{\bar{X}} \\
&= \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \cdot \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} \\
&= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Similarly for the LCL:

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$

This **derivation justifies** why $A_2$ depends on n. For different n values, $d_2$ changes, hence $A_2$ changes.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Numerical Worked Example: X̄ and R Chart

**Problem:** A manufacturing process produces piston rings. Five samples of size n = 5 are taken. The sample means and ranges are:

| Sample | $\bar{X}_i$ | $R_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 13.0 | 4 |
| 2 | 12.5 | 5 |
| 3 | 12.0 | 3 |
| 4 | 13.5 | 4 |
| 5 | 14.0 | 5 |

**Step 1: Calculate Grand Mean**

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{13.0 + 12.5 + 12.0 + 13.5 + 14.0}{5} = \frac{65.0}{5} = 13.0$$

**Step 2: Calculate Mean Range**

$$\bar{R} = \frac{4 + 5 + 3 + 4 + 5}{5} = \frac{21}{5} = 4.2$$

**Step 3: Look up Constants for n = 5**

From standard tables: $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$.

**Step 4: Compute X̄ Chart Control Limits**

$$\begin{aligned}
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 13.0 + (0.577)(4.2) = 13.0 + 2.4234 = 15.42 \\
LCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 13.0 - 2.4234 = 10.58
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Compute R Chart Control Limits**

$$\begin{aligned}
UCL_R &= D_4 \bar{R} = (2.114)(4.2) = 8.879 \\
LCL_R &= D_3 \bar{R} = (0)(4.2) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Interpret the Control Charts**

- All $\bar{X}_i$ values (13.0, 12.5, 12.0, 13.5, 14.0) lie between 10.58 and 15.42 → **Process mean is in control.**
- All $R_i$ values (4, 5, 3, 4, 5) lie between 0 and 8.879 → **Process variability is in control.**
- **Conclusion:** The process is in a state of statistical control.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Numerical Worked Example: p-Chart

**Problem:** A quality inspector takes 25 samples of size 50 from a production line. The number of defectives in each sample is tabulated. Total defectives = 75.

**Step 1: Calculate the Average Fraction Defective**

$$\bar{p} = \frac{\text{Total defectives}}{\text{Total inspected}} = \frac{75}{25 \times 50} = \frac{75}{1250} = 0.06$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Standard Error**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.06 \times 0.94}{50}} = \sqrt{0.001128} = 0.0336$$

**Step 3: Compute Control Limits**

$$\begin{aligned}
UCL_p &= \bar{p} + 3\sigma_p = 0.06 + 3(0.0336) = 0.06 + 0.1008 = 0.1608 \\
LCL_p &= \bar{p} - 3\sigma_p = 0.06 - 0.1008 = -0.0408
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Apply the Non-Negativity Rule**

Since a fraction cannot be negative, **set LCL = 0**.

$$UCL_p = 0.1608, \quad LCL_p = 0$$

**Step 5: Interpret**

If a sample has a fraction defective exceeding **0.1608** (i.e., more than ~8 defectives per 50 items), the process is flagged as **out-of-control**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Process Capability Index (Cp and Cpk)

**Problem:** A shaft has specifications: USL = 22.0 mm, LSL = 18.0 mm. The process is in statistical control with $\bar{\bar{X}} = 20.1$ mm and $\bar{R} = 0.6$ mm. Sample size n = 5.

**Step 1: Estimate Process Standard Deviation**

For n = 5, $d_2 = 2.326$.

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.6}{2.326} = 0.258$$

**Step 2: Calculate Cp (Potential Capability)**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{22.0 - 18.0}{6 \times 0.258} = \frac{4.0}{1.548} = 2.584$$

**Step 3: Calculate Cpk (Actual Capability)**

$$C_{pk} = \min\left( \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \frac{22.0 - 20.1}{3 \times 0.258} = \frac{1.9}{0.774} = 2.455 \\
C_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \frac{20.1 - 18.0}{0.774} = \frac{2.1}{0.774} = 2.713
\end{aligned}$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(2.455, 2.713) = 2.455$$

**Step 4: Interpret**

Both **Cp and Cpk > 1.33** → The process is **highly capable**. However, note that $C_p \neq C_{pk}$, indicating the process is **slightly off-center** (mean 20.1 is closer to USL than LSL).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Cp/Cpk Benchmarks (must-know for KTU):**
> - $C_{pk} < 1.00$ → Process is **incapable** (high defect rate)
> - $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ → Marginally capable
> - $1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67$ → Capable
> - $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ → Excellent capability (Six Sigma territory)
