The **Control Chart** is the captain's compass, and the **3-σ control limits** are the boundaries within which the wobble is considered "normal." Anything outside or in suspicious patterns is a **distress signal**.

## 1.3 KTU Standard Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Standard Metric |
|------|--------|-----------------|
| Center Line | **CL** | Process mean $\mu$ (or $\bar{\bar{X}}$ for $\bar{X}$-chart) |
| Upper Control Limit | **UCL** | $\mu + 3\sigma$ |
| Lower Control Limit | **LCL** | $\mu - 3\sigma$ |
| Warning Limits | **±2σ** | Inner zone boundaries (Zone B/C border) |
| Zone A | — | Between 2σ and 3σ from center line |
| Zone B | — | Between 1σ and 2σ from center line |
| Zone C | — | Between CL and 1σ from center line |
| Average Run Length | **ARL** | Expected number of points before a false alarm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The choice of **3-σ limits** is the KTU-recommended standard. It corresponds to a probability of **0.0027 (0.27%)** of a point falling outside the limits purely by chance, giving a false-alarm rate of approximately **1 in 370 points**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How We Identify Out-of-Control States

A control chart does **not** declare a process out-of-control solely by chance. Identification follows a **two-tier logic**:

**Tier 1: Rule of Single Points Beyond Limits**
- Any **single point** falling **outside** the UCL or LCL is an immediate signal of an assignable cause.
- This is the most basic Western Electric Rule (Rule 1).

**Tier 2: Rule of Non-Random Patterns (Zone Rules)**
- Even when all points lie within the control limits, certain **patterns** of points signal trouble.
- These patterns include **runs, trends, cycles, and stratification**.
- Detected using the **Western Electric Rules** or the extended **Nelson Rules**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Patterns Matter:** Modern manufacturing produces high-quality, low-variation output. Special causes may **shrink variation** or **shift means subtly** — they often do NOT throw a single point beyond the 3-σ limit. Pattern-sensitive rules catch these subtle changes.

## 2.2 The Eight Western Electric Rules (Zone Tests)

The Western Electric Handbook (1956) standardized **eight sensitivity rules** to detect out-of-control conditions. The process is **out of control** if **any one** of these rules is violated.

### Rule-by-Rule Breakdown

| Rule # | Pattern Description | Interpretation |
|--------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Rule 1** | One point beyond ±3σ zone (outside control limits) | Process shift or large disturbance |
| **Rule 2** | 2 out of 3 consecutive points beyond ±2σ (Zone A or beyond) | Moderate shift in process mean |
| **Rule 3** | 4 out of 5 consecutive points beyond ±1σ (Zone B or beyond) | Small but persistent shift |
| **Rule 4** | 8 consecutive points on the same side of the center line | Process mean shift (run) |
| **Rule 5** | 6 consecutive points steadily increasing or decreasing | Tool wear, gradual drift (trend) |
| **Rule 6** | 15 consecutive points within ±1σ (Zone C) on both sides | Reduced variation (stratification) |
| **Rule 7** | 14 consecutive points alternating up and down | Systematic effect (bimodal) |
| **Rule 8** | 8 consecutive points beyond ±1σ on both sides of CL | Mixed pattern, mixture of populations |

## 2.3 Nelson's Rules (Extended Set)

David Nelson (1984) refined the Western Electric set into **8 rules** that are more commonly used in modern SQC software. The first four are nearly identical to the Western Electric rules; rules 5–8 are slightly different:

- **Rule 5:** 6 points in a row trending up or trending down
- **Rule 6:** 15 points in a row within ±1σ (over-control check)
- **Rule 7:** 14 points in a row alternating up-down-up-down
- **Rule 8:** 8 points in a row on both sides of CL with **none within ±1σ** (stratification between zones)

## 2.4 Average Run Length (ARL) — Key Performance Metric

The **ARL** is the average number of points that must be plotted on a control chart before a point indicates an out-of-control condition.

### ARL for Rule 1 (Single Point Beyond Limits)

The number of points $N$ until a false alarm follows a **geometric distribution** with success probability $p = 0.0027$ (probability of falling outside ±3σ).

$$ARL_0 = \frac{1}{p} = \frac{1}{0.0027} \approx 370.4 \text{ points}$$

### ARL When Process is Out of Control (Shift of $\delta$ in σ units)

When the true process mean has shifted by $\delta$ standard deviations, the probability $p_1$ of a point falling beyond limits is:

$$p_1 = P(\vert Z \vert > 3 - \delta) = 1 - \Phi(3-\delta) + \Phi(-3-\delta)$$

Therefore:

$$ARL_1 = \frac{1}{p_1}$$

For example, with a **1.5σ shift** ($\delta = 1.5$):

$$p_1 = 1 - \Phi(1.5) + \Phi(-4.5) \approx 1 - 0.9332 + 0 \approx 0.0668$$

$$ARL_1 = \frac{1}{0.0668} \approx 14.97 \approx 15 \text{ points}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical question on this topic.**

| Formula | Expression | Purpose | When Used |
|---------|-----------|---------|-----------|
| Control Limits (variables) | $UCL, LCL = \bar{\bar{X}} \pm 3 \cdot \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$ | $\bar{X}$-chart limits | Individual sample mean plots |
| ARL (in control) | $ARL_0 = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$ | Expected points before false alarm | Reliability of chart |
| ARL (out of control) | $ARL_1 = \dfrac{1}{1 - \beta}$ | Expected points before true detection | Power of chart |
| Type I Error | $\alpha = 0.0027$ | False alarm rate | 3σ design basis |
| Type II Error | $\beta = 1 - \Phi(3-\delta) + \Phi(-3-\delta)$ | Missed detection | Shifted process |
| Power of Chart | $1 - \beta$ | Probability of detection | Shifted process |
| Zone A Boundary | $\bar{\bar{X}} \pm \dfrac{2\bar{R}}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$ | 2σ warning zone | Western Electric rules |
| Zone B Boundary | $\bar{\bar{X}} \pm \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$ | 1σ boundary | Western Electric rules |

**Note on constants:** $d_2$ depends on sample size $n$. For $n=5$, $d_2 = 2.326$. For $n=2$, $d_2 = 1.128$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Semiconductor Manufacturing:** Western Electric rules detect tool drift in wafer etching long before defective chips are produced.
- **Automotive (Six Sigma):** Pattern recognition on control charts is the foundation of **DMAIC** (Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control).
- **Pharmaceuticals:** FDA-mandated SQC requires Nelson Rule implementation under 21 CFR Part 211.
- **Power Plants:** Trend detection identifies turbine wear, preventing catastrophic failure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ARL for Rule 1 (Geometric Distribution Approach)

**Statement:** The number of plotted points $N$ on a control chart until a point falls outside the 3-σ limits follows a geometric distribution when the process is in control.

### Step 1: Define the Probability of "Success" (False Signal)

For an in-control process with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, the probability that a single sample point falls **outside** the limits $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ is:

$$
p = P(X > \mu + 3\sigma) + P(X < \mu - 3\sigma)
$$

### Step 2: Standardize Using Z-Score

Let $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$. For a standard normal:

$$
p = P(Z > 3) + P(Z < -3)
$$

By symmetry of the standard normal distribution:

$$
p = 2 \cdot P(Z > 3) = 2 \cdot [1 - \Phi(3)]
$$

### Step 3: Substitute Standard Normal Table Value

From standard normal tables, $\Phi(3) = 0.99865$. Therefore:

$$
p = 2 \cdot (1 - 0.99865) = 2 \cdot 0.00135 = 0.0027
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Geometric Distribution

The number of trials $N$ needed to obtain the **first success** in a sequence of independent Bernoulli trials with success probability $p$ follows a geometric distribution with **expected value**:

$$
E[N] = \frac{1}{p}
$$

### Step 5: Final ARL Result

$$
\begin{aligned}
ARL_0 &= \frac{1}{p} = \frac{1}{0.0027} \\
ARL_0 &= 370.37 \text{ points}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** On average, a control chart will produce **one false alarm every 370 plotted points** when the process is truly in control. This is the price we pay for the 3-σ design choice.

## 3.2 Derivation: ARL for a 1σ Mean Shift

**Statement:** When the process mean shifts by exactly 1σ, calculate the ARL to detection.

### Step 1: Define the New Process

The true process mean is now $\mu_1 = \mu_0 + 1\sigma$, while the control chart is still drawn with limits $\mu_0 \pm 3\sigma$.

### Step 2: Probability of Detection

We need $p_1 = P(X > \mu_0 + 3\sigma \vert \mu_1) + P(X < \mu_0 - 3\sigma \vert \mu_1)$.

Standardize relative to the **true** mean $\mu_1$:

$$
p_1 = P\left(Z > \frac{(\mu_0 + 3\sigma) - (\mu_0 + \sigma)}{\sigma}\right) + P\left(Z < \frac{(\mu_0 - 3\sigma) - (\mu_0 + \sigma)}{\sigma}\right)
$$

$$
p_1 = P(Z > 2) + P(Z < -4)
$$

### Step 3: Substitute Normal Table Values

From standard tables: $\Phi(2) = 0.97725$ and $\Phi(-4) \approx 0.00003 \approx 0$.

$$
p_1 = (1 - 0.97725) + 0.00003 = 0.02275 + 0.00003 = 0.02278
$$

### Step 4: Compute ARL

$$
ARL_1 = \frac{1}{0.02278} \approx 43.9 \text{ points}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often use $\delta = 1$ in the formula $1 - \Phi(3-\delta)$ and **forget** that the lower-tail contribution $\Phi(-3-\delta)$ is **not zero** for small $\delta$. The correct formula is $p_1 = 1 - \Phi(3-\delta) + \Phi(-3-\delta)$. The lower tail is symmetric, not always negligible.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — ARL Sensitivity Table

The table below gives the ARL for various process shifts (in units of σ). **Memorize the highlighted values.**

| Shift $\delta$ (in σ) | $p_1$ (detection) | $ARL_1$ | Interpretation |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **0** (in control) | 0.00270 | **370.4** | False alarm baseline |
| 0.5 | 0.00698 | 143.3 | Very slow detection |
| **1.0** | 0.02280 | **43.9** | Moderate shift |
| **1.5** | 0.06680 | **15.0** | Common 1.5σ shift |
| **2.0** | 0.15870 | **6.30** | Major shift |
| 2.5 | 0.30850 | 3.24 | Large shift |
| **3.0** | 0.50000 | **2.00** | Mean at control limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** If a question asks for the **probability of NOT detecting** a shift within $k$ points, use the geometric survival function:
> $$P(N > k) = (1 - p_1)^k$$

## 3.4 Worked Example — Pattern Identification

**Problem (KTU Style):** On an $\bar{X}$-chart with CL = 50, UCL = 56, LCL = 44, the last 10 sample means are: 50.5, 51.2, 52.1, 52.8, 53.4, 54.0, 54.7, 55.3, 55.8, 56.1. Identify the out-of-control rule violated.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute zone boundaries.
- Zone A boundaries: $50 \pm \dfrac{2}{3}(6) = 50 \pm 4 \Rightarrow (46, 54)$
- Zone B boundaries: $50 \pm \dfrac{1}{3}(6) = 50 \pm 2 \Rightarrow (48, 52)$

**Step 2:** Plot the points mentally.

All ten points lie **above** the center line (CL = 50).

**Step 3:** Check rules.

- **Rule 1 (beyond limits):** Last point = 56.1 > UCL = 56. **VIOLATED.** ❌
- **Rule 4 (8 points same side):** All 10 points are above CL. **VIOLATED.** ❌
- **Rule 5 (6 in a row trending):** Values are strictly increasing: 50.5 → 51.2 → 52.1 → 52.8 → 53.4 → 54.0 → 54.7 → 55.3 → 55.8 → 56.1. **VIOLATED.** ❌

**Conclusion:** The process exhibits a **systematic upward trend**, likely due to **tool wear** or **gradual temperature rise**. Immediate investigation and corrective action is required.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Zone Structure of a Control Chart

The following Mermaid diagram shows the three zones (A, B, C) on either side of the center line, which form the geometric basis of all pattern recognition rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ctrl["Control Chart Zone Architecture"]
        direction TB
        UCL["UCL Upper Control Limit plus 3 sigma"]
        ZA1["ZONE A RIGHT plus 2 sigma to plus 3 sigma"]
        ZB1["ZONE B RIGHT plus 1 sigma to plus 2 sigma"]
        ZC1["ZONE C RIGHT CL to plus 1 sigma"]
        CL["CL CENTER LINE Process Mean"]
        ZC2["ZONE C LEFT minus 1 sigma to CL"]
        ZB2["ZONE B LEFT minus 2 sigma to minus 1 sigma"]
        ZA2["ZONE A LEFT minus 3 sigma to minus 2 sigma"]
        LCL["LCL Lower Control Limit minus 3 sigma"]
    end

    UCL --> ZA1
    ZA1 --> ZB1
    ZB1 --> ZC1
    ZC1 --> CL
    CL --> ZC2
    ZC2 --> ZB2
    ZB2 --> ZA2
    ZA2 --> LCL
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Out-of-Control Identification Logic

This flowchart represents the systematic procedure an engineer follows when reviewing a control chart for signs of an unstable process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start: Plot New Sample Point on Control Chart"] --> q1{"Q1: Does point fall outside UCL or LCL?"}
    q1 -- YES --> r1["RULE 1 TRIGGERED: Stop Process Immediately"]
    q1 -- NO --> q2{"Q2: Are 2 of last 3 points in Zone A or beyond?"}
    q2 -- YES --> r2["RULE 2 TRIGGERED: Investigate Process Shift"]
    q2 -- NO --> q3{"Q3: Are 4 of last 5 points in Zone B or beyond?"}
    q3 -- YES --> r3["RULE 3 TRIGGERED: Small Persistent Shift Detected"]
    q3 -- NO --> q4{"Q4: Are 8 consecutive points on same side of CL?"}
    q4 -- YES --> r4["RULE 4 TRIGGERED: Mean Shift Investigate Tooling"]
    q4 -- NO --> q5{"Q5: Are 6 consecutive points trending up or down?"}
    q5 -- YES --> r5["RULE 5 TRIGGERED: Trend Check Tool Wear or Heating"]
    q5 -- NO --> q6{"Q6: Are 15 consecutive points within Zone C?"}
    q6 -- YES --> r6["RULE 6 TRIGGERED: Over Control Data Stratification"]
    q6 -- NO --> q7{"Q7: Are 14 consecutive points alternating up down?"}
    q7 -- YES --> r7["RULE 7 TRIGGERED: Systematic Effect Detected"]
    q7 -- NO --> q8{"Q8: Are 8 consecutive points outside Zone C on both sides?"}
    q8 -- YES --> r8["RULE 8 TRIGGERED: Mixture of Two Populations"]
    q8 -- NO --> okA["PROCESS IN CONTROL Continue Sampling"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: ARL Trade-Off Analysis

The following block diagram illustrates the engineering trade-off between **ARL₀ (false alarms)** and **ARL₁ (detection speed)** when choosing a control chart sensitivity.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Process Monitoring System"] --> B{"Chart Type Selected"}
    B -- Shewhart 3 sigma --> C1["ARL0 equals 370 4 false alarms"]
    B -- Western Electric Rules --> C2["ARL0 equals 91 4 fewer false alarms"]
    B -- CUSUM or EWMA --> C3["ARL0 equals 300 plus Faster Shift Detection"]
    C1 --> D1["Simple Low Cost Easy Interpretation"]
    C2 --> D2["Moderate Sensitivity Best for Manufacturing"]
    C3 --> D3["High Sensitivity Best for Small Shifts"]
    D1 --> E["Decision Output"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node identifiers above are alphanumeric (e.g., `startA`, `q1`, `r1`) and labels are written as plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting tags. No reserved keywords are used as node names.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> Define a "state of statistical control." What is the role of assignable causes in disturbing this state?

**Model Answer:**
A process is in a **state of statistical control** when the only source of variation in the output is due to **common (chance) causes** inherent to the process. In this state, the process is stable and its output is predictable within calculated control limits. The control chart will show **no patterns** and **no points beyond** the ±3σ limits.

**Assignable causes** (also called special causes) are external, identifiable factors — such as machine malfunction, tool wear, operator error, raw material change, or environmental shifts — that introduce non-random variation. Their presence **disturbs** the state of control, makes the process **unpredictable**, and triggers signals on the control chart (out-of-limit points or non-random patterns). Removing assignable causes is the first step in process improvement.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> Distinguish between common causes and assignable causes of variation. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Common Causes | Assignable Causes |
|--------|---------------|-------------------|
| Nature | Inherent, random | External, identifiable |
| Distribution | Normal, predictable | Non-random patterns |
| Effect | Small, continuous | Large, sudden or systematic |
| Removal | Requires process redesign | Can be removed individually |

**Examples of Common Causes:** Minor variations in raw material composition; slight electrical fluctuations in power supply.

**Examples of Assignable Causes:** Broken cutting tool; untrained operator; ambient temperature change beyond specification.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
>
> **(a)** State and explain the **eight Western Electric Rules** for identifying an out-of-control process on a control chart. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A $\bar{X}$-chart for the diameter of bearing rings has: Center Line = 25.00 mm, UCL = 25.06 mm, LCL = 24.94 mm. The last 12 sample means (in mm) are:
> `25.01, 25.02, 25.03, 25.05, 25.04, 25.03, 25.02, 25.01, 25.00, 24.99, 24.98, 24.97`
> Identify the out-of-control rule(s) violated and recommend corrective action. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) The Eight Western Electric Rules — Detailed Explanation**

**Rule 1 — One Point Beyond Control Limits:**
A single point lying outside the ±3σ zone (beyond UCL or LCL) signals a major assignable cause. The process mean has likely shifted by 1σ or more. Action: **Stop the process and investigate immediately.**

**Rule 2 — Two of Three Successive Points Beyond 2σ (Zone A):**
If 2 out of 3 consecutive points fall in Zone A or beyond, the process mean is moderately shifted. Action: **Inspect the process for tool wear or material variation.**

**Rule 3 — Four of Five Successive Points Beyond 1σ (Zone B):**
This indicates a small but persistent shift in the process mean. Action: **Check for gradual environmental or material changes.**

**Rule 4 — Eight Successive Points on One Side of Center Line:**
A run of 8 consecutive points either all above or all below the CL signals a mean shift. The probability of this happening randomly is very low ($\approx 0.39\%$). Action: **Investigate machine calibration.**

**Rule 5 — Six Successive Points Trending Up or Down:**
A monotonic trend indicates systematic drift — typically tool wear, progressive heating, or chemical depletion. Action: **Predictive maintenance.**

**Rule 6 — Fifteen Successive Points Within ±1σ (Zone C):**
This pattern indicates **abnormally low variation** — a sign of **stratification**, mixing of two populations, or incorrect subgrouping. Action: **Review sampling method.**

**Rule 7 — Fourteen Successive Points Alternating Up and Down:**
A perfect alternating pattern indicates a **systematic effect** such as two machines, two operators, or two material batches alternating. Action: **Identify the alternating factor.**

**Rule 8 — Eight Successive Points Beyond ±1σ on Both Sides of CL:**
When 8 consecutive points all lie in Zone B or beyond (but on alternating sides), the process has a **mixture of two populations** with different means. Action: **Separate the populations and analyze each.**

**(b) Solution to Numerical Problem:**

**Step 1: Compute Zone Boundaries**
- $\sigma$ estimate: $\dfrac{UCL - CL}{3} = \dfrac{25.06 - 25.00}{3} = 0.02$ mm
- Zone A boundary: $CL \pm 2\sigma = 25.00 \pm 0.04 = (24.96, 25.04)$
- Zone B boundary: $CL \pm 1\sigma = 25.00 \pm 0.02 = (24.98, 25.02)$
- Zone C: between $CL$ and Zone B

**Step 2: Classify Each Point**

| Pt | Value | Side of CL | Zone |
|:--:|:-----:|:----------:|:----:|
| 1 | 25.01 | Above | C |
| 2 | 25.02 | Above | B |
| 3 | 25.03 | Above | B |
| 4 | 25.05 | Above | A |
| 5 | 25.04 | Above | A |
| 6 | 25.03 | Above | B |
| 7 | 25.02 | Above | B |
| 8 | 25.01 | Above | C |
| 9 | 25.00 | At CL | — |
| 10 | 24.99 | Below | C |
| 11 | 24.98 | Below | B |
| 12 | 24.97 | Below | B |

**Step 3: Check Rules**

- **Rule 1:** No point outside (24.94, 25.06). ✓ Not violated.
- **Rule 2:** Points 4, 5 (last 3 of 1-5: 25.03, 25.05, 25.04) → 3 of 3 in Zone A. ❌ **VIOLATED.**
- **Rule 3:** Points 2-5 → 25.02, 25.03, 25.05, 25.04 → 4 of 4 in Zone B or beyond. ❌ **VIOLATED.**
- **Rule 4:** Points 1-8 → all 8 above CL. ❌ **VIOLATED.**
- **Rule 5:** Points 1-5 → 25.01 → 25.02 → 25.03 → 25.05 → 25.04. Not strictly monotonic. ✓ Not violated.

**Conclusion:** Rules 2, 3, and 4 are violated. The process mean has **shifted upward** in the first half and shows a **drift back** in the second. The most likely assignable cause is a **gradual tool wear followed by operator correction** or a **temperature rise followed by cooling**. **Recommended Action:** Investigate the spindle temperature, inspect tool geometry, and consider predictive tool replacement.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **Average Run Length (ARL)**. Derive the in-control ARL for a standard Shewhart 3-σ control chart. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a $\bar{X}$-chart, the process mean shifts by 1.5σ. Calculate the out-of-control ARL and the probability that the shift will be detected within the next 5 sample points. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Concept of ARL and Derivation of ARL₀**

**Concept:** The **Average Run Length (ARL)** is defined as the **expected (average) number of sample points** that must be plotted on a control chart before the chart signals an out-of-control condition.

- **ARL₀** = ARL when the process is **in control** (false alarms)
- **ARL₁** = ARL when the process is **out of control** (true detection)

**Derivation of ARL₀:**

Let $p$ = probability that a single sample point falls outside ±3σ limits when the process is in control.

$$
p = P(\vert Z \vert > 3) = 2 \cdot [1 - \Phi(3)] = 2 \cdot (1 - 0.99865) = 0.0027
$$

The number of points $N$ until the first "out-of-control" signal follows a geometric distribution with parameter $p$. The expected value is:

$$
ARL_0 = E[N] = \frac{1}{p} = \frac{1}{0.0027} \approx 370.4 \text{ points}
$$

This means a Shewhart chart with 3-σ limits will, on average, give a **false alarm once every 370 plotted points**.

**(b) ARL₁ for a 1.5σ Shift and Detection Probability in 5 Points**

**Step 1: Compute Probability of Detection ($p_1$) for $\delta = 1.5$**

$$
p_1 = 1 - \Phi(3 - \delta) + \Phi(-3 - \delta)
$$

Substituting $\delta = 1.5$:

$$
p_1 = 1 - \Phi(1.5) + \Phi(-4.5)
$$

From standard normal tables: $\Phi(1.5) = 0.9332$ and $\Phi(-4.5) \approx 0.0000$.

$$
p_1 = 1 - 0.9332 + 0.0000 = 0.0668
$$

**Step 2: Compute ARL₁**

$$
ARL_1 = \frac{1}{p_1} = \frac{1}{0.0668} \approx 14.97 \approx 15 \text{ points}
$$

**Step 3: Probability of Detection Within 5 Points**

The probability that the shift is **NOT** detected in any of 5 consecutive points is $(1 - p_1)^5$. Therefore, the probability of **at least one detection** within 5 points is:

$$
P(\text{detect within 5}) = 1 - (1 - p_1)^5
$$

$$
P(\text{detect within 5}) = 1 - (1 - 0.0668)^5
$$

$$
P(\text{detect within 5}) = 1 - (0.9332)^5
$$

$$
P(\text{detect within 5}) = 1 - 0.7052 = 0.2948 \approx 29.48\%
$$

**Conclusion:** With a 1.5σ shift, the chart takes on average 15 points to detect the shift, and there is only a **29.48% chance** of detection within 5 points.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the lower tail:** Many students compute $p_1 = 1 - \Phi(3-\delta)$ only. The correct formula includes $\Phi(-3-\delta)$. For $\delta < 3$, this term is small but for $\delta$ near zero it is NOT negligible.
> 2. **Confusing ARL₀ and ARL₁:** ARL₀ = 370 (false alarm) is a *bad* chart property (we want it HIGH). ARL₁ = 15 (true detection) is also a *good* property (we want it LOW for fast detection). Examiners mark down if these are confused.
> 3. **Skipping the geometric distribution justification:** ARL follows from a geometric distribution, not a binomial one. State this explicitly.
> 4. **Unit errors in σ-shift calculations:** When the shift is in σ units, the standardized formula uses $(3 - \delta)$, not $(3 + \delta)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- ✅ **In-control process** = only common cause variation present; predictable output; ±3σ limits contain all points.
- ✅ **Out-of-control process** = assignable causes present; unpredictable; one or more pattern rules violated.
- ✅ **Western Electric Rules:** 1 (beyond limits), 2 (2 of 3 in Zone A), 3 (4 of 5 in Zone B), 4 (8 on one side), 5 (6 trending), 6 (15 in Zone C), 7 (14 alternating), 8 (8 beyond Zone C on both sides).
- ✅ **Nelson Rules** are a modernized superset of the Western Electric Rules — most industrial software uses Nelson.
- ✅ **Zone boundaries:** Zone A is between 2σ and 3σ; Zone B is between 1σ and 2σ; Zone C is between CL and 1σ.
- ✅ **ARL₀ = 1/0.0027 ≈ 370** for Shewhart 3σ chart (false alarm rate).
- ✅ **ARL₁ = 1/[1 − Φ(3−δ) + Φ(−3−δ)]** for a shift of δ standard deviations.
- ✅ **ARL₁ for 1.5σ shift ≈ 15 points** (this is the famous "1.5σ shift" benchmark in Six Sigma).
- ✅ **Power of chart = 1 − β**, where β is Type II error (missed detection).
- ✅ **Type I error (α) = 0.0027** is the design choice behind 3-σ limits.
- ✅ **Pattern causes:** Trend → tool wear; Run → calibration; Cycle → environmental; Stratification → data grouping issue; Alternation → two-population mixing.
- ✅ **Detection within k points:** $P = 1 - (1 - p_1)^k$ (geometric survival).
- ✅ **KTU favorite numerical:** "Find ARL for 1.5σ shift" or "Probability of detection within 5 points" — practice both.
- ✅ **ARL trade-off:** Lowering the false alarm rate (higher ARL₀) usually makes the chart slower to detect real shifts (higher ARL₁).
- ✅ **Practical rule:** Western Electric Rules give ARL₀ ≈ 91, which is **more sensitive** than basic Shewhart (ARL₀ = 370) but produces more false alarms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Capability – Process Capability Indices and Process Capability Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Capability – Process Capability Indices and Process Capability Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Capability** is a statistical measure of the *inherent ability* of a stable, in-control industrial process to produce output that consistently conforms to the pre-defined engineering specification limits. It is quantified by comparing the **Voice of the Process** (the natural spread, $6\sigma$) against the **Voice of the Customer** (the specification width, $USL - LSL$).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Process Capability is evaluated **only after** the process has been demonstrated to be **statistically in-control** (i.e., free from assignable causes of variation via $\bar{X}-R$ or $\bar{X}-s$ control charts). Evaluating capability on an unstable process is a fundamental analytical error.

**Key terminology (KTU Board Standard):**

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Upper Specification Limit | **USL** | Maximum acceptable value (engineering tolerance ceiling) |
| Lower Specification Limit | **LSL** | Minimum acceptable value (engineering tolerance floor) |
| Target Value | **T** | Nominal/ideal midpoint of specification |
| Process Mean | **μ** | Average output of the process (long-run) |
| Process Standard Deviation | **σ** | Inherent variability of the process |
| Natural Tolerance Limits | **NTL** | $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ (99.73% process spread) |

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Basketball Free Throw"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Basketball Shooter:**
> Imagine a basketball player shooting free throws. The **basket** represents the specification window $[LSL, USL]$. The **cluster of the player's shots** represents the process distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$.
>
> - A player whose shots form a **tight cluster squarely under the basket** has **high capability** (high $C_p$, high $C_{pk}$).
> - A player whose shots are **tight but consistently to the left** of the basket has **high $C_p$ but low $C_{pk}$** — the process is *precise but not accurate* (off-center).
> - A player whose shots are **scattered everywhere** has **low $C_p$** — the process is inherently incapable, regardless of aiming.
>
> Capability indices tell the engineer: *"Can this process, as it currently operates, hit the target window reliably?"*

## 1.3 Why Capability Matters — The KTU Examiner's Perspective

A process may be **in-control** (stable, predictable) yet still be **incapable** (producing excessive scrap). Capability indices bridge the gap between **process control** (Module 1 / Module 2 control charts) and **process improvement** (Six Sigma DMAIC, Kaizen). The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires students to distinguish **control** from **capability**:

- **In-control** = Common cause variation only (statistically stable)
- **Capable** = Variation is small enough to fit inside specification limits with high yield

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Highlight:** A process can be in-control but NOT capable. Conversely, a process can be incapable but stable. Capability is a *geometry* problem; control is a *time-series* problem. Both must be assessed separately.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Capability Indices

### 2.1.1 The $C_p$ Index — *Potential Capability*

The **$C_p$ index** measures the *potential* capability of a process assuming it is **perfectly centered** between the specification limits. It is the ratio of specification width to natural process spread.

$$
C_{p} \;=\; \frac{USL \;-\; LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

**Logical interpretation:**
- Numerator: the **engineering tolerance window** (what the customer allows)
- Denominator: $6\sigma$ = the **natural process spread** (what the process produces)
- If $C_{p} = 1.0$: the process spread *exactly fills* the specification window (boundary case — 0.27% defect rate)
- If $C_{p} > 1.33$: industry standard for a capable process
- $C_p$ **ignores centering** — it only measures *spread*

### 2.1.2 The $C_{pk}$ Index — *Actual Capability*

The **$C_{pk}$ index** accounts for the **centring** of the process. It is the minimum of two one-sided capability ratios.

$$
C_{pk} \;=\; \min\!\left(\, C_{pu},\; C_{pl} \,\right)
$$

Where the upper and lower one-sided indices are:

$$
C_{pu} \;=\; \frac{USL \;-\; \mu}{3\sigma} \qquad\qquad C_{pl} \;=\; \frac{\mu \;-\; LSL}{3\sigma}
$$

**Key relationships:**
- $C_{pk} \le C_{p}$  **always**
- $C_{pk} = C_{p}$ if and only if $\mu = T$ (perfectly centered)
- $C_{pk} < C_{p}$ implies the process is **off-center**

### 2.1.3 The Centering Factor $k$

The **centering factor** $k$ quantifies how far the process mean drifts from the target.

$$
k \;=\; \frac{\,\vert\, \mu \;-\; T \,\vert\,}{\,(USL - LSL)\,/\,2\,}
$$

And the elegant relationship connecting all three:

$$
C_{pk} \;=\; C_{p}\,(1 - k)
$$

## 2.2 Short-Term vs Long-Term Indices ($P_p, P_{pk}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Distinction:** The $C$ family uses **within-subgroup** standard deviation $\sigma_{within}$ (short-term, common-cause only). The $P$ family uses **overall** standard deviation $\sigma_{overall}$ (long-term, includes all variation sources).

| Index | Symbol | Variance Used | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Potential Capability | $C_p$ | $\sigma_{within}$ | Best-case spread |
| Actual Capability | $C_{pk}$ | $\sigma_{within}$ | Centered, real-world capability |
| Overall Performance | $P_p$ | $\sigma_{overall}$ | Total observed spread |
| Overall Performance (indexed) | $P_{pk}$ | $\sigma_{overall}$ | Total spread + centering |

When $C_{pk} \approx P_{pk}$, the process has minimal between-batch/assignable variation. When $P_{pk} \ll C_{pk}$, significant long-term drift exists and must be eliminated.

## 2.3 The Taguchi Capability Index $C_{pm}$

For processes where **targeting the nominal value** $T$ is critical (e.g., bolt tensile strength, resistor values), Taguchi proposed:

$$
C_{pm} \;=\; \frac{USL - LSL}{6\,\sqrt{\,\sigma^{2} + (\mu - T)^{2}\,}}
$$

Notice $C_{pm}$ *penalizes* deviation from the target — a process with $\mu \ne T$ suffers a strict capability penalty, unlike $C_{pk}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — *Memorize This Table*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula you will need for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic.

| # | Formula Name | LaTeX Expression | Engineering Meaning | Typical Value for Capable Process |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Process Capability ($C_p$) | $C_{p} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Spread vs tolerance | $\ge 1.33$ |
| 2 | One-sided Upper | $C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$ | Distance of mean to USL | — |
| 3 | One-sided Lower | $C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$ | Distance of mean to LSL | — |
| 4 | Actual Capability ($C_{pk}$) | $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$ | Capability with centering | $\ge 1.33$ |
| 5 | Centering Factor | $k = \dfrac{\vert\mu - T\vert}{(USL-LSL)/2}$ | Off-center deviation | $\to 0$ |
| 6 | $C_p$–$C_{pk}$ Link | $C_{pk} = C_{p}(1 - k)$ | Compact form | — |
| 7 | Overall Performance ($P_p$) | $P_{p} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{overall}}$ | Total long-term spread | $\ge 1.33$ |
| 8 | Overall Performance ($P_{pk}$) | $P_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma_{overall}},\dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma_{overall}}\right)$ | Long-term + centering | $\ge 1.33$ |
| 9 | Taguchi Index | $C_{pm} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\sigma^{2} + (\mu - T)^{2}}}$ | Target-sensitive | $\ge 1.33$ |
| 10 | Sigma Level Relation | $\sigma\text{-level} = 3 C_{pk}$ | Convert to defects/million | — |

## 2.5 Industry Benchmarks — KTU Standard Interpretation

| $C_{pk}$ Value | Industry Interpretation | Approximate Defect Rate (ppm) |
|---|---|---|
| $< 1.00$ | **Incapable** — process must be improved | $> 66{,}807$ |
| $1.00 - 1.33$ | **Marginal** — monitor continuously | $2,700 - 66{,}807$ |
| $1.33 - 1.67$ | **Capable** — meets most industry norms | $63 - 2{,}700$ |
| $> 1.67$ | **Excellent / Six Sigma Class** | $< 63$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Sigma equivalence:** A process with $\mu$ shifted $1.5\sigma$ from target and $C_{pk} = 2.0$ achieves the famed $3.4$ defects per million opportunities (DPMO).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Supplier Qualification:** Automotive OEMs (Toyota, Ford) require $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ from tier-1 suppliers.
2. **Six Sigma DMAIC:** The *Analyze* phase computes $C_{pk}$ to quantify the baseline process health.
3. **Process Validation:** FDA / ISO 9001 / AS9100 mandates capability evidence for medical and aerospace parts.
4. **Cost Reduction:** $C_{pk} < 1.0$ implies the process is generating scrap and rework — economic warning sign.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Derivation of the $C_p$ Index from First Principles

**Step 1 — Define the process output model.**

Assume the quality characteristic $X$ follows a stable normal distribution:

$$
X \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}(\mu,\; \sigma^{2})
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the natural tolerance limits.**

By the empirical 6-sigma rule, virtually all (99.73%) output lies in the interval:

$$
[\mu - 3\sigma,\;\; \mu + 3\sigma]
$$

The *width* of this interval is therefore:

$$
\text{Natural Process Spread} \;=\; (\mu + 3\sigma) - (\mu - 3\sigma) \;=\; 6\sigma
$$

**Step 3 — Define the engineering specification window.**

$$
\text{Specification Width} \;=\; USL - LSL
$$

**Step 4 — Form the dimensionless capability ratio.**

To compare two quantities of the same dimension, we form their ratio:

$$
C_{p} \;=\; \frac{\text{Specification Width}}{\text{Process Spread}} \;=\; \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret geometrically.**

$C_{p}$ is the number of "natural process spreads" that fit inside the specification window. If $C_p > 1$, the process *could* be capable if centered; if $C_p < 1$, it is **fundamentally incapable** no matter how it is centered.

## 3.2 Derivation of $C_{pk} = C_{p}(1 - k)$

**Step 1 — Express both one-sided indices in expanded form.**

$$
C_{pu} \;=\; \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \qquad\qquad C_{pl} \;=\; \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the symmetric case.**

If $\mu = T = (USL+LSL)/2$, then $USL - \mu = \mu - LSL = (USL-LSL)/2$, giving $C_{pu} = C_{pl} = C_p$. The minimum equals $C_p$.

**Step 3 — Express $C_{pu}$ in terms of $C_p$.**

Using $USL = T + (USL-LSL)/2$ and $T = (USL+LSL)/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
USL - \mu &= \left(T + \frac{USL - LSL}{2}\right) - \mu \\
&= \frac{USL - LSL}{2} - (\mu - T)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_{pu} \;=\; \frac{(USL-LSL)/2 \;-\; (\mu - T)}{3\sigma}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide numerator and denominator by $3\sigma$ and substitute $C_p$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pu} &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} - \frac{\mu - T}{3\sigma} \\
C_{pu} &= C_{p} - \frac{\mu - T}{3\sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Repeat for $C_{pl}$ to obtain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pl} &= C_{p} + \frac{\mu - T}{3\sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Take the minimum.**

When $\mu \ge T$ (the mean is above target), the lower one-sided index $C_{pl}$ becomes smaller:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} = C_{pl} &= C_{p} + \frac{\mu - T}{3\sigma} \\
&= C_{p}\left(1 - \frac{\mu - T}{(USL-LSL)/2}\cdot\frac{(USL-LSL)/2}{3\sigma\cdot C_{p}} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

A cleaner substitution using the centering factor $k = \vert\mu - T\vert / [(USL-LSL)/2]$ yields the compact result:

$$
C_{pk} \;=\; C_{p}\,(1 - k)
$$

where $0 \le k \le 1$. This identity is **frequently tested** in KTU ESE.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example (14-Mark Standard)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A CNC milling machine produces a critical shaft diameter. Engineering specifications are: $USL = 24.00$ mm, $LSL = 12.00$ mm, with a target $T = 18.00$ mm. After collecting 25 subgroups of size 5, the quality engineer computes:
> - Within-subgroup standard deviation: $\bar{s} = 1.50$ mm
> - Process mean: $\bar{\bar{X}} = 16.00$ mm
> - Overall standard deviation: $s_{overall} = 1.80$ mm
>
> **Compute $C_p$, $C_{pu}$, $C_{pl}$, $C_{pk}$, $P_p$, $P_{pk}$, $k$, and the expected PPM defect rate. Comment on the process.**

### Step 1 — Compute $C_p$

$$
C_{p} \;=\; \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \;=\; \frac{24.00 - 12.00}{6 \times 1.50}
$$

$$
C_{p} \;=\; \frac{12.00}{9.00} \;=\; \mathbf{1.333}
$$

**Comment:** $C_p = 1.333$ indicates *potential* capability (industry-acceptable threshold met).

### Step 2 — Compute $C_{pu}$

$$
C_{pu} \;=\; \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} \;=\; \frac{24.00 - 16.00}{3 \times 1.50} \;=\; \frac{8.00}{4.50} \;=\; \mathbf{1.778}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $C_{pl}$

$$
C_{pl} \;=\; \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \;=\; \frac{16.00 - 12.00}{3 \times 1.50} \;=\; \frac{4.00}{4.50} \;=\; \mathbf{0.889}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute $C_{pk}$

$$
C_{pk} \;=\; \min(C_{pu},\; C_{pl}) \;=\; \min(1.778,\; 0.889) \;=\; \mathbf{0.889}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the centering factor $k$

$$
k \;=\; \frac{\vert\mu - T\vert}{(USL-LSL)/2} \;=\; \frac{\vert 16.00 - 18.00 \vert}{12.00/2} \;=\; \frac{2.00}{6.00} \;=\; \mathbf{0.333}
$$

### Step 6 — Verify via the compact identity

$$
C_{pk} \;=\; C_{p}(1 - k) \;=\; 1.333 \times (1 - 0.333) \;=\; 1.333 \times 0.667 \;=\; \mathbf{0.889} \;\checkmark
$$

### Step 7 — Compute $P_p$

$$
P_{p} \;=\; \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{overall}} \;=\; \frac{12.00}{6 \times 1.80} \;=\; \frac{12.00}{10.80} \;=\; \mathbf{1.111}
$$

### Step 8 — Compute $P_{pk}$

$$
P_{pk,upper} \;=\; \frac{24.00 - 16.00}{3 \times 1.80} \;=\; \frac{8.00}{5.40} \;=\; 1.481
$$

$$
P_{pk,lower} \;=\; \frac{16.00 - 12.00}{3 \times 1.80} \;=\; \frac{4.00}{5.40} \;=\; 0.741
$$

$$
P_{pk} \;=\; \min(1.481,\; 0.741) \;=\; \mathbf{0.741}
$$

### Step 9 — Estimate Defect Rate (PPM)

For $C_{pk} = 0.889$, we compute the Z-scores at each limit:

$$
Z_{upper} \;=\; \frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma} \;=\; \frac{24.00 - 16.00}{1.50} \;=\; 5.333
$$

$$
Z_{lower} \;=\; \frac{\mu - LSL}{\sigma} \;=\; \frac{16.00 - 12.00}{1.50} \;=\; 2.667
$$

Using standard normal tables:
- $P(Z > 5.333) \approx 4.8$ ppm (negligible upper tail)
- $P(Z < 2.667) \approx 3800$ ppm (lower tail dominates)

$$
\text{Total Defect Rate} \;\approx\; 3800 \;+\; 5 \;\approx\; 3805 \text{ ppm} \;\approx\; \mathbf{0.38\%}
$$

### Step 10 — Engineering Verdict

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Interpretation:**
> 1. $C_p = 1.333$ — spread is **acceptable** in isolation.
> 2. $C_{pk} = 0.889 < 1.00$ — process is **NOT capable** due to off-centering.
> 3. $P_p = 1.111 < C_p = 1.333$ — long-term variability exceeds short-term, signalling **between-batch drift**.
> 4. $P_{pk} = 0.741$ — confirms long-term capability is even worse.
> 5. **Recommended Action:** Re-center the process (shift mean from $16.00$ toward $T=18.00$ mm by adjusting machine offset); then eliminate between-batch variation (machine warm-up, tool wear control).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Capability Analysis — Master Flowchart

The complete procedural logic a KTU engineer follows when conducting capability analysis on any industrial process:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Step 1: Define the Quality Characteristic and Specification Limits USL LSL and Target T] --> stepB[Step 2: Collect Rational Subgroups of Data Typically 25 Subgroups of Size 4 to 5]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Verify Statistical Stability using Control Charts Xbar R or Xbar s]
    stepC --> stepD{Is the Process In Control}
    stepD -->|No| stepE[Step 4a: Investigate and Remove Assignable Causes Then Return to Step 3]
    stepE --> stepC
    stepD -->|Yes| stepF[Step 4b: Verify Normality using Normal Probability Plot or Anderson Darling Test]
    stepF --> stepG{Is Data Approximately Normal}
    stepG -->|No| stepH[Step 5a: Apply Transformation such as Box Cox or Johnson Then Return to Step 4b]
    stepH --> stepF
    stepG -->|Yes| stepI[Step 6: Compute Within Subgroup Sigma and Overall Sigma]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 7: Compute Process Mean and Capability Indices Cp Cpk Pp Ppk]
    stepJ --> stepK[Step 8: Construct the Six Pack Capability Report]
    stepK --> stepL[Step 9: Interpret Results and Benchmark Against 1.00 1.33 1.67 Thresholds]
    stepL --> stepM{Is Cpk greater than or equal to 1.33}
    stepM -->|Yes| stepN[Step 10a: Process is Capable Proceed to Monitor and Control]
    stepM -->|No| stepO[Step 10b: Process is Incapable Initiate DMAIC Improvement Cycle]
    O --> stepJ
```

## 4.2 Six-Pack Capability Report — Functional Block Architecture

In Minitab / SPSS / JMP, the standard output of a capability analysis is the **"Six-Pack"** — six interconnected views of the process. The following block diagram maps their informational roles:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[Block A: Xbar Control Chart - Detects Shifts in Process Mean Over Time]
    blockB[Block B: R or s Control Chart - Detects Changes in Process Variability]
    blockC[Block C: Histogram with Fitted Normal Curve - Visualizes Distribution Shape Versus Specification]
    blockD[Block D: Normal Probability Plot - Confirms Normality Assumption Visually]
    blockE[Block E: Capability Histogram with USL LSL and PPM - Shows Defect Density Against Specs]
    blockF[Block F: Last 25 Subgroups Run Chart - Reveals Recent Trends and Drift]
    blockG[Central Engine: Capability Index Calculator - Computes Cp Cpk Pp Ppk Cpm]
    blockA --> blockG
    blockB --> blockG
    blockC --> blockG
    blockD --> blockG
    blockE --> blockG
    blockF --> blockG
```

## 4.3 $C_p$ vs $C_{pk}$ — Visual Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    case1[Case 1: Centered Process - Mean equals Target - Cp equals Cpk - Bell Curve Symmetric in Specification Window]
    case2[Case 2: Off Center to Left - Mean less than Target - Cpk less than Cp - Left Tail Risks LSL Violation]
    case3[Case 3: Off Center to Right - Mean greater than Target - Cpk less than Cp - Right Tail Risks USL Violation]
    case4[Case 4: Process Inherently Incapable - Cp less than 1.0 - Variation Exceeds Specification Width Regardless of Centering]
    case1 --> verdict1[Verdict: Capable and Centered - Green Light]
    case2 --> verdict2[Verdict: Recenter Process via Mean Adjustment]
    case3 --> verdict3[Verdict: Recenter Process via Mean Adjustment]
    case4 --> verdict4[Verdict: Fundamental Redesign Required Reduce Sigma via Six Sigma DMAIC]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Which Index to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Does the Specification Have a Single Limit Either USL or LSL}
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{Is the Process Short Term or Long Term}
    q1 -->|No| q3{Is the Target Value Critically Important}
    q3 -->|Yes| q4[Use Cpm - Taguchi Index Penalizes Target Deviation]
    q3 -->|No| q5{Does Process Have Long Term Drift}
    q5 -->|Yes| q6[Use Ppk - Captures Overall Variability]
    q5 -->|No| q7[Use Cpk - Captures Within Subgroup Performance]
    q2 -->|Short Term| q8[Use Cp or Cpu or Cpl - One Sided Index]
    q2 -->|Long Term| q9[Use Pp or Ppu or Ppl - One Sided Overall]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### Q1. Define Process Capability and Process Capability Index. State the significance of $C_p$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Process Capability** is the measured, *inherent* ability of a stable industrial process to produce output within the prescribed engineering specification limits (USL and LSL). It is quantified by comparing the natural process spread ($6\sigma$) to the specification width ($USL - LSL$).

The **Process Capability Index** is a dimensionless numerical measure of this ability, with $C_p$ being the foundational index:

$$
C_{p} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

**Significance:**
- $C_p \ge 1.33$ indicates the process *potentially* meets industry-acceptable quality standards.
- $C_p = 1.00$ is the *boundary* case (process spread exactly fills tolerance).
- $C_p$ ignores centering — it evaluates *spread only*; a centered but offset process can have the same $C_p$.
- It provides a common, unitless language across processes with different units and tolerances, enabling direct benchmarking.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Q2. Distinguish between $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. When is $C_{pk} < C_p$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis of Difference | $C_p$ | $C_{pk}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $(USL-LSL)/6\sigma$ | $\min[(USL-\mu)/(3\sigma),\; (\mu-LSL)/(3\sigma)]$ |
| Accounts for Centering | **No** | **Yes** |
| Type of Capability | Potential (best case) | Actual (real case) |
| Numerical Inequality | $C_p \ge C_{pk}$ always | $C_{pk} \le C_p$ always |

$C_{pk} < C_p$ whenever the process mean $\mu$ is **not equal to the target value** $T$ (i.e., when the centering factor $k > 0$). The further the mean drifts from $T$, the smaller $C_{pk}$ becomes relative to $C_p$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks Total)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 2 Internal Choice A]**

### Question A

**(a)** Derive the relationship $C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k)$ where $k$ is the centering factor. Define each term clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pharmaceutical tablet press produces tablets with target weight $T = 500$ mg. Specifications are $USL = 520$ mg and $LSL = 480$ mg. From 30 subgroups of size 5, the following statistics are computed:
- Process mean: $\bar{\bar{X}} = 503.5$ mg
- Within-subgroup standard deviation: $\bar{s} = 3.0$ mg
- Overall standard deviation: $s_{overall} = 4.2$ mg

Compute $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $P_p$, $P_{pk}$, and $k$. Estimate the expected defect rate (in ppm) and provide a one-paragraph engineering verdict. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation of $C_{pk} = C_p(1-k)$** *(7 marks)*

The one-sided capability indices are:

$$
C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \qquad C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}
$$

and $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$. The *centering factor* is:

$$
k = \frac{\vert\mu - T\vert}{(USL-LSL)/2}
$$

Express $USL = T + (USL-LSL)/2$ and $LSL = T - (USL-LSL)/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pu} &= \frac{T + (USL-LSL)/2 - \mu}{3\sigma} \\
&= \frac{(USL-LSL)/2 - (\mu - T)}{3\sigma} \\
&= \frac{USL-LSL}{6\sigma} - \frac{\mu - T}{3\sigma} \\
&= C_{p} - \frac{\mu - T}{3\sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly:

$$
C_{pl} = C_{p} + \frac{\mu - T}{3\sigma}
$$

When $\mu > T$ the limiting case is $C_{pu}$, hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} = C_{pu} &= C_{p} - \frac{\mu - T}{3\sigma} \\
&= C_{p}\left(1 - \frac{\mu - T}{(USL-LSL)/2}\cdot\frac{(USL-LSL)/2}{3\sigma\cdot C_{p}}\right) \\
&= C_{p}\left(1 - \frac{\mu - T}{(USL-LSL)/2}\right) \\
&= C_{p}\,(1 - k)
\end{aligned}
$$

By symmetry the same expression holds for $\mu < T$ via $C_{pl}$. Thus $C_{pk} = C_p(1-k)$. ∎

**(b) Numerical Computation** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — $C_p$:**

$$
C_{p} = \frac{520 - 480}{6 \times 3.0} = \frac{40}{18} = \mathbf{2.222}
$$

**Step 2 — One-sided indices:**

$$
C_{pu} = \frac{520 - 503.5}{3 \times 3.0} = \frac{16.5}{9.0} = 1.833
$$

$$
C_{pl} = \frac{503.5 - 480}{3 \times 3.0} = \frac{23.5}{9.0} = 2.611
$$

**Step 3 — $C_{pk}$:**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.833,\; 2.611) = \mathbf{1.833}
$$

**Step 4 — Centering factor:**

$$
k = \frac{\vert 503.5 - 500 \vert}{40/2} = \frac{3.5}{20} = \mathbf{0.175}
$$

**Step 5 — $P_p$:**

$$
P_{p} = \frac{520 - 480}{6 \times 4.2} = \frac{40}{25.2} = \mathbf{1.587}
$$

**Step 6 — $P_{pk}$:**

$$
P_{pu} = \frac{16.5}{3 \times 4.2} = \frac{16.5}{12.6} = 1.310
$$

$$
P_{pl} = \frac{23.5}{12.6} = 1.865
$$

$$
P_{pk} = \min(1.310,\; 1.865) = \mathbf{1.310}
$$

**Step 7 — Defect Rate:**

Z-scores against the within-subgroup $\sigma$:

$$
Z_{upper} = \frac{520 - 503.5}{3.0} = 5.50, \qquad Z_{lower} = \frac{503.5 - 480}{3.0} = 7.833
$$

Both tails are extremely small; using standard normal tables:

- $P(Z > 5.50) \approx 1.9$ ppm
- $P(Z < 7.833) \approx 0$ ppm

Total defect rate $\approx$ **2 ppm**. Excellent.

**Engineering Verdict:** Both $C_{pk} = 1.833$ and $P_{pk} = 1.310$ exceed the $1.33$ threshold, with $P_{pk} < C_{pk}$ indicating modest between-batch variation. The process is **highly capable and well-centered** ($k = 0.175$ is small). Recommended action: maintain current process controls, continue periodic capability recalculation, and monitor for tool wear effects (the source of the slight $P_p$ depression).

---

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks Total)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 2 Internal Choice B]**

### Question B

**(a)** Explain the Six-Pack capability analysis. List its components and state what each one diagnoses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bearing inner-race diameter has $USL = 30.005$ mm, $LSL = 29.985$ mm, target $T = 29.995$ mm. From a control-chart analysis the process is in-control with $\bar{\bar{X}} = 29.996$ mm and $\bar{s} = 0.0035$ mm. If the engineer demands a $C_{pk} \ge 1.50$, determine whether the current process satisfies this requirement. If not, compute the maximum allowable $\sigma$ that would satisfy the requirement. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Six-Pack Capability Analysis** *(7 marks)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The Six-Pack is a standardized graphical + numerical diagnostic suite used in commercial statistical software (Minitab, JMP, SPSS) to fully characterize a process's capability. It is the gold standard for KTU practical/ESE answers involving "describe the analysis procedure."

**The Six Components:**

| # | Component | What It Diagnoses |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **$\bar{X}$ Control Chart** | Shifts and trends in the process mean over time |
| 2 | **$R$ or $s$ Control Chart** | Changes in process variability/spread |
| 3 | **Histogram with Normal Curve** | Shape of distribution vs specification; visual fit |
| 4 | **Normal Probability Plot** | Linearity confirms normality assumption |
| 5 | **Capability Plot** | PPM defect zones shaded against USL/LSL; numerical $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $P_p$, $P_{pk}$, PPM |
| 6 | **Last 25 Observations Run Chart** | Recent short-term trends, autocorrelation, drift |

The **logical sequence** is: (i) confirm stability via (1) & (2); (ii) confirm distribution via (3) & (4); (iii) compute and visualize capability via (5); (iv) verify recency via (6).

**(b) Numerical Computation** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Compute current $C_p$:**

$$
C_{p} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{30.005 - 29.985}{6 \times 0.0035} = \frac{0.020}{0.021} = \mathbf{0.952}
$$

**Step 2 — One-sided indices:**

$$
C_{pu} = \frac{30.005 - 29.996}{3 \times 0.0035} = \frac{0.009}{0.0105} = 0.857
$$

$$
C_{pl} = \frac{29.996 - 29.985}{0.0105} = \frac{0.011}{0.0105} = 1.048
$$

**Step 3 — Current $C_{pk}$:**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(0.857,\; 1.048) = \mathbf{0.857}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

$$
C_{pk} = 0.857 \;<\; 1.50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Requirement NOT met.}
$$

**Step 5 — Find maximum allowable $\sigma$ for $C_{pk} \ge 1.50$:**

$C_{pk}$ is dominated by $C_{pu}$ (the upper side, since the mean sits closer to USL). The required condition is:

$$
C_{pu} \ge 1.50 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} \ge 1.50
$$

Solving for $\sigma$:

$$
\sigma \le \frac{USL - \mu}{3 \times 1.50} = \frac{0.009}{4.50} = \mathbf{0.00200 \text{ mm}}
$$

Cross-check with the lower side:

$$
\sigma \le \frac{\mu - LSL}{4.50} = \frac{0.011}{4.50} = 0.00244 \text{ mm}
$$

The **binding constraint** is the smaller value:

$$
\sigma_{\max} = \min(0.00200,\; 0.00244) = \mathbf{0.00200 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The current $\sigma = 0.0035$ mm must be reduced by a factor of approximately $1.75$ (i.e., $0.00200/0.0035$) to meet $C_{pk} \ge 1.50$. This requires process improvement — better machine calibration, tighter tool-wear compensation, and tighter raw-material specifications.

---

## 5.4 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1: Confusing $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$:** Students frequently write "$C_p = 1.33$, therefore the process is capable." This ignores centering. **Always report both $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.**
>
> **Pitfall 2: Forgetting to verify in-control first:** A KTU examiner will deduct up to **2 marks** if you compute capability indices without first stating *"the process has been verified to be in statistical control via $\bar{X}-R$ chart."*
>
> **Pitfall 3: Using $\sigma_{overall}$ for $C_p$:** The $C$ family must use *within-subgroup* standard deviation $\bar{s}/c_4$. Using the overall $s$ yields $P_p$, not $C_p$.
>
> **Pitfall 4: Miscalculating $k$:** Students often write $k = (\mu-T)/(USL-LSL)$ instead of $k = \vert\mu-T\vert/[(USL-LSL)/2]$. The denominator is the *half-width*, not the full width.
>
> **Pitfall 5: Skipping units and benchmarks:** Always state the final interpretation in words ("$C_{pk} = 1.45$ → capable per industry threshold of 1.33"). Numerical answers without interpretation receive partial credit only.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Process Capability** = inherent ability of a *stable* process to meet specs.
- [x] Always confirm **in-control** before computing capability indices.
- [x] **$C_p$** = potential capability (spread only); **$C_{pk}$** = actual capability (spread + centering).
- [x] $C_{pk} \le C_p$ **always**; equality iff $\mu = T$.
- [x] Centering factor $k = \vert\mu - T\vert / [(USL-LSL)/2]$; identity $C_{pk} = C_p(1-k)$.
- [x] **$P$ family** uses $\sigma_{overall}$ (long-term); **$C$ family** uses $\sigma_{within}$ (short-term).
- [x] **Taguchi $C_{pm}$** penalizes target deviation — use when targeting the nominal is critical.
- [x] Industry benchmark: $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ for capable; $\ge 1.67$ for excellent / Six-Sigma.
- [x] **Six-Pack** = $\bar{X}$ chart + $R/s$ chart + Histogram + Probability Plot + Capability Plot + Last-25 Run Chart.
- [x] Off-center process → improve by re-centering *first* before reducing $\sigma$.
- [x] Incapable but stable → initiate DMAIC; incapable and unstable → eliminate assignable causes first.
- [x] Defect rate $\approx 2{,}700$ ppm at $C_{pk}=1.00$; $\approx 63$ ppm at $C_{pk}=1.50$ (with $1.5\sigma$ shift assumption).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Concept of Acceptance Sampling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Concept of Acceptance Sampling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Acceptance Sampling** is a statistical quality control procedure in which a **random sample** is drawn from a production lot (or batch), and based on the inspection results of that sample, a decision is made to either **accept** or **reject** the entire lot. It is fundamentally a **lot-by-lot sentencing** tool used when **100% inspection is impractical, uneconomical, or destructive**.

Mathematically, it is the application of **Hypothesis Testing** to industrial quality assurance, where the two competing hypotheses are:

$$
H_0: \text{The lot fraction defective } (p) \leq p_1 \text{ (Acceptable Quality Level)}
$$
$$
H_1: \text{The lot fraction defective } (p) > p_1 \text{ (Rejectable Quality Level)}
$$

The plan is completely defined by the tuple:

$$
(n, c) \rightarrow \text{Sample size } n, \text{ Acceptance number } c
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:** "Acceptance sampling is the process of evaluating a portion of a lot or batch in order to make a decision to accept or reject the entire lot. It is a compromise between no inspection at all and 100% inspection."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a head chef in a large hotel receiving a shipment of **10,000 eggs**. You obviously cannot crack open every egg to check if it's good. So you randomly pick, say, **20 eggs** (the sample). If more than **2 eggs** are rotten (c = 2), you reject the entire shipment back to the supplier.

| Real-World Element | Statistical Counterpart |
|---|---|
| The shipment of eggs | The **Lot** or **Batch** (size $N$) |
| The 20 eggs you crack | The **Sample** (size $n$) |
| The 2 rotten-egg limit | **Acceptance Number** ($c$) |
| The decision to keep/reject | **Accept / Reject Lot** |
| The unknown % of bad eggs in the truck | **Lot Fraction Defective** ($p$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not inspect everything?** Three classic reasons:
> 1. **Destructive Testing** (testing a bulb by burning it).
> 2. **High cost of 100% inspection** (fatigue leads to MORE defects missed than sampling).
> 3. **Administrative / Time constraints** (inspection of millions of items is infeasible).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlighted for KTU

- **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level):** The worst tolerable process average when a continuing series of lots is submitted for acceptance sampling. Expressed as a **percentage of defective items**. Typically ranges from **0.1% to 10%**.
- **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective):** The level of quality that is considered **poor** and should be rejected most of the time. Often associated with a **Producer's Risk Point**.
- **α (Producer's Risk):** Probability of rejecting a **good lot** ($\beta$ in some literature is Consumer's Risk).
- **β (Consumer's Risk):** Probability of accepting a **bad lot**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Do NOT confuse **Acceptance Sampling** with **Process Control (SPC)**. Control charts (X-bar, R) monitor *process stability over time*. Acceptance sampling makes a *single accept/reject decision on one lot*. They solve different problems!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Structured Steps

Acceptance Sampling operates on a clean, repeatable workflow. Each step is governed by probability.

**Step 1 — Lot Formation**
The producer groups items into discrete, homogeneous lots. Homogeneity is critical: a lot should contain items produced under similar conditions (same machine, material, shift).

**Step 2 — Random Sample Selection**
A sample of size $n$ is drawn **randomly** from the lot of size $N$. This ensures the sample represents the lot's true quality $p$.

**Step 3 — Inspection & Counting**
Inspect the $n$ items. Count the number of defectives observed, denoted $d$ (or $X$).

**Step 4 — Decision Rule (The Heart of the Plan)**
$$
\text{If } d \leq c \rightarrow \text{ACCEPT the lot}
$$
$$
\text{If } d > c \rightarrow \text{REJECT the lot}
$$

**Step 5 — Disposition of Rejected Lots**
Rejected lots are typically **100% inspected, scrapped, or returned to the supplier**. This is a critical difference from SPC.

## 2.2 The Two Universes of Sampling Plans

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Students must clearly separate these two domains.

| Attribute | **Sampling Plan for Attributes** | **Sampling Plan for Variables** |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Type** | Pass/Fail, Go/No-Go, Defect/Good | Continuous measurement (length, weight, voltage) |
| **Statistic Used** | Count of defectives $d$ | Sample mean $\bar{x}$ and standard deviation $s$ |
| **Information Used** | Less (qualitative) | More (quantitative) → smaller $n$ for same protection |
| **KTU Module Focus** | **Primary focus of Module 3** | Module 4 focus |
| **Example Plan** | MIL-STD-105E, ANSI/ASQ Z1.4 | MIL-STD-414 |

## 2.3 Advantages vs Disadvantages (A 4-Mark Favourite)

**Advantages:**
- Reduced inspection cost and fatigue.
- Suitable for **destructive testing**.
- Less handling damage to the product.
- Provides a documented, objective criterion.
- Accepts the reality of economic sampling (some risk is unavoidable).

**Disadvantages:**
- Involves **sampling risk** (accepting bad, rejecting good).
- Requires planning and documentation.
- Provides **less information** about the product than 100% inspection.
- The rejected lot still needs disposition (often 100% screening).

## 2.4 The Four Categories of Acceptance Sampling

1. **By Attributes** — Go/No-Go inspection (this module).
2. **By Variables** — Measured on a continuous scale (Module 4).
3. **Rectifying vs Non-Rectifying** — Whether rejected lots undergo 100% screening.
4. **Single, Double, Multiple, and Sequential** — Number of samples drawn.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Acceptance Probability $P_a$ | $P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ | Binomial probability of accepting a lot with fraction defective $p$ |
| 2 | Operating Characteristic (OC) | Plot of $P_a$ vs $p$ | The "fingerprint" of a sampling plan |
| 3 | Producer's Risk $\alpha$ | $1 - P_a(p_1)$ where $p_1 = $ AQL | P(rejecting a good lot) |
| 4 | Consumer's Risk $\beta$ | $P_a(p_2)$ where $p_2 = $ LTPD | P(accepting a bad lot) |
| 5 | Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) | $AOQ = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{(N - n) + P_a \cdot p \cdot \text{(if rectifying)}}$ | Quality of product after sampling + screening |
| 6 | Average Total Inspection (ATI) | $ATI = n + (1-P_a)(N-n)$ | Expected items inspected per lot |
| 7 | Hypergeometric (small lot, $n/N > 0.1$) | $P(d) = \dfrac{\binom{D}{d}\binom{N-D}{n-d}}{\binom{N}{n}}$ | Use when population is finite and small |
| 8 | Poisson Approximation | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$ | Used when $n \geq 50$ and $p \leq 0.10$ |
| 9 | AQL | Typical values: $0.1\%$, $0.65\%$, $1.0\%$, $4.0\%$ | Worst acceptable process level |
| 10 | LTPD | Symbol $p_2$ or LTPD | Lot Tolerance Percent Defective |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Absolute value or "given" in the formulas above uses parentheses or descriptive words (like `(1-p)^{n-d}`). Do **not** write $\vert x \vert$ — instead use $(x)$ or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX to maintain KTU markdown integrity.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Electronics:** Incoming resistor/capacitor lot testing (MIL-STD-105E).
- **Automotive:** Ball bearing inspection where testing is destructive.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Sterility testing of vaccine batches.
- **Aerospace:** Critical fastener (bolt) tensile strength testing — each bolt is destroyed, so $n$ is small.
- **Textiles:** Tensile strength and color-fastness testing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve

The **OC curve** is the single most important analytical tool in acceptance sampling. We will derive it from first principles using the Binomial distribution.

### 3.1.1 Starting Assumption

Each item in the lot is independently defective with probability $p$ (the lot fraction defective). When we draw a sample of size $n$, the number of defectives $d$ follows a **Binomial distribution**:

$$
P(d) = \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}, \quad d = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

### 3.1.2 Acceptance Rule Translation to Probability

The lot is **accepted** if and only if the number of defectives $d$ in the sample does not exceed the acceptance number $c$. Therefore, the probability of acceptance $P_a$ is the **cumulative** Binomial probability from $0$ to $c$:

$$
P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

### 3.1.3 Building the OC Curve

By varying $p$ from $0$ to $1$ in small increments and computing $P_a(p)$ for each value, we obtain the **OC curve**. The shape of this curve is characteristic of the sampling plan $(n, c)$.

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem)

**Problem:** A sampling plan has $n = 50$, $c = 1$. Compute the probability of acceptance when the lot fraction defective is $p = 0.02$.

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters.
- $n = 50$, $c = 1$, $p = 0.02$, $q = 1 - p = 0.98$

**Step 2:** Apply the acceptance probability formula.

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{1} \binom{50}{d} (0.02)^d (0.98)^{50-d}
$$

**Step 3:** Split the summation.

$$
P_a = \binom{50}{0}(0.02)^0(0.98)^{50} + \binom{50}{1}(0.02)^1(0.98)^{49}
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the first term.

$$
\text{Term 1} = (1)(1)(0.98)^{50}
$$

$$
(0.98)^{50} = e^{50 \ln(0.98)} = e^{50 \times (-0.02020)} = e^{-1.0101} \approx 0.3642
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the second term.

$$
\text{Term 2} = 50 \times 0.02 \times (0.98)^{49}
$$

$$
(0.98)^{49} = (0.98)^{50} / 0.98 = 0.3642 / 0.98 \approx 0.3716
$$

$$
\text{Term 2} = 50 \times 0.02 \times 0.3716 = 1.0 \times 0.3716 = 0.3716
$$

**Step 6:** Sum the terms.

$$
P_a = 0.3642 + 0.3716 = 0.7358
$$

**Conclusion:** The probability of accepting a lot with 2% defectives, using the plan $(n=50, c=1)$, is approximately **0.7358 or 73.58%**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)

The **AOQ** measures the expected quality of product leaving the inspection system, assuming rejected lots are **rectified** (100% screened and defectives replaced).

### 3.2.1 Expected Defectives in Accepted Lots

If a lot is accepted (with probability $P_a$), the expected number of defectives that **leak through** to the customer is:

$$
E[\text{defectives accepted}] = P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)
$$

The factor $(N - n)$ is used because we already inspected the $n$ items in the sample.

### 3.2.2 The AOQ Formula

The AOQ is the average fraction defective in the outgoing product:

$$
AOQ = \dfrac{\text{Expected defectives accepted}}{\text{Lot size}} = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}
$$

When $n \ll N$, the approximation $(N - n) \approx N$ yields:

$$
AOQ \approx P_a \cdot p
$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Illustration

**Problem:** A lot of $N = 1000$ items with $p = 0.03$ is subjected to a plan $n = 50$, $c = 1$. Assume the plan yields $P_a = 0.560$ (from prior computation or table). Compute AOQ.

**Step 1:** Substitute into the formula.

$$
AOQ = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}
$$

**Step 2:** Plug in the values.

$$
AOQ = \dfrac{0.560 \times 0.03 \times (1000 - 50)}{1000}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate numerator.

$$
0.560 \times 0.03 = 0.0168
$$
$$
0.0168 \times 950 = 15.96
$$

**Step 4:** Divide by lot size.

$$
AOQ = \dfrac{15.96}{1000} = 0.01596
$$

**Step 5:** Express as a percentage.

$$
AOQ = 1.596\%
$$

This means after rectifying inspection, the outgoing product has approximately **1.6% defectives** on average.

## 3.3 Derivation of Average Total Inspection (ATI)

The **ATI** is the average number of items inspected per lot — including the original sample and the rectifying inspection of rejected lots.

### 3.3.1 Logic

- **Always** we inspect the sample $n$ items.
- **Only when the lot is rejected** (probability $1 - P_a$) do we inspect the remaining $N - n$ items.

$$
ATI = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)
$$

### 3.3.2 Numerical Illustration

**Problem:** For the same plan, compute ATI.

**Step 1:** Substitute known values.

$$
ATI = 50 + (1 - 0.560)(1000 - 50)
$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate each piece.

$$
(1 - 0.560) = 0.440
$$
$$
(1000 - 50) = 950
$$
$$
0.440 \times 950 = 418
$$

**Step 3:** Add the sample inspection.

$$
ATI = 50 + 418 = 468 \text{ items per lot}
$$

This means on average, only **468 out of 1000** items are inspected (saving 53.2% inspection effort).

## 3.4 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Metric | Numerical Value (Example Above) | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $P_a$ (at $p=0.03$) | 0.560 | 56% chance an incoming lot is accepted |
| Producer's Risk $\alpha$ (at $p_1 = 0.01$) | depends on $P_a(0.01)$ | Risk to supplier |
| Consumer's Risk $\beta$ (at $p_2 = 0.05$) | depends on $P_a(0.05)$ | Risk to buyer |
| $AOQ$ | 1.596% | Average defectives reaching customer |
| $ATI$ | 468 items | Average inspection load per lot |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pro Tip for KTU 2024 Examinations:** Always state the **assumptions** before solving: (1) Binomial distribution applicable? (2) Is lot size large or small? (3) Is the plan rectifying or non-rectifying? These three lines earn easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Acceptance Sampling

Below is a complete Mermaid block diagram mapping the entire functional flow of acceptance sampling, from lot formation to final disposition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lot Formation by Producer] --> B[Random Selection of n Items from N]
    B --> C[Inspection of Sample: Count Defectives d]
    C --> D{Decision Rule}
    D -->|d less than or equal to c| E[ACCEPT the Lot]
    D -->|d greater than c| F[REJECT the Lot]
    E --> G[Lot Sent to Customer]
    F --> H[Disposition Options]
    H --> H1[100 Percent Rectifying Inspection]
    H --> H2[Return to Supplier]
    H --> H3[Scrap the Lot]
    H1 --> I[Defectives Replaced]
    I --> G
    E --> J[Record Pa and p for OC Curve Construction]
    F --> J
    J --> K[Update Sampling Plan if Needed]
```

## 4.2 The OC Curve Block Diagram (Conceptual Shape)

The OC curve has a characteristic "S-shape" or "reverse-S" depending on the parameters. Below is a textual block diagram of the OC curve's key landmarks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lot Quality p on X axis] --> B[Pa on Y axis between 0 and 1]
    B --> C[Three Key Zones]
    C --> D[Zone 1: p less than AQL - High Pa - Good Lots Accepted]
    C --> E[Zone 2: Transition Region - Indifference Region]
    C --> F[Zone 3: p greater than LTPD - Low Pa - Bad Lots Rejected]
    D --> G[Producer's Risk alpha at p1]
    F --> H[Consumer's Risk beta at p2]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Rectifying vs Non-Rectifying Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Submitted Lot N] --> B[Draw Sample n]
    B --> C[Inspect Sample]
    C --> D{Acceptance Decision}
    D -->|Accept| E[Sent to Customer As Is]
    D -->|Reject - Non Rectifying| F[Lot Returned or Scrapped]
    D -->|Reject - Rectifying| G[100 Percent Screening of N minus n]
    G --> H[Replace Defectives with Good Items]
    H --> E
    E --> I[Customer Receives Product with AOQ]
    F --> J[Customer Receives Nothing or Screened Lot]
```

## 4.4 The Position of Acceptance Sampling in the Quality System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Statistical Quality Control] --> B[SPC - Process Control]
    A --> C[Acceptance Sampling - Lot Control]
    B --> B1[Control Charts X bar R p c]
    B --> B2[Process Capability Cp Cpk]
    C --> C1[Attribute Plans MIL STD 105E]
    C --> C2[Variable Plans MIL STD 414]
    C --> C3[Sequential Plans]
    C1 --> D1[Single Double Multiple]
    C2 --> D2[k Method M Method sigma Method]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** Physical curves (like the OC curve) cannot be drawn in Mermaid's syntax. These block diagrams are **academically richer** because they map decision logic, which is the core competency KTU examiners test. For the actual OC curve, students are expected to sketch the $P_a$ vs $p$ plot in their answer booklets.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define acceptance sampling. List any two situations where acceptance sampling is preferred over 100% inspection.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Acceptance sampling is a statistical quality control technique in which a random sample of items is drawn from a lot, and based on the number of defectives found in the sample, a decision is made to either accept or reject the entire lot.

**Two situations:**
1. **Destructive Testing:** When the test itself destroys the item (e.g., tensile strength of steel, bomb testing, light bulb life test). 100% inspection would destroy the entire production.
2. **High Cost / Fatigue:** When 100% inspection is more expensive than the cost of the few defectives that might slip through, or when inspector fatigue causes more defects to be missed (e.g., inspection of millions of small electronic components).
3. (Bonus) **Administrative infeasibility:** When lot size is in millions and time constraints prevent total inspection.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Distinguish between acceptance sampling and statistical process control (SPC).**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Acceptance Sampling | Statistical Process Control (SPC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Make accept/reject decision on a lot | Monitor and control the process over time |
| **Applicability** | Applied to already produced lots | Applied during the production process |
| **Output** | A single accept/reject verdict | Continues to monitor; flags assignable causes |
| **Rejected Lots** | 100% screened, scrapped, or returned | Process adjusted; no lot to reject |
| **Risk** | Producer's risk $\alpha$ and Consumer's risk $\beta$ | Type I and Type II errors on control chart limits |
| **Tools** | Sampling plans, OC curve, AOQ | X-bar, R, p, c, CUSUM, EWMA charts |
| **Module in KTU** | Module 3 | Module 5 |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve** in detail. What are the **two points** that completely describe an OC curve? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single sampling plan has $n = 80$ and $c = 2$. Find the probability of acceptance of a lot with fraction defective $p = 0.04$ using the **Binomial distribution**. Also compute the **Producer's risk** if the AQL is 1% and **Consumer's risk** if the LTPD is 6%. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition:** The Operating Characteristic (OC) curve is a graph that shows the probability of acceptance $P_a$ of a lot as a function of the lot fraction defective $p$, for a given sampling plan $(n, c)$.

**Construction:** For each value of $p$ from 0 to 1, we compute:

$$
P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

**Two Key Points on OC Curve:**

1. **Producer's Risk Point** $(p_1, 1-\alpha)$: Where $p_1 = $ AQL, and $1 - \alpha$ is the probability of acceptance. The producer wants lots of this quality or better to be accepted with high probability ($1 - \alpha \geq 0.90$ or 0.95).

2. **Consumer's Risk Point** $(p_2, \beta)$: Where $p_2 = $ LTPD, and $\beta$ is the probability of acceptance (typically $\beta \leq 0.10$). The consumer wants lots of this poor quality to be rejected with high probability.

**Properties of OC Curve:**
- It is a monotonically decreasing function of $p$.
- An **ideal** OC curve would be a step function (accept all $p \leq p^*$ and reject all $p > p^*$), but this requires $n = N$ (impossible).
- The **steeper** the OC curve, the more **discriminating** the sampling plan.
- Increasing $n$ makes the curve steeper.
- Increasing $c$ for fixed $n$ shifts the curve to the right.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $n = 80$, $c = 2$, $p = 0.04$.

**Step 1 — Compute $P_a$ at $p = 0.04$:**

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{2} \binom{80}{d} (0.04)^d (0.96)^{80-d}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term:**

For $d = 0$:

$$
P_0 = \binom{80}{0}(0.04)^0(0.96)^{80} = (0.96)^{80}
$$

$$
\ln(0.96) = -0.04082 \Rightarrow 80 \times (-0.04082) = -3.2656
$$
$$
P_0 = e^{-3.2656} \approx 0.0381
$$

For $d = 1$:

$$
P_1 = \binom{80}{1}(0.04)^1(0.96)^{79} = 80 \times 0.04 \times (0.96)^{79}
$$
$$
(0.96)^{79} = (0.96)^{80} / 0.96 = 0.0381 / 0.96 \approx 0.0397
$$
$$
P_1 = 3.2 \times 0.0397 \approx 0.1270
$$

For $d = 2$:

$$
P_2 = \binom{80}{2}(0.04)^2(0.96)^{78}
$$
$$
\binom{80}{2} = \frac{80 \times 79}{2} = 3160
$$
$$
(0.04)^2 = 0.0016
$$
$$
(0.96)^{78} = (0.96)^{80} / (0.96)^2 = 0.0381 / 0.9216 \approx 0.0413
$$
$$
P_2 = 3160 \times 0.0016 \times 0.0413 \approx 0.2088
$$

**Step 3 — Sum for total $P_a$:**

$$
P_a = 0.0381 + 0.1270 + 0.2088 = 0.3739
$$

**Step 4 — Producer's Risk at AQL = 0.01:**

Using a Binomial table or recomputing with $p = 0.01$:

$$
P_a(p=0.01) = \sum_{d=0}^{2} \binom{80}{d}(0.01)^d(0.99)^{80-d}
$$

$(0.99)^{80} = e^{-0.802} \approx 0.4481$

For $d=1$: $80 \times 0.01 \times (0.99)^{79} = 0.8 \times (0.4481/0.99) \approx 0.3621$

For $d=2$: $3160 \times 0.0001 \times (0.99)^{78} \approx 0.316 \times 0.4526 \approx 0.1430$

$$
P_a(0.01) = 0.4481 + 0.3621 + 0.1430 = 0.9532
$$

Therefore:
$$
\alpha = 1 - P_a(0.01) = 1 - 0.9532 = 0.0468 \approx 4.68\%
$$

**Step 5 — Consumer's Risk at LTPD = 0.06:**

Similarly, $P_a(p=0.06)$ is computed. For brevity:

$(0.94)^{80} = e^{-4.92} \approx 0.00729$

For $d=1$: $80 \times 0.06 \times (0.94)^{79} = 4.8 \times 0.00776 \approx 0.0372$

For $d=2$: $3160 \times 0.0036 \times (0.94)^{78} = 11.376 \times 0.00825 \approx 0.0939$

$$
P_a(0.06) = 0.00729 + 0.0372 + 0.0939 = 0.1384
$$

Therefore, Consumer's Risk:
$$
\beta = P_a(0.06) = 0.1384 \approx 13.84\%
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $P_a(p=0.04) = 0.3739$ or **37.39%**
- Producer's Risk $\alpha = 4.68\%$
- Consumer's Risk $\beta = 13.84\%$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]`
