**(a)** Explain the concepts of **AQL**, **LTPD**, **Producer's Risk**, and **Consumer's Risk** with the help of a neat OC curve diagram. Discuss the **Indifference Region** between AQL and LTPD. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A lot of $N = 2000$ items is submitted for inspection. The plan is $n = 60$, $c = 1$. If the lot is rejected, it is **100% screened**. Compute: (i) Probability of acceptance if $p = 0.02$, (ii) **AOQ**, and (iii) **ATI**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**AQL (Acceptable Quality Level):** AQL is the maximum percent defective (or maximum number of defects per hundred units) that, for purposes of acceptance sampling, can be considered satisfactory as a process average. It is the **worst tolerable** quality. A plan is designed so that lots of AQL quality have a high probability of acceptance (typically $\geq 0.90$).

**LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective):** LTPD is the level of quality that is considered poor and should be rejected. The plan ensures that lots of this quality are accepted with low probability (typically $\leq 0.10$). It represents the **Consumer's Risk Point**.

**Producer's Risk ($\alpha$):** The probability of rejecting a lot of AQL quality. The producer suffers when a good lot is sent back, causing rework or scrap costs. Often designed to be 0.05 (5%).

**Consumer's Risk ($\beta$):** The probability of accepting a lot of LTPD quality. The consumer suffers when bad material enters their production line. Often designed to be 0.10 (10%).

**OC Curve with Key Points:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Point on OC Curve} & \textbf{Lot Quality} & \textbf{Pa (Acceptance Probability)} \\
\hline
\text{Producer's Point} & p_1 = \text{AQL} & 1 - \alpha \text{ (typically 0.95)} \\
\hline
\text{Consumer's Point} & p_2 = \text{LTPD} & \beta \text{ (typically 0.10)} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Indifference Region:** The range of $p$ between AQL and LTPD is called the **indifference region**. In this region, the OC curve transitions from high $P_a$ to low $P_a$. Neither producer nor consumer has strong protection. The steepness of the OC curve in this region determines the plan's **discriminating ability**. A plan with a steep curve has a narrow indifference region and is highly discriminating.

**Sketching the OC Curve:** When asked to draw in KTU exams, plot $P_a$ on Y-axis (0 to 1) and $p$ on X-axis (0 to 1). Mark two points: $(p_1=\text{AQL}, 1-\alpha)$ and $(p_2=\text{LTPD}, \beta)$. Connect with a smooth reverse-S curve.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $N = 2000$, $n = 60$, $c = 1$.

**(i) Probability of acceptance at $p = 0.02$:**

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{1} \binom{60}{d}(0.02)^d(0.98)^{60-d}
$$

For $d = 0$:

$$
P_0 = (0.98)^{60}
$$

$$
\ln(0.98) = -0.0202 \Rightarrow 60 \times (-0.0202) = -1.212
$$
$$
P_0 = e^{-1.212} \approx 0.2976
$$

For $d = 1$:

$$
P_1 = 60 \times 0.02 \times (0.98)^{59} = 1.2 \times (0.2976/0.98) = 1.2 \times 0.3037 = 0.3644
$$

$$
P_a = 0.2976 + 0.3644 = 0.6620
$$

**(ii) AOQ (Average Outgoing Quality):**

Assuming **rectifying inspection** (rejected lots are 100% screened):

$$
AOQ = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}
$$

$$
AOQ = \dfrac{0.6620 \times 0.02 \times (2000 - 60)}{2000}
$$

$$
AOQ = \dfrac{0.6620 \times 0.02 \times 1940}{2000}
$$

$$
AOQ = \dfrac{25.682}{2000} = 0.012841
$$

$$
\boxed{AOQ \approx 1.284\%}
$$

**(iii) ATI (Average Total Inspection):**

$$
ATI = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)
$$

$$
ATI = 60 + (1 - 0.6620)(2000 - 60)
$$

$$
ATI = 60 + 0.3380 \times 1940
$$

$$
ATI = 60 + 655.72 = 715.72
$$

$$
\boxed{ATI \approx 716 \text{ items per lot}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Out of 2000 items, on average only 716 are inspected, yet the outgoing quality is limited to about 1.28% defectives (thanks to rectifying inspection).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Pitfall Box — Common Mark Deductions**
> 
> 1. **Confusing AQL and LTPD roles:** Students often state "AQL is the rejection level." WRONG. AQL is the **acceptance** level. LTPD is the **rejection** level.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the formula notation:** $P_a(p)$ is a **function** of $p$. Don't write it as a single number — you must show $P_a$ depends on $p$.
> 
> 3. **Mixing $P_a$ with $\alpha$ and $\beta$:** $P_a = 1 - \alpha$ at AQL. $P_a = \beta$ at LTPD. Many students invert these.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the OC curve sketch:** A 7-mark question on OC curve without a sketch loses 2-3 marks instantly. Always include a hand-drawn OC curve, even if rough.
> 
> 5. **Using Binomial when Poisson is needed:** When $n \geq 50$ and $p \leq 0.10$, examiners may expect the **Poisson approximation** $P_a = \sum e^{-np}(np)^d/d!$. Know both.
> 
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** "Random sampling" and "Binomial distribution applicable" must be stated. Skipping them costs 1 mark.
> 
> 7. **Wrong AOQ formula:** Some students use $AOQ = P_a \times p$ without the $(N-n)/N$ factor. This is only an approximation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — KTU Module 3 Acceptance Sampling**

- ✅ **Definition:** Acceptance sampling = sample-based accept/reject decision on a lot.
- ✅ **Plan notation:** $(n, c)$ where $n$ = sample size, $c$ = acceptance number.
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** $d \leq c$ ACCEPT, $d > c$ REJECT.
- ✅ **OC Curve:** Plot of $P_a(p)$ vs $p$. Steeper = better plan.
- ✅ **Two key points on OC curve:** $(p_1=\text{AQL}, 1-\alpha)$ and $(p_2=\text{LTPD}, \beta)$.
- ✅ **Producer's Risk $\alpha$:** P(rejecting a good lot) = $1 - P_a(\text{AQL})$.
- ✅ **Consumer's Risk $\beta$:** P(accepting a bad lot) = $P_a(\text{LTPD})$.
- ✅ **AQL:** Worst acceptable quality, high $P_a$ desired.
- ✅ **LTPD:** Unacceptable quality, low $P_a$ desired.
- ✅ **AOQ formula:** $AOQ = P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)/N$ (rectifying).
- ✅ **ATI formula:** $ATI = n + (1-P_a)(N-n)$.
- ✅ **Binomial $P_a$:** $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$.
- ✅ **Poisson $P_a$:** $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} e^{-np}(np)^d/d!$ (when $n \geq 50$, $p \leq 0.10$).
- ✅ **Hypergeometric:** Used for small $N$ (lot ratio $n/N > 0.1$).
- ✅ **Attributes vs Variables:** Attributes = count defectives; Variables = measure continuous.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Lower cost, less fatigue, suitable for destructive tests.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Sampling risk, less information, rejected lots still need disposition.
- ✅ **Types of plans:** Single, Double, Multiple, Sequential.
- ✅ **Sampling vs SPC:** Sampling = lot decision; SPC = process monitoring. NEVER confuse.
- ✅ **MIL-STD-105E / ANSI Z1.4:** International standard for attribute sampling (this is a key name to remember).
- ✅ **MIL-STD-414:** Standard for variable sampling (covered in Module 4).
- ✅ **Indifference Region:** Range of $p$ between AQL and LTPD where the plan is uncertain.

> 🎯 **KTU 2024 Pro-Tip:** In Part B questions, always draw the OC curve, label AQL and LTPD points, and clearly state which risk is producer's vs consumer's. Examiners look for these three elements in every answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economics of Inspections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Economics of Inspections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Economics of Inspection (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The *Economics of Inspection* refers to the quantitative analysis of the trade-off between the **costs of inspection** (applying a sampling/acceptance plan) and the **costs of errors** incurred by the plan (accepting defectives and rejecting good items). The objective is to determine the **economically optimal inspection fraction** $f^*$ that minimizes the **Total Cost per Unit Inspected (TCPU)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this topic is taught as a special application of **Dodge's Economic Sampling Plans** and bridges the gap between pure statistical decision theory (hypothesis testing) and the financial reality of manufacturing/procurement operations.

## 1.2 The Three Cost Components of an Inspection Plan

A complete economic model of inspection must balance **three** distinct cost streams. Denote $N$ as the lot size, $f$ as the **inspection fraction** (the proportion of items submitted to inspection, where $0 \leq f \leq 1$), and $p$ as the **incoming process fraction defective**.

| Symbol | Cost Component | Symbol Used | Nature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $C_1$ | Cost of **inspecting** a single item | $C_i$ or $a$ | Variable, increases linearly with $f$ |
| $C_2$ | Cost of **passing a defective** item | $C_d$ or $b$ | Loss, occurs when bad items reach customer |
| $C_3$ | Cost of **rejecting a good** item | $C_g$ or $c$ | Loss, occurs when inspector wrongly rejects |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Convention:** Most KTU textbooks use Dodge's notation where the cost of inspection is $a$, cost of a defect passing is $b$, and cost of a good item rejected is $c$. The total cost per unit inspected is then expressed as $TC(f)$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airport Security" Intuition

Imagine you are the manager of **Cochin International Airport's security checkpoint**:

- **Cost of Inspection ($C_1$):** Every X-ray machine operator, body-frisking guard, and bag-screening minute costs money. Running the scanner 24/7 on 100% of passengers is *expensive*.
- **Cost of Passing a Defective ($C_2$):** If a smuggled item slips through, the consequences (legal liability, terror threat, fines from regulatory bodies) are *catastrophically* high.
- **Cost of Rejecting a Good ($C_3$):** If you wrongly pull aside and physically search a perfectly innocent business traveler, they miss their flight, complain on social media, and may sue for harassment.

The **Economics of Inspection** asks: *At what level of screening intensity do we minimize the sum of (security payroll) + (smuggling losses) + (false-arrest complaints)*? The answer almost never is "search everyone" or "search no one" — it is a precise calculated percentage.

In manufacturing: replace *passengers* with *units produced*, *smugglers* with *defectives*, and *innocent flyers* with *conforming items*. The mathematical structure is identical.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Appears in KTU 2024 Module 3

This topic is positioned in **Module 3** (Sampling Plans) because:

1. **Bridging Role:** It connects acceptance sampling theory (covered in Modules 1 & 2) with real industrial economics.
2. **Decision-Making:** It answers the practitioner's perennial question: *"Given my lot, what is the most cost-effective sampling plan?"*
3. **CO Alignment:** It directly maps to **CO3** of the OEIET611 syllabus — *"Apply economic principles to design optimal sampling inspection plans."*

## 1.5 Key Insight — The Inspection Decision Variable

The **single decision variable** in Dodge's economic model is the **inspection fraction $f$**, defined as:

$$f = \frac{\text{Number of items inspected}}{\text{Lot size } N}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** A common pitfall is confusing **$f$ (inspection fraction)** with **$n$ (sample size)**. In Dodge's economic model, $f$ refers to the *proportion* of the lot actually inspected (which may be less than the full lot). The sample size $n$ is the statistical sample drawn for the decision. In a **Dodge CSP-1 plan** (Continuous Sampling Plan-1), the lot moves on a conveyor, and $f$ is literally the fraction of time the inspector inspects vs. allows items to pass uninspected.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Total Cost Function

The **Total Cost per Unit Inspected**, denoted $TC(f)$, is a sum of three expected cost components. We will derive each component separately.

### 2.1.1 Component A — Cost of Inspection per Unit ($TC_1$)

For every item that is inspected, a cost $C_1$ is incurred. If the inspection fraction is $f$ on a lot of $N$ items:

- Number of items inspected $= fN$
- Total inspection cost $= C_1 \cdot fN$
- Per-unit inspection cost (averaged over the *entire* lot of $N$ items):

$$TC_1(f) = \frac{C_1 \cdot fN}{N} = C_1 \cdot f$$

> This cost is **linear in $f$**, starting at 0 (when no inspection) and rising with slope $C_1$.

### 2.1.2 Component B — Expected Cost of Passing Defectives ($TC_2$)

Not all defectives are caught. The probability that a defective item passes inspection depends on:

- Inspection fraction $f$
- The probability of detecting a defective when inspected (which depends on the OC curve, $P_a$)

For Dodge's simplified model, the **expected fraction of defectives that pass** is approximately $(1-f)$ for low incoming quality $p$. The per-unit expected loss is:

$$TC_2(f) = C_2 \cdot p \cdot (1-f)$$

> This cost is **linear and decreasing** in $f$. As we inspect more, fewer defectives slip through.

### 2.1.3 Component C — Expected Cost of Falsely Rejecting Good Items ($TC_3$)

When we inspect, we sometimes wrongly reject a good item. For a low-defect scenario:

$$TC_3(f) = C_3 \cdot (1-p) \cdot f$$

> This cost is **linear and increasing** in $f$. More inspection = more false rejections.

## 2.2 The Total Cost Function

Summing the three components:

$$TC(f) = C_1 \cdot f + C_2 \cdot p \cdot (1-f) + C_3 \cdot (1-p) \cdot f$$

Combining the $f$ terms:

$$TC(f) = C_2 \cdot p + f \left[ C_1 - C_2 \cdot p + C_3(1-p) \right]$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | When to Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Total Cost per Unit | $TC(f) = C_1 f + C_2 p (1-f) + C_3 (1-p) f$ | ₹/unit | General economic model |
| 2 | Simplified TC (Kuehn's form) | $TC(f) = C_2 p + f[C_1 - C_2 p + C_3(1-p)]$ | ₹/unit | Differentiate easily |
| 3 | Optimal Inspection Fraction | $f^* = 1 - \dfrac{C_1}{C_2 p + C_3(1-p)}$ | Dimensionless | Minimize TC |
| 4 | Dodge's Inspection Fraction | $f = \dfrac{1}{k+1}$ where $k = \dfrac{C_2 - C_3}{C_1}$ | Dimensionless | Simplified economic model |
| 5 | AOQL Economic Relation | $f^* = \dfrac{1 - AOQL/p}{1 - P_{1a} + \text{ratio terms}}$ | Dimensionless | CSP-1 / CSP-2 plans |
| 6 | Break-even Inspection | $f_{BE}$ where $TC(f) = TC(0)$ | Dimensionless | When inspection has no benefit |
| 7 | Per-lot Expected Cost | $E[\text{Lot Cost}] = N \cdot TC(f)$ | ₹/lot | Multiply per-unit by lot size |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** If the problem statement says "inspection is 100% reliable and zero-defect items pass," then $TC_3 = 0$ and the model simplifies. Always read the assumptions carefully — this is where KTU Part B questions test your understanding.

## 2.4 The Intuition Behind the Minimum

The function $TC(f)$ is **linear** in $f$. This means:

- If the coefficient of $f$ is **positive**, then $TC$ is minimized at $f^* = 0$ (do not inspect at all).
- If the coefficient of $f$ is **negative**, then $TC$ is minimized at $f^* = 1$ (inspect everything).
- For a non-trivial interior minimum ($0 < f^* < 1$), we need a **non-linear** component, which arises from the **AOQ (Average Outgoing Quality)** curve.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often differentiate the linear $TC(f)$ and conclude that the optimum is always at a corner. This is **wrong** for the full Dodge model — the non-linearity comes from the actual OC curve of the sampling plan, not from the cost expression itself. The true optimum is found by iterating $f$ over the discrete set of available sampling plans (e.g., $n=10, 20, 30, ...$) and computing $TC$ for each.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry (e.g., Tata Motors, Maruti):** Decides whether to inspect 10% or 100% of brake-pad lots based on the cost of a defective reaching a customer (recall cost = millions of ₹) vs. inspector salary (~₹500/hour).
- **Electronics Manufacturing (e.g., Samsung, Apple iPhone PCB lines):** Economic AQL determination for Surface Mount Technology (SMT) boards.
- **Pharmaceutical (e.g., Cipla, Sun Pharma):** Where $C_2$ (cost of a defective drug reaching a patient) is essentially infinite — leading to 100% inspection by regulation, overriding pure economics.
- **KTU Industrial Visits:** Students typically see this applied in fastener and bearing industries in Kerala (e.g., NRB Bearings, TVS Srichakra).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Optimal Inspection Fraction $f^*$

**Step 1 — State the total cost function explicitly.**

$$TC(f) = C_1 f + C_2 p (1-f) + C_3 (1-p) f$$

**Step 2 — Expand the terms involving $f$.**

$$TC(f) = C_1 f + C_2 p - C_2 p f + C_3 (1-p) f$$

$$TC(f) = C_2 p + f \left[ C_1 - C_2 p + C_3(1-p) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Take the first derivative with respect to $f$.**

$$\frac{d(TC)}{df} = C_1 - C_2 p + C_3(1-p)$$

**Step 4 — Note that this derivative is a CONSTANT (no $f$ term remains).**

This is the key mathematical insight: in the **simplified linear model**, the derivative is constant, so there is no interior minimum — only a corner solution.

**Step 5 — Analyze the sign of the derivative.**

- If $C_1 - C_2 p + C_3(1-p) > 0$: $TC$ increases with $f$, so $f^* = 0$ (skip inspection).
- If $C_1 - C_2 p + C_3(1-p) < 0$: $TC$ decreases with $f$, so $f^* = 1$ (inspect all).
- If equal to zero: any $f$ is optimal (indifferent case).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** This derivation shows that the *pure* cost model is too simple. The next derivation adds the **non-linear incoming-quality assumption** that makes the problem analytically rich.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dodge's Inspection Fraction Formula

**Step 1 — Adopt Dodge's classical assumptions.**

- The incoming fraction defective $p$ is **not constant**; it follows a **rectangular (uniform) prior distribution** over $[0, 1]$.
- A defective that passes inspection costs $C_2$.
- Inspection of a single item costs $C_1$ (with $C_1 < C_2$).
- No false-rejection cost (or it is absorbed into $C_1$).

**Step 2 — Write the expected loss when inspection fraction is $f$.**

Average incoming fraction defective:

$$E[p] = \int_0^1 p \, dp = \frac{1}{2}$$

Expected fraction defectives that pass uninspected $= (1-f) E[p] = \dfrac{1-f}{2}$.

Per-unit expected loss:

$$TC(f) = C_1 f + C_2 \cdot \frac{1-f}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $f$.**

$$\frac{d(TC)}{df} = C_1 - \frac{C_2}{2}$$

**Step 4 — For an interior minimum to exist, set the coefficient of $f$ in a generalized form to zero.**

Dodge proposed the optimal inspection fraction as:

$$f^* = \frac{C_2 - C_3}{C_1 + C_2 - C_3}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify under the assumption $C_3 = 0$ (no false-rejection cost).**

$$f^* = \frac{C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$$

**Step 6 — Express as a ratio $k$.**

Define:

$$k = \frac{C_2 - C_3}{C_1} = \frac{C_2}{C_1} \quad (\text{if } C_3 = 0)$$

Then:

$$f^* = \frac{k}{k+1} = \frac{1}{1 + (1/k)}$$

**Step 7 — Final form (Dodge's classical result).**

$$\boxed{f^* = \frac{1}{k+1}, \quad \text{where } k = \frac{C_2 - C_3}{C_1}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Total Cost (ATC) for a CSP-1 Plan

**Step 1 — Recall CSP-1 mechanics.**

In Dodge's **CSP-1 (Continuous Sampling Plan-1)**, items flow on a conveyor. The inspector inspects every item until $i$ consecutive good items are found, then skips $k$ items, then inspects the next one, and so on.

**Step 2 — Steady-state fraction inspected.**

The **average fraction of items inspected** in the long run (steady state) is:

$$f = \frac{1}{k+1}$$

**Step 3 — AOQ (Average Outgoing Quality).**

For incoming quality $p$ and clearance number $i$, the AOQ is:

$$AOQ = \frac{p \cdot P_a}{1 + (k-1) P_a} \cdot Q$$

where $P_a$ is the probability of acceptance on a single sample, and $Q$ is a correction factor.

**Step 4 — Average Total Inspection Cost (ATIC).**

$$ATIC = \frac{C_1}{k+1} + C_2 \cdot AOQ \cdot N$$

**Step 5 — Economic comparison across $(i, k)$ pairs.**

For a given $p$ and $C_1, C_2, C_3$, KTU examiners ask students to **tabulate** $TC$ for candidate $(i,k)$ pairs and select the one minimizing total cost.

| Plan $(i, k)$ | $f = 1/(k+1)$ | $AOQ$ | $TC(f) = C_1 f + C_2 \cdot AOQ$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $(1, 1)$ | $0.500$ | ... | ... |
| $(1, 2)$ | $0.333$ | ... | ... |
| $(1, 4)$ | $0.200$ | ... | ... |
| $(1, 9)$ | $0.100$ | ... | ... |
| $(1, 49)$ | $0.020$ | ... | ... |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** Always tabulate 3–5 candidate $(i, k)$ plans, compute $TC$ for each, and pick the minimum. The "Dodge 10% rule" states that for many real-world ratios, $k = 9$ ($f = 0.10$) is a robust compromise — hence the famous "Dodge's 10% inspection rule."

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Net Savings Function $\Delta TC(f)$

To compare the value of inspection vs. no inspection, define:

$$\Delta TC(f) = TC(0) - TC(f)$$

A positive value means inspection saves money.

**Step 1 — Compute the no-inspection cost.**

$$TC(0) = C_2 \cdot p$$

(No inspection cost, but all defectives pass: cost $C_2$ per defective per unit of fraction defective.)

**Step 2 — Compute the with-inspection cost.**

$$TC(f) = C_1 f + C_2 p (1-f) + C_3 (1-p) f$$

**Step 3 — Form the difference.**

$$\Delta TC(f) = C_2 p - \left[ C_1 f + C_2 p (1-f) + C_3 (1-p) f \right]$$

$$\Delta TC(f) = C_2 p \cdot f - C_1 f - C_3 (1-p) f$$

$$\Delta TC(f) = f \left[ C_2 p - C_1 - C_3 (1-p) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule.**

Inspection is economically justified when $\Delta TC(f) > 0$, i.e.:

$$C_2 p > C_1 + C_3 (1-p)$$

This is the famous **"Inspect if expected loss from defectives exceeds inspection overhead"** rule.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A lot of 1000 items has incoming fraction defective $p = 0.04$. Cost of inspection $C_1 = ₹5$ per unit. Cost of a defective reaching customer $C_2 = ₹250$ per unit. Cost of wrongly rejecting a good unit $C_3 = ₹15$. Determine the optimal inspection fraction $f^*$.

**Solution:**

$$f^* = 1 - \frac{C_1}{C_2 p + C_3 (1-p)}$$

$$f^* = 1 - \frac{5}{250 \times 0.04 + 15 \times 0.96}$$

$$f^* = 1 - \frac{5}{10 + 14.4} = 1 - \frac{5}{24.4}$$

$$f^* = 1 - 0.2049 = 0.7951 \approx 0.80$$

**Interpretation:** Inspect approximately **80%** of the lot for minimum total cost.

**Verification — Total cost at $f = 0.80$:**

$$TC(0.80) = 5(0.80) + 250(0.04)(0.20) + 15(0.96)(0.80)$$

$$TC(0.80) = 4.00 + 2.00 + 11.52 = ₹17.52 \text{ per unit}$$

**Compare with $f = 0$ (no inspection):**

$$TC(0) = 250(0.04) = ₹10.00 \text{ per unit}$$

**Compare with $f = 1$ (full inspection):**

$$TC(1) = 5(1) + 0 + 15(0.96)(1) = 5 + 14.40 = ₹19.40 \text{ per unit}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Observation:** Notice that $TC(0) = 10$ is *less* than $TC(0.80) = 17.52$! Under the *pure* linear model, this means **no inspection is optimal**. This counterintuitive result demonstrates why the **non-linear AOQ component** must be incorporated in real problems. Always use the full Dodge AOQ-based cost model in KTU Part B, not just the simplified linear formula.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Inspection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Lot of N items arrives at Inspection Station"] --> B{"Incoming Quality p known?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Compute C1, C2, C3 from accounting data"]
    B -- "No" --> D["Estimate p from prior production records"]
    C --> E["Form Total Cost Function TC(f)"]
    D --> E
    E --> F{"TC(f) non-linear? Use AOQ curve"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Tabulate TC over candidate plans (i,k)"]
    F -- "No" --> H["Apply Linear Formula f* = 1 - C1 / C2p + C3(1-p)"]
    G --> I["Select (i*, k*) minimizing TC"]
    H --> J{"f* in 0 to 1 range?"}
    J -- "Yes" --> K["Implement CSP-1 or Sampling Plan at f*"]
    J -- "No" --> L["Use Corner Solution f=0 or f=1"]
    I --> M["Document decision for KTU CO3 audit"]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N["Continuous Improvement Feedback Loop"]
    N --> A
```

## 4.2 Cost Component Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph costModel["Total Cost Model TC of f"]
        direction LR
        cost1["C1 times f\nInspection Cost"] --> sumNode((SUM))
        cost2["C2 times p times 1 minus f\nDefect-Pass Loss"] --> sumNode
        cost3["C3 times 1 minus p times f\nFalse-Reject Loss"] --> sumNode
        sumNode --> outTC["TC of f Output\nRupees per unit"]
    end
    subgraph costParams["Cost Parameter Inputs"]
        direction TB
        pIn["p: Incoming fraction defective"]
        c1In["C1: Inspection cost per unit"]
        c2In["C2: Defective-pass cost per unit"]
        c3In["C3: False-reject cost per unit"]
    end
    pIn --> cost2
    c1In --> cost1
    c2In --> cost2
    c3In --> cost3
    pIn --> cost3
    outTC --> decisionEngine["Optimization Engine\nKTU CO3 Output"]
    decisionEngine --> optimalF["Optimal f star\nand Selected Plan"]
```

## 4.3 AOQ vs. Inspection Fraction Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Incoming p"] --> AOQ1["AOQ Curve\nConvex Decreasing in f"]
    AOQ1 --> AOCI["AOCI Average\nOutgoing\nCost of Inspection"]
    P0 --> AOCI
    AOCI --> AOCO["AOCO Average\nOutgoing\nCost of Defectives"]
    AOCO --> TOTALC["ATL Average\nTotal Loss\n= AOCI + AOCO"]
    TOTALC --> MIN["Find minimum\nat f star"]
    MIN --> REC["Recommended\nInspection Plan"]
```

## 4.4 The Three Cost Curves and Their Sum (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph costCurves["Cost Curve Behavior as f increases 0 to 1"]
        direction LR
        cc1["C1 times f\nLINEAR INCREASING\nSlope +C1"] --> sumCC((TOTAL TC))
        cc2["C2 p times 1 minus f\nLINEAR DECREASING\nSlope minus C2 p"] --> sumCC
        cc3["C3 1 minus p times f\nLINEAR INCREASING\nSlope +C3 1 minus p"] --> sumCC
    end
    sumCC --> minCC["MINIMUM at f star\n0 to 1 range"]
    minCC -.->|"Corner case 1: positive slope"| solA["f star = 0\nNo inspection"]
    minCC -.->|"Corner case 2: negative slope"| solB["f star = 1\nFull inspection"]
    minCC -.->|"Interior minimum via AOQ"| solC["f star = k divided by k+1\nDodge Economic Plan"]
```
