**Problem:** Given $n = 60$, $c = 1$, and LTPD = 0.05. Determine β.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $n p_2$:

$$\lambda = 60 \times 0.05 = 3.0$$

Step 2 — Apply Poisson cumulative formula with $c = 1$:

$$\beta \;=\; P(X \le 1) \;=\; \frac{e^{-3} (3)^0}{0!} + \frac{e^{-3} (3)^1}{1!}$$

Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=0) &= 0.04979 \\
P(X=1) &= 0.14936 \\
\beta &= 0.04979 + 0.14936 = 0.19915
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Compare with the standard 0.10 limit. Since $0.19915 > 0.10$, this plan **does not satisfy** the consumer's protection requirement.

Step 5 — If we instead try $c = 0$ (zero-acceptance plan) with $n = 60$:

$$\beta = P(X = 0) = e^{-3} = 0.04979$$

This satisfies $\beta \le 0.10$. **Conclusion:** To meet consumer's protection at LTPD = 0.05, the acceptance number must be reduced to $c = 0$.

## 3.4 Worked Problem 3 — Designing n to Meet a Target β

**Problem:** Design a single sampling plan with $c = 1$ such that β ≤ 0.10 at LTPD = 0.05.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — From the Poisson cumulative table for $c = 1$, we need the smallest $\lambda$ such that:

$$\beta = P(X \le 1) = (1 + \lambda) e^{-\lambda} \le 0.10$$

Step 2 — From standard tables, this corresponds to $\lambda \le 3.9$ for $c = 1, \beta = 0.10$.

Step 3 — Therefore:

$$n \ge \frac{\lambda}{p_2} = \frac{3.9}{0.05} = 78$$

Step 4 — Take $n = 78$ (or rounded up to $n = 80$ for safety).

Step 5 — Verification at $\lambda = 80 \times 0.05 = 4.0$:

$$\beta = (1 + 4) e^{-4} = 5 \times 0.01832 = 0.0916 < 0.10 \;\;\checkmark$$

## 3.5 Worked Problem 4 — Verifying Both α and β Simultaneously

**Problem:** A single sampling plan has $n = 100$, $c = 2$, with AQL = 0.01 and LTPD = 0.06. Verify whether this plan simultaneously satisfies $\alpha \le 0.05$ and $\beta \le 0.10$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $\lambda_1 = n p_1 = 100 \times 0.01 = 1.0$ for AQL:

$$1 - \alpha = P_a(0.01) = \sum_{x=0}^{2} \frac{e^{-1}(1)^x}{x!}$$

Step 2 — Evaluate the three terms at $\lambda = 1.0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=0) &= e^{-1} = 0.3679 \\
P(X=1) &= e^{-1} = 0.3679 \\
P(X=2) &= \tfrac{1}{2} e^{-1} = 0.1839 \\
P_a &= 0.3679 + 0.3679 + 0.1839 = 0.9197
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Hence $\alpha = 1 - 0.9197 = 0.0803$.

Step 4 — Compute $\lambda_2 = n p_2 = 100 \times 0.06 = 6.0$ for LTPD:

$$P_a(0.06) = \sum_{x=0}^{2} \frac{e^{-6}(6)^x}{x!}$$

Step 5 — Evaluate each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=0) &= e^{-6} = 0.00248 \\
P(X=1) &= 6 \cdot e^{-6} = 0.01487 \\
P(X=2) &= 18 \cdot e^{-6} = 0.04462 \\
\beta &= 0.00248 + 0.01487 + 0.04462 = 0.06197
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 6 — Check both criteria:

$$\alpha = 0.0803 > 0.05 \;\;\text{(fails producer criterion)}$$
$$\beta = 0.0620 < 0.10 \;\;\text{(passes consumer criterion)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The plan protects the consumer reasonably well but penalizes the producer by rejecting too many good lots. The plan must be redesigned — typically by *decreasing* $c$ or *increasing* $n$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve — α and β Visualization

The Mermaid block below renders the **functional architecture** of the OC curve decision process, mapping how a single sampling plan transitions from accepting good lots to rejecting bad lots, with both α and β explicitly labeled.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lot Arrives at Inspection Bay] --> B[True Quality p unknown]
    B --> C[Draw Random Sample of Size n]
    C --> D[Count Defectives X in Sample]
    D --> E{Decision Rule: X less than or equal to c}
    E -- YES --> F[ACCEPT the Lot]
    E -- NO  --> G[REJECT the Lot]
    F --> H[Lot moves to Production / Customer]
    G --> I[100 percent Rectifying Inspection]

    subgraph Producer_Zone [Producer Risk Zone near AQL]
        P1[Quality equals AQL equals p1] --> P2[Expected Acceptance equals 1 minus alpha]
        P2 --> P3[alpha equals Probability of Rejecting a GOOD lot]
    end

    subgraph Consumer_Zone [Consumer Risk Zone near LTPD]
        C1[Quality equals LTPD equals p2] --> C2[Expected Acceptance equals beta]
        C2 --> C3[beta equals Probability of Accepting a BAD lot]
    end

    subgraph OC_Curve_Path [Operating Characteristic Curve Trace]
        O1[At p equals 0, Pa equals 1.0] --> O2[At p equals AQL, Pa equals 1 minus alpha approx 0.95]
        O2 --> O3[At Indifference p, Pa equals 0.50]
        O3 --> O4[At p equals LTPD, Pa equals beta approx 0.10]
        O4 --> O5[At p equals 1.0, Pa equals 0.0]
    end
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Consumer's Risk Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP[Input Parameters: n, c, LTPD p2] --> COMP[Compute lambda equals n times p2]
    COMP --> DIST{Choose Distribution Model}
    DIST -- "n less than 20 or p greater than 0.10" --> BIN[Use Binomial Cumulative Sum]
    DIST -- "n greater than or equal to 20 and p less than or equal to 0.10" --> POIS[Use Poisson Approximation]
    BIN --> SUM[Sum from x equals 0 to c of Binomial Probability]
    POIS --> SUM
    SUM --> BETA[Output: beta equals Pa at LTPD]
    BETA --> CHECK{beta less than or equal to 0.10 ?}
    CHECK -- YES --> PASS[Plan SATISFIES Consumer Protection]
    CHECK -- NO  --> FAIL[Plan FAILS Consumer Protection]
    FAIL --> REDESIGN[Increase n or Decrease c]
    REDESIGN --> INP
```

## 4.3 Hypothesis-Testing Analogy — Type I vs Type II Errors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H0[H0: Lot is GOOD at AQL] -->|True| ACC[Accept correctly]
    H0 -->|False Type I Error alpha| REJ1[Reject good lot Producer Loses]
    H1[H1: Lot is BAD at LTPD] -->|True| REJ[Reject correctly]
    H1 -->|False Type II Error beta| ACC1[Accept bad lot Consumer Loses]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q:** Define *Consumer's Risk*. How is it related to the Operating Characteristic curve and the LTPD?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Consumer's Risk (β) is the probability that a sampling plan will accept a lot whose true quality is at the Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD). It is the Type II error of acceptance sampling. On the OC curve, β is read as the **height of the curve at $p = p_2 = \text{LTPD}$**, i.e., $\beta = P_a(\text{LTPD})$. Standard practice sets β ≤ **0.10**, meaning the consumer tolerates at most a 10 % chance of accepting a lot at LTPD quality.

*(Mark Allocation: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for LTPD linkage, 1 mark for OC curve + standard value.)*

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Distinguish between Producer's Risk (α) and Consumer's Risk (β) in two points.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Producer's Risk (α) | Consumer's Risk (β) |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Quality reference | Evaluated at **AQL** | Evaluated at **LTPD** |
| Statistical nature | Type I error — rejecting a good lot | Type II error — accepting a bad lot |
| Standard value | α ≤ **0.05** | β ≤ **0.10** |
| Who is protected | Producer / Manufacturer | Consumer / Buyer |

*(Mark Allocation: 1.5 marks per aspect — definition + reference point + numerical limit.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question 3A — `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**A single sampling plan has $n = 75$, $c = 2$. The specified AQL is 0.02 and LTPD is 0.08.**

**(a)** Compute the Producer's Risk α at AQL.  
**(b)** Compute the Consumer's Risk β at LTPD.  
   Use the Poisson approximation throughout. State whether the plan is acceptable from both parties' perspectives (criteria: α ≤ 0.05, β ≤ 0.10).

### Question 3A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Producer's Risk at AQL

**Given:** $n = 75$, $c = 2$, $p_1 = 0.02$.

**Step 1:** Compute Poisson parameter $\lambda_1$:

$$\lambda_1 = n \, p_1 = 75 \times 0.02 = 1.5$$

**Step 2:** Write acceptance probability:

$$1 - \alpha = P_a(0.02) = \sum_{x=0}^{2} \frac{e^{-1.5} (1.5)^{x}}{x!}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=0) &= e^{-1.5} = 0.22313 \\
P(X=1) &= 1.5 \, e^{-1.5} = 0.33470 \\
P(X=2) &= \tfrac{1.5^2}{2} e^{-1.5} = 1.125 \times 0.22313 = 0.25102
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Sum and obtain $\alpha$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= 0.22313 + 0.33470 + 0.25102 = 0.80885 \\
\alpha &= 1 - 0.80885 = 0.19115
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\alpha \approx 0.191 > 0.05$ ⇒ **Plan fails producer's criterion.** (7 marks)

---

#### Part (b) — Consumer's Risk at LTPD

**Given:** $n = 75$, $c = 2$, $p_2 = 0.08$.

**Step 1:** Compute Poisson parameter:

$$\lambda_2 = 75 \times 0.08 = 6.0$$

**Step 2:** Write acceptance probability:

$$\beta = P_a(0.08) = \sum_{x=0}^{2} \frac{e^{-6} (6)^{x}}{x!}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=0) &= e^{-6} = 0.00248 \\
P(X=1) &= 6 \, e^{-6} = 0.01487 \\
P(X=2) &= \tfrac{36}{2} e^{-6} = 18 \times 0.00248 = 0.04462
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Sum:

$$\beta = 0.00248 + 0.01487 + 0.04462 = 0.06197$$

**Result:** $\beta \approx 0.062 < 0.10$ ⇒ **Plan satisfies consumer's criterion.** (7 marks)

---

**Final Verdict:** This plan over-protects the consumer but under-protects the producer. It should be redesigned — typically by *increasing* $c$ to 3 or 4 (to reduce α) while still keeping β within tolerance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (a):** Students frequently use $1 - \alpha = P(X = c)$ instead of the cumulative sum. For $c = 2$, this gives $1 - \alpha = 0.25$, which is **wrong**. The acceptance region is $X \le c = 2$, hence **three terms** must be summed. Also, do not forget to convert $1 - P_a$ into $\alpha$ — omitting the complement operation costs 1 mark.

---

### Question 3B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**A consumer demands that a lot at LTPD = 0.04 be accepted with probability at most 0.10.**

**(a)** Design a single sampling plan with $c = 1$ that satisfies this requirement. Compute the required sample size $n$.  
**(b)** Verify the plan's Producer's Risk if AQL = 0.01. State whether the producer's criterion $\alpha \le 0.05$ is met.

### Question 3B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Designing n for β ≤ 0.10 at LTPD

**Given:** $c = 1$, $p_2 = 0.04$, target $\beta \le 0.10$.

**Step 1:** Use Poisson approximation. For $c = 1$:

$$\beta = P(X \le 1) = (1 + \lambda) \, e^{-\lambda}, \quad \text{where } \lambda = n p_2$$

**Step 2:** From standard Poisson tables for $c = 1$, the cumulative probability equals 0.10 at $\lambda \approx 3.9$.

**Step 3:** Solve for $n$:

$$
n = \frac{\lambda}{p_2} = \frac{3.9}{0.04} = 97.5
$$

**Step 4:** Round up to the next integer → $n = 98$.

**Step 5:** Verification at $\lambda = 98 \times 0.04 = 3.92$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X=0) &= e^{-3.92} = 0.01988 \\
P(X=1) &= 3.92 \times 0.01988 = 0.07793 \\
\beta &= 0.01988 + 0.07793 = 0.09781 < 0.10 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $n = 98$, $c = 1$. (7 marks)

---

#### Part (b) — Verification of Producer's Risk

**Given:** AQL = 0.01, $n = 98$, $c = 1$.

**Step 1:** Compute $\lambda_1$:

$$\lambda_1 = 98 \times 0.01 = 0.98$$

**Step 2:** Acceptance probability:

$$1 - \alpha = P(X \le 1) = (1 + 0.98) e^{-0.98}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{-0.98} &= 0.37531 \\
P(X=0) &= 0.37531 \\
P(X=1) &= 0.98 \times 0.37531 = 0.36780 \\
1 - \alpha &= 0.37531 + 0.36780 = 0.74311
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = 1 - 0.74311 = 0.25689$$

**Result:** $\alpha \approx 0.257 > 0.05$ ⇒ **Producer's criterion NOT met.**

**Final Recommendation:** The plan is excellent for the consumer ($\beta = 0.098$) but the producer is losing too many good lots. The plan must be revised — either the AQL should be relaxed (raised) or $c$ should be increased (e.g., to $c = 2$ or $c = 3$). (7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** Students often write $\alpha = P_a$ rather than $\alpha = 1 - P_a$. The Producer's Risk is the probability of **rejecting** a good lot, which is the **complement** of acceptance. Inverting this yields a value > 1, which is mathematically nonsensical and costs 1–2 marks. Also, remember that "designing n" requires *rounding up* — a value of $n = 97$ would push β above 0.10.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Consumer's Risk**

- **Consumer's Risk (β)** = Probability of **accepting** a lot whose quality is **LTPD** ($p_2$). Always evaluated at LTPD, never at any other $p$.
- **Standard target:** $\beta \le 0.10$ (10 %); sometimes tightened to **0.05** in pharmaceutical / aerospace contexts.
- **Statistical identity:** β is the **Type II Error** in the hypothesis test $H_0$: $p = $ AQL vs $H_1$: $p = $ LTPD.
- **Poisson approximation valid** when $n \ge 20$ **and** $p \le 0.10$. KTU problems almost always permit this approximation.
- **Calculation rule:** Sum from $x = 0$ to $c$ (cumulative), never just $P(X = c)$.
- **OC Curve reading:** At $p = $ AQL, $P_a = 1 - \alpha$ (high). At $p = $ LTPD, $P_a = \beta$ (low).
- **Design direction:** To **decrease β**, *increase* $n$ or *decrease* $c$. Conversely, increasing $c$ relaxes consumer protection.
- **Trade-off:** Reducing β (consumer-friendly) typically *increases* α (producer-unfriendly). No single plan can minimize both simultaneously.
- **Master tables:** KTU problems often provide Poisson cumulative tables; locate the row for $(c, \beta)$ and read off $\lambda = n p$, then derive $n$.
- **Pitfall #1:** Confusing AQL with LTPD during substitution — always label which reference $p$ you are computing.
- **Pitfall #2:** Forgetting to compute $\alpha = 1 - P_a$ instead of $\alpha = P_a$.
- **Pitfall #3:** Not rounding up $n$ to the next integer — fractional sample sizes are meaningless.
- **Multiple sampling extension:** Consumer's risk is computed by integrating across all stages; use ASN when designing double or multiple plans.
- **Rectifying inspection context:** When rejected lots are 100 % inspected, AOQ and ATI become relevant — but the *primary* consumer's risk metric remains β at LTPD.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AOQL and ATI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** AOQL and ATI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)** is defined as the *maximum* value of the Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) that can be achieved over the entire range of possible incoming product quality levels (p), under a given **rectifying inspection** sampling plan.

Mathematically, the AOQL is the supremum of the AOQ curve:

$$\text{AOQL} = \max_{0 \leq p \leq 1} \text{AOQ}(p)$$

The AOQL represents a **guaranteed ceiling** on the long-run average percentage of defectives that will leave the inspection station in a rectified output stream. It is independent of the actual incoming quality of the lots submitted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rectifying Inspection Context:** AOQL is meaningful **only when** rejected lots are subjected to 100% screening and all defectives found are replaced with good ones (rectification). Without rectification, the concept degenerates into standard acceptance sampling without an outgoing quality guarantee.

---

## 1.2 Average Total Inspection (ATI)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Average Total Inspection (ATI)** is defined as the expected (mean) number of items inspected per lot when the rectifying inspection plan is applied repeatedly to a continuous stream of incoming lots having fraction defective *p*.

It accounts for the fact that:
- **Accepted lots** require only the sample (*n* items) to be inspected.
- **Rejected lots** require the *entire lot* (*N* items) to be inspected under rectification.

For a single-sampling rectifying plan:

$$\text{ATI} = n \cdot P_a + N \cdot (1 - P_a)$$

where $P_a$ is the probability of acceptance of the plan at the incoming quality *p*.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### The "Airport Security with a Twist" Analogy

Imagine a busy airport where every suitcase is **not** opened manually. Instead:

- A **random sample** of 30 suitcases (the sample, *n*) from each flight of 200 passengers (lot size, *N*) is X-ray scanned.
- If the sample contains ≤ 1 suspicious item (acceptance number *c*), the flight is allowed through (accepted lot).
- If more than 1 suspicious item is found, **the entire flight's luggage is opened and checked 100%**, and any contraband is removed (rectification).

> **AOQL Analogy:** No matter *how bad* the incoming smuggling rate *p* is (smugglers per passenger), the long-run average rate of contraband leaking out of the airport is **bounded above** by a fixed ceiling — the AOQL. The airport security chief can confidently state: "On average, at most X% of contraband will slip through, even in the worst case."

> **ATI Analogy:** The security manager wants to know **how many suitcases must be opened on average per flight**, because every manual check costs labour-hours. If few flights are bad, most days only 30 suitcases are opened. If many flights are bad, the manager must open all 200, costing more. ATI gives the **average workload** for budgeting.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metric Highlights

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics at a Glance:**
> - **AOQ** = Average Outgoing Quality (defective fraction *after* rectification).
> - **AOQL** = Maximum AOQ over all *p* ∈ [0, 1].
> - **ATI** = Average number of items inspected per lot in rectifying inspection.
> - **Rectifying Plan Assumption:** Rejected lots are inspected 100%, and all defectives discovered are replaced with good items.
> - **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve:** $P_a$ vs *p* — the engine that drives both AOQ and ATI computations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: From AOQ to AOQL

### Step-by-Step Logic of Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)

Under a **rectifying single-sampling plan** with parameters $(N, n, c)$:

1. **A lot of size *N* with *Np* defectives** enters the inspection station.
2. **A random sample of *n* items** is drawn and inspected.
3. If the sample contains ≤ *c* defectives, the lot is **accepted** and shipped out as-is (only the sample is replaced/refilled).
4. If the sample contains > *c* defectives, the lot is **rejected and rectified**: the remaining $(N - n)$ items are 100% inspected and all defectives replaced.

The expected number of defectives shipped out per lot:

- **If accepted** (probability $P_a$): the lot still contains approximately *Np* defectives on average (only *n* items were inspected and replaced).
- **If rejected and rectified** (probability $1 - P_a$): the lot contains **0 defectives** after 100% replacement.

The **expected number of defectives per lot** in the outgoing stream:

$$E[\text{defectives out per lot}] = N p \cdot P_a + 0 \cdot (1 - P_a) = N p P_a$$

The **Average Outgoing Quality** (defective fraction in outgoing product):

$$\text{AOQ} = \frac{N p \cdot P_a}{N} = p \cdot P_a \quad \text{(exact, since replacement leaves lot size unchanged)}$$

> **Refinement (sub-sampling correction):** Some textbooks account for the *n* inspected items that are known to be good:

$$\text{AOQ} = \frac{(N - n) \, p \, P_a}{N} \approx p \, P_a \quad \text{for large } N$$

---

### Step-by-Step Logic of AOQL

- The AOQ function $f(p) = p \cdot P_a(p)$ is a **continuous, bounded** function on [0, 1] (since $P_a(0) = 1$ and $P_a(1) \to 0$).
- The AOQ curve rises from 0, peaks at some **worst-case** value, then falls back toward 0 as $p \to 1$.
- The peak of this curve is the **AOQL**.

$$\text{AOQL} = \max_{p} \, p \, P_a(p)$$

### Step-by-Step Logic of ATI

- **Accepted lot** (occurs with probability $P_a$): requires inspection of *n* items only.
- **Rejected lot** (occurs with probability $1 - P_a$): requires inspection of all *N* items under rectification.

$$\text{ATI}(p) = n \cdot P_a(p) + N \cdot \bigl(1 - P_a(p)\bigr)$$

Equivalently:

$$\text{ATI}(p) = N - (N - n)(1 - P_a(p))$$

---

## 2.2 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Why AOQL and ATI Matter to Industry:**
> - **AOQL** is the foundation of **MIL-STD-105 / MIL-STD-414 / MIL-STD-1235** and the Dodge–Romig sampling tables. Quality managers select a rectifying plan whose AOQL does **not exceed** the customer's specified tolerance for defectives in delivered lots.
> - **ATI** is the **labour and cost driver** of inspection. It is used to estimate the **inspection budget**, the number of QA inspectors required, and the throughput capacity of the QA department.
> - **Double-sampling** ATI extensions (Curtis–Jorgensen form) allow comparison of **economic efficiency** between single, double, and multiple sampling plans.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Average Outgoing Quality (exact) | $\text{AOQ} = p \cdot P_a$ | Holds when replacement restores lot to size *N*. |
| 2 | AOQ (sub-sampling correction) | $\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{(N-n) \, p \, P_a}{N}$ | Used when the *n* inspected items are **not** replaced. |
| 3 | Average Outgoing Quality Limit | $\text{AOQL} = \max_p \, p \, P_a(p)$ | Worst-case AOQ; parameter of the sampling plan. |
| 4 | ATI (single sampling, rectifying) | $\text{ATI} = n P_a + N(1 - P_a)$ | Expected items inspected per lot. |
| 5 | ATI alternative form | $\text{ATI} = N - (N-n)(1 - P_a)$ | Useful for graphing. |
| 6 | ATI at $p = 0$ (perfect lots) | $\text{ATI} = n$ | Since $P_a(0) = 1$. |
| 7 | ATI at $p = 1$ (all defective) | $\text{ATI} = N$ | Since $P_a(1) \approx 0$. |
| 8 | Double-sampling ATI | $\text{ATI} = n_1 + n_2 \, P_{a2} + N(1 - P_{a1} - P_{a2})$ | $n_1$ first sample, $n_2$ second sample. |
| 9 | AOQ in fraction defective | $\text{AOQ} = p \, P_a$ | Multiplied by 100 for percentage. |
| 10 | Rectifying plan parameters | $(N, n, c)$ | *c* = acceptance number. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Pipe-Symbol Safeguard:** All absolute-value bars in the table above have been avoided to preserve Markdown rendering. When you write expressions like $|p - p_0|$ in your own notes, prefer `\vert p - p_0 \vert`.

---

## 2.4 Why $P_a(p)$ Is the Engine

Both AOQ and ATI are functions of *p* only because the plan's parameters $(N, n, c)$ are fixed. The probability of acceptance $P_a(p)$ depends on *p* through the **hypergeometric** (for finite *N*) or **binomial / Poisson** (for large *N*) distribution:

- **Binomial approximation:** $\displaystyle P_a = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} p^i (1-p)^{n-i}$
- **Poisson approximation:** $\displaystyle P_a = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^i}{i!}$ (used when *p* ≤ 0.10 and *np* ≤ 10).

The AOQL is then found by **differentiating** $f(p) = p P_a(p)$ w.r.t. *p*, setting $f'(p) = 0$, and solving for the critical $p^*$:

$$\frac{d}{dp} \bigl[ p P_a(p) \bigr] = P_a(p) + p \, P_a'(p) = 0$$

This gives the **worst-case incoming quality** $p^*$ at which AOQL is realized.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the AOQ Expression (Rectifying Single-Sampling Plan)

**Setup:** Lot size *N*, sample size *n*, acceptance number *c*, incoming fraction defective *p*.

**Step 1:** State the expected number of defectives in the accepted portion of the lot after rectification.

- If accepted, only *n* items are inspected. The remaining $(N-n)$ items are accepted as-is.
- The expected fraction defective in the un-inspected portion is still *p* (sampling is random).
- Expected defectives in the outgoing lot **given acceptance**:

$$\text{E[defectives} \mid \text{accepted]} = (N - n) \cdot p \approx N \cdot p \quad (\text{for } n \ll N)$$

**Step 2:** State the expected defectives in the outgoing lot **given rejection and rectification**.

$$\text{E[defectives} \mid \text{rejected and rectified]} = 0$$

(all defectives found in 100% inspection are replaced).

**Step 3:** Use the **law of total expectation** to obtain the unconditional expected defectives per outgoing lot:

$$E[\text{defectives per outgoing lot}] = (N-n) p \cdot P_a + 0 \cdot (1 - P_a) = (N-n) p \, P_a$$

**Step 4:** Divide by the lot size *N* to get the AOQ (defective fraction):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{AOQ} &= \frac{(N-n)\, p\, P_a}{N} \\
&= p \, P_a \cdot \left(1 - \frac{n}{N}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** For large *N* (the usual MIL-STD assumption with $n/N < 0.1$), $(1 - n/N) \approx 1$, so:

$$\boxed{\text{AOQ} \approx p \, P_a}$$

This is the expression plotted vs *p* to obtain the **AOQ curve**, whose maximum ordinate is the **AOQL**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the AOQL — Binomial Single-Sampling Case

Consider a plan $(n, c)$ with binomial $P_a(p) = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} p^i (1-p)^{n-i}$.

Define the **AOQ function**:

$$f(p) = p \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} p^i (1-p)^{n-i}$$

**Step 1:** Write out the first two terms explicitly (binomial expansion):

$$\begin{aligned}
P_a(p) &= \binom{n}{0}(1-p)^n + \binom{n}{1} p (1-p)^{n-1} + \binom{n}{2} p^2 (1-p)^{n-2} + \dots + \binom{n}{c} p^c (1-p)^{n-c}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by *p*:

$$f(p) = p(1-p)^n + n p^2 (1-p)^{n-1} + \binom{n}{2} p^3 (1-p)^{n-2} + \dots$$

**Step 3:** Take the derivative $f'(p)$ and set to zero. The critical-point equation:

$$P_a(p) + p \, \frac{dP_a}{dp} = 0$$

**Step 4:** For Poisson approximation $\lambda = np$:

$$P_a(p) = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^i}{i!}, \quad \lambda = np$$

The AOQ becomes:

$$f(p) = p \sum_{i=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np} (np)^i}{i!}$$

The critical-point equation simplifies (via Leibniz rule) to:

$$P_a(p) - \frac{np \, e^{-np} (np)^c}{c!} = 0$$

since the $(c+1)$-th Poisson term appears as the derivative contribution. This yields:

$$P_a(p^*) = \frac{(np^*)^{c+1} e^{-np^*}}{c!}$$

Solving this numerically (or via tables) gives $p^*$, and the AOQL is then:

$$\text{AOQL} = p^* \cdot P_a(p^*)$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Finding AOQL for $n = 50, c = 1$

**Step 1:** Use Poisson approximation with $\lambda = np$.

The probability of acceptance:

$$P_a(p) = e^{-np} (1 + np)$$

The AOQ function:

$$f(p) = p \cdot e^{-np}(1 + np)$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate and set to zero. Let $u = np$, then $f(p) = \frac{u}{n} e^{-u}(1+u)$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{du}\left[\frac{u}{n} e^{-u}(1+u)\right] &= 0 \\
\frac{1}{n}\left[e^{-u}(1+u) + u \cdot e^{-u} \cdot (-1)(1+u) + u \cdot e^{-u}\right] &= 0 \\
e^{-u}\left[(1+u) - u(1+u) + u\right] &= 0 \\
e^{-u}\left[1 + u - u - u^2 + u\right] &= 0 \\
e^{-u}\left[1 + u - u^2\right] &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Since $e^{-u} \neq 0$:

$$1 + u - u^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad u^2 - u - 1 = 0$$

**Step 3:** Solve the quadratic:

$$u = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1+4}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}$$

Taking the positive root:

$$u^* = \frac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p^* = \frac{u^*}{n} = \frac{1.618}{50} \approx 0.03236$$

**Step 4:** Compute $P_a(p^*)$ at $u^* = 1.618$:

$$P_a(p^*) = e^{-1.618}(1 + 1.618) = e^{-1.618} \cdot 2.618$$

Using $e^{-1.618} \approx 0.1985$:

$$P_a(p^*) \approx 0.1985 \times 2.618 \approx 0.5196$$

**Step 5:** Compute the AOQL:

$$\boxed{\text{AOQL} = p^* \cdot P_a(p^*) = 0.03236 \times 0.5196 \approx 0.01682}$$

So AOQL ≈ **1.682 %** for the plan $(n=50, c=1)$.

---

## 3.4 ATI Derivations: Single and Double Sampling

### Single Sampling ATI

Starting from first principles:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ATI}(p) &= n \cdot P_a + N \cdot (1 - P_a) \\
&= n P_a + N - N P_a \\
&= N - (N - n)(1 - P_a) \\
&= n + (N - n)(1 - P_a)
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification of limiting cases:**

- If $p = 0$ (all lots perfect): $P_a = 1$, so ATI = $n \cdot 1 + N \cdot 0 = n$ ✓
- If $p = 1$ (all lots defective): $P_a \to 0$, so ATI = $0 + N \cdot 1 = N$ ✓
- Maximum ATI: When $P_a$ is small (high *p*), nearly every lot goes to 100% screening, so ATI → *N*.

### Double Sampling ATI (Curtis Form)

For a double-sampling plan with first sample $n_1$, second sample $n_2$, and combined probability of acceptance:

$$P_a^{\text{total}} = P_{a1} + P_{a2}$$

Three possible outcomes:

| Outcome | Probability | Items Inspected |
|---|---|---|
| Accept on first sample | $P_{a1}$ | $n_1$ |
| Take second sample, then accept | $P_{a2}$ | $n_1 + n_2$ |
| Reject after second sample (rectify) | $1 - P_{a1} - P_{a2}$ | $n_1 + n_2$ (still only $n_1 + n_2$ items are individually inspected before the **entire** lot goes to 100%) |

Wait — under rectification, a **rejected** lot is 100% inspected. So:

| Outcome | Probability | Items Inspected |
|---|---|---|
| Accept on first sample | $P_{a1}$ | $n_1$ |
| Go to second sample | $1 - P_{a1}$ | $n_1 + n_2$ |

After the second sample, the lot is either **accepted** (only $n_1 + n_2$ items inspected) or **rejected and rectified** (entire *N* items inspected).

Therefore:

$$\text{ATI}_{\text{double}} = n_1 + n_2 (1 - P_{a1}) + N \cdot \bigl[1 - P_{a1} - P_{a2}\bigr]$$

which is the **Curtis form** used in Dodge–Romig tables.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Computing ATI

**Given:** $N = 1000, n = 50, c = 1$. Suppose incoming $p = 0.02$.

**Step 1:** Compute $P_a$ at $p = 0.02$ using Poisson with $\lambda = np = 1.0$:

$$P_a = e^{-1.0}(1 + 1.0) = e^{-1} \cdot 2 \approx 0.3679 \times 2 \approx 0.7358$$

**Step 2:** Compute ATI:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ATI} &= n \cdot P_a + N \cdot (1 - P_a) \\
&= 50 \times 0.7358 + 1000 \times (1 - 0.7358) \\
&= 36.79 + 1000 \times 0.2642 \\
&= 36.79 + 264.20 \\
&\approx \boxed{300.99 \text{ items per lot}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** On average, only ~301 items per 1000-item lot must be inspected when the process has 2 % defectives, saving ~70 % of inspection effort compared to 100 % screening.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Rectifying Inspection with AOQ/ATI Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Incoming Lot Size N with fraction defective p"] --> B["Draw Random Sample of Size n"]
    B --> C{"Inspect Sample: Compare Defects to c"}
    C -->|"Defects <= c"| D["Accept Lot: Ship N-n Items After Sample Replacement"]
    C -->|"Defects > c"| E["Reject Lot: Trigger Rectification"]
    E --> F["100 percent Inspection of All N Items"]
    F --> G["Replace All Defectives with Good Items"]
    G --> H["Ship Rectified Lot of Size N with 0 Defects"]
    D --> I["Compute P_a = P accept at this p"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["AOQ Module: AOQ = p * P_a"]
    I --> K["ATI Module: ATI = n P_a + N 1 - P_a"]
    J --> L["AOQ Curve: AOQ vs p Plot"]
    L --> M["Identify Peak: AOQL = max AOQ"]
    K --> N["ATI Curve: ATI vs p Plot"]
    N --> O["Inspection Budget Forecast"]
```

**Figure interpretation:** The diagram maps the rectifying inspection workflow. Each path emits its own contribution to the expected outcomes. The two output modules (AOQ, ATI) consume the same $P_a$ signal to produce their respective curves, from which the AOQL is extracted as the curve's maximum.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: AOQL Derivation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Step 1: Fix Plan Parameters n and c"] --> P2["Step 2: Express P_a of p via Binomial or Poisson"]
    P2 --> P3["Step 3: Form AOQ of p = p times P_a of p"]
    P3 --> P4["Step 4: Differentiate AOQ w.r.t. p and Solve"]
    P4 --> P5["Step 5: Obtain p Star Critical Point"]
    P5 --> P6["Step 6: Evaluate AOQL = p Star times P_a of p Star"]
    P6 --> P7["Step 7: Compare AOQL to Customer Specified Limit"]
    P7 --> P8{"AOQL less than or equal to Spec?"}
    P8 -->|"Yes"| P9["Plan Accepted for Use"]
    P8 -->|"No"| P10["Tighten Plan: Increase n or Reduce c"]
```

**Figure interpretation:** This pipeline shows the engineering decision chain. The AOQL must satisfy the contractual specification. If not, the sampling plan must be tightened (larger *n* or smaller *c*), which in turn shifts the AOQ curve's peak downward.

---

## 4.3 Comparison Schematic: ATI Behaviour at Extreme Incoming Qualities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Incoming Quality p"] --> R1["p approaches 0: Lots Nearly Perfect"]
    Q --> R2["p equals p Star: Worst Case for AOQ"]
    Q --> R3["p approaches 1: Lots Almost All Defective"]
    R1 --> S1["P_a approaches 1: Most Lots Accepted"]
    S1 --> T1["ATI approaches n: Minimal Inspection"]
    R2 --> S2["P_a moderate: Mix of Accept and Reject"]
    S2 --> T2["ATI intermediate: Partial Screening Load"]
    R3 --> S3["P_a approaches 0: Almost All Lots Rejected"]
    S3 --> T3["ATI approaches N: Full Rectification Load"]
    T1 --> U["ATI vs p Curve: U-shaped Saturation"]
    T2 --> U
    T3 --> U
```

**Figure interpretation:** The ATI curve is a **monotonically increasing** function of *p* that starts at *n* (when $P_a = 1$) and asymptotes to *N* (when $P_a = 0$). It is *not* peaked like the AOQ curve. This is a critical exam-level distinction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Conceptual
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q: Define Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL). Why is it significant in rectifying inspection plans?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)** is defined as the maximum value of the Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) over all possible values of incoming fraction defective *p*, for a given rectifying inspection sampling plan.

$$\text{AOQL} = \max_{0 \le p \le 1} \text{AOQ}(p) = \max_{p} p \cdot P_a(p)$$

**Significance (2 marks for definition + significance, 1 mark for example):**

- It provides a **guaranteed upper bound** on the long-run defective rate in the output stream, irrespective of incoming quality.
- It is the key parameter used in **Dodge–Romig sampling tables** to select rectifying plans matching the customer's AOQL specification.
- **Example:** A plan with AOQL = 1 % ensures that even in the worst case, no more than 1 % defectives will be present in the rectified outgoing product on average.

---

### Question A2 — Conceptual
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q: Define Average Total Inspection (ATI). How does it vary with the incoming fraction defective *p*?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Average Total Inspection (ATI)** is the expected number of items inspected per lot when a rectifying sampling plan is applied to a continuous stream of lots with incoming fraction defective *p*.

For a single-sampling rectifying plan:

$$\text{ATI}(p) = n \cdot P_a(p) + N \cdot \bigl[1 - P_a(p)\bigr]$$

**Variation with *p* (2 marks):**

- At $p = 0$ (perfect lots): $P_a = 1$, so ATI = *n* (only the sample is inspected).
- As *p* increases, $P_a$ decreases, so ATI **increases monotonically**.
- At $p = 1$ (all defective): $P_a \to 0$, so ATI → *N* (every lot goes to 100 % inspection).
- The ATI curve is therefore **monotonically increasing** from *n* to *N* — a shape fundamentally different from the AOQ curve.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — AOQL Computation Problem

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**A rectifying single-sampling plan with $N = 2000, n = 80, c = 2$ is to be applied to a continuous stream of lots with incoming fraction defective $p = 0.025$.**

**(a)** Compute the Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) and the Average Total Inspection (ATI) at $p = 0.025$ using the Poisson approximation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the AOQL of this plan by locating the worst-case $p^*$ and evaluating the peak AOQ. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution (a) — AOQ and ATI at $p = 0.025$**

**Step 1: Compute $\lambda = np$.**

$$\lambda = np = 80 \times 0.025 = 2.0$$

**Step 2: Compute $P_a$ via Poisson with $c = 2$.**

$$P_a(p) = \sum_{i=0}^{2} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^i}{i!} = e^{-2.0} \left[\frac{2^0}{0!} + \frac{2^1}{1!} + \frac{2^2}{2!}\right]$$

$$P_a = e^{-2.0} \left[1 + 2 + 2\right] = e^{-2.0} \times 5$$

Using $e^{-2.0} \approx 0.1353$:

$$P_a = 0.1353 \times 5 \approx 0.6767$$

**Step 3: Compute the AOQ.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{AOQ} &= p \cdot P_a \cdot \left(1 - \frac{n}{N}\right) \\
&= 0.025 \times 0.6767 \times \left(1 - \frac{80}{2000}\right) \\
&= 0.025 \times 0.6767 \times 0.96 \\
&= 0.01624 \\
&\approx 1.624\%
\end{aligned}$$

*(If the simpler approximation $\text{AOQ} \approx p P_a$ is used, then $\text{AOQ} \approx 1.692\%$.)*

**Step 4: Compute the ATI.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ATI} &= n \cdot P_a + N \cdot (1 - P_a) \\
&= 80 \times 0.6767 + 2000 \times (1 - 0.6767) \\
&= 54.14 + 2000 \times 0.3233 \\
&= 54.14 + 646.6 \\
&\approx 700.7 \text{ items per lot}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### **Solution (b) — Locating the AOQL**

**Step 1: General Poisson AOQ function.**

$$\text{AOQ}(p) = p \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{2} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^i}{i!}$$

Let $u = np$. Then:

$$f(u) = \frac{u}{n} \cdot e^{-u}\left[1 + u + \frac{u^2}{2}\right]$$

**Step 2: Differentiate $f(u)$ w.r.t. *u* and set to zero.**

Let $g(u) = u e^{-u}\left[1 + u + \frac{u^2}{2}\right] = e^{-u}\left[u + u^2 + \frac{u^3}{2}\right]$.

$$\frac{dg}{du} = e^{-u}\left[(1 + 2u + \tfrac{3u^2}{2}) - (u + u^2 + \tfrac{u^3}{2})\right]$$

Setting the bracket to zero:

$$1 + 2u + \tfrac{3u^2}{2} - u - u^2 - \tfrac{u^3}{2} = 0$$

$$1 + u + \tfrac{u^2}{2} - \tfrac{u^3}{2} = 0$$

Multiply by 2:

$$u^3 - u^2 - 2u - 2 = 0$$

**Step 3: Solve the cubic numerically.**

Test $u = 2$: $\;8 - 4 - 4 - 2 = -2 \neq 0$.

Test $u = 2.5$: $\;15.625 - 6.25 - 5 - 2 = 2.375 \neq 0$.

Test $u = 2.3$: $\;12.167 - 5.29 - 4.6 - 2 = 0.277 \neq 0$.

Test $u = 2.25$: $\;11.39 - 5.0625 - 4.5 - 2 = -0.17 \neq 0$.

So $u^* \approx 2.28$ (interpolating).

**Step 4: Compute $p^*$ and $P_a(p^*)$.**

$$p^* = \frac{u^*}{n} = \frac{2.28}{80} \approx 0.0285$$

$$P_a(p^*) = e^{-2.28}\left[1 + 2.28 + \frac{2.28^2}{2}\right] = e^{-2.28}[1 + 2.28 + 2.599]$$

$$P_a(p^*) = e^{-2.28} \times 5.879 \approx 0.1023 \times 5.879 \approx 0.6016$$

**Step 5: Compute the AOQL.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{AOQL} &= p^* \cdot P_a(p^*) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{n}{N}\right) \\
&= 0.0285 \times 0.6016 \times 0.96 \\
&\approx 0.01645 \\
&\approx 1.645\%
\end{aligned}$$

*(The simpler $p P_a$ approximation gives $\text{AOQL} \approx 0.0285 \times 0.6016 \approx 0.01714$ or 1.714 %.)*

**Conclusion:** The plan's worst-case outgoing defect rate is ≈ **1.65 %**. If the customer demands AOQL ≤ 1.5 %, this plan must be tightened.

---

### Question B — ATI Comparison and Curve Sketching Problem

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**A manufacturer uses a rectifying single-sampling plan with $N = 500, n = 30, c = 1$ on a production line.**

**(a)** Construct a table of $P_a$, AOQ, and ATI for $p = 0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.05, 0.08, 0.10$ using the Poisson approximation. Hence, identify the approximate AOQL. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A competing plan uses double sampling with $n_1 = 20, n_2 = 20, c_1 = 0, c_2 = 3$. Compute the ATI of the double-sampling plan at $p = 0.02$ and compare it to the single-sampling plan. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution (a) — AOQ / ATI Table for Single Sampling**

**Setup:** $N = 500, n = 30, c = 1$.

For each *p*, compute $\lambda = np = 30p$, then:

$$P_a = e^{-\lambda}(1 + \lambda), \quad \text{AOQ} = p \cdot P_a, \quad \text{ATI} = 30 P_a + 500(1 - P_a)$$

| *p* | $\lambda = 30p$ | $P_a = e^{-\lambda}(1+\lambda)$ | $\text{AOQ} = p P_a$ | ATI |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.01 | 0.30 | $e^{-0.30}(1.30) = 0.7408 \times 1.30 = 0.9630$ | $0.01 \times 0.9630 = 0.00963$ | $30(0.9630) + 500(0.037) = 28.89 + 18.50 = 47.4$ |
| 0.02 | 0.60 | $e^{-0.60}(1.60) = 0.5488 \times 1.60 = 0.8781$ | $0.02 \times 0.8781 = 0.01756$ | $30(0.8781) + 500(0.1219) = 26.34 + 60.95 = 87.3$ |
| 0.03 | 0.90 | $e^{-0.90}(1.90) = 0.4066 \times 1.90 = 0.7725$ | $0.03 \times 0.7725 = 0.02318$ | $30(0.7725) + 500(0.2275) = 23.18 + 113.75 = 136.9$ |
| 0.05 | 1.50 | $e^{-1.50}(2.50) = 0.2231 \times 2.50 = 0.5578$ | $0.05 \times 0.5578 = 0.02789$ | $30(0.5578) + 500(0.4422) = 16.73 + 221.10 = 237.8$ |
| 0.08 | 2.40 | $e^{-2.40}(3.40) = 0.0907 \times 3.40 = 0.3084$ | $0.08 \times 0.3084 = 0.02467$ | $30(0.3084) + 500(0.6916) = 9.25 + 345.80 = 355.1$ |
| 0.10 | 3.00 | $e^{-3.00}(4.00) = 0.0498 \times 4.00 = 0.1991$ | $0.10 \times 0.1991 = 0.01991$ | $30(0.1991) + 500(0.8009) = 5.97 + 400.45 = 406.4$ |

**Approximate AOQL identification:** The peak AOQ is **0.02789 ≈ 2.79 %** occurring near $p^* \approx 0.05$.

**Answer:** AOQL ≈ **2.79 %** at $p^* \approx 0.05$.

**Graphical sketch (qualitative):**
- **AOQ curve:** Rises from 0, peaks at $(p^* \approx 0.05, \; \text{AOQ} \approx 0.0279)$, then descends toward 0.
- **ATI curve:** Monotonically rises from ATI = 30 at $p = 0$ to ATI = 500 at $p = 1$. The curve has **no peak**; it is bounded above by *N*.

---

### **Solution (b) — Double-Sampling ATI at $p = 0.02$**

**Plan:** $n_1 = 20, c_1 = 0, n_2 = 20, c_2 = 3$ (combined acceptance after second sample: total ≤ 3).

**Step 1: Compute $P_{a1}$ (accept on first sample).**

With $n_1 = 20, c_1 = 0, p = 0.02$:

$$P_{a1} = (1 - p)^{n_1} = (0.98)^{20}$$

Using logarithm: $\ln(0.98) = -0.02020$, so $20 \ln(0.98) = -0.4040$:

$$P_{a1} = e^{-0.4040} \approx 0.6678$$

**Step 2: Compute $P_{r1}$ (probability of going to second sample).**

$$P_{r1} = 1 - P_{a1} = 1 - 0.6678 = 0.3322$$

**Step 3: Compute $P_{a2}$ (probability of accepting after second sample).**

Given that the first sample contained exactly $d = 1$ (since $c_1 = 0$, we reject if $d \ge 1$), the second sample of $n_2 = 20$ may contain up to $(c_2 - 1) = 2$ defectives for combined acceptance.

Wait — reinterpreting the plan: $c_1 = 0$ means **reject first sample if any defective is found**. After first sample rejection, take second sample of $n_2 = 20$ more items. Combined: accept lot if total defectives in $n_1 + n_2 = 40$ items is ≤ $c_2 = 3$.

**Step 3 (revised): Compute combined $P_a^{\text{total}}$ via binomial with $n = 40, c = 3$.**

$$P_a^{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=0}^{3} \binom{40}{i} (0.02)^i (0.98)^{40-i}$$

Compute each term:
- $i = 0$: $(0.98)^{40} = e^{40 \ln 0.98} = e^{-0.8080} \approx 0.4457$
- $i = 1$: $40 \times 0.02 \times (0.98)^{39} = 0.8 \times 0.4548 = 0.3638$
- $i = 2$: $\binom{40}{2}(0.02)^2(0.98)^{38} = 780 \times 0.0004 \times 0.4641 = 0.1448$
- $i = 3$: $\binom{40}{3}(0.02)^3(0.98)^{37} = 9880 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.4736 = 0.0374$

$$P_a^{\text{total}} = 0.4457 + 0.3638 + 0.1448 + 0.0374 \approx 0.9917$$

**Step 4: Compute $P_{a2} = P_a^{\text{total}} - P_{a1}$.**

$$P_{a2} = 0.9917 - 0.6678 = 0.3239$$

**Step 5: Compute the double-sampling ATI using Curtis form.**

$$\text{ATI}_{\text{double}} = n_1 + n_2 \, P_{r1} + N \, \bigl(1 - P_a^{\text{total}}\bigr)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ATI}_{\text{double}} &= 20 + 20 \times 0.3322 + 500 \times (1 - 0.9917) \\
&= 20 + 6.644 + 500 \times 0.0083 \\
&= 20 + 6.644 + 4.15 \\
&\approx 30.79 \text{ items per lot}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Compare with single-sampling plan from part (a) at $p = 0.02$.**

From the table: $\text{ATI}_{\text{single}} = 87.3$ items/lot.

$$\Delta \text{ATI} = 87.3 - 30.8 \approx 56.5 \text{ items/lot saved}$$

**Conclusion:** The double-sampling plan inspects **only ~31 items per lot** at $p = 0.02$, a **~65 % reduction** in inspection workload compared to the single-sampling plan. This is the primary economic advantage of double sampling under rectifying inspection.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing AOQL with AQL (Acceptable Quality Level).** AQL is a **producer's risk** parameter tied to the OC curve at a specific *p*. AOQL is the **maximum of the AOQ curve** over *all* *p*. They are conceptually distinct and answer different engineering questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the rectification factor.** The AOQ formula $p P_a$ assumes rejected lots are 100% inspected and defectives replaced. Without rectification, the concept of AOQ/AOQL does not apply in the same way.
> 3. **Using the wrong formula for ATI in double sampling.** Many students write $\text{ATI} = n_1 + n_2 P_{r1} + N(1 - P_a)$ but forget the **Curtis correction** that the second-sample path itself requires $n_1 + n_2$ items to be inspected *before* the rectification decision. Always use the **Curtis form**: $\text{ATI} = n_1 + n_2 P_{r1} + N(1 - P_{a1} - P_{a2})$.
> 4. **Misreading $P_a$ values.** In tables, $P_a$ is always the **acceptance probability**, not the rejection probability. Double-check whether you need $P_a$, $1 - P_a$, or $P_{r1}$.
> 5. **AOQ units confusion.** AOQ is a **fraction defective** (between 0 and 1). AOQ × 100 gives percentage. Express the final answer with correct units.
> 6. **ATI vs ASN confusion.** **ASN (Average Sample Number)** is the average number of items inspected **in the sampling process only** (no rectification). **ATI** includes the cost of rectifying rejected lots. They are equal only when no lot is ever rejected.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — AOQL & ATI**
> - [x] **AOQ** $= p \cdot P_a$ (under rectifying inspection, large *N*).
> - [x] **AOQ (exact)** $= (N - n)p P_a / N$ when inspected sample items are not replaced.
> - [x] **AOQL** is the **peak** of the AOQ curve; obtain by differentiating $p P_a(p)$ and solving for $p^*$.
> - [x] For binomial/Poisson models, the critical-point equation involves the $(c+1)$-th term of $P_a$.
> - [x] **ATI (single)** $= n P_a + N(1 - P_a) = n + (N-n)(1 - P_a)$.
> - [x] ATI **monotonically increases** with *p* (in contrast to AOQ's bell shape).
> - [x] ATI = *n* when $p = 0$; ATI = *N* when $p \to 1$.
> - [x] **ATI (double, Curtis form)** $= n_1 + n_2 P_{r1} + N(1 - P_{a1} - P_{a2})$.
> - [x] Double sampling reduces ATI (inspection cost) but does **not** reduce AOQL — both plans can be tuned to the same AOQL.
> - [x] **Dodge–Romig tables** select rectifying plans that satisfy a target **AOQL** while minimizing ATI (or AOQL for given ATI).
> - [x] **Poisson approximation** to binomial is valid when $p \le 0.10$ and $np \le 10$.
> - [x] **Hypergeometric** is used only when $n/N > 0.10$; in industrial practice, binomial/Poisson suffices.
> - [x] **Customer's contract** typically specifies AOQL; the QA department picks a plan whose AOQ curve never exceeds this ceiling.
> - [x] **MIL-STD links**: MIL-STD-105 / ANSI Z1.4 use AQL; **Dodge–Romig** uses AOQL for rectifying inspection.
> - [x] **Engineering trade-off**: Tightening the plan (↑ *n* or ↓ *c*) **lowers AOQL** but **raises ATI** at every *p*.
> - [x] Always quote the **worst-case incoming quality** $p^*$ along with the AOQL value when answering descriptive questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Sampling Plans – MIL-STD 105E Sampling Method and Its Equivalents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Standard Sampling Plans – MIL-STD 105E Sampling Method and Its Equivalents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MIL-STD 105E** (Military Standard 105E) is a **standardized acceptance sampling plan** developed by the U.S. Department of Defense in **1963** (revised version E) for inspecting lot-by-lot acceptance of discrete products. It provides a statistically valid framework for switching between **Normal**, **Tightened**, and **Reduced** inspection based on the lot's quality history, using a pre-calculated set of **Acceptance Numbers (Ac)** and **Rejection Numbers (Re)** derived from the **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level)** and **Sample Size Code Letter**.

The standard was designed to replace judgment-based inspection with a uniform, statistically defensible procedure used widely across military procurement, manufacturing, and supply chain operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalents of MIL-STD 105E** around the world:
> - **United States:** ANSI/ASQ Z1.4 (current commercial version)
> - **International:** ISO 2859-1
> - **United Kingdom:** BS 6001
> - **India:** IS 2500 (Part I)
> - **Canada:** CSA Z234.4

All of these are **functionally identical** — they use the same AQL tables, sample size code letters, and switching rules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of MIL-STD 105E like a "Traffic Light System" for Quality Inspection.**
> 
> Imagine you are a customs officer inspecting shipments at a port:
> - If a **supplier (producer)** has been consistently sending good cargo, you don't waste time opening every container (this is **Reduced Inspection** — the green light).
> - If the cargo quality is normal, you inspect a small-to-moderate sample (this is **Normal Inspection** — the yellow light).
> - If the supplier recently sent bad cargo, you tighten checks drastically (this is **Tightened Inspection** — the red light).
> 
> The "switching rules" are exactly like automatic traffic lights that change based on the supplier's recent track record. The **AQL** is the "acceptable dirtiness" — a tolerable level of bad items you will allow in the shipment.

## 1.3 Why MIL-STD 105E Matters in Industry

- **Producer's Risk (α) = 0.05**: A producer of good lots (at AQL) has only a 5% chance of unfair rejection.
- **Consumer's Risk (β) = 0.10**: A consumer has only a 10% chance of accepting a poor lot (at LTPD).
- These fixed risk values are the **statistical backbone** that make the standard trustworthy in contractual and regulatory settings.
- The **Producer's Point** corresponds to a probability of acceptance **Pa = 0.90** (1 − α) at the AQL.
- The **Consumer's Point** corresponds to a probability of acceptance **Pa = 0.10** (β) at the LTPD.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** MIL-STD 105E is fundamentally an **AQL-based system**, not an LTPD-based system. It does NOT have a fixed consumer's risk across all plans; the LTPD varies depending on the chosen sample size.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Parameters of MIL-STD 105E

### 2.1.1 Acceptable Quality Level (AQL)
- The **maximum percentage of defective items** (or defects per hundred units) that, for purposes of acceptance sampling, can be considered satisfactory as a process average.
- AQL is the **quality level** that the producer aims to maintain and the consumer is willing to accept.
- Common AQL values: **0.065, 0.10, 0.15, 0.25, 0.40, 0.65, 1.0, 1.5, 2.5, 4.0, 6.5**
- Expressed as a **percent defective** or **defects per 100 units (per 100)**.

### 2.1.2 Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD)
- The **poorest level of quality** that the consumer is willing to accept in an individual lot.
- Sometimes called the **"Rejectable Quality Level (RQL)"** in older terminology.
- Corresponds to **β = 0.10** (consumer's risk) under the standard.
- **Operating Ratio (OR):** OR = LTPD / AQL (a measure of the discrimination of the plan).

### 2.1.3 Inspection Levels
The standard defines **seven inspection levels** that determine the sample size for a given lot size:

> [!NOTE]
> **General Inspection Levels:**
> - **Level I:** Reduced sample size (used when less discrimination is acceptable)
> - **Level II:** Normal sample size (default for most inspections)
> - **Level III:** Increased sample size (used for greater discrimination or costly destructive tests)
> 
> **Special Inspection Levels:**
> - **S-1, S-2, S-3, S-4:** Used for **expensive destructive tests** or when sample size must be very small (e.g., aerospace components, life-safety devices).

## 2.2 Sample Size Code Letters (A through R)

The lot size is mapped to a **Code Letter** (A, B, C, ..., R) using the standard's Table I. Each code letter corresponds to a specific sample size for a given inspection level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code Letter → Sample Size Mapping (for Normal Inspection, Level II):**
> - A → 2, B → 3, C → 5, D → 8, E → 13, F → 20, G → 32, H → 50
> - J → 80, K → 125, L → 200, M → 315, N → 500, P → 800, Q → 1250, R → 2000

**Procedure to find Code Letter:**
1. Determine **Lot Size** (N).
2. Select **Inspection Level** (default: Level II).
3. Look up the corresponding **Code Letter** in Table I.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the complete high-yield formula sheet for MIL-STD 105E calculations.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Producer's Risk (α) | **α = 1 − Pa at AQL** | α = **0.05** in MIL-STD 105E |
| 2 | Consumer's Risk (β) | **β = Pa at LTPD** | β = **0.10** in MIL-STD 105E |
| 3 | Operating Ratio | **OR = LTPD ÷ AQL** | Dimensionless ratio; smaller = sharper plan |
| 4 | Producer's Point | Pa(at AQL) = 0.90 | 90% acceptance at AQL |
| 5 | Consumer's Point | Pa(at LTPD) = 0.10 | 10% acceptance at LTPD |
| 6 | Operating Characteristic (Binomial) | Pa(p) = Σ [C(n,i) · p^i · (1−p)^(n−i)] | n = sample size, p = incoming quality |
| 7 | Operating Characteristic (Poisson) | Pa(p) = Σ [e^(−np) · (np)^i ÷ i!] | Used when n large, p small |
| 8 | Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) | AOQ = [Pa(p) · p · (N − n)] ÷ N | N = lot size, n = sample size |
| 9 | Average Outgoing Quality Limit | AOQL = max(AOQ) over all p | Worst-case AOQ |
| 10 | Average Sample Number (ASN) | ASN = (n₁·P₁) + (n₂·P₂) + ... | Sum across multi-stage plans |
| 11 | Rectification | Defectives replaced with good | Reduces outgoing defectives |
| 12 | Probability of Acceptance (Hypergeometric approx) | Pa ≈ C(D, Ac)·C(N−D, n−Ac) ÷ C(N, n) | For finite lot (exact) |

## 2.4 Switching Rules Between Inspection Severities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Traffic Light" Decision System:**
> 
> 1. **Start at NORMAL Inspection** (default).
> 2. **NORMAL → TIGHTENED:** When **2 out of any 5 consecutive lots** have been rejected on Original Inspection (i.e., while on Normal). Tightening shifts Ac downward (stricter acceptance).
> 3. **TIGHTENED → NORMAL:** When **5 consecutive lots** have been accepted on Tightened Inspection.
> 4. **NORMAL → REDUCED:** When **10 consecutive lots** have been accepted on Normal Inspection (and other criteria met). Reduced inspection increases Ac upward.
> 5. **REDUCED → NORMAL:** When any of the following occur on Reduced Inspection:
>    - A lot is rejected
>    - 2 or more lots are not submitted for inspection
>    - Production becomes irregular/delayed
>    - Other conditions warranting return
> 6. **TIGHTENED → DISCONTINUE:** When **10 consecutive lots** remain on Tightened Inspection (inspection is suspended pending quality improvement).

## 2.5 Types of Sampling Plans in MIL-STD 105E

> [!NOTE]
> The standard provides **three forms** of sampling plans at each severity:
> 1. **Single Sampling Plan:** One sample of size n is drawn; lot accepted if defects ≤ Ac, rejected if defects > Ac.
> 2. **Double Sampling Plan:** First sample n₁ drawn; if decisive (accept or reject), stop. Otherwise draw second sample n₂. Combined decisions determine fate.
> 3. **Multiple Sampling Plan:** Successive samples (up to 7 stages) taken until a decision can be made.

All three plans are **matched** so that they have approximately the same OC curve for a given AQL and code letter.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Incoming inspection of bolts, sensors, and electronic components.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Batch testing of tablets, vials, and packaging integrity.
- **Aerospace:** First-article and lot inspection of turbine blades, fasteners, and electronic modules.
- **Electronics manufacturing:** PCB assembly, semiconductor wafer acceptance.
- **Textile industry:** Fabric defect inspection, color fastness verification.
- **Defense procurement:** Mandatory in many countries for military contracts.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve for Single Sampling

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a single sampling plan with:
- **n** = sample size
- **Ac** = acceptance number
- **N** = lot size
- **D** = number of defectives in the lot
- **p** = fraction defective in the lot (p = D/N)

The lot is **accepted** if the number of defectives in the sample, **d**, satisfies:

$$d \leq Ac$$

### 3.1.2 Hypergeometric Distribution (Exact)

When the lot is finite and sampling is **without replacement**, the number of defectives in the sample follows a **hypergeometric distribution**:

$$P(d) = \frac{\binom{D}{d} \binom{N-D}{n-d}}{\binom{N}{n}}$$

The **probability of acceptance** is:

$$P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{Ac} \frac{\binom{D}{d} \binom{N-D}{n-d}}{\binom{N}{n}}$$

### 3.1.3 Binomial Approximation

When **N >> n** (lot size much greater than sample size, typically N ≥ 10n), the hypergeometric can be approximated by the **binomial distribution**:

$$P(d) = \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$$

The probability of acceptance becomes:

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{Ac} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

### 3.1.4 Poisson Approximation

When **n ≥ 20** and **p ≤ 0.10**, the binomial can be further approximated by the **Poisson distribution** with mean **np**:

$$P(d) = \frac{e^{-np} (np)^d}{d!}$$

The probability of acceptance is:

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{Ac} \frac{e^{-np} (np)^d}{d!}
$$

### 3.1.5 Worked Example: Constructing an OC Curve

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** For a single sampling plan with n = 50, Ac = 1, compute Pa at p = 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10 using the Poisson approximation.

**Step 1:** Compute np for each value of p.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } p = 0.01: \quad np &= 50 \times 0.01 = 0.5 \\
\text{At } p = 0.02: \quad np &= 50 \times 0.02 = 1.0 \\
\text{At } p = 0.05: \quad np &= 50 \times 0.05 = 2.5 \\
\text{At } p = 0.10: \quad np &= 50 \times 0.10 = 5.0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Poisson formula for Ac = 1.

$$
P_a = P(d=0) + P(d=1) = e^{-np} + e^{-np}(np) = e^{-np}(1 + np)
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate at each p.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } p = 0.01: \quad P_a &= e^{-0.5}(1 + 0.5) = 0.6065 \times 1.5 = 0.9098 \\
\text{At } p = 0.02: \quad P_a &= e^{-1.0}(1 + 1.0) = 0.3679 \times 2.0 = 0.7358 \\
\text{At } p = 0.05: \quad P_a &= e^{-2.5}(1 + 2.5) = 0.0821 \times 3.5 = 0.2873 \\
\text{At } p = 0.10: \quad P_a &= e^{-5.0}(1 + 5.0) = 0.00674 \times 6.0 = 0.0404 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Plot these points to get the OC curve.

| Incoming Quality (p) | np | Pa (Probability of Acceptance) |
|---------------------|-----|-------------------------------|
| 0.01 | 0.5 | **0.9098** |
| 0.02 | 1.0 | **0.7358** |
| 0.05 | 2.5 | **0.2873** |
| 0.10 | 5.0 | **0.0404** |

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** If the lot quality is at p = 0.01 (1% defective), the plan accepts the lot 91% of the time. If quality worsens to p = 0.10 (10% defective), the plan accepts only 4% of the time. The "ideal" OC curve would be a step function jumping from 1 to 0 at the LTPD.

## 3.2 Derivation of Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)

### 3.2.1 Setup

After acceptance sampling, defectives found in the sample are typically **replaced with good items** (rectification). The AOQ is the **long-run average quality** of lots **after** inspection.

### 3.2.2 Formula Derivation

**Step 1:** If the lot is accepted (probability Pa), the expected number of defectives **remaining in the lot** is:

$$
\text{Defectives in accepted lot} = (D - d) \approx p(N - n)
$$

where d is the number of defectives in the sample (replaced with good items).

**Step 2:** The expected fraction defective in the accepted lot, given acceptance, is:

$$
p_{\text{out,acc}} = \frac{p(N-n)}{N}
$$

**Step 3:** If the lot is **rejected** (probability 1 − Pa), it is **100% inspected**, and all defectives are replaced. Thus, the outgoing fraction defective is **0** (assuming 100% screening).

**Step 4:** The overall AOQ is the weighted average:

$$
\text{AOQ} = P_a \cdot \frac{p(N-n)}{N} + (1 - P_a) \cdot 0
$$

This simplifies to:

$$
\boxed{\text{AOQ} = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}}
$$

### 3.2.3 Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)

The **AOQL** is the **maximum** value of AOQ over all possible values of incoming quality p. It represents the **worst-case** average outgoing quality.

> [!NOTE]
> The AOQL is a property of the plan and lot size; it can be found by plotting AOQ vs. p and identifying the peak. MIL-STD 105E provides pre-tabulated AOQL values for each plan.

### 3.2.4 Worked Example: Computing AOQ

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Lot size N = 1000, sample size n = 80, Ac = 2. If incoming quality p = 0.03, find the AOQ (assume Pa at p = 0.03 is 0.677 from the Poisson table).

**Step 1:** Apply the AOQ formula:

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{0.677 \times 0.03 \times (1000 - 80)}{1000}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the parentheses:

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{0.677 \times 0.03 \times 920}{1000}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the numerator:

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{0.677 \times 27.6}{1000} = \frac{18.685}{1000} = 0.01869
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to percent (multiply by 100):

$$
\boxed{\text{AOQ} = 1.869\%}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** Even though incoming quality is 3% defective, the inspection-and-rectification process brings the **average outgoing quality down to 1.87%** — a significant quality improvement from the buyer's perspective.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Average Sample Number (ASN) for Double Sampling

### 3.3.1 Setup

A **double sampling plan** involves:
- **First sample** of size n₁, with acceptance number Ac₁ and rejection number Re₁.
- **Second sample** of size n₂ (taken only if the first sample is inconclusive), with combined acceptance number Ac₂.

### 3.3.2 Decision Outcomes

| First Sample Outcome | Action |
|---------------------|--------|
| d₁ ≤ Ac₁ | **Accept** the lot (stop). |
| d₁ ≥ Re₁ | **Reject** the lot (stop). |
| Ac₁ < d₁ < Re₁ | **Draw second sample** of size n₂. |

### 3.3.3 ASN Formula

The probability of going to the second sample is:

$$
P_{\text{2nd}} = \sum_{d_1 = Ac_1 + 1}^{Re_1 - 1} \binom{n_1}{d_1} p^{d_1} (1-p)^{n_1 - d_1}
$$

The Average Sample Number is:

$$
\boxed{\text{ASN} = n_1 + n_2 \cdot P_{\text{2nd}}}
$$

### 3.3.4 Worked Example: Computing ASN

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Double sampling plan with n₁ = 50, n₂ = 50, Ac₁ = 1, Re₁ = 4, Ac₂ = 4. At p = 0.02, find the ASN.

**Step 1:** Compute np₁ = 50 × 0.02 = 1.0

**Step 2:** Compute the probability of inconclusive first sample (d₁ = 2 or 3):

$$
P(d_1 = 2) = \frac{e^{-1.0}(1.0)^2}{2!} = \frac{0.3679}{2} = 0.1839
$$

$$
P(d_1 = 3) = \frac{e^{-1.0}(1.0)^3}{3!} = \frac{0.3679}{6} = 0.0613
$$

**Step 3:** Sum them:

$$
P_{\text{2nd}} = 0.1839 + 0.0613 = 0.2452
$$

**Step 4:** Compute ASN:

$$
\text{ASN} = 50 + 50 \times 0.2452 = 50 + 12.26 = 62.26
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ASN} \approx 62.3 \text{ units}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** The double sampling plan inspects an **average of 62.3 units** instead of a fixed 100 — this is the operational saving when incoming quality is good.

## 3.4 Procedure for Implementing MIL-STD 105E (Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Implementation Workflow:**
> 
> 1. **Step 1 — Define Lot:** Specify lot size N (group of products submitted for inspection).
> 2. **Step 2 — Choose Inspection Level:** Default is **Level II**; choose S-1 to S-4 for destructive tests, Level I or III for other needs.
> 3. **Step 3 — Find Code Letter:** Use Table I (Sample Size Code Letters) to map N + Level → Code Letter.
> 4. **Step 4 — Select AQL:** From contract, specification, or supplier agreement.
> 5. **Step 5 — Choose Plan Type:** Single, Double, or Multiple (all are matched — equivalent OC).
> 6. **Step 6 — Read Ac/Re:** From Table II-A (Normal), II-B (Tightened), or II-C (Reduced) for the given code letter and AQL.
> 7. **Step 7 — Draw Sample:** Randomly select n units.
> 8. **Step 8 — Inspect:** Count defectives d.
> 9. **Step 9 — Decide:**
>    - If d ≤ Ac → **Accept the lot**.
>    - If d > Ac → **Reject the lot**.
> 10. **Step 10 — Apply Switching Rules:** Update the running tally of accepted/rejected lots and shift between Normal/Tightened/Reduced as per the rules.

## 3.5 Comparative Analysis: AQL vs. LTPD-Based Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MIL-STD 105E is an AQL system and not an LTPD system:**

| Feature | AQL System (MIL-STD 105E) | LTPD System (e.g., Dodge-Romig) |
|---------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Primary Protection | **Producer** (α = 0.05 fixed) | **Consumer** (β = 0.10 fixed) |
| LTPD | Varies by plan | Fixed by user specification |
| Sample Size | Determined by AQL and code letter | Determined by LTPD and β |
| Use Case | Continuous production, supplier monitoring | Critical components, isolated lots |
| Switching Rules | Yes (Normal/Tightened/Reduced) | No |
| OC Curve | Hits producer's point at AQL | Hits consumer's point at LTPD |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MIL-STD 105E Decision Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete decision workflow of MIL-STD 105E, from lot submission through inspection and switching rule evaluation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Lot Arrives at Inspection]) --> B[Determine Lot Size N]
    B --> C[Select Inspection Level]
    C --> D{Special Level?}
    D -- Yes --> E[S-1 to S-4]
    D -- No --> F[Level I / II / III]
    E --> G[Lookup Sample Size Code Letter]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Determine AQL from Specification]
    H --> I{Choose Plan Type}
    I -- Single --> J1[Single Sampling Plan]
    I -- Double --> J2[Double Sampling Plan]
    I -- Multiple --> J3[Multiple Sampling Plan]
    J1 --> K[Read Ac and Re from Table]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    K --> L[Draw Random Sample of Size n]
    L --> M[Inspect and Count Defectives d]
    M --> N{d Ac?}
    N -- Yes --> O([Accept the Lot])
    N -- No --> P([Reject the Lot])
    O --> Q[Update Acceptance Tally]
    P --> R[Update Rejection Tally]
    Q --> S{Current Severity?}
    R --> S
    S -- Normal --> T{2 of last 5 rejected?}
    T -- Yes --> U[Switch to TIGHTENED]
    T -- No --> V{10 consecutive accepted?}
    V -- Yes --> W[Switch to REDUCED]
    V -- No --> X[Stay on NORMAL]
    S -- Tightened --> Y{5 consecutive accepted?}
    Y -- Yes --> Z[Return to NORMAL]
    Y -- No --> AA{10 lots on tightened?}
    AA -- Yes --> AB[DISCONTINUE Inspection]
    AA -- No --> AC[Stay on TIGHTENED]
    S -- Reduced --> AD{Lot rejected?}
    AD -- Yes --> AE[Return to NORMAL]
    AD -- No --> AF[Stay on REDUCED]
    U --> AG([Proceed to Next Lot])
    W --> AG
    X --> AG
    Z --> AG
    AC --> AG
    AE --> AG
    AF --> AG
    AB --> AH([Quality Improvement Required])
```

## 4.2 Architecture of an OC Curve in MIL-STD 105E (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Pa 1.0<br>100%] --> A1[Pa 0.90<br>at AQL<br>Producer Point]
    A1 --> A2[OC Curve<br>Descent Region]
    A2 --> A3[Pa 0.50<br>Median Quality]
    A3 --> A4[Pa 0.10<br>at LTPD<br>Consumer Point]
    A4 --> A5[Pa 0.0<br>Beyond LTPD]
    
    B0[Lot Quality p, percent defective] --> B1[p = 0 = AQL_best]
    B1 --> B2[p = AQL]
    B2 --> B3[p increases]
    B3 --> B4[p = LTPD]
    B4 --> B5[p large]
    
    A0 --- B0
    A1 --- B1
    A2 --- B2
    A3 --- B3
    A4 --- B4
    A5 --- B5
```

## 4.3 Sampling Plan Type Comparison (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Lot to be Inspected]) --> Type{Choose Plan Type}
    
    Type -- Single --> S1[Draw n₁ units]
    S1 --> S2{Count d₁}
    S2 -- d1 <= Ac --> SA([Accept])
    S2 -- d1 > Ac --> SR([Reject])
    
    Type -- Double --> D1[Draw n₁ units]
    D1 --> D2{Count d₁}
    D2 -- d1 <= Ac1 --> DA([Accept])
    D2 -- d1 >= Re1 --> DR([Reject])
    D2 -- Ac1 < d1 < Re1 --> D3[Draw n₂ more units]
    D3 --> D4{Total d1 + d2}
    D4 -- d1d2 <= Ac2 --> DA2([Accept])
    D4 -- d1d2 > Ac2 --> DR2([Reject])
    
    Type -- Multiple --> M1[Stage 1: Draw n₁]
    M1 --> M2{Decisive?}
    M2 -- Accept --> MA([Accept])
    M2 -- Reject --> MR([Reject])
    M2 -- Continue --> M3[Stage 2: Draw n₂]
    M3 --> M4{Decisive?}
    M4 -- Accept --> MA
    M4 -- Reject --> MR
    M4 -- Continue --> M5[...]
    M5 --> M6[Stage k: Final Decision]
    M6 --> MA
    M6 --> MR
```

## 4.4 Equivalence Mapping of International Standards

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MIL[MIL-STD 105E<br>USA Military 1963] --> ISO[ISO 2859-1<br>International]
    MIL --> ANSI[ANSI/ASQ Z1.4<br>USA Commercial]
    MIL --> BS[BS 6001<br>United Kingdom]
    MIL --> IS[IS 2500<br>India]
    MIL --> CSA[CSA Z234.4<br>Canada]
    MIL --> JIS[JIS Z 9015<br>Japan]
    MIL --> AS[AS 1199<br>Australia]
    
    ISO --- Shared[(Common AQL Tables<br>Common Code Letters<br>Common Switching Rules)]
    ANSI --- Shared
    BS --- Shared
    IS --- Shared
    CSA --- Shared
    JIS --- Shared
    AS --- Shared
```

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Aid:** All standards under "2859-family" are **statistical twins** of MIL-STD 105E. A given (code letter, AQL) tuple gives the same Ac and Re across all of them.
