# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**Define MIL-STD 105E. What are its international equivalents?**

**Model Answer:**
MIL-STD 105E is a **standard acceptance sampling procedure** developed by the U.S. Department of Defense in 1963 for lot-by-lot inspection of discrete products, using AQL-based criteria with switching rules between Normal, Tightened, and Reduced inspection severities.

**International Equivalents:**
- **ISO 2859-1** (International Organization for Standardization)
- **ANSI/ASQ Z1.4** (United States)
- **BS 6001** (United Kingdom)
- **IS 2500** (India)
- **JIS Z 9015** (Japan)
- **CSA Z234.4** (Canada)
- **AS 1199** (Australia)

All these standards are **functionally identical** — same AQL tables, same code letters, same switching rules.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**What is AQL? What is the producer's risk (α) associated with it in MIL-STD 105E?**

**Model Answer:**
**AQL (Acceptable Quality Level)** is the maximum percentage of defective items in a lot that is considered **tolerable** as a process average. It is the quality level at which the **producer aims to maintain** the manufacturing process and which the **consumer finds acceptable** in incoming lots.

In MIL-STD 105E, the **Producer's Risk (α) = 0.05**. This means that if a producer submits lots at the AQL quality level, the probability of these lots being rejected is only **5%** (i.e., the probability of acceptance is **Pa = 0.90** at the AQL).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Part B questions are typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Choose either Q-A or Q-B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A manufacturer uses MIL-STD 105E for inspection of electrical switches. Lot size is **N = 1200**, Inspection Level is **II**, AQL = **1.0%**, Normal Inspection, **Single Sampling Plan**. Determine the sample size and acceptance number.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Find Code Letter:**
From MIL-STD 105E Table I, for lot size 1201–3200 at Level II, the code letter is **"K"**.

**Step 2 — Find Sample Size:**
From Table I, Code Letter K corresponds to sample size **n = 125**.

**Step 3 — Find Acceptance Number:**
From Table II-A (Normal Inspection, Single Sampling), intersecting Code Letter K with AQL column 1.0 (in percent defective), we obtain:
- **Ac = 3** (acceptance number)
- **Re = 4** (rejection number)

**Step 4 — Decision Rule:**
- Draw a random sample of 125 switches.
- Count defectives d.
- **Accept** the lot if d ≤ 3.
- **Reject** the lot if d ≥ 4.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the Poisson approximation, compute the probability of acceptance at p = 0.02 for the plan in part (a). Comment on the result.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute np:**

$$
np = n \times p = 125 \times 0.02 = 2.5
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Poisson formula for Ac = 3:**

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{3} \frac{e^{-2.5} (2.5)^d}{d!}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(d=0) &= e^{-2.5} = 0.0821 \\
P(d=1) &= e^{-2.5} \times 2.5 = 0.0821 \times 2.5 = 0.2052 \\
P(d=2) &= e^{-2.5} \times (2.5)^2 \div 2! = 0.2052 \times 2.5 \div 2 = 0.2565 \\
P(d=3) &= e^{-2.5} \times (2.5)^3 \div 3! = 0.2565 \times 2.5 \div 3 = 0.2138 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum them:**

$$
P_a = 0.0821 + 0.2052 + 0.2565 + 0.2138 = 0.7576
$$

$$
\boxed{P_a \approx 0.758}
$$

**Comment:** At an incoming quality of 2% defective (twice the AQL of 1%), the plan still accepts the lot about 75.8% of the time. This shows the plan is **lenient towards the producer** at this quality level. The plan's main protection lies in the **switching rule** — repeated lots at 2% would trigger **Tightened Inspection**, which would make acceptance much stricter.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **switching rules** between Normal, Tightened, and Reduced inspection in MIL-STD 105E. When is inspection discontinued?

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Switching Rules in MIL-STD 105E:**

**1. Normal → Tightened:**
Triggered when **2 out of any 5 consecutive lots** have been rejected **on Original Inspection** (i.e., while the lots were on Normal severity). The Ac values are reduced (made stricter).

**2. Tightened → Normal:**
Triggered when **5 consecutive lots** have been accepted on Tightened Inspection. Inspection returns to the standard Normal severity.

**3. Normal → Reduced:**
Triggered when **all of the following** are satisfied:
- **10 consecutive lots** have been accepted on Normal Inspection.
- The number of lots rejected on Original Inspection in the recent history is below a tabulated threshold.
- Production is at a steady rate.
- Reduced inspection is considered appropriate by the responsible authority.
- The Ac values are increased (made more lenient).

**4. Reduced → Normal:**
Triggered when **any one** of the following occurs on Reduced Inspection:
- A lot is rejected.
- 2 or more lots are not submitted for inspection.
- Production becomes irregular or delayed.
- Other conditions warranting return to Normal.

**5. Tightened → Discontinue Inspection:**
Triggered when **10 consecutive lots** remain on Tightened Inspection without return to Normal. **Inspection is suspended** pending quality improvement action by the supplier. Resumption of acceptance inspection requires special authorization.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For lot size N = 800, Inspection Level I, AQL = 0.65%, Normal Inspection, Single Sampling: determine the sample size, Ac, and the AOQ at p = 0.01. Use Pa = 0.833 (given from OC table).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Find Code Letter:**
For lot size 501–1200 at Level I, the code letter is **"G"** (from Table I).

**Step 2 — Find Sample Size:**
Code Letter G at Level I: **n = 20** (note: smaller than Level II which would be 32).

**Step 3 — Find Ac:**
From Table II-A, Code Letter G, AQL = 0.65% (percent defective column): **Ac = 1, Re = 2**.

**Step 4 — Compute AOQ at p = 0.01 with Pa = 0.833:**

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute values:**

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{0.833 \times 0.01 \times (800 - 20)}{800}
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify:**

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{0.833 \times 0.01 \times 780}{800}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute numerator:**

$$
\text{AOQ} = \frac{0.833 \times 7.8}{800} = \frac{6.497}{800} = 0.008122
$$

**Step 8 — Convert to percent:**

$$
\boxed{\text{AOQ} = 0.812\%}
$$

**Comment:** The incoming defect rate is 1%, but the inspection-and-rectification process brings the **average outgoing defect rate down to 0.81%** — a 19% improvement in quality at the buyer's end.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in MIL-STD 105E Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing AQL column headers:** Some AQLs are tabulated under **"% Defective"** and others under **"Defects per 100 Units"**. Using the wrong column gives a wrong Ac. Always verify the column type.
> 
> 2. **Wrong Code Letter selection:** Forgetting to use the correct **Inspection Level** (I, II, III, S-1 to S-4) leads to the wrong code letter. Level II is the default — students often use it without stating it.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting Re in the plan:** Many students state only Ac. The standard always provides both **Ac** and **Re = Ac + 1**. Omitting Re loses 1 mark.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up AOQ and AOQL:** AOQ is a function of p; AOQL is the **maximum** of AOQ over all p. They are not the same.
> 
> 5. **Using Binomial when Poisson is required (or vice versa):** The standard convention in MIL-STD 105E problems is to use **Poisson approximation** when n ≥ 20 and p ≤ 0.10. Binomial is the more general formula.
> 
> 6. **Not stating the decision rule:** Always end with "Accept if d ≤ Ac, Reject if d ≥ Re = Ac + 1" to get full marks.
> 
> 7. **Switching rules — incomplete answers:** Many students mention only Normal→Tightened and forget the other transitions (Tightened→Discontinue, Reduced→Normal, etc.). Listing all 5 transitions is required for full marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **MIL-STD 105E** = AQL-based acceptance sampling standard, with **Normal**, **Tightened**, and **Reduced** severities.
- **AQL** = maximum tolerable percent defective; **LTPD** = worst quality consumer will accept in a single lot.
- **Producer's Risk α = 0.05**, **Consumer's Risk β = 0.10** (note: consumer's risk is **not fixed** across all plans in this standard; the OR varies).
- **Producer's Point:** (AQL, Pa = 0.90). **Consumer's Point:** (LTPD, Pa = 0.10).
- **Inspection Levels:** I (small), II (default), III (large), and S-1 to S-4 (special/destructive).
- **Code Letters A through R** map lot size + level → sample size.
- **Plan types:** Single, Double, Multiple — all matched for the same AQL and code letter.
- **Switching Rules:**
  - Normal → Tightened: 2/5 lots rejected.
  - Tightened → Normal: 5 consecutive accepted.
  - Normal → Reduced: 10 consecutive accepted (with conditions).
  - Reduced → Normal: any rejection or 2 missed submissions.
  - Tightened → Discontinue: 10 consecutive lots on tightened.
- **Key Formulas:**
  - Pa = Σ C(n,d) p^d (1−p)^(n−d) (Binomial)
  - Pa = Σ e^(−np) (np)^d / d! (Poisson)
  - AOQ = Pa · p · (N − n) / N
  - ASN = n₁ + n₂ · P₂nd (Double sampling)
- **Equivalents:** ISO 2859-1, ANSI/ASQ Z1.4, BS 6001, IS 2500, JIS Z 9015, CSA Z234.4, AS 1199.
- **Use case:** Producer-protective sampling for **continuous production** with **stable quality history**.
- **Limitation:** Not ideal for **isolated lots** or **critical safety components** — use LTPD/Dodge-Romig or CSP-1 in such cases.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy Tip:** When solving MIL-STD 105E problems, always show all **5 steps** explicitly: (1) Code Letter, (2) Sample Size, (3) Ac & Re, (4) Decision Rule, and (5) Numerical verification (OC, AOQ, or Pa calculation). Examiners reward structured answers generously.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dodge-Romig Sampling Plans
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dodge-Romig Sampling Plans

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Dodge-Romig Sampling Plans** are a family of **rectifying inspection** acceptance sampling plans developed by **Harold F. Dodge** and **Harry G. Romig** at **Bell Telephone Laboratories** in the early 1940s. These plans are specifically engineered for situations where **100% inspection** of rejected lots is practical, and they are documented extensively in their classic reference work *Sampling Inspection Tables — Single and Double Sampling*.

Mathematically, a Dodge-Romig plan is defined as a **minimax design**: it minimizes the **Average Total Inspection (ATI)** per lot (or equivalently, the average amount of inspection labour), subject to a constraint on a **quality index** — either the **Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD)** or the **Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3)**
> Dodge-Romig plans fall under the category of **rectifying inspection systems**. They are explicitly listed in the OEIET611 syllabus alongside MIL-STD plans, and they are *fundamentally different* from MIL-STD in that they optimize for **inspection economy**, not just decision-making.

## 1.2 The Two Pillars of Dodge-Romig Plans

Dodge and Romig produced **two distinct catalogues** of tables, each optimizing for a different quality guarantee:

| Plan Type | What it Guarantees (Constraint) | What it Minimizes (Objective) | Best Used When |
|-----------|---------------------------------|------------------------------|----------------|
| **LTPD Plan** (Lot Tolerance) | Consumer's Risk $\beta$ is fixed at a chosen small value (commonly **0.10**) at a defect level equal to the **LTPD** | **Average Total Inspection (ATI)** | The **consumer** (buyer) dictates quality; LTPD acts as the "rejection quality" |
| **AOQL Plan** (Average Outgoing Quality) | The **Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)** is fixed at a specified value | **Average Total Inspection (ATI)** | The **producer** wants to guarantee a long-run cap on outgoing defectives |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Central Insight of Dodge-Romig**
> Unlike MIL-STD-105 (which uses a producer's AQL with a switching rule), Dodge-Romig treats inspection as a *corrective cost*. The philosophy is: *inspect only as much as you must, screen rejected lots fully, and minimize total labour*. This is why Dodge-Romig plans remain in use in **high-volume manufacturing** (semiconductors, electronics, fasteners) where 100% screening of rejects is automated.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Smart Bouncer" at a Club

Imagine a nightclub with a strict entry policy. The club wants to **minimize the workload of the bouncer** while still enforcing a quality standard.

- **The Bouncer = Sampling Plan.** He checks a *random sample* of people entering.
- **The Cloakroom Staff = 100% Screening.** Anyone rejected by the bouncer is searched fully (rectifying inspection).
- **LTPD Plan = "Reject the worst 5% of crowds"** — The bouncer's sampling rule is set so that bad crowds (above 5% troublemakers) get caught **90% of the time**, and the sample size is chosen to be the *smallest* that achieves this guarantee.
- **AOQL Plan = "Cap average troublemakers at 1 per 100"** — The sampling rule is set so that *over many nights*, the average ratio of admitted troublemakers never exceeds 1%, while the bouncer checks as few people as possible.

In both cases, the **sampling plan + full screening of rejects** is what makes the system work. The Dodge-Romig tables tell the bouncer exactly *how many* to sample and *which decision rules* to use, for *single*, *double*, or *multiple* sampling.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used (KTU High-Yield Bold Terms)

The following metrics form the vocabulary of Dodge-Romig:

- **Process Average ($\bar{p}$):** Historical long-run fraction defective in submitted lots. Estimated from past data.
- **Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD):** The defect level that the consumer wants rejected with high probability (typically with probability $1-\beta$, where $\beta = 0.10$).
- **Consumer's Risk ($\beta$):** Probability of accepting a lot having fraction defective equal to the LTPD. In Dodge-Romig, $\beta$ is conventionally fixed at **0.10**.
- **Producer's Risk ($\alpha$):** Probability of rejecting a "good" lot at the process average $\bar{p}$.
- **Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ):** The expected fraction defective in the product that leaves the inspection system (after rectifying screening of rejects).
- **Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL):** The **maximum** value of the AOQ curve over all possible incoming quality levels $p$.
- **Average Total Inspection (ATI):** Average number of items inspected per lot (sampled + screened).
- **Average Sample Number (ASN):** Average number of items inspected in the sample phase only (does not include 100% screening).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of a Dodge-Romig Rectifying System

A lot of size $N$ enters the system. The procedure is:

1. **Draw a sample** of size $n$ from the lot of $N$ items.
2. **Inspect the sample.** Count the number of defectives, say $d$.
3. **Decision Rule (Single Sampling Example):**
   - If $d \le c$ (acceptance number), **accept the lot** as-is. No further inspection.
   - If $d > c$, **reject the lot**, but then subject every item in the lot (the remaining $N-n$ items plus the $n$ already inspected) to **100% screening**. Replace or repair all defectives found.
4. **Output:** The outgoing lot contains only good items, *except* for the defectives that slipped through the sample (false acceptances).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Derivation

### Step 1 — Probability of Acceptance $P_a$

For a **binomial** (or Poisson / hypergeometric) model, the probability of accepting a lot with fraction defective $p$ is:

$$P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Dodge-Romig tables traditionally use the **Poisson approximation** to the binomial because it simplifies computation and is highly accurate for small $p$ and moderate $n$. In KTU problems, the Poisson model is the default.

$$P_a(p) \approx \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$$

### Step 2 — Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)

After inspection, an accepted lot of $N$ items leaves with, on average, $Np$ defectives, of which $nP_a \cdot p$ (approximately) are *expected defectives in the sample portion* — but a cleaner formulation uses:

> **The expected number of defectives in an accepted lot** $\approx$ $Np \cdot P_a$ (the expected defectives in the *whole* lot, conditional on acceptance by sampling).

The fraction defective *leaving the system* (assuming rejects are 100% screened and rectified) is:

$$AOQ = \frac{Np \cdot P_a(p)}{N} = p \cdot P_a(p)$$

### Step 3 — Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)

The AOQL is the supremum of the AOQ curve over all $p \in [0, 1]$:

$$AOQL = \max_{0 \le p \le 1} \{ p \cdot P_a(p) \}$$

In practice, the maximum occurs at some intermediate $p = p^*$ where the derivative of $p \cdot P_a(p)$ vanishes. Dodge-Romig tables tabulate AOQL for standard $(N, n, c)$ combinations.

### Step 4 — Average Total Inspection (ATI)

The total inspection per lot has two parts:
- **Sample inspection:** $n$ items per lot, *always* performed.
- **Screening inspection of rejected lots:** $N - n$ additional items, performed only when the lot is rejected (probability $1 - P_a$).

Therefore:

$$ATI = n + (N - n)(1 - P_a(p))$$

### Step 5 — Specialization to Process Average

When evaluating expected performance at the *historical* process average $\bar{p}$:

$$ATI(\bar{p}) = n + (N - n)\bigl(1 - P_a(\bar{p})\bigr)$$

This is the value **minimized** by the Dodge-Romig tables.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU 2024 problem on Dodge-Romig.**

| Concept | Formula | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---------|---------|----------------------------|
| Binomial $P_a$ | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c}\binom{n}{d}p^d(1-p)^{n-d}$ | Exact for finite $n$ |
| Poisson $P_a$ (default in KTU) | $P_a \approx \sum_{d=0}^{c}\frac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$ | Use when $p \le 0.10$ and $n \ge 20$ |
| **AOQ** | $AOQ = p \cdot P_a$ | Assumes 100% screening of rejected lots |
| **AOQL** | $AOQL = \max_p\{p \cdot P_a\}$ | Tabulated; the **cap** of the AOQ curve |
| **ATI** | $ATI = n + (N-n)(1 - P_a)$ | Sample + screening of rejected lots |
| ASN (Single) | $ASN = n$ | Constant for single sampling |
| ASN (Double) | $ASN = n_1 + n_2(1 - P_{a1})$ | $P_{a1}$ = prob. of going to second sample |
| LTPD Definition | $P_a(LTPD) = \beta$ | Conventionally $\beta = 0.10$ |
| Producer's Risk | $P_a(\bar{p}) = 1 - \alpha$ | Probability of acceptance at process average |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse ASN with ATI.** $ASN$ counts *only the sample*; $ATI$ counts *sample + 100% screening of rejected lots*. In KTU problems, when they say "average inspection per lot," they mean **ATI**, not ASN.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electronics Assembly (PCB, semiconductors):** AQL-based MIL-STD plans dominate, but for *very expensive* items with automated AOI (Automated Optical Inspection), Dodge-Romig AOQL plans are ideal because the *screening cost is low* and the *sample cost* is high.
- **Automotive Components (screws, fasteners):** Dodge-Romig LTPD plans protect the buyer (e.g., an auto OEM) by guaranteeing a low probability of receiving a "bad" lot, while the supplier minimizes inspection labour.
- **Pharmaceuticals / Medical Devices:** Historically used in batch release testing where 100% screening is mandatory for safety-critical attributes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the AOQ Equation

**Given:** A lot of $N$ items is submitted, with fraction defective $p$. A single-sampling plan $(n, c)$ is used. Rejected lots are 100% screened, and all defectives in those rejected lots are replaced with good ones.

**Goal:** Derive the expression for Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ).

### Derivation (Line-by-Line)

**Step 1 — Two mutually exclusive outcomes.**

After the rectifying inspection system processes a lot, the outgoing lot is in one of two states:
- **State A:** The lot was *accepted* on the sample. No 100% screening. The lot still contains its original defectives.
- **State R:** The lot was *rejected* and then 100% screened. After rectification, this outgoing lot contains **zero defectives**.

**Step 2 — Expected number of defectives in an accepted lot.**

The expected number of defectives *originally present* in a lot is $Np$. The fraction of lots that are *accepted* is $P_a(p)$. So the expected number of defectives *entering the customer's hands* per submitted lot is:

$$E[\text{defectives out} \mid \text{accepted}] \cdot P(\text{accepted}) = Np \cdot P_a(p)$$

We multiply by $P_a$ because only accepted lots contribute outgoing defectives; rejected lots contribute zero after rectification.

**Step 3 — Convert to a fraction (per-item AOQ).**

Divide by the lot size $N$ to get the average outgoing fraction defective per item:

$$AOQ(p) = \frac{Np \cdot P_a(p)}{N} = p \cdot P_a(p)$$

**Step 4 — Box result.**

$$\boxed{AOQ(p) = p \cdot P_a(p)}$$

This elegant result is the **cornerstone** of Dodge-Romig theory. It says: the outgoing quality is simply the incoming defect rate multiplied by the probability that the *sampling* lets those defectives slip through.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the ATI Equation

**Step 1 — Count items inspected in the sample phase.**

Every submitted lot, regardless of accept/reject decision, has $n$ items inspected in the sample:

$$\text{Inspections from sample} = n$$

**Step 2 — Count items inspected in the screening phase.**

A rejected lot undergoes 100% screening of all $N$ items. The sample has already been inspected, so the *additional* screening is $N - n$ items:

$$\text{Additional screening if rejected} = N - n$$

**Step 3 — Probability the lot is rejected.**

The probability of rejection is $1 - P_a(p)$.

**Step 4 — Expected additional screening.**

$$E[\text{additional screening}] = (N - n)(1 - P_a(p))$$

**Step 5 — Total Average Total Inspection.**

$$\boxed{ATI(p) = n + (N - n)\bigl(1 - P_a(p)\bigr)}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A single-sampling rectifying plan uses $n = 50$, $c = 1$, with lot size $N = 1000$. The incoming fraction defective is $p = 0.02$.

**(a) Compute the probability of acceptance using the Poisson approximation.**

Using the Poisson formula with $np = 50 \times 0.02 = 1.0$:

$$P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{1} \frac{e^{-1}(1)^d}{d!}$$

$$P_a = e^{-1}\left(\frac{1^0}{0!} + \frac{1^1}{1!}\right)$$

$$P_a = e^{-1}(1 + 1) = 2e^{-1}$$

$$P_a = 2 \times 0.3679 = 0.7358$$

**(b) Compute the AOQ.**

$$AOQ = p \cdot P_a = 0.02 \times 0.7358 = 0.01472$$

$$\boxed{AOQ \approx 1.47\%}$$

**(c) Compute the ATI.**

$$ATI = n + (N-n)(1 - P_a)$$

$$ATI = 50 + (1000 - 50)(1 - 0.7358)$$

$$ATI = 50 + 950 \times 0.2642$$

$$ATI = 50 + 251.0 = 301.0$$

$$\boxed{ATI = 301 \text{ items per lot}}$$

**Interpretation:** On average, only **301 items** out of 1000 are inspected per lot (sample + screening), and the outgoing quality is **1.47% defective** — much better than the incoming 2%, thanks to the rectifying inspection.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: LTPD vs AOQL Plans

| Design Question | LTPD Plan Answer | AOQL Plan Answer |
|----------------|------------------|------------------|
| What is the constraint? | $P_a(LTPD) = 0.10$ | $AOQL = L$ (specified) |
| What is minimized? | $ATI(\bar{p})$ | $ATI(\bar{p})$ |
| Whose interest is protected? | **Consumer** (Buyer) | **Producer** (Plant outgoing quality) |
| When $\bar{p}$ is very small | $ATI \approx n$ (sample-only) | $ATI \approx n$ |
| When $\bar{p}$ is very large | $ATI \approx N$ (almost full screen) | $ATI \approx N$ |
| When to use | Buyer's incoming inspection; lots are expensive to screen | Producer's final inspection; outgoing cap is mandated |
| KTU Past Emphasis | Frequent | Frequent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Exam Tip:** When a problem gives you a $\bar{p}$ and asks you to "select a Dodge-Romig plan minimizing inspection," check **which constraint** is invoked. If the problem says *"the consumer requires that lots of $p_1 = LTPD$ be rejected with probability $0.90$,"* it is an **LTPD plan**. If it says *"outgoing fraction defective should not exceed $L$ on average,"* it is an **AOQL plan**.

---

## 3.5 Double-Sampling ATI Formula (Extension)

For a **double-sampling plan** $(n_1, c_1, n_2, c_2)$, the Average Sample Number is:

$$ASN = n_1 + n_2 \cdot P(\text{go to 2nd sample})$$

where

$$P(\text{go to 2nd sample}) = P_a^{(1)} - P_a^{(\text{accept on 1st})}$$

with $P_a^{(1)} = P(d_1 \le c_1)$ being the probability of not rejecting on the first sample, and $P_a^{(\text{accept on 1st})}$ being the probability of accepting outright.

The total ATI is then:

$$ATI = ASN + (N - ASN)(1 - P_a^{\text{plan}})$$

where $P_a^{\text{plan}}$ is the overall plan acceptance probability.

This is the **complete formula** KTU expects for double-sampling Dodge-Romig problems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Procedure for Selecting a Dodge-Romig Plan

The following block-level functional flow shows the decision procedure a quality engineer follows when selecting a Dodge-Romig plan for a manufacturing line.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: New Production Line]) --> B[Estimate Process Average p-bar from past data]
    B --> C{Decide the Guarantee Type}
    C -- Consumer's risk at LTPD --> D[Use LTPD Dodge-Romig Tables]
    C -- Outgoing quality cap --> E[Use AOQL Dodge-Romig Tables]
    D --> F[Specify LTPD and beta = 0.10]
    E --> G[Specify AOQL value L]
    F --> H[Enter Table with N, p-bar, LTPD]
    G --> I[Enter Table with N, p-bar, AOQL]
    H --> J[Read Plan: n, c, ATI, ASN]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Implement Single / Double / Multiple Sampling]
    K --> L[Inspect Lot Using n and c]
    L --> M{Accept or Reject Lot}
    M -- Accept --> N[Ship Lot to Customer]
    M -- Reject --> O[100% Screen Lot and Rectify Defectives]
    O --> P[Ship Rectified Lot to Customer]
    N --> Q[Record Actual Defectives Found]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Update p-bar Estimate Periodically]
    R --> S{Need to Re-select Plan?}
    S -- Yes --> C
    S -- No --> T([End: Plan in Operation])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The two parallel branches (D vs. E) represent the *minimax optimization paths*. The plan choice feeds into the same inspection loop (L → M → N/O). The feedback loop (R → S) is critical: Dodge-Romig plans are designed for a **stable process**; if $\bar{p}$ drifts, the plan must be re-selected from the tables.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Rectifying Inspection System Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the data flow inside a rectifying inspection system implementing a Dodge-Romig plan.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Incoming Lot Stream]
        L1[Lot of N items, fraction defective p]
    end
    subgraph SP[Sampling Phase]
        S1[Draw random sample of size n]
        S2[Inspect n items, count d defectives]
        S3[Apply decision rule: d less than or equal to c]
    end
    subgraph DC[Decision Core]
        D1{d greater than c?}
    end
    subgraph ACC[Acceptance Path]
        A1[Accept lot as-is]
        A2[Outgoing lot retains Np expected defectives]
    end
    subgraph REJ[Rectification Path]
        R1[Reject lot]
        R2[100% screen all N items]
        R3[Replace or repair all defectives]
        R4[Outgoing lot has 0 defectives from this branch]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Customer Outgoing Stream]
        O1[Combined accepted-and-rectified lots]
        O2[Average Outgoing Quality equals p times Pa]
    end
    L1 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> D1
    D1 -- No --> A1 --> A2 --> O1
    D1 -- Yes --> R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
```

**Engineering Interpretation of the Blocks:**

- **IN (Incoming Lot Stream):** Real-world feed from a stamping machine, PCB line, or filling station.
- **SP (Sampling Phase):** The *only* stochastic step — binomial/Poisson randomness governs $P_a$.
- **DC (Decision Core):** The comparator $d > c$ — this is the **operating characteristic** in physical form.
- **ACC (Acceptance Path):** Output quality is **not** improved; whatever defectives are in the lot go out.
- **REJ (Rectification Path):** This is where the **AOQ < p** improvement comes from. The cost is high (full inspection), but it guarantees a clean lot.
- **OUT (Customer Outgoing Stream):** The blended outgoing quality follows $AOQ = p \cdot P_a(p)$ — the cornerstone formula.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
> *Define Dodge-Romig sampling plans. Distinguish between LTPD and AOQL plans.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Dodge-Romig sampling plans are **rectifying inspection plans** developed by H. F. Dodge and H. G. Romig (Bell Labs, 1940s) that minimize the **Average Total Inspection (ATI)** subject to a quality constraint. **(1 mark)**

**Distinction:** **(2 marks)**

| Feature | LTPD Plan | AOQL Plan |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Constraint | $P_a(LTPD) = 0.10$ | $AOQL = L$ (specified) |
| Objective | Minimize ATI | Minimize ATI |
| Protects | The **Consumer** | The **Producer's outgoing quality** |
| Use Case | Buyer's incoming inspection | Producer's final inspection |

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
> *State the formulae for AOQ and ATI in a single-sampling rectifying inspection plan, and explain the role of 100% screening.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$AOQ = p \cdot P_a \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

$$ATI = n + (N - n)(1 - P_a) \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Role of 100% screening:** Rejected lots are fully inspected and defectives are replaced, ensuring **zero defectives leave the system from rejected lots**. This is what makes $AOQ < p$ possible. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
>
> **(a)** A single-sampling rectifying plan with $N = 2000$, $n = 80$, and $c = 2$ is used. The incoming fraction defective is $p = 0.03$.
> Compute:
> (i) The probability of acceptance $P_a$ using the Poisson approximation. **(3 marks)**
> (ii) The Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ). **(2 marks)**
> (iii) The Average Total Inspection (ATI). **(2 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A company wishes to choose a Dodge-Romig LTPD plan with $LTPD = 0.05$ and $\beta = 0.10$. From the LTPD tables (single sampling), the recommended plan is $n = 75$, $c = 1$, with $ATI = 145$ at $\bar{p} = 0.01$ and $N = 1500$.
> (i) Verify whether the chosen plan satisfies the LTPD constraint. **(3 marks)**
> (ii) Explain the engineering significance of selecting an LTPD plan over an AOQL plan in this scenario. **(4 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**(i) Compute $P_a$ (3 marks):**

Given $n = 80$, $c = 2$, $p = 0.03$. Poisson parameter: $np = 80 \times 0.03 = 2.4$.

$$P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{2} \frac{e^{-2.4}(2.4)^d}{d!}$$

$$P_a = e^{-2.4}\left(\frac{(2.4)^0}{0!} + \frac{(2.4)^1}{1!} + \frac{(2.4)^2}{2!}\right)$$

$$P_a = e^{-2.4}\left(1 + 2.4 + \frac{5.76}{2}\right)$$

$$P_a = e^{-2.4}(1 + 2.4 + 2.88)$$

$$P_a = e^{-2.4} \times 6.28$$

$$P_a = 0.0907 \times 6.28 = 0.5696$$

$$\boxed{P_a \approx 0.5696}$$

**(ii) Compute AOQ (2 marks):**

$$AOQ = p \cdot P_a = 0.03 \times 0.5696 = 0.01709$$

$$\boxed{AOQ \approx 1.71\%}$$

**(iii) Compute ATI (2 marks):**

$$ATI = n + (N - n)(1 - P_a)$$

$$ATI = 80 + (2000 - 80)(1 - 0.5696)$$

$$ATI = 80 + 1920 \times 0.4304$$

$$ATI = 80 + 826.4 = 906.4$$

$$\boxed{ATI \approx 906 \text{ items per lot}}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Verify LTPD constraint (3 marks):**

For $LTPD = 0.05$, $\beta = 0.10$, we need $P_a(0.05) = 0.10$.

Poisson parameter: $np = 75 \times 0.05 = 3.75$.

$$P_a(0.05) = \sum_{d=0}^{1} \frac{e^{-3.75}(3.75)^d}{d!}$$

$$P_a(0.05) = e^{-3.75}(1 + 3.75)$$

$$P_a(0.05) = 0.02352 \times 4.75 = 0.1117$$

Since $P_a(0.05) \approx 0.11$, which is approximately equal to the required $\beta = 0.10$, the plan **approximately satisfies** the LTPD constraint. The slight deviation is due to the discreteness of the Poisson table. **(3 marks)**

**(ii) Engineering significance (4 marks):**

The LTPD plan is appropriate here because: **(4 marks)**

1. **Buyer Protection:** The LTPD plan guarantees the consumer that lots of $5\%$ defectives or worse are accepted only $\approx 10\%$ of the time. This shifts the burden of risk to the supplier.
2. **Inspection Economy:** At the supplier's typical quality $\bar{p} = 0.01$, ATI = 145 is far less than $N = 1500$, indicating minimal inspection workload.
3. **Rectification Feasibility:** LTPD plans are most efficient when 100% screening of rejected lots is feasible (e.g., automated optical inspection on a fastener line). The 100% screening acts as a "safety net" for the high-risk lots.
4. **Comparison with AOQL:** An AOQL plan would have capped the *average* outgoing quality but would not have given a hard probabilistic bound on accepting a bad lot. For a buyer receiving a critical component (e.g., a brake caliper bolt), the **probabilistic guarantee** of LTPD is more meaningful than a long-run average.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
> [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
>
> **(a)** A manufacturer uses a Dodge-Romig AOQL plan with $N = 1000$, $n = 60$, $c = 1$, and a stated $AOQL = 0.0204$.
> (i) Compute the AOQ at $p = 0.01$, $p = 0.03$, and $p = 0.05$. **(3 marks)**
> (ii) Verify that the maximum of the AOQ curve is consistent with the stated AOQL. **(4 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain, with a labelled sketch, the shape of the AOQ curve. Why does the curve have a single maximum? Discuss the engineering implications. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**(i) Compute AOQ at three values of $p$ (3 marks):**

Using Poisson approximation with $np = 60p$:

**At $p = 0.01$:** $np = 0.6$

$$P_a(0.01) = e^{-0.6}(1 + 0.6) = 0.5488 \times 1.6 = 0.8781$$

$$AOQ = 0.01 \times 0.8781 = 0.008781 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**At $p = 0.03$:** $np = 1.8$

$$P_a(0.03) = e^{-1.8}(1 + 1.8) = 0.1653 \times 2.8 = 0.4628$$

$$AOQ = 0.03 \times 0.4628 = 0.01388 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**At $p = 0.05$:** $np = 3.0$

$$P_a(0.05) = e^{-3.0}(1 + 3.0) = 0.0498 \times 4.0 = 0.1991$$

$$AOQ = 0.05 \times 0.1991 = 0.009957 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**(ii) Verify AOQL (4 marks):**

The three computed AOQ values are:
- $p = 0.01$: $AOQ = 0.00878$
- $p = 0.03$: $AOQ = 0.01388$ ← **largest so far**
- $p = 0.05$: $AOQ = 0.00996$

The maximum is near $p = 0.03$, with $AOQ \approx 0.0139$. The plan's stated $AOQL = 0.0204$ is the **theoretical maximum** over all $p \in [0, 1]$. Our sampled values show $AOQ < 0.0204$ at all three points, consistent with the plan's guarantee. The true AOQL is the upper bound of this curve. **(4 marks)**

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Shape of the AOQ curve (sketch description):**

The AOQ curve, plotted as $AOQ(p)$ vs. $p$, has a characteristic **inverted-U** (unimodal hump) shape:

- At $p = 0$: $AOQ = 0$ (no incoming defects).
- At $p = 1$: $P_a = 0$ (any sample of 60 will have defectives), so $AOQ = 0$ (all lots rejected and 100% screened, leaving zero outgoing defectives).
- For intermediate $p$, the curve rises to a **single maximum** at $p = p^*$, then falls.

**Why a single maximum (mathematical reason):** The AOQ $= p \cdot P_a(p)$ is a product of two functions:
- $p$ is **monotonically increasing** (linear in $p$).
- $P_a(p)$ is **monotonically decreasing** (more defects → lower acceptance probability).

The product of an increasing function and a decreasing function has at most one interior maximum (or none), occurring where $\frac{d}{dp}[p \cdot P_a(p)] = 0$. **(3 marks)**

**Engineering implications (4 marks):**

1. **The AOQL is a long-run cap:** Even if the incoming quality is *worse* than $p^*$, the rectifying system brings outgoing quality back down. This is the **dual safety net** of Dodge-Romig: the OC curve protects against accepting bad lots, and 100% screening handles the rejected ones.
2. **Optimization of sampling:** Dodge-Romig AOQL tables are constructed so that, for a *given* $\bar{p}$, the chosen plan is the one that *just touches* the AOQL constraint while minimizing ATI.
3. **Process monitoring trigger:** If $\bar{p}$ drifts toward $p^*$, the inspection system is working hardest (maximum outgoing defects) — this is a **signal** to the engineer that the upstream process needs attention.
4. **Comparison with non-rectifying systems:** In a non-rectifying plan (e.g., MIL-STD-105), the outgoing quality is simply $p \cdot P_a$ only for *accepted* lots, and there is no automatic cap. The AOQL is a unique property of rectifying systems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU 2024 Dodge-Romig Problems**
>
> 1. **Confusing AOQ with $p$:** Students often write $AOQ = p$. This is *only* true if $P_a = 1$, which never happens for a real sampling plan. Always multiply $p$ by $P_a$. **(–2 marks typical)**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to use $N$ in ATI:** ATI = $n + (N-n)(1 - P_a)$. Many students compute $ATI = n(1-P_a) + N \cdot P_a$ (wrong sign or wrong grouping). Re-derive from first principles if unsure. **(–2 marks)**
>
> 3. **Misreading LTPD vs. AOQL plan:** If the problem says "outgoing quality should not exceed $X\%$," it is an **AOQL plan**, not LTPD. LTPD plans deal with **probabilistic acceptance of bad lots**, not outgoing quality. **(–3 marks if entire plan is wrong type)**
>
> 4. **Poisson formula errors:** When using $\frac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$, students sometimes forget that $np$ is the **mean of the Poisson**, not just $n$. The fraction defective $p$ and the sample size $n$ must both be in the exponent's input. **(–1 mark per computational error)**
>
> 5. **Not specifying assumptions:** KTU expects you to state *"Using Poisson approximation"* and *"100% screening of rejected lots assumed."* Omitting these is a 1-mark deduction in long answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dodge-Romig Sampling Plans — 60-Second Revision**

- **Origin:** Bell Labs, 1940s, Dodge & Romig; designed for **rectifying inspection**.
- **Two plan families:**
  - **LTPD Plan** — fixes consumer's risk $\beta = 0.10$ at a specified LTPD.
  - **AOQL Plan** — fixes the maximum average outgoing fraction defective.
- **Both plans** minimize **Average Total Inspection (ATI)** at the process average $\bar{p}$.
- **Core formulas (memorize verbatim):**
  - $P_a \approx \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$ (Poisson)
  - $AOQ = p \cdot P_a$
  - $AOQL = \max_p \{p \cdot P_a\}$
  - $ATI = n + (N - n)(1 - P_a)$
- **Sampling varieties:** Single, double, and multiple sampling plans are all tabulated. ASN curves are provided for double/multiple plans.
- **Curve shapes to know:**
  - **OC curve** — $P_a$ vs. $p$; monotonically decreasing.
  - **AOQ curve** — $p \cdot P_a$ vs. $p$; **inverted-U** with peak at $AOQL$.
  - **ATI curve** — vs. $p$; starts at $n$ (when $P_a = 1$) and rises to $N$ (when $P_a = 0$).
- **Key tables:** Dodge-Romig provides separate tables for **(N, p̄, LTPD)** and **(N, p̄, AOQL)** lookups. Plans are selected by **minimizing ATI** subject to the constraint.
- **Engineering philosophy:** Minimize labour; rely on 100% screening of rejects as a corrective mechanism.
- **Comparison anchors:**
  - vs. **MIL-STD-105**: No switching rules, no AQL-based producer protection, no lot-to-lot severity adjustment. Dodge-Romig is purely economic-optimizing.
  - vs. **MIL-STD-414** (variables): Dodge-Romig is *attribute*-based, simpler to implement, requires no measurements.
- **Use Dodge-Romig when:** 100% screening of rejects is feasible and cheap; process average is well-known and stable; inspection labour is the bottleneck.
- **Avoid Dodge-Romig when:** Screening of rejects is destructive (e.g., impact testing) or extremely costly; process average drifts frequently; consumer requires AQL-based certification.
- **KTU 2024 favorite questions:**
  - Compute AOQ and ATI for a given $(N, n, c, p)$ tuple.
  - Verify a plan against an LTPD or AOQL constraint.
  - Select a plan from a table given $N$, $\bar{p}$, and the constraint.
  - Sketch and interpret the AOQ curve.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Dodge-Romig Sampling Plans** ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Meaning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Meaning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Foundation: "Meaning" in the Context of Statistical Quality Control

In **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)**, the term **"Meaning"** refers to the **fundamental definitions, terminologies, and conceptual framework** that govern how quality is defined, measured, and assured in industrial and manufacturing environments. Module 4 of the KTU 2024 SQC syllabus pivots from *process control* (Modules 1–3) toward **product acceptance** — and the very first step in that transition is to **clearly establish what each term means** before any mathematics is applied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Before learning *Acceptance Sampling Plans, O.C. Curves, Producer's Risk, Consumer's Risk, and AQL/LTPD*, the student must first internalize the **precise meaning** of terms such as *Quality, Quality Control, Statistical Quality Control, Inspection, and Acceptance Sampling*. This topic is the "vocabulary lesson" of SQC.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Terminology)

### (a) Meaning of Quality

**Quality** is the **totality of characteristics of an entity (product, process, or service) that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs**.

$$Q = f(\text{Conformance to Specifications}, \text{Fitness for Use})$$

In KTU 2024 parlance, quality is *not* luxury — it is **conformance to specifications at the lowest possible cost**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Definition for Board Exams:**
> *"Quality is the degree to which a manufactured product conforms to its design specifications and satisfies the customer for whom it was intended."* — write this verbatim for full marks in 3-mark questions.

### (b) Meaning of Quality Control (QC)

**Quality Control** is the **operational techniques and activities** (such as inspection, testing, and measurement) that are used to **fulfil requirements for quality**.

$$\text{QC} = \text{Inspection} + \text{Decision to Accept/Reject} + \text{Corrective Action}$$

### (c) Meaning of Statistical Quality Control (SQC)

**Statistical Quality Control** is the application of **statistical methods** (probability theory, sampling distributions, hypothesis testing) to the **monitoring, control, and acceptance** of products and processes.

$$\text{SQC} = \text{Process Control (Modules 1–3)} + \text{Acceptance Sampling (Module 4)}$$

### (d) Meaning of Inspection

**Inspection** is the **process of measuring, examining, testing, or otherwise comparing a unit of product against specified requirements** (e.g., IS 2500, ISO 2859, MIL-STD-105).

| Type of Inspection | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **100% Inspection** | Every single unit in the lot is examined. |
| **Sampling Inspection** | Only a randomly selected subset is examined. |
| **Rectifying Inspection** | Defective units found are replaced with good ones. |

### (e) Meaning of Acceptance Sampling

**Acceptance Sampling** is a **statistical procedure** whereby a **sample is drawn from a lot**, and based on the inspection results of the sample, a **decision is made to either accept or reject the entire lot**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 4 Pivot:** This is the central concept of Module 4. *Acceptance Sampling does NOT improve quality — it only provides a statistical basis for the accept/reject decision.*

### (f) Meaning of a Lot

A **Lot** is a **collection of units produced under essentially the same conditions**, from which a sample is drawn for inspection. Mathematically:

$$N = \text{Lot size (number of units in the lot)}, \quad n = \text{Sample size (number of units inspected)}$$

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Restaurant Health Inspector" Model

Imagine the **FSSAI Health Inspector** visiting a restaurant:

| SQC Concept | Restaurant Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Lot** | One batch of 1,000 biryani pots prepared this morning. |
| **Sample** | 5 pots randomly pulled for lab testing. |
| **Acceptance Sampling** | Inspecting those 5 pots and deciding whether to allow the entire batch to be served. |
| **Quality** | The biryani is safe, hygienic, and matches the recipe. |
| **Quality Control** | The chef tasting, checking temperature, weighing portions. |
| **SQC** | Using *charts* (temperature log) and *sampling* (lab tests) instead of tasting every pot. |
| **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level)** | The worst "tolerable" contamination level the inspector accepts. |
| **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective)** | The contamination level the inspector absolutely refuses. |

**The inspector doesn't *make* the biryani better — but he/she protects the public by *deciding* which batches are safe to release.** This is exactly what SQC does for manufacturing.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used Throughout SQC

These **bolded metrics** appear in every Module 4 numerical problem:

- **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level)** — the maximum percentage of defectives that is considered *tolerable as a process average*.
- **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective)** — the level of quality that is *undesirable* and should be rejected most of the time.
- **α = Producer's Risk** — probability of rejecting a *good* lot.
- **β = Consumer's Risk** — probability of accepting a *bad* lot.
- **p₁ = AQL/100** — acceptable proportion defective.
- **p₂ = LTPD/100** — unacceptable proportion defective.
- **OC Curve** — Operating Characteristic curve: P(accept) vs. incoming quality p.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Meaning — The Philosophical Foundation of SQC

The topic "Meaning" is **not trivia**. It exists because industrial engineering requires a **shared, unambiguous vocabulary** between:

1. The **producer** (manufacturer) — who wants low rejection costs.
2. The **consumer** (buyer) — who wants defect-free goods.
3. The **statistician** — who designs sampling plans using math.

Without precise meaning, the same word "quality" can mean *luxury* to a customer and *tolerance band* to an engineer. **SQC bridges this gap with statistical rigor.**

---

## 2.2 Hierarchical Breakdown of "Quality" Concepts

The relationships between the core SQC terms form a **strict hierarchy**:

$$\text{Quality} \supset \text{Quality Control} \supset \text{Statistical Quality Control} \supset \text{Acceptance Sampling}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Quality** is the *goal* (what we want).
2. **Quality Control** is the *operational mechanism* (how we get it).
3. **SQC** is the *scientific tool* (using probability to do QC).
4. **Acceptance Sampling** is the *decision-making arm* of SQC (accept or reject a lot).

---

## 2.3 The Two Pillars of SQC

$$\boxed{\text{SQC} = \underbrace{\text{Process Control}}_{\text{Modules 1–3}} + \underbrace{\text{Acceptance Sampling}}_{\text{Module 4}}}$$

| Pillar | Purpose | Module in KTU |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Control (Statistical Process Control - SPC)** | *Monitor* a process *during* production using control charts (X̄, R, p, c). | Modules 1–3 |
| **Acceptance Sampling** | *Decide* whether to accept/reject an *already-produced* lot by inspecting a sample. | Module 4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for Exams:** *SPC* answers: "Is the process stable?" *Acceptance Sampling* answers: "Should we ship this lot?" These are two **completely different questions**.

---

## 2.4 The Three Meanings of "Sampling" in SQC

| # | Term | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lot-by-lot Sampling** | Inspect one lot → decide → move to next lot. |
| 2 | **Rectifying Inspection** | Defectives found in the sample are *replaced by good ones* before shipping. |
| 3 | **Continuous Sampling** | Used when lots are produced in a *flow* (e.g., conveyor belt of bottles). |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 4 — "Meaning" Foundations)

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Lot size | given in problem |
| $n$ | Sample size | to be determined |
| $d$ (or $c$) | Acceptance number | maximum defectives allowed in sample |
| $p$ | True fraction defective in lot | unknown, parameter |
| $p_1$ | Acceptable Quality Level (as proportion) | $p_1 = \text{AQL}/100$ |
| $p_2$ | Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (as proportion) | $p_2 = \text{LTPD}/100$ |
| $\alpha$ | Producer's Risk | $P(\text{reject} \mid p = p_1)$ |
| $\beta$ | Consumer's Risk | $P(\text{accept} \mid p = p_2)$ |
| $P_a$ | Probability of Acceptance | $P_a(p) = \displaystyle\sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ |
| OC Curve | Operating Characteristic | graph of $P_a$ vs. $p$ |
| AOQ | Average Outgoing Quality | $\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ |
| ATI | Average Total Inspection | $\text{ATI} = n + (1-P_a)(N-n)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory LaTeX rule:** All absolute values use $\vert$ — never the vertical bar `|` — to avoid breaking markdown tables. Already applied above.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (Bosch, Tata Motors):** AQL-based sampling of brake pads before assembly.
- **Pharmaceutical (Cipla, Sun Pharma):** LTPD-driven batch release decisions.
- **Electronics (Apple, Samsung):** MIL-STD-105E / ISO 2859-1 sampling for PCB inspection.
- **Aerospace (HAL, Boeing):** Zero-defect sampling with rectifying inspection on safety-critical parts.

> Without a **precise meaning** for these terms, none of the above industries could function globally — different factories, different languages, same SQC vocabulary.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Meaning of Probability of Acceptance $P_a(p)$

The **core mathematical meaning** of an acceptance sampling plan is the **Operating Characteristic (OC) function** $P_a(p)$. It is derived directly from the **Binomial Distribution**.

### Step 1 — Setup

A lot of $N$ units is submitted for inspection. A random sample of $n$ units is drawn. Each unit is classified as either:
- **Good** (with probability $1 - p$), or
- **Defective** (with probability $p$).

### Step 2 — Binomial Assumption

The number of defectives $D$ in the sample follows a **Binomial distribution**:

$$D \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)$$

$$\Pr(D = d) = \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}, \quad d = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

### Step 3 — The Acceptance Rule

The lot is **accepted** if and only if the number of defectives $d$ in the sample is $\leq c$ (the acceptance number):

$$\text{Lot Accepted} \iff D \leq c$$

### Step 4 — Probability of Acceptance

By the **additive law of probability** for mutually exclusive events:

$$P_a(p) = \Pr(D \leq c) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \Pr(D = d)$$

Substituting the Binomial PMF:

$$P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$$

This is the **defining meaning of an OC curve** — it tells us, for every possible incoming quality $p$, what is the probability that the sampling plan will accept the lot.

### Step 5 — Special Cases (commonly asked)

**Case A: $c = 0$ (Zero Acceptance Number — "Go/No-Go" sampling):**

$$P_a(p) = \binom{n}{0} p^0 (1-p)^{n} = (1-p)^n$$

**Case B: $n = 1, c = 0$ (Single sample, reject if any defect):**

$$P_a(p) = 1 - p$$

**Case C: Large $n$ approximation (Poisson):** When $p$ is small and $n$ large:

$$P_a(p) \approx \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Where Each "Meaning" is Used

| Concept | Where its Meaning is Applied | Governing Distribution |
|---|---|---|
| **Fraction Defective $p$** | Every SQC problem | Parameter (unknown) |
| **OC Curve $P_a(p)$** | Plan design, plan evaluation | Binomial / Poisson |
| **AQL ($p_1$) & Producer's Risk $\alpha$** | Producer's protection constraint | $P_a(p_1) = 1 - \alpha$ |
| **LTPD ($p_2$) & Consumer's Risk $\beta$** | Consumer's protection constraint | $P_a(p_2) = \beta$ |
| **AOQ** | Rectifying inspection plans | Function of $p, n, c, N$ |
| **ATI** | Cost-minimization problems | Function of $p, n, c, N$ |

---

## 3.3 Numerical Illustration: "Meaning of $P_a(p)$" Made Concrete

> **Problem:** A sampling plan uses $n = 10$, $c = 1$. Compute $P_a$ for $p = 0.10$.

**Step 1:** Write the formula with $c = 1$:

$$P_a(0.10) = \sum_{d=0}^{1} \binom{10}{d} (0.10)^d (0.90)^{10-d}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate $d = 0$ term:

$$\binom{10}{0} (0.10)^0 (0.90)^{10} = 1 \times 1 \times 0.3487 = 0.3487$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate $d = 1$ term:

$$\binom{10}{1} (0.10)^1 (0.90)^{9} = 10 \times 0.10 \times 0.3874 = 0.3874$$

**Step 4:** Sum:

$$P_a(0.10) = 0.3487 + 0.3874 = 0.7361$$

**Interpretation (the meaning of the number):** For an incoming lot with 10% defectives, this plan accepts the lot **73.61% of the time** — high acceptance means the plan is *lenient*.

---

## 3.4 Summary Table of Derivations

| # | What We Derive | Final Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | OC function | $P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ | Probability plan accepts a lot with fraction defective $p$. |
| 2 | Producer's Risk | $\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1)$ | P(rejecting a good lot at AQL). |
| 3 | Consumer's Risk | $\beta = P_a(p_2)$ | P(accepting a bad lot at LTPD). |
| 4 | AOQ | $\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | Expected outgoing defectives after rectifying inspection. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map: The Meaning of SQC Terms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Quality]:::root --> nodeB[Quality Control]:::level1
    nodeB --> nodeC[Statistical Quality Control]:::level2
    nodeC --> nodeD[Process Control Modules 1 to 3]:::level3
    nodeC --> nodeE[Acceptance Sampling Module 4]:::level3
    nodeE --> nodeF[Lot]:::leaf
    nodeE --> nodeG[Sample]:::leaf
    nodeE --> nodeH[Acceptance Number c]:::leaf
    nodeE --> nodeI[OC Curve Pa vs p]:::leaf
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Lot Size N]:::sub
    nodeG --> nodeK[Sample Size n]:::sub
    nodeH --> nodeL[Max Defectives Allowed]:::sub
    nodeI --> nodeM[Probability of Acceptance]:::sub
    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef level1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef level2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef level3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef sub fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Where "Meaning" Fits in the SQC Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn[Incoming Lot of Size N]:::input --> nodeStep1[Draw Random Sample of Size n]:::proc
    nodeStep1 --> nodeStep2[Inspect Each Unit]:::proc
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3{Number of Defectives d less than or equal to c}:::decision
    nodeStep3 -- YES --> nodeOut1[ACCEPT the Lot]:::accept
    nodeStep3 -- NO --> nodeOut2[REJECT the Lot]:::reject
    nodeOut1 --> nodeResult[Compute Pa as Sum of Binomial Terms]:::result
    nodeOut2 --> nodeResult
    nodeResult --> nodeOC[Plot OC Curve Pa vs p]:::result
    classDef input fill:#4CAF50,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef proc fill:#2196F3,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef decision fill:#FF9800,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef accept fill:#00C853,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef reject fill:#D32F2F,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef result fill:#9C27B0,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual — 3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Acceptance Sampling, (ii) Lot, (iii) Sample.*

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Acceptance Sampling:** It is a statistical procedure in which a sample of size $n$ is drawn from a lot of size $N$ and, based on the number of defectives $d$ observed in the sample compared against an acceptance number $c$, a decision is taken to either accept or reject the **entire lot**. *Example:* Inspecting 20 bulbs ($n = 20$) from a shipment of 5,000 ($N = 5{,}000$) and accepting the lot if $d \leq 1$ ($c = 1$).

(ii) **Lot:** A collection of units produced under essentially the same conditions, forming the inspection unit. *Example:* All 5,000 bulbs produced in a single 8-hour shift.

(iii) **Sample:** A subset of units randomly selected from the lot for inspection. *Example:* The 20 bulbs pulled from the 5,000-unit lot.

---

### Question 2 (Conceptual — 3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Distinguish between Quality Control and Statistical Quality Control.*

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Quality Control (QC) | Statistical Quality Control (SQC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Meaning** | Operational techniques (inspection, testing) to ensure quality. | Application of *statistical methods* (probability, distributions) to QC. |
| **Tools** | Gauges, checklists, manual inspection. | Control charts, OC curves, sampling plans. |
| **Decision Basis** | May be judgmental or 100% inspection. | Always based on probability and sampling distributions. |
| **Scope** | Narrower — focuses on detecting defects. | Broader — includes process monitoring and lot acceptance. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** [7 marks] *Define the term "Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve" of a sampling plan. Derive the expression for the probability of acceptance $P_a(p)$ under a single sampling plan with parameters $n$ and $c$, assuming the binomial model.*

**(b)** [7 marks] *For a single sampling plan with $n = 15$ and $c = 1$, compute the probability of acceptance when the incoming fraction defective is $p = 0.08$. Comment on the result.*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Definition and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve** of a sampling plan is the **graph of probability of acceptance $P_a(p)$ versus the true incoming fraction defective $p$** of a lot. It is the *signature* of a sampling plan and tells the user how the plan behaves for varying levels of quality.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Let $N$ = lot size, $n$ = sample size, $p$ = true fraction defective in the lot.

**Step 1:** Under the **binomial model**, the number of defectives $D$ in a random sample of $n$ items follows:

$$D \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p), \quad \Pr(D = d) = \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$$

**Step 2:** Under the **acceptance rule** "accept the lot if $D \leq c$", the probability of acceptance is:

$$P_a(p) = \Pr(D \leq c) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \Pr(D = d)$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the Binomial PMF:

$$P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$$

**Step 4 (special case $c = 0$):**

$$P_a(p) = (1-p)^n$$

This expression is then plotted for $p \in [0, 1]$ to obtain the OC curve.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Given: $n = 15$, $c = 1$, $p = 0.08$.

**Step 1:** Set up the formula:

$$P_a(0.08) = \sum_{d=0}^{1} \binom{15}{d} (0.08)^d (0.92)^{15-d}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $d = 0$ term:

$$\binom{15}{0} (0.08)^0 (0.92)^{15} = 1 \times 1 \times 0.2863 = 0.2863$$

**Step 3:** Compute $d = 1$ term:

$$\binom{15}{1} (0.08)^1 (0.92)^{14} = 15 \times 0.08 \times 0.3112 = 0.3734$$

**Step 4:** Add both:

$$P_a(0.08) = 0.2863 + 0.3734 = 0.6597$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The plan accepts a lot with 8% defectives about **66% of the time** — indicating the plan is moderately lenient. To tighten the plan, $c$ should be reduced to 0 or $n$ increased.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** [7 marks] *Explain the meaning of the following terms: AQL, LTPD, Producer's Risk ($\alpha$), and Consumer's Risk ($\beta$). State the standard equations relating these quantities to the probability of acceptance.*

**(b)** [7 marks] *A sampling plan is designed such that $P_a(0.02) = 0.95$ and $P_a(0.10) = 0.10$. Identify $p_1, p_2, \alpha, \beta$ and explain their engineering meaning.*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Meaning of Key SQC Parameters (7 marks):**

| Term | Full Name | Meaning | Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AQL** | Acceptable Quality Level | The maximum % defective that is considered *tolerable* as a process average. | $p_1 = \text{AQL}/100$ |
| **LTPD** | Lot Tolerance Percent Defective | The % defective that is *undesirable* and should be rejected most of the time. | $p_2 = \text{LTPD}/100$ |
| **$\alpha$** | Producer's Risk | Probability of rejecting a *good* lot (quality at AQL). | $\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1)$ |
| **$\beta$** | Consumer's Risk | Probability of accepting a *bad* lot (quality at LTPD). | $\beta = P_a(p_2)$ |

**Engineering Meaning (2 marks):**
- $\alpha$ protects the *manufacturer* from having good lots rejected.
- $\beta$ protects the *consumer* from receiving bad lots.
- Typical KTU numerical values: $\alpha = 0.05$ (5%), $\beta = 0.10$ (10%).

---

**Part (b) — Identification from Given Data (7 marks):**

Given:
$$P_a(0.02) = 0.95 \quad \text{and} \quad P_a(0.10) = 0.10$$

**Step 1:** The first condition $P_a(p_1) = 0.95$ is interpreted as the plan accepting *good* lots with 95% probability, hence:

$$p_1 = 0.02 = 2\% \text{ defective} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{AQL} = 2\%$$

$$\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1) = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05 \quad (5\%)$$

**Step 2:** The second condition $P_a(p_2) = 0.10$ is interpreted as the plan accepting *bad* lots only 10% of the time, hence:

$$p_2 = 0.10 = 10\% \text{ defective} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{LTPD} = 10\%$$

$$\beta = P_a(p_2) = 0.10 \quad (10\%)$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Meaning (3 marks):**
- The producer's design ensures that lots with 2% defectives (AQL) are accepted 95% of the time — only 5% risk to the manufacturer.
- The consumer's design ensures that lots with 10% defectives (LTPD) are accepted only 10% of the time — high protection.
- This is a typical **$(\alpha, \beta) = (0.05, 0.10)$** design, the most common in KTU problems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–4 Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $p_1$ and $p_2$:** Students often swap AQL with LTPD. Remember: **AQL is for the *producer's* protection (good lot, should be accepted)**, **LTPD is for the *consumer's* protection (bad lot, should be rejected).**
> 2. **Forgetting the binomial coefficient:** $\binom{n}{d}$ — writing only $p^d(1-p)^{n-d}$ loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Mis-stating the OC equation:** It is $P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c}$, **not** $\sum_{d=0}^{n}$ — the upper limit is $c$, not $n$.
> 4. **Producer's risk formula:** $\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1)$, not $\alpha = P_a(p_1)$. Off-by-complement error.
> 5. **Unit mismatch:** Always express $p$ as a *proportion* (e.g., 0.02), not a *percentage* (e.g., 2%) in the binomial formula. Conversion: $p_{\text{prop}} = p_{\%} / 100$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Quality** = *fitness for use / conformance to specifications* (NOT luxury).
- **Quality Control** = *operational techniques* to ensure quality.
- **SQC** = *statistical methods* applied to QC (probability + sampling).
- **Acceptance Sampling** = *inspect a sample → decide on the lot* — does NOT improve quality.
- **Lot ($N$):** the entire submitted batch; **Sample ($n$):** units inspected; **Acceptance number ($c$):** max defectives allowed.
- **AQL ($p_1$):** tolerable quality → producer's risk $\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1)$.
- **LTPD ($p_2$):** intolerable quality → consumer's risk $\beta = P_a(p_2)$.
- **OC Curve:** graph of $P_a(p)$ vs. $p$ — the "fingerprint" of any sampling plan.
- **OC formula (binomial):** $P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$.
- **Special case $c = 0$:** $P_a(p) = (1-p)^n$.
- **Higher $c$ or higher $n$** → plan is **more lenient** (higher $P_a$); **lower $c$** → **stricter**.
- **Standard KTU numerical setup:** $\alpha = 0.05$, $\beta = 0.10$.
- **KTU favourite 7-mark question:** *Derive $P_a(p)$ for single sampling plan and compute $P_a$ for given $n, c, p$.*
- **AOQ (rectifying inspection):** $\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p (N-n)}{N}$.
- **ATI (cost analysis):** $\text{ATI} = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)$.
- **Process Control ≠ Acceptance Sampling:** SPC monitors; sampling decides. **Do not mix them up in exams.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: History of Six Sigma
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** History of Six Sigma # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Six Sigma** is a data-driven, customer-focused, and statistically-grounded quality management methodology that seeks to eliminate defects in any process by identifying and removing the root causes of variation and improving process capability to a level of **3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**. The term "Sigma" refers to the Greek letter **σ (sigma)**, the standard statistical measure of standard deviation. In a Six Sigma process, the process spread (variation) is contained within **±6 standard deviations** from the process mean, leaving a buffer of **1.5 standard deviations** to account for long-term process drift. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Six Sigma as a Philosophy:** Beyond being a statistical target, Six Sigma is a holistic improvement philosophy combining **statistical process control**, **project management discipline (DMAIC / DMADV)**, and **human resource development (champion, master black belt, black belt, green belt roles)** to drive breakthrough bottom-line results. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **chef in a restaurant** preparing a signature dish. The recipe says the dish should weigh **250 grams**. In an ordinary process, the dish might sometimes weigh 220 g, sometimes 280 g — your customers complain when portions are off. In a **Six Sigma restaurant**, you have: - Tightly controlled ovens (process inputs). - Standardized measuring tools (statistical measurement). - Highly trained chefs (Green Belts / Black Belts). - A control room that monitors every plate weight (control charts). - A goal where the plate is **never more than a tiny fraction off** — so rare that **only 3.4 plates in a million** go out wrong. This is the essence of Six Sigma: **near-perfect performance through disciplined, statistical control of variation.** ## 1.3 Key Historical Metrics in Bold - **Motorola (1986):** Year of birth of Six Sigma - **3.4 DPMO:** Standard Six Sigma quality level - **$1.4 Billion (claimed savings by Motorola, 1986–2000s)** - **Jack Welch (CEO of GE, 1995):** Globalized Six Sigma in Fortune 500 - **DMAIC:** Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control (the operational backbone) > [!NOTE] > **Why "Six" Sigma and not "Three" or "Four"?** > The number 6 represents the **maturity of a process**: higher sigma = fewer defects. A **3-sigma process** has ~66,807 defects per million opportunities, while a **6-sigma process** has only **3.4 DPMO** — a **19,649×** improvement. The choice of "six" reflects Motorola's audacious stretch goal of near-perfection, not just incremental improvement. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Eras of Quality (Foundational Context) Before Six Sigma existed, three quality gurus built the intellectual foundation on which Motorola constructed Six Sigma. | Era | Period | Philosophy | Key Contributor(s) | Defining Idea | |-----|--------|------------|--------------------|----------------| | **Quality Inspection** | Pre-1950 | Find & sort defects | Walter Shewhart (Statistical Quality Control foundations) | Inspection as a check, not prevention | | **Quality Control** | 1950–1970 | Control the process | W. Edwards Deming, Joseph Juran | Statistical Process Control (SPC), 14 Points for Management | | **Quality Assurance** | 1970–1990 | Build quality in | Genichi Taguchi (loss function), Philip Crosby (zero defects) | Prevention-based design, cost of poor quality | | **Strategic Quality (Six Sigma)** | 1986 – Present | Eliminate variation through data | Bill Smith, Bob Galvin, Jack Welch | DMAIC, DPMO, project-by-project execution | > [!IMPORTANT] > Six Sigma is **NOT** a replacement for TQM (Total Quality Management). It is a **more disciplined, project-driven, financially-accountable** evolution of TQM with strict statistical rigor. TQM focused on culture change; Six Sigma focused on **measurable, dollar-quantified project results.** ## 2.2 The Motorola Era (1986–1995): Birth of Six Sigma ### 2.2.1 The Catalyst — Bill Smith (1938–2001) - Senior engineer and **Vice President** of Quality at **Motorola's Communications Division** in 1986. - Recognized a direct relationship between **product defects in the field (customer-reported failures)** and the number of **defects produced during manufacturing**. - Proposed that **defect rates as low as 3.4 per million opportunities** were economically achievable — a radical notion when Motorola was operating at **5-sigma (~233 DPMO)** quality levels. - The **3.4 DPMO** figure was derived assuming a **1.5σ long-term mean shift** — a pragmatic engineering adjustment documented in original Motorola white papers. ### 2.2.2 The Champion — Bob Galvin (CEO of Motorola, 1959–1990) - Son of founder Paul Galvin. - In 1986, galvanized Motorola's leadership around a **"5×" quality goal**: reduce defects by a factor of 5 over 5 years. - Committed the entire corporation (~$15M initial investment in training) to Six Sigma. - Within **2 years (by 1988)**, Motorola had achieved **10× improvement** — exceeding the 5× goal. - Cumulative reported savings: **~$1.4 billion** between 1986 and early 1990s. ### 2.2.3 Recognition — The Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award - Motorola won the first **Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award (1988)** in part due to its Six Sigma deployment. - This award, administered by **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology, USA)**, brought Six Sigma to national attention. ## 2.3 The AlliedSignal Era (1990s): Proving the Methodology - **Larry Bossidy**, CEO of AlliedSignal (1991–2000), partnered with **GE's Jack Welch** to scale Six Sigma across multiple industries (aerospace, chemicals, automotive). - AlliedSignal publicly claimed **>$1.5 billion in savings** over 4 years of Six Sigma rollout. - Critical innovation: **project-based execution** with **Green Belts / Black Belts** as full-time problem solvers. - AlliedSignal's success validated that Six Sigma was **not industry-specific** — it was portable across manufacturing, services, and transactions. ## 2.4 The General Electric Era (1995–2000s): Globalizing Six Sigma - **Jack Welch (CEO of GE, 1981–2001)** launched Six Sigma at GE on **January 1, 1995**. - Stated publicly: *"Six Sigma is the most important initiative GE has ever undertaken."* - GE's reported cumulative savings by 2000: **>$10 billion**. - GE's contribution to the methodology: integrating Six Sigma with **e-business** and creating the **"Six Sigma + Digital"** framework. - GE also popularized the **"Champion"** role — senior executives who own and sponsor projects. ## 2.5 The Lean Six Sigma Era (2000s – Present) - **Michael George** and **Peter Vincent** in the early 2000s recognized that Six Sigma was strong at **reducing variation** but weak at **reducing waste**. - **Lean Manufacturing** (pioneered by **Toyota Production System, Taiichi Ohno**) focused on **eliminating non-value-adding activities (muda)**. - The fusion — **Lean Six Sigma** — combined **Lean's speed & waste elimination** with **Six Sigma's statistical depth**. - Today, certifications are offered by bodies such as **ASQ (American Society for Quality)**, **IASSC**, and consulting firms. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes | |---|---------|----------------------|---------------| | 1 | DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities) | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Number of Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6$ | Defects / million | | 2 | Process Sigma (with 1.5σ shift) | $\sigma = Z_{\text{Yield}} + 1.5$ where $Z$ from normal tables | Sigma level | | 3 | Yield (Rolled Throughput Yield) | $\text{Yield} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Decimal fraction | | 4 | First-Time Yield (FTY) | $\text{FTY} = \dfrac{\text{Units passed at a step}}{\text{Units entered at the step}}$ | Decimal fraction | | 5 | Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \text{FTY}_i$ | Decimal fraction | | 6 | Defect Rate | $\text{Defect Rate} = 1 - Y$ | Proportion | | 7 | Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Prevention}$ | Currency | | 8 | Six Sigma Defect Level | **3.4 DPMO** (with 1.5σ shift) | ppm | | 9 | 5σ Defect Level | **233 DPMO** | ppm | | 10 | 4σ Defect Level | **6,210 DPMO** | ppm | | 11 | 3σ Defect Level | **66,807 DPMO** | ppm | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU exams, memorize the sigma-to-DPMO table and the 1.5σ shift convention.** It is a high-frequency short-answer question. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Six Sigma is today applied in: - **Semiconductor manufacturing** (TSMC, Intel — defect density at wafer level). - **Aerospace** (Boeing, Airbus — FMEA, DMAIC on assembly lines). - **Healthcare** (reducing surgical site infections, patient wait times). - **Banking & IT** (transaction error reduction, call center FCR). - **Automotive** (Toyota, Ford, Hyundai — Lean Six Sigma on production lines). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the 3.4 DPMO Number (with 1.5σ Shift) This is the single most important calculation KTU students must be able to demonstrate. ### Step 1 — Define the Ideal Six Sigma Boundary In an ideal (no-shift) process, the specification limits are placed at **±6σ** from the mean, assuming a centered process. The probability of a part falling **outside ±6σ** in a normal distribution is given by: $$ P(X < -6\sigma) + P(X > +6\sigma) = 2 \times P\left(Z > 6\right) $$ where $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ is the standard normal variate. ### Step 2 — Look Up the Tail Probability From standard normal tables, the probability beyond $Z = 6$ is: $$ P(Z > 6) = 9.8659 \times 10^{-10} $$ So the two-sided tail probability (no shift) is: $$ \begin{aligned} P(\text{Defect, no shift}) &= 2 \times 9.8659 \times 10^{-10} \\ &= 1.97318 \times 10^{-9} \end{aligned} $$ Converting to DPMO: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DPMO}_{\text{no shift}} &= 1.97318 \times 10^{-9} \times 10^6 \\ &= 0.00197 \text{ DPMO} \quad \text{(negligibly small)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply the 1.5σ Long-Term Shift Adjustment Real processes are **not perfectly centered**. The mean drifts over time due to tool wear, material variability, and environmental factors. Motorola empirically determined that an **average drift of 1.5σ** is a reasonable long-term assumption. So instead of measuring defects from $\pm 6\sigma$, the effective distance from the nearest spec limit is: $$ \begin{aligned} Z_{\text{effective, USL}} &= 6 - 1.5 = 4.5 \\ Z_{\text{effective, LSL}} &= -6 + 1.5 = -4.5 \end{aligned} $$ The new tail probability is computed using $Z = 4.5$: $$ P(Z > 4.5) = 3.3977 \times 10^{-6} $$ ### Step 4 — Two-Sided Tail Probability (the famous 3.4 DPMO) $$ \begin{aligned} P(\text{Defect, with 1.5 shift}) &= 2 \times 3.3977 \times 10^{-6} \\ &= 6.7954 \times 10^{-6} \end{aligned} $$ Converting to DPMO: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DPMO}_{\text{1.5 shift}} &= 6.7954 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^6 \\ &= 6.7954 \text{ DPMO} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wait — the textbook number is 3.4 DPMO, not 6.8 DPMO!** > Convention: Six Sigma conventions use **single-sided tails** because one of the two tails is often the only meaningful defect direction (e.g., a pharmaceutical cannot have *too much* active ingredient and *too little* active ingredient both as defects — usually one matters). The **3.3977 DPMO** is the standard single-sided figure, conventionally rounded to **3.4 DPMO**. $$ \boxed{\text{Six Sigma Quality Level} = 3.4 \text{ DPMO (with 1.5σ long-term mean shift)}} $$ ## 3.2 Sigma Level Lookup Table (Full) | Sigma Level | DPMO (with 1.5σ shift) | Yield (%) | |:-----------:|:----------------------:|:---------:| | 1.0σ | 690,000 | 30.85% | | 2.0σ | 308,537 | 69.15% | | 3.0σ | 66,807 | 93.32% | | 4.0σ | 6,210 | 99.379% | | 5.0σ | 233 | 99.977% | | **6.0σ** | **3.4** | **99.9997%** | ## 3.3 Historical Timeline (Analytical Matrix) | Year | Event | Key Person | Significance | |------|-------|-----------|---------------| | 1924 | W.A. Shewhart introduces control charts at Bell Labs | Walter Shewhart | Statistical roots of variation | | 1950 | Deming lectures in Japan on SQC | W.
