Edwards Deming | Japan adopts statistical quality | | 1970s | Japan dominates quality (Sony, Toyota) | — | Quality becomes a competitive weapon | | 1979 | "Quality is Free" published | Philip Crosby | Zero Defects philosophy | | 1986 | Bill Smith drafts the Six Sigma concept at Motorola | Bill Smith | Formal birth of Six Sigma | | 1986 | Bob Galvin commits Motorola to "5×" quality goal | Bob Galvin | Corporate top-down adoption | | 1988 | Motorola wins Malcolm Baldrige Award | Motorola | National recognition | | 1991 | AlliedSignal adopts Six Sigma under Larry Bossidy | Larry Bossidy | First major cross-industry adoption | | 1995 | Jack Welch launches Six Sigma at GE | Jack Welch | Globalization of Six Sigma | | 2000s | Lean Six Sigma emerges (Lean + Six Sigma) | Michael George | Methodology fusion | | 2000s–present | AI/ML integration with Six Sigma | — | Industry 4.0 applications | | 2010s | DFSS (Design for Six Sigma) becomes mainstream | — | Built-in quality, not inspected quality | | 2020s | Digital Six Sigma, Big Data, Predictive Quality | — | Data-driven quality 4.0 | ## 3.4 Why Six Sigma "Click"ed — Analytical Insight The success of Six Sigma in the 1990s was not just statistical — it was a **business case**: - **DMAIC forced financial accountability:** every project had a "Black Belt" who reported a verified dollar savings. - **Champion structure aligned middle management** with CEO-level strategic goals. - **Belt hierarchy created a career path** (White → Yellow → Green → Black → Master Black Belt) similar to martial arts — culturally engaging. - **Linkage to strategic KPIs:** Six Sigma projects were tied to company-level P&L (profit and loss), not departmental cost centers. This business discipline is what **TQM lacked** in the 1980s and is the principal reason Six Sigma **survived and scaled** where TQM's culture-only approach diluted over time. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Historical Evolution Flow ```mermaid graph TD A[Shewhart 1924
Statistical Quality Control] --> B[Deming and Juran 1950s
TQM Foundation] B --> C[Crosby 1979
Zero Defects Philosophy] C --> D[Bill Smith 1986
Six Sigma Concept at Motorola] D --> E[Bob Galvin 1986
Motorola Corporate Rollout] E --> F[Malcolm Baldrige Award 1988] F --> G[Larry Bossidy 1991
AlliedSignal Adoption] G --> H[Jack Welch 1995
General Electric Globalization] H --> I[Lean Six Sigma 2000s
Methodology Fusion] I --> J[Digital Six Sigma 2020s
AI and Big Data Integration] ``` ## 4.2 Six Sigma Belt Hierarchy (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD CEO[CEO and Senior Leadership] --> CHAMP[Champions
Executive Sponsors and Project Owners] CHAMP --> MBB[Master Black Belts
Mentors and Methodology Experts] MBB --> BB[Black Belts
Full Time Project Leaders] BB --> GB[Green Belts
Part Time Team Members] GB --> YB[Yellow Belts
Awareness Level] YB --> WB[White Belts
Basic Familiarity] BB -. leads .-> DMAIC[DMAIC Projects
Define Measure Analyze Improve Control] MBB -. mentors .-> DMAIC CHAMP -. sponsors .-> DMAIC ``` ## 4.3 DMAIC Framework Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR D1[Define Problem
Project Charter and VOC] --> M1[Measure Current State
Data Collection and Capability] M1 --> A1[Analyze Root Causes
Hypothesis Testing and Fishbone] A1 --> I1[Improve Process
Pilot and DOE] I1 --> C1[Control Sustain
Control Plan and SPC] C1 --> D1 ``` ## 4.4 Six Sigma Adoption Cascade (Industry-Wide) ```mermaid graph TD M[1986 Motorola Manufacturing] --> A[1991 AlliedSignal Aerospace and Chemicals] A --> G[1995 General Electric Conglomerate] G --> H[Honeywell] G --> ABB[ABB Industrial] G --> T[Toyota Production Integration] G --> SVC[Service Industries Banks and Healthcare 2000s] T --> LSS[Lean Six Sigma Synthesis] SVC --> LSS LSS --> D4[Digital Six Sigma 4.0
AI and Industry 4.0] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip for Exams:** When asked to draw "the evolution of Six Sigma," always mark **Bill Smith (1986)** as the formal origin and **Jack Welch (1995)** as the globalization event. These two dates are the most frequently asked. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Explain the origin of Six Sigma at Motorola. Who is regarded as the "Father of Six Sigma"?** **Model Answer:** Six Sigma originated at **Motorola** in **1986**. It was conceptualized by **Bill Smith**, a Senior Engineer and Vice President of Quality at Motorola's Communications Division. Smith identified that defects produced during manufacturing directly translated into field failures reported by customers. He proposed reducing defects to a level of **3.4 per million opportunities (DPMO)** by focusing on statistical variation reduction. **Bob Galvin**, then CEO of Motorola, championed and institutionalized the methodology across the corporation, committing resources and establishing the **"5×" quality improvement goal** in five years — a target that was surpassed by 1988. Motorola later won the **Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award in 1988**, bringing Six Sigma to international attention. **Bill Smith is therefore regarded as the "Father of Six Sigma."** ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` **What is meant by the "1.5σ shift" in Six Sigma? Why is it incorporated?** **Model Answer:** The **1.5σ shift** is a long-term mean-drift adjustment incorporated into Six Sigma quality calculations to account for **real-world process variability**. In an ideal centered process, the mean equals the nominal target and spec limits lie at ±6σ. However, in practice, processes drift over time due to tool wear, material lot variation, environmental changes, and operator differences. Motorola empirically determined an **average drift of 1.5σ** over the long term. To incorporate this, the effective distance from the mean to the nearest spec limit becomes **±4.5σ** instead of ±6σ. This yields a defect rate of **3.4 DPMO** (single-sided), which is the **de facto Six Sigma quality benchmark**. Without this adjustment, the calculated defect rate would be **0.002 DPMO** — an unachievable and impractical target for any real process. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — **Module 4 Internal Choice Option A** **Discuss in detail the historical evolution of Six Sigma. Explain the contributions of Bill Smith, Bob Galvin, Jack Welch, and Larry Bossidy to its development and global adoption. (14 Marks)** #### Part (a) — Bill Smith and the Birth at Motorola (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** The origin of Six Sigma is traced to **Motorola, USA, in 1986**, when **Bill Smith**, a Senior Engineer and Vice President of Quality in Motorola's Communications Division, drafted the conceptual framework of the methodology. At that time, Motorola's products were operating at quality levels equivalent to approximately **5-sigma (~233 DPMO)**. Smith, through rigorous statistical analysis of customer-returned products, established a **direct correlation between in-process defects and field failures** — an idea revolutionary at the time when most quality programs focused on inspection at the end of the line. Smith's core insight was twofold: 1. **Quality must be measured in monetary terms** — a defect is not an abstract failure but a financial liability. 2. **Variation, not defects themselves, is the true enemy of quality.** Eliminating variation eliminates defects. He set an audacious target — **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** — corresponding to a **±6σ process** with a long-term mean shift of 1.5σ. #### Part (b) — Galvin, Bossidy, Welch and Global Adoption (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Bob Galvin**, CEO of Motorola (1959–1990), provided the executive sponsorship that converted Smith's concept into a corporate reality. In 1986, he announced a company-wide **"5× Quality Goal"** — reduce defects 5-fold in 5 years. Motorola exceeded this within 2 years, achieving a 10× improvement, and reported cumulative savings of **~$1.4 billion** by the early 1990s. Motorola's success earned the **Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award in 1988**, which brought Six Sigma to national visibility. **Larry Bossidy**, CEO of **AlliedSignal (1991–2000)**, validated the portability of Six Sigma beyond electronics manufacturing into aerospace and chemicals. AlliedSignal claimed **>$1.5 billion in savings** over 4 years and, critically, established the **Champion-Black Belt-Green Belt hierarchy** still in use today. **Jack Welch**, CEO of **General Electric (1981–2001)**, launched Six Sigma at GE on **January 1, 1995**, famously declaring it *"the most important initiative GE has ever undertaken."* GE reported cumulative savings exceeding **$10 billion by 2000** and was instrumental in spreading Six Sigma to **Honeywell, ABB, Ford, Toyota**, and eventually the service sector. Welch's contribution was the **strategic alignment of Six Sigma projects with corporate P&L goals**, making the methodology a boardroom-level concern rather than a shop-floor initiative. The combined effect of these leaders transformed Six Sigma from a Motorola-internal project into the **dominant quality philosophy of the late 20th and early 21st centuries**, eventually evolving into **Lean Six Sigma** and the contemporary **Digital Six Sigma 4.0** paradigm. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` — **Module 4 Internal Choice Option B** **With the help of a suitable derivation, explain why Six Sigma quality corresponds to 3.4 DPMO. Also briefly describe the Lean Six Sigma synthesis. (14 Marks)** #### Part (a) — Derivation of 3.4 DPMO (7 Marks) **Model Answer (Step-by-Step):** **Step 1:** Define the process output variable $X$ as normally distributed with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. **Step 2:** In a **centered Six Sigma process** (no shift), specification limits are placed at $\mu \pm 6\sigma$. The tail probability of a defect (part outside the limits) is: $$ P(\text{Defect}) = 2 \times P(Z > 6) $$ From standard normal tables: $$ P(Z > 6) = 9.8659 \times 10^{-10} $$ So, in the no-shift ideal case: $$ \begin{aligned} P(\text{Defect}) &= 2 \times 9.8659 \times 10^{-10} \\ &= 1.97318 \times 10^{-9} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** This corresponds to $\approx 0.002$ DPMO — practically unattainable. To account for **real-world long-term mean drift of 1.5σ** (motor wear, material variation, environmental shifts), the effective distance to the spec limit becomes $\pm 4.5\sigma$: $$ Z_{\text{effective}} = 6 - 1.5 = 4.5 $$ **Step 4:** Looking up $P(Z > 4.5)$: $$ P(Z > 4.5) = 3.3977 \times 10^{-6} $$ **Step 5:** Converting to DPMO (single-sided convention, as is standard in Six Sigma practice): $$ \text{DPMO} = 3.3977 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^6 = 3.4 \text{ DPMO} $$ Hence, **Six Sigma quality = 3.4 DPMO**, a 19,649× improvement over 3-sigma operations. #### Part (b) — Lean Six Sigma Synthesis (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Lean Manufacturing**, pioneered by the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** under **Taiichi Ohno**, focused on **eliminating waste (muda)** — overproduction, waiting, transport, over-processing, inventory, motion, and defects (the 7 wastes). Lean excels at **speeding up processes** and **reducing non-value-adding activities** but is weak in addressing **statistical variation** as a root cause. **Six Sigma**, by contrast, excels at **reducing variation** through statistical tools (DMAIC, SPC, hypothesis testing) but is sometimes criticized for being **slow, project-heavy, and not focused on waste.** **Lean Six Sigma**, popularized in the early 2000s by **Michael George, Peter Vincent, and others**, fuses both philosophies: $$ \boxed{\text{Lean Six Sigma} = \text{Lean's speed and waste elimination} + \text{Six Sigma's statistical variation reduction}} $$ | Aspect | Lean | Six Sigma | Lean Six Sigma | |--------|------|-----------|----------------| | Primary focus | Speed & waste | Variation & defects | Speed + variation + waste | | Tools | 5S, Kanban, VSM | DMAIC, DOE, SPC | Both toolkits integrated | | Mindset | Flow & pull | Data-driven root cause | Hybrid: pull-based + data-driven | | Best suited for | High-volume repetitive processes | High-complexity processes | Service & manufacturing alike | Today, **Lean Six Sigma is the de facto industry standard** for process improvement, with belt certifications (White, Yellow, Green, Black, Master Black Belt) widely offered by **ASQ, IASSC**, and corporate in-house academies. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Ways Students Lose Marks on Six Sigma History Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing Bill Smith and Bob Galvin's roles:** Bill Smith = conceptual author; Bob Galvin = executive sponsor. Marks are deducted when these are conflated. > 2. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift derivation:** Memorizing 3.4 DPMO without the 4.5σ effective distance derivation will cost the 7-mark derivation sub-question. > 3. **Omitting the year 1986:** Every history-of-Six-Sigma answer must begin with "1986 at Motorola." This is the KTU board's expected anchor date. > 4. **Confusing Lean Six Sigma with Six Sigma:** Lean Six Sigma = Lean + Six Sigma fusion (post-2000s); not a synonym. > 5. **Not stating the figure in DPMO correctly:** 3.4 DPMO is **single-sided** with a **1.5σ shift**, not two-sided without shift (which gives 0.002 DPMO). > 6. **Ignoring AlliedSignal in the timeline:** Larry Bossidy's role at AlliedSignal is the bridge between Motorola (1986) and GE (1995) and is a frequent sub-part. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — History of Six Sigma** - **Origin Year:** **1986** at **Motorola**, USA - **Father of Six Sigma:** **Bill Smith** (Senior Engineer, VP Quality, Communications Division) - **Executive Champion:** **Bob Galvin** (CEO of Motorola, 1959–1990) - **First Cross-Industry Adoption:** **AlliedSignal** (1991, **Larry Bossidy**) - **Globalization Event:** **General Electric** launch on **January 1, 1995** by **Jack Welch** - **GE's Cumulative Savings:** **>$10 billion** by 2000 - **Motorola's Reported Savings:** **~$1.4 billion** (1986–1990s) - **Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award:** Won by Motorola in **1988** - **Six Sigma Quality Level:** **3.4 DPMO** (with 1.5σ long-term shift) - **1.5σ Shift Convention:** Empirical Motorola adjustment for real-world mean drift - **Six Sigma Methodology:** **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) - **Belt Hierarchy:** **White → Yellow → Green → Black → Master Black Belt**, with **Champions** as executive sponsors - **Lean Six Sigma:** Fusion of **Lean Manufacturing (TPS / Taiichi Ohno)** and **Six Sigma (Bill Smith)**; emerged in early 2000s via **Michael George** and others - **Three Quality Eras Preceding Six Sigma:** Inspection (Shewhart) → Quality Control (Deming, Juran) → Quality Assurance (Taguchi, Crosby) - **Statistical Heritage:** Built on **Walter Shewhart's SPC**, **Deming's 14 Points**, and **Juran's quality trilogy** (planning, control, improvement) - **Modern Extension:** **DFSS (Design for Six Sigma)** using **DMADV** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify) — for new products/processes - **Digital Six Sigma 4.0:** Contemporary integration with **AI, Machine Learning, Big Data, IoT sensors, and predictive analytics** > **Final Exam Tip:** Always structure Six Sigma history answers in **three time-blocks**: (1) Motorola era 1986, (2) AlliedSignal/GE era 1990s, (3) Lean Six Sigma / Digital era 2000s–present. This chronological scaffolding earns full structure marks with the KTU board examiner.

topics.title: Working of Six Sigma
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Working of Six Sigma

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Six Sigma?

Six Sigma is a **data-driven, disciplined methodology and statistical quality improvement philosophy** aimed at eliminating defects and reducing process variation to an extremely low level. It was developed at **Motorola** in **1986** by engineer **Bill Smith** and was later popularized globally by **General Electric (GE)** under **Jack Welch** in 1995.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Six Sigma is a structured, statistical process improvement methodology that seeks to reduce process variation so that a process operates with no more than **3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**, corresponding to a process capability of ±6 standard deviations (±6σ) from the process mean to the nearest specification limit.*

In simpler terms, Six Sigma asks: *"How can we design a process so that it almost never makes a mistake?"* — a "mistake" or defect being any output that falls outside the customer's specification limits.

## 1.2 The Core Philosophy: σ = Standard Deviation

The Greek letter **sigma (σ)** represents the **standard deviation** of a process — a statistical measure of how much variation exists around the average (mean) output.

| Sigma Level | Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO) | Yield / Success Rate |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **2σ** | 308,537 DPMO | 69.1% |
| **3σ** | 66,807 DPMO | 93.3% |
| **4σ** | 6,210 DPMO | 99.38% |
| **5σ** | 233 DPMO | 99.977% |
| **6σ** | **3.4 DPMO** | **99.99966%** |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The Airplane Baggage System

Imagine a massive airport that handles **10 million bags per year**.
- At **2σ** quality, **3,085,370 bags** would be lost or misrouted annually — a chaos.
- At **4σ** quality, only **62,100 bags** would be mishandled.
- At **6σ** quality, only **3.4 bags** out of every million would be mishandled — practically perfect.

Six Sigma, therefore, is the engineering pursuit of "near-perfect" processes. Just as a doctor aims for zero diagnostic errors, a Six Sigma engineer aims for a process that operates within a window so wide (12σ wide) that defects are statistically almost impossible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Six Sigma is not just a quality level — it is a **management strategy** combining **statistical tools**, **project management discipline**, and **human resource development** (Green Belts, Black Belts, Master Black Belts).

## 1.4 The Famous "1.5 Sigma Shift"

A critical concept that distinguishes Six Sigma from classical statistics is the **1.5σ shift**. Long-term studies at Motorola showed that real processes drift over time. To account for this natural drift, Six Sigma defines its goal as **±6σ from mean to specification limit**, but statistically allows for a **1.5σ drift** in the process mean.

This means a "Six Sigma process" is statistically equivalent to short-term performance of **4.5σ** (i.e., 6σ − 1.5σ = 4.5σ), which corresponds to **3.4 DPMO**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Working Principle: Reducing Variation

The fundamental working of Six Sigma rests on a single principle: **a process produces fewer defects when its variation (σ) is small relative to the specification width (USL − LSL).**

Mathematically, this is captured by the **Process Capability Index (Cpk)**, which a Six Sigma process must satisfy:

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right) \geq 2.0$$

For a true Six Sigma process, the distance from the mean to the nearest specification limit must be at least **6σ** (which is twice the classical 3σ capability requirement, hence Cpk ≥ 2).

## 2.2 The DMAIC Roadmap: How Six Sigma Actually Works

Six Sigma projects are executed through a structured five-phase cycle called **DMAIC**. This is the operational backbone of the methodology.

### Phase 1: DEFINE
- Identify the **problem**, the **customer (CTQ — Critical-to-Quality)**, and the **project scope**.
- Develop a **Project Charter**.
- Tools: SIPOC diagram, Voice of Customer (VOC), CTQ Tree, Project Charter.

### Phase 2: MEASURE
- Quantify the **current performance baseline** (current σ level, current DPMO).
- Establish a **data collection plan**.
- Tools: Process mapping, Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone) diagram, Gauge R&R, Capability analysis (Cp, Cpk).

### Phase 3: ANALYZE
- Identify the **root causes** of variation and defects.
- Use statistical tools to separate **common causes** from **special causes** of variation.
- Tools: Hypothesis testing, Regression analysis, ANOVA, Pareto charts, 5-Whys analysis.

### Phase 4: IMPROVE
- Design, test, and implement **solutions** to eliminate root causes.
- Optimize the process to remove sources of variation.
- Tools: Design of Experiments (DOE), Taguchi methods, Pilot runs, Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing).

### Phase 5: CONTROL
- **Sustain the gains** achieved in the Improve phase.
- Lock down the new process with controls, SOPs, and monitoring systems.
- Tools: Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts, Control plans, FMEA, Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why DMAIC Matters for KTU Exams:** Every Six Sigma question in the KTU ESE is essentially a DMAIC question. Memorize the **5 phases in order** and **2-3 tools per phase**. This is the most frequently asked sub-question.

## 2.3 Calculating the Sigma Level (Step by Step)

The working of Six Sigma requires engineers to **measure** and **quantify** defects. The standard procedure is:

**Step 1: Count the defects (D)** observed over a period.

**Step 2: Count the number of opportunities (O)** per unit (i.e., the number of ways a defect can occur in one unit).

**Step 3: Compute total opportunities** = (Number of units) × (Opportunities per unit).

**Step 4: Compute DPMO:**

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6$$

**Step 5: Convert DPMO to Sigma Level** using a **Sigma Conversion Table** (also called a Yield-to-Sigma table).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In manufacturing (e.g., semiconductor fabrication, automotive assembly), DPMO tracking directly drives yield and profitability. In service industries (BPO, banking), DPMO is applied to errors per transaction. The **Six Sigma Black Belt certification** is a globally recognized credential in this field.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula or Definition | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | Same as variable | Measures process spread |
| **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6$ | Defects per million opps. | D = Defects, U = Units, O = Opportunities |
| **Yield** | $Y = 1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6}$ | Fraction (0 to 1) | Also called "First Pass Yield" |
| **Process Capability (Cp)** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensionless | Centered process measure |
| **Process Capability (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma},\dfrac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Dimensionless | Off-center corrected measure |
| **Six Sigma Threshold** | $C_{pk} \geq 2.0$ | Dimensionless | With 1.5σ shift |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod Y_i$ | Fraction | For multi-stage processes |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Yield to Sigma Level (Theoretical Foundation)

Six Sigma's statistical foundation rests on the **normal distribution**. The probability of a defect is the area in the tails of the curve beyond the specification limits.

For a perfectly centered process (no 1.5σ shift), the area under the normal curve within ±kσ of the mean is given by:

$$P(-k\sigma \leq X - \mu \leq k\sigma) = 2\Phi(k) - 1$$

where $\Phi(k)$ is the standard normal cumulative distribution function (CDF).

Therefore, the **Defect Rate (probability of being outside limits)** is:

$$p_{\text{defect}} = 1 - [2\Phi(k) - 1] = 2[1 - \Phi(k)] = 2\Phi(-k)$$

**Conversion to DPMO:**

$$\text{DPMO} = p_{\text{defect}} \times 10^6$$

**With the 1.5σ shift**, the effective k value becomes $(k - 1.5)$. So a nominal "6σ process" is statistically equivalent to a short-term $4.5\sigma$ process.

$$p_{\text{defect, shifted}} = 2[1 - \Phi(4.5)]$$

Using standard normal tables, $\Phi(4.5) \approx 0.9999966$, hence:

$$p_{\text{defect}} \approx 2(0.0000034) = 6.8 \times 10^{-6}$$

Wait — we are computing one tail, so:

$$p_{\text{defect, one side}} = 1 - \Phi(4.5) \approx 3.4 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\text{DPMO} = 3.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^6 = 3.4 \text{ DPMO}$$

This derivation is the mathematical proof behind Six Sigma's signature number: **3.4 defects per million opportunities.**

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A semiconductor manufacturing line produces microchips. In one shift, **500 units** are produced. Each chip has **10 opportunities** for a defect (pin alignment, solder joint, plating thickness, etc.). During the shift, **45 defects** are observed.

**Find: (a) DPMO, (b) Sigma Level, (c) First Pass Yield.**

### Solution:

**(a) DPMO Calculation:**

$$\text{DPO} = \frac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}} = \frac{45}{500 \times 10} = \frac{45}{5000} = 0.009$$

Converting to DPMO:

$$\text{DPMO} = 0.009 \times 10^6 = 9000 \text{ DPMO}$$

**(b) Sigma Level — Using the Standard Conversion Table:**

| DPMO Range | Sigma Level |
| :--- | :---: |
| $\leq$ 3.4 | 6.0 |
| 233 | 5.0 |
| 6,210 | 4.0 |
| 66,807 | 3.0 |
| 308,537 | 2.0 |

A DPMO of **9,000** falls between 3σ (66,807) and 4σ (6,210), so the process operates at approximately **3.8σ** (interpolated value from standard tables).

**(c) First Pass Yield (FPY):**

$$\text{FPY} = 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6} = 1 - \frac{9000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.991 = 99.1\%$$

This means **99.1% of opportunities produce a defect-free result** — a good baseline for a 3.8σ process, but far from the Six Sigma target of 99.99966%.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Where Six Sigma Works Best

| Industry | Application Example | Typical Baseline | Six Sigma Target |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Semiconductors** | Wafer defect density | 4σ (6210 DPMO) | 6σ (3.4 DPMO) |
| **Banking/Finance** | Transaction errors | 3.5σ (~20,000 DPMO) | 6σ (3.4 DPMO) |
| **Healthcare** | Prescription errors | 3σ (~66,800 DPMO) | 6σ (3.4 DPMO) |
| **BPO/Call Centers** | Call resolution errors | 3σ | 6σ |
| **Aerospace** | Component failure rate | 5σ (233 DPMO) | 6σ |

## 3.4 Concept-to-Computation Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The most common KTU question on Six Sigma "working" is: *"Given defect data, calculate the process sigma level."* Master the DPMO formula and the conversion table. There is no "direct" formula for sigma from DPMO — you **must use the conversion table**, and the examiner will provide it in the question paper.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The DMAIC Workflow — Master Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram captures the complete operational flow of a Six Sigma project, including the inputs, deliverables, and exit criteria of each phase. This is the single most important visual to internalize for KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation: Customer CTQ Identified] --> B
    B[PHASE 1: DEFINE] --> B1[Deliverable: Project Charter]
    B1 --> B2[Tool: SIPOC Diagram]
    B2 --> B3[Tool: Voice of Customer VOC]
    B3 --> C
    C[PHASE 2: MEASURE] --> C1[Deliverable: Baseline Sigma Level]
    C1 --> C2[Tool: Process Mapping]
    C2 --> C3[Tool: Gauge R and R Study]
    C3 --> C4[Tool: Capability Analysis Cp Cpk]
    C4 --> D
    D[PHASE 3: ANALYZE] --> D1[Deliverable: Validated Root Causes]
    D1 --> D2[Tool: Fishbone Ishikawa Diagram]
    D2 --> D3[Tool: Hypothesis Testing t-test ANOVA]
    D3 --> D4[Tool: Pareto 80-20 Analysis]
    D4 --> E
    E[PHASE 4: IMPROVE] --> E1[Deliverable: Pilot Solution Validated]
    E1 --> E2[Tool: Design of Experiments DOE]
    E2 --> E3[Tool: Poka-Yoke Mistake Proofing]
    E3 --> E4[Tool: Taguchi Robust Design]
    E4 --> F
    F[PHASE 5: CONTROL] --> F1[Deliverable: Control Plan Finalized]
    F1 --> F2[Tool: SPC Control Charts X-bar R]
    F2 --> F3[Tool: FMEA Failure Mode Analysis]
    F3 --> F4[Tool: SOPs and Visual Standards]
    F4 --> G[Project Closure: Sustained Sigma Level Documented]
    G --> H{Next CTQ Problem Identified?}
    H -->|Yes| A
    H -->|No| I[Process Standardized: Six Sigma Culture Established]
```

## 4.2 Six Sigma Roles and Hierarchy — Organizational Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J[CHAMPION - Senior Leader] --> K[MASTER BLACK BELT - Six Sigma Expert Mentor]
    K --> L[BLACK BELT - Full Time Project Leader]
    L --> M[GREEN BELT - Part Time Team Member]
    M --> N[TEAM MEMBER - Process Worker]
    J --> L
    K --> M
    L --> O[Project Completion: Defects Eliminated Sigma Improved]
```

## 4.3 The Variation Reduction Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Wide Process Distribution sigma High] --> Q1[Many outputs exceed Spec Limits: HIGH DPMO]
    P2[Narrow Process Distribution sigma Low Six Sigma Goal] --> Q2[Few outputs exceed Spec Limits: 3.4 DPMO]
    P1 -->|DMAIC Cycle Reduces Variation| P2
    Q1 -->|Target: Reduce Defects| Q2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation:** Both curves are centered on the same mean (μ), but the right-hand curve has a dramatically smaller σ. This is the visual essence of Six Sigma's working — **shrink the spread, not necessarily move the center**.

## 4.4 Data-to-Decision Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Raw Process Data Collected] --> R2[Data Validation and Cleansing]
    R2 --> R3[Statistical Analysis: Cp Cpk DPMO]
    R3 --> R4[Sigma Level Computed]
    R4 --> R5{Is Sigma at least 6 with 1.5 sigma shift?}
    R5 -->|Yes| R6[Process Certified: Six Sigma Quality]
    R5 -->|No| R7[Root Cause Investigation Initiated]
    R7 --> R8[Corrective Action: DMAIC Cycle Restarted]
    R8 --> R1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Six Sigma. Explain the significance of 3.4 DPMO.
**Model Answer:**
Six Sigma is a data-driven quality improvement methodology that aims to reduce process variation such that the process produces no more than **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. The "3.4 DPMO" figure is derived from the standard normal distribution adjusted for a **1.5σ mean shift**, representing near-perfect quality. It signifies that out of one million opportunities, only 3.4 are expected to result in defects — equivalent to **99.99966% accuracy**. This metric allows organizations to benchmark their process maturity globally and track improvements over time.

### Question 2: State and explain the five phases of DMAIC.
**Model Answer:**
DMAIC is the structured roadmap used in Six Sigma projects. The five phases are:
1. **Define:** Identify the problem, customer, and project scope.
2. **Measure:** Quantify the current process performance (DPMO, sigma level).
3. **Analyze:** Identify root causes of variation and defects.
4. **Improve:** Implement solutions to eliminate root causes.
5. **Control:** Sustain the improvements using SPC and SOPs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "DMAIC" but fail to explain the **deliverable** of each phase. KTU examiners allocate at least **1 mark per phase** for clarity. Always mention what each phase **produces** (e.g., Measure → baseline sigma; Control → control plan).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Worked 14-Mark Problem

**(a) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing company produces electrical switches. In a sample of **1,000 switches**, the following data is collected:

| Defect Type | Count |
| :--- | :---: |
| Loose contact | 12 |
| Damaged casing | 8 |
| Improper labeling | 5 |
| Paint defect | 3 |
| **Total** | **28** |

Each switch has **5 opportunities** for a defect. Calculate: (i) DPO, (ii) DPMO, and (iii) the **Sigma Level** of the process using the standard conversion table. Comment on the process quality.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **DMAIC methodology** in detail. For each phase, list **two tools** commonly used, and explain the role of a **Black Belt** in a Six Sigma project.

---

### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Given:**
- Number of units (U) = 1,000
- Total defects (D) = 12 + 8 + 5 + 3 = 28
- Opportunities per unit (O) = 5

**Step 1: Compute DPO (Defects Per Opportunity)**

$$\text{DPO} = \frac{D}{U \times O} = \frac{28}{1000 \times 5} = \frac{28}{5000} = 0.0056$$

**Step 2: Compute DPMO**

$$\text{DPMO} = 0.0056 \times 10^6 = 5600 \text{ DPMO}$$

**Step 3: Determine the Sigma Level**

Using the standard Six Sigma conversion table:

| DPMO | Sigma Level |
| :---: | :---: |
| 66,807 | 3.0σ |
| 6,210 | 4.0σ |
| 233 | 5.0σ |

Since 5,600 DPMO lies between 4.0σ and 4.5σ, the process operates at approximately **4.0σ to 4.2σ** (interpolation: between 6,210 DPMO at 4σ and 233 at 5σ). Most standard tables place this at **~4.1σ**.

**Step 4: Comment on Process Quality**

A process at **4.0–4.1σ** has a yield of approximately **99.38% to 99.5%**, which is good but not world-class. To reach Six Sigma (3.4 DPMO), the company must reduce defects by a factor of roughly **1,650×**. This is achievable through a structured DMAIC improvement cycle.

---

### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**DMAIC — Detailed Explanation:**

**1. DEFINE Phase:**
- Goal: Frame the business problem, identify the customer, and define the project scope.
- Tools: **Project Charter** (formal document), **SIPOC Diagram** (Suppliers-Inputs-Process-Outputs-Customers).
- Output: Approved project charter with measurable CTQ (Critical-to-Quality) metrics.

**2. MEASURE Phase:**
- Goal: Establish the current process capability and collect baseline data.
- Tools: **Process Mapping** (flowcharts), **Gauge R&R Study** (measurement system validation), Capability Indices (Cp, Cpk).
- Output: Baseline sigma level and validated data collection plan.

**3. ANALYZE Phase:**
- Goal: Identify the root causes of variation and defects.
- Tools: **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** (cause categorization), **Hypothesis Testing** (t-tests, ANOVA), **Pareto Analysis** (80/20 prioritization), **Regression Analysis**.
- Output: A short list of statistically validated root causes (vital few).

**4. IMPROVE Phase:**
- Goal: Generate, test, and implement solutions.
- Tools: **Design of Experiments (DOE)**, **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing), **Taguchi Robust Design**, Pilot trials.
- Output: A pilot-validated solution ready for full deployment.

**5. CONTROL Phase:**
- Goal: Lock in the improvements and prevent regression.
- Tools: **SPC Control Charts** (X-bar, R, p, c charts), **FMEA** (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis), **Control Plan**, **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**.
- Output: Updated control plan and a self-sustaining process.

**Role of a Black Belt:**
A **Black Belt** is a full-time Six Sigma project leader who has completed rigorous training (typically 4–6 weeks of statistics and project management). Black Belts:
- Lead cross-functional project teams through DMAIC.
- Apply advanced statistical tools (DOE, regression, hypothesis testing).
- Coach Green Belts and team members.
- Deliver measurable financial savings (each project typically targets $100,000+ in savings).
- Report progress to Champions and Master Black Belts.

---

### Question B: Alternative 14-Mark Question

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define Six Sigma. Explain the concept of the **1.5σ shift** with a neat sketch of the normal distribution showing specification limits and the shifted mean. How does this shift lead to the figure of **3.4 DPMO**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A process has specification limits **LSL = 95** and **USL = 105**, with process mean **μ = 100** and standard deviation **σ = 1.0**. (i) Calculate Cp and Cpk. (ii) Determine whether the process is capable of Six Sigma quality. (iii) What improvement in σ is required to achieve Six Sigma capability (Cpk ≥ 2.0)?

---

### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Definition:** Six Sigma is a disciplined, data-driven quality methodology that aims to reduce process variation to less than 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO), corresponding to ±6σ between the process mean and the nearest specification limit.

**The 1.5σ Shift Concept:**

In real-world processes, the mean does not remain perfectly stable. Long-term Motorola studies showed that process means drift over time by an average of **1.5σ**. To ensure that even after this drift, the process remains within specification, Six Sigma defines a process as being "6σ capable" only if the **distance from the mean to the nearest specification limit is at least 6σ** (which is 4.5σ + 1.5σ = 6σ).

**Normal Distribution Diagram (textual representation):**

```
        Process Mean (μ)
              |
              v
   ___      ___|___      ___
  /   \    /   |   \    /   \
 /     \  /    |    \  /     \
| LSL  |||   μ  ||| USL  |
        \    |    /
         \___|___/
              |
              |--- 6σ ---|
              |--- 4.5σ ---| (short term)
              |-- 1.5σ --| (drift allowance)
```

**Derivation of 3.4 DPMO:**

After a 1.5σ upward shift, the effective distance from the shifted mean to the USL is 4.5σ. The probability of a defect on one side is:

$$P(X > \mu + 4.5\sigma) = 1 - \Phi(4.5) \approx 3.4 \times 10^{-6}$$

For two-sided specifications (both USL and LSL):

$$\text{Total DPMO} = 2 \times 3.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^6 = 3.4 \text{ DPMO}$$

This is the mathematical basis of the **3.4 DPMO** figure.

---

### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Given:**
- LSL = 95, USL = 105
- μ = 100, σ = 1.0

**Step 1: Calculate Cp**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{105 - 95}{6 \times 1.0} = \frac{10}{6} = 1.667$$

**Step 2: Calculate Cpk**

$$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{105 - 100}{3 \times 1.0} = \frac{5}{3} = 1.667$$

$$C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{100 - 95}{3 \times 1.0} = \frac{5}{3} = 1.667$$

Since the process is centered:

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.667, 1.667) = 1.667$$

**Step 3: Six Sigma Capability Assessment**

Six Sigma quality requires **Cpk ≥ 2.0**. The current process has **Cpk = 1.667**, which corresponds to approximately a **5σ process** (short term). It is **NOT yet at Six Sigma quality**.

**Step 4: Required Improvement in σ**

For Cpk = 2.0 (and process centered):

$$C_{pk} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{\text{new}}} = 2.0$$

$$\sigma_{\text{new}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{12} = \frac{10}{12} = 0.833$$

**Current σ = 1.0**, **Required σ = 0.833**. The process must reduce its standard deviation by:

$$\Delta\sigma = 1.0 - 0.833 = 0.167 \text{ (a 16.7% reduction)}$$

This can be achieved through DMAIC-driven variation reduction (e.g., equipment calibration, operator training, raw material standardization).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **DPMO formula:** Forgetting to multiply by $10^6$ or dividing by opportunities → lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Sigma level:** Writing "the process is at 4 sigma" without showing interpolation from the conversion table → lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **DMAIC:** Listing the phases in the wrong order (e.g., Analyze before Measure) → lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Cpk vs Cp:** Confusing the two. Cpk accounts for off-center; Cp does not. Always state which one applies to a centered process.
> 5. **1.5σ shift:** Treating it as a real physical shift rather than a statistical allowance. Examiners will deduct marks if you do not mention it is a long-term drift assumption.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam):**

- ✅ **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO = 99.99966% yield** (this single line appears in nearly every KTU question).
- ✅ **DPMO formula** = $\dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}} \times 10^6$ — memorize the symbols D, U, O.
- ✅ **DMAIC = Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control** — sequence is non-negotiable.
- ✅ **1.5σ shift** is a long-term allowance, not a real-time event; it converts the 6σ nominal goal to a 4.5σ short-term performance.
- ✅ **Cp** measures capability for a centered process; **Cpk** corrects for off-centering.
- ✅ **Six Sigma threshold:** $C_{pk} \geq 2.0$ (with 1.5σ shift), equivalently $\geq 1.5$ in the short term.
- ✅ **Roles hierarchy:** Champion → Master Black Belt → Black Belt → Green Belt → Team Member.
- ✅ **Originator:** Bill Smith, Motorola, 1986. **Popularizer:** Jack Welch, General Electric, 1995.
- ✅ **Conversion table values** for DPMO at 3σ, 4σ, 5σ, 6σ (66,807 / 6,210 / 233 / 3.4) — memorize them; the exam may not always provide the full table.
- ✅ **Two tools per DMAIC phase** — at minimum remember: SIPOC + Project Charter (Define); Process Map + Gauge R&R (Measure); Fishbone + Hypothesis Test (Analyze); DOE + Poka-Yoke (Improve); SPC + FMEA (Control).
- ✅ **Six Sigma is data-driven** — every decision must be backed by statistical evidence, not intuition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Six Sigma Roles and Responsibilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Six Sigma Roles and Responsibilities # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of Six Sigma as practiced in modern Total Quality Management (TQM), **Six Sigma Roles and Responsibilities** refer to the formally structured hierarchy of personnel designations, accountability matrices, and chartered duties that govern the deployment, execution, and sustenance of Six Sigma improvement projects across an organisation. Each role is assigned a specific place in the **DMAIC project lifecycle** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) and is graded according to its statistical competence, leadership span, and authority to commit organisational resources. The Six Sigma hierarchy (top-down) consists of the following chartered roles: 1. **Executive Leadership** (CEO, COO, Senior Vice Presidents) 2. **Champions** (often Directors or Senior Managers) 3. **Master Black Belts** (Full-time Six Sigma experts and mentors) 4. **Black Belts** (Full-time project leaders) 5. **Green Belts** (Part-time project participants trained in DMAIC) 6. **Yellow Belts** (Frontline employees with foundational knowledge) 7. **Process Owners** (Functional managers responsible for sustained process performance) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome mapped to this topic requires students to identify, differentiate, and articulate the charter of each Six Sigma role, the RACI (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) matrix, and the alignment between roles and DMAIC phases. Expect short conceptual questions (3 marks) and detailed application/role-mapping questions (14 marks). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **modern hospital surgical team performing a complex heart operation**. The operation is not done by one person; it requires a structured team: - The **Hospital Director (Executive Leadership)** does not perform the surgery but ensures the operation theatre exists, funds are approved, and the strategic mission of the hospital aligns with the procedure. - The **Head of Surgery (Champion)** selects the lead surgeon, removes administrative barriers (insurance approvals, scheduling), and ensures the project is on track. - The **Senior Consultant Surgeon (Master Black Belt)** is a master of the craft, who has performed the operation hundreds of times and now trains, mentors, and audits the lead surgeon. - The **Lead Surgeon (Black Belt)** performs the critical steps of the surgery using specialised tools. - The **Resident Doctors (Green Belts)** assist during the surgery and may perform simpler procedures under supervision. - The **Nurses and Support Staff (Yellow Belts)** know the basic instruments, prepare the environment, and support the procedure. - The **ICU In-Charge (Process Owner)** takes over after the surgery to ensure the patient stabilises and does not relapse. In this analogy, **Six Sigma is the surgery**, and the **roles are the team**. Each role has a unique charter, and **failure of any single role collapses the entire project**. This is exactly how Motorola (1986), AlliedSignal (Larry Bossidy, 1990s), and General Electric (Jack Welch, 1995–2001) structured Six Sigma deployments. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Six Sigma Metric (must memorise):** > $$\sigma = 6 \;\text{Sigma} \;\Rightarrow\; 3.4 \;\text{defects per million opportunities (DPMO)}$$ > $$\text{Yield} = e^{-\text{DPO}} \times 100\%$$ > where $\text{DPO}$ is defects per opportunity. These metrics justify the rigour of the role hierarchy — every role exists to drive DPMO down toward 3.4. ## 1.3 The Underlying Logic of Role Structuring The Six Sigma role hierarchy is engineered around three pillars: 1. **Strategic Alignment** — Executive Leadership translates business strategy into Six Sigma project portfolios. 2. **Technical Mastery** — Master Black Belts and Black Belts provide the statistical and DMAIC competence. 3. **Operational Embedding** — Green Belts, Yellow Belts, and Process Owners ensure that improvements are sustained at the gemba (shop floor / process level). > [!TIP] > A common KTU board pitfall: students describe the roles as merely "job titles". In valuation, examiners reward answers that **explicitly link each role to a DMAIC phase and to the RACI matrix**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Seven Six Sigma Roles — Detailed Charter ### 2.1.1 Executive Leadership - **Who:** CEO, COO, CFO, Senior VPs. - **Strategic Function:** Sets the organisational vision, allocates multi-million-dollar budgets, and embeds Six Sigma into the corporate scorecard (BSC). - **Authority:** Final accountability for Six Sigma ROI. - **DMAIC Link:** Primarily **Define** phase — sets project selection criteria tied to business strategy. ### 2.1.2 Champions (Sponsors) - **Who:** Senior Managers, Directors, Business Unit Heads. - **Strategic Function:** Act as project sponsors; identify and prioritise projects; remove organisational, political, and resource barriers. - **DMAIC Link:** **Define** phase — they sign off the project charter, business case, and expected savings. - **RACI Position:** **Accountable** for project success. ### 2.1.3 Master Black Belts (MBB) - **Who:** Career Six Sigma experts, typically reporting to Champions or a central Six Sigma office (CSSO). - **Strategic Function:** Train and mentor Black Belts and Green Belts; lead the most complex, cross-functional projects; maintain the Six Sigma tools/methodology repository. - **DMAIC Link:** All five phases — acts as a consultant and auditor across projects. - **RACI Position:** **Consulted** as the methodology expert. ### 2.1.4 Black Belts (BB) - **Who:** Full-time Six Sigma project leaders, typically reporting to Champions; technical specialists. - **Strategic Function:** Lead 4 to 6 projects per year; apply DMAIC rigorously; coach Green Belts; train team members in advanced statistical tools. - **DMAIC Link:** **Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control** — the operational backbone. - **RACI Position:** **Responsible** for project delivery. ### 2.1.5 Green Belts (GB) - **Who:** Subject-matter experts who work on Six Sigma projects alongside their regular duties (typically 25–50% of their time). - **Strategic Function:** Execute sub-projects, collect data, run smaller DMAIC projects; act as the bridge between Black Belts and Yellow Belts. - **DMAIC Link:** All five phases at a smaller scale. ### 2.1.6 Yellow Belts (YB) - **Who:** Frontline employees, clerks, shop-floor workers with introductory Six Sigma awareness. - **Strategic Function:** Participate in brainstorming, support data collection, identify local problems. - **DMAIC Link:** Mainly **Define** (problem identification) and **Control** (sustaining improvements). ### 2.1.7 Process Owners - **Who:** Functional/departmental managers whose process is being improved. - **Strategic Function:** Own the process post-handover; ensure controls are sustained, KPIs are met, and the improvement does not regress. - **DMAIC Link:** Primarily **Control** phase. ## 2.2 RACI Matrix — The Responsibility Assignment Engine The **RACI Matrix** is the formal accountability chart used by Champions and Black Belts to ensure every DMAIC task has exactly one **Accountable** owner. | Letter | Role | Meaning | |--------|------|---------| | **R** | Responsible | Person who *does* the work | | **A** | Accountable | Person who *owns* the outcome (only ONE per task) | | **C** | Consulted | Subject-matter expert whose input is sought *before* action | | **I** | Informed | Stakeholder updated *after* action | ## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Although Six Sigma roles is largely conceptual, KTU examiners expect numerical fluency in the following formulas whenever a project context is given in the question. | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Notes | |---------|-------------------|---------------| | DPMO (Defects per Million Opportunities) | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}$ | defects / million | | Defects Per Opportunity (DPO) | $\text{DPO} = \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}}$ | dimensionless | | Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_{i}$ | dimensionless, $0 < \text{RTY} \leq 1$ | | Process Sigma Level | $\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(1 - \text{DPO} + 0.5 \times \text{DPO}_{\text{adj}}\right) + 1.5$ | $\sigma \geq 6$ is world-class | | Project ROI | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Savings} - \text{Project Cost}}{\text{Project Cost}} \times 100\%$ | percent | | Defect Rate (per unit) | $p = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units}}$ | dimensionless | ## 2.4 Why This Structure Matters in Engineering Practice The role hierarchy is not a managerial fad. It exists because: - **Statistical depth** of DMAIC requires full-time Black Belts (a part-time manager cannot run regression, DOE, and hypothesis tests with rigour). - **Cultural embedding** requires Yellow Belts at the gemba — improvements fail without frontline ownership. - **Sustainability** requires Process Owners — projects handed over to no-one invariably regress to baseline within 6 months (this is called *control loss*). - **Strategic ROI** requires Champions to filter projects by payback period and strategic fit. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From DPMO to Sigma Level (The Conversion Logic) Because KTU frequently gives a numerical "project context" and asks which role validates which phase, you must know how a process metric links back to a role's responsibility. **Given:** A Black Belt reports that in a stamping process producing automotive clutch plates: - Sample size: $N = 5000$ units - Defects observed: $D = 90$ - Opportunities per unit (defects a plate can have): $O = 4$ **Step 1 — Compute DPO.** The number of defect opportunities is: $$N \times O = 5000 \times 4 = 20{,}000 \text{ opportunities}$$ Therefore: $$\text{DPO} = \dfrac{D}{N \times O} = \dfrac{90}{20{,}000} = 0.0045$$ **Step 2 — Convert DPO to DPMO.** $$\text{DPMO} = \text{DPO} \times 10^{6} = 0.0045 \times 10^{6} = 4500$$ **Step 3 — Convert DPMO to Process Sigma.** Using the short-term yield-to-sigma conversion (with the 1.5σ shift adjustment): $$\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}}\right) + 0.5\right) + 1.5$$ Plugging in: $$\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2} \times (1 - 0.0045) + 0.5\right) + 1.5$$ $$\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(0.5 \times 0.9955 + 0.5\right) + 1.5$$ $$\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(0.99775\right) + 1.5$$ Since $\text{NORMSINV}(0.99775) \approx 2.85$: $$\sigma \approx 2.85 + 1.5 = 4.35$$ **Step 4 — Role-based Interpretation.** $$\boxed{\sigma_{\text{process}} \approx 4.35 \sigma,\quad \text{DPMO} = 4500}$$ This is a **sub-world-class process**. The role-based conclusion: - **Champion**: Justifies business case for a new Black Belt project. - **Black Belt**: Initiates DMAIC — Measure phase first. - **Process Owner**: Currently the owner of a 4.35σ process; must co-sign the project charter. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Role × DMAIC Phase × RACI The following exhaustive matrix is **high-yield for 14-mark questions** in KTU. | DMAIC Phase | Executive Leadership | Champion | Master Black Belt | Black Belt | Green Belt | Yellow Belt | Process Owner | |--------------|----------------------|----------|-------------------|------------|------------|-------------|---------------| | **Define** | A | A, R | C | R | R | I | C | | **Measure** | I | A | C | R | R | C | C | | **Analyze** | I | A | C | R | R | C | C | | **Improve** | I | A | C | R | R | C | C | | **Control** | I | A | C | C | R | I | A, R | Reading the matrix: - A project can have **only one A (Accountable)** per task; Champions are the conventional Accountable for the project as a whole. - Black Belts are **Responsible** for delivery across Measure→Improve. - Process Owners become **Accountable + Responsible** at the Control phase — this is the *handover point*. ## 3.3 Worked Example: RACI Application **Scenario:** A Six Sigma project aims to reduce the rejection rate in an injection-moulding line. **Tasks:** 1. Approve project budget (₹15 lakh). 2. Run DOE on moulding temperature and pressure. 3. Update control charts weekly. 4. Brainstorm root causes with shop-floor workers. 5. Audit the project's statistical validity. **Solution Mapping:** | Task | Activity | R | A | C | I | |------|----------|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Approve ₹15 L budget | Finance Mgr | Champion | Exec. Leadership | BB | | 2 | DOE on temp & pressure | BB | Champion | MBB | GB | | 3 | Weekly control charts | GB | Process Owner | BB | YB | | 4 | Brainstorm with workers | BB | Champion | YB, GB | Process Owner | | 5 | Audit statistical validity | MBB | Champion | BB | Exec. Leadership | This table, when reproduced in a KTU answer, secures **full 14 marks** because it covers all four RACI letters and ties every role to a phase. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Six Sigma Role Hierarchy (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Executive Leadership
CEO COO CFO] nodeB[Champions
Senior Managers Directors] nodeC[Master Black Belts
Six Sigma Methodologists] nodeD[Black Belts
Full Time Project Leaders] nodeE[Green Belts
Part Time Project Members] nodeF[Yellow Belts
Frontline Awareness] nodeG[Process Owners
Functional Managers] nodeA -->|Sets Vision Allocates Budget| nodeB nodeB -->|Sponsors Removes Barriers| nodeC nodeB -->|Sponsors Approves Charter| nodeD nodeC -->|Trains Coaches Audits| nodeD nodeC -->|Trains Coaches| nodeE nodeD -->|Leads DMAIC| nodeE nodeD -->|Leads DMAIC| nodeF nodeE -->|Supports| nodeF nodeD -->|Handover Controls| nodeG nodeE -->|Handover Controls| nodeG ``` ## 4.2 DMAIC Phase Flow with Role Engagement ```mermaid graph LR stepD[D
Define] stepM[M
Measure] stepA[A
Analyze] stepI[I
Improve] stepC[C
Control] roleE[Executive Leadership] roleCH[Champion] roleMBB[Master Black Belt] roleBB[Black Belt] roleGB[Green Belt] roleYB[Yellow Belt] rolePO[Process Owner] stepD --> stepM --> stepA --> stepI --> stepC roleE -.->|Accountable| stepD roleCH -.->|Accountable| stepD roleCH -.->|Accountable| stepM roleCH -.->|Accountable| stepA roleCH -.->|Accountable| stepI roleCH -.->|Accountable| stepC roleMBB -.->|Consults| stepD roleMBB -.->|Consults| stepM roleMBB -.->|Consults| stepA roleMBB -.->|Consults| stepI roleBB -.->|Responsible| stepD roleBB -.->|Responsible| stepM roleBB -.->|Responsible| stepA roleBB -.->|Responsible| stepI roleGB -.->|Responsible| stepM roleGB -.->|Responsible| stepA roleGB -.->|Responsible| stepI roleGB -.->|Responsible| stepC roleYB -.->|Informed| stepD roleYB -.->|Consults| stepM roleYB -.->|Informed| stepC rolePO -.->|Consults| stepM rolePO -.->|Consults| stepA rolePO -.->|Accountable Responsible| stepC ``` ## 4.3 RACI Decision Flow for Project Governance ```mermaid graph TD startA[New Six Sigma Project Initiated] q1{Is Strategic Decision Required} q2{Is Statistical Expertise Required} q3{Is Frontline Data Required} q4{Is Long Term Process Control Required} a1[Champions and Executive Leadership
Accountable] a2[Master Black Belt
Consulted] a3[Black Belt and Green Belt
Responsible] a4[Yellow Belts
Consulted and Informed] a5[Process Owner
Accountable and Responsible] startA --> q1 q1 -->|Yes| a1 q1 -->|No| q2 q2 -->|Yes| a2 q2 -->|No| q3 q3 -->|Yes| a3 q3 -->|No| q4 q4 -->|Yes| a5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The arrows in the diagrams above represent the *flow of authority and information*, not reporting lines. Champions *delegate* to Black Belts but remain *Accountable*. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** *Define the role of a Champion in a Six Sigma project. Why is this role critical for project success?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Champion** is a senior manager or director who acts as the *project sponsor* in a Six Sigma deployment. The Champion's duties include: 1. **Project Selection:** Identifies and prioritises projects based on strategic fit, ROI, and feasibility (1 mark). 2. **Resource Mobilisation:** Allocates Black Belts, budgets, and removes organisational or political barriers (1 mark). 3. **Project Sign-off:** Signs the project charter and is *Accountable* (in RACI terms) for project success across all DMAIC phases (1 mark). The Champion is critical because without executive sponsorship, DMAIC projects stall due to lack of authority, funding, and cross-functional access. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1]** *Differentiate between a Black Belt and a Master Black Belt in terms of time commitment, statistical depth, and project complexity.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Dimension | Black Belt | Master Black Belt | |-----------|------------|-------------------| | Time commitment | Full-time on projects (100%) | Full-time on mentoring + 1–2 flagship projects | | Statistical depth | Advanced (DOE, regression, SPC) | Expert-level (designs training, audits methods) | | Project complexity | 4–6 projects/year, single-function | Cross-functional, enterprise-wide | | Reporting | To Champion | To CSSO (Central Six Sigma Office) | | Mentoring | Coaches Green Belts | Coaches Black Belts and Green Belts | | Career stage | Mid-career | Senior technical career path | (Marks: 1 for time + 1 for technical depth + 1 for project/mentoring difference.) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) — Model Solution **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Equivalent]** **(a)** *Explain the Six Sigma role hierarchy with the key responsibilities of Executive Leadership, Champions, Master Black Belts, Black Belts, Green Belts, and Yellow Belts.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Six Sigma is executed through a structured role hierarchy in which each layer carries a distinct charter aligned to the DMAIC lifecycle. 1. **Executive Leadership (CEO/COO/CFO):** Sets the organisational vision, embeds Six Sigma into the corporate Balanced Scorecard, and commits multi-year budgets. Their primary DMAIC linkage is the **Define** phase, where strategic priorities dictate project selection. They are ultimately *Accountable* for the Six Sigma ROI. **(1 mark)** 2. **Champions (Senior Managers/Directors):** Act as project sponsors. They select Black Belts, approve project charters, and remove cross-functional barriers. Champions are *Accountable* across all five DMAIC phases and must ensure project outcomes tie to business KPIs such as cost-of-poor-quality (COPQ). **(1 mark)** 3. **Master Black Belts (MBB):** Career Six Sigma methodologists. They design the curriculum, train Black Belts and Green Belts, audit project statistical rigour, and lead the most complex, cross-functional projects. MBBs are *Consulted* throughout DMAIC as the resident experts on advanced tools (regression, DOE, hypothesis testing). **(1.5 marks)** 4. **Black Belts (BB):** Full-time project leaders who apply DMAIC rigorously. They own the project plan, conduct Measure/Analyze/Improve phases, mentor Green Belts, and deliver quantified savings (e.g., DPMO reduction). BBs are *Responsible* for project delivery from Measure through Improve. **(1.5 marks)** 5. **Green Belts (GB):** Subject-matter experts who participate in projects part-time. They lead sub-projects, collect data, run smaller DMAIC cycles, and act as the bridge between BBs and Yellow Belts. **(1 mark)** 6. **Yellow Belts (YB):** Frontline employees with foundational Six Sigma awareness. They participate in brainstorming (Define), support data collection (Measure), and sustain improvements at the gemba (Control). **(1 mark)** **(b)** *Construct the RACI matrix for the following Six Sigma project activities in a CNC machining cell:* 1. *Approve ₹20 lakh project budget.* 2. *Conduct Gauge R&R study.* 3. *Run a 2k full factorial DOE on cutting speed and feed.* 4. *Implement Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts on the shop floor.* 5. *Sustain the improved process for 12 months post-handover.* *Clearly mark who is Responsible (R), Accountable (A), Consulted (C), and Informed (I).* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | # | Activity | R (Responsible) | A (Accountable) | C (Consulted) | I (Informed) | |---|----------|-----------------|-----------------|---------------|--------------| | 1 | Approve ₹20 L budget | Finance Mgr | Champion | Exec. Leadership | Black Belt, Process Owner | | 2 | Gauge R&R study | Black Belt | Champion | Master Black Belt | Green Belt, Process Owner | | 3 | 2k factorial DOE | Black Belt | Champion | Master Black Belt | Green Belt | | 4 | SPC charts on shop floor | Green Belt | Process Owner | Black Belt | Yellow Belt, Champion | | 5 | Sustain process 12 months | Process Owner | Process Owner | Black Belt (audit) | Champion, Exec. Leadership | **Justification (each row carries ~1.2 marks):** - *Row 1*: Budget approval is a strategic decision — only the Champion is Accountable. **(1.2 marks)** - *Row 2*: Gauge R&R is a Measure-phase statistical study, owned by the Black Belt with Master Black Belt as methodology consultant. **(1.2 marks)** - *Row 3*: DOE is an Improve-phase advanced tool — Black Belt executes; MBB validates design. **(1.2 marks)** - *Row 4*: SPC is a Control-phase activity handed over to Green Belts and Process Owners; Black Belt consults during the initial setup. **(1.2 marks)** - *Row 5*: Post-handover sustainability is the *Process Owner's* charter — they are both Accountable and Responsible; Black Belt audits periodically. **(1.2 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 3]** **(a)** *Explain the responsibilities of the Champion, Black Belt, Green Belt, and Process Owner across each phase of DMAIC. Use a phase-wise table.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | DMAIC Phase | Champion | Black Belt | Green Belt | Process Owner | |-------------|----------|------------|------------|---------------| | **Define** | Selects project, signs charter, allocates team | Drafts project charter, defines CTQ | Provides subject-matter input on VOC | Identifies the process and its boundaries | | **Measure** | Approves data-collection plan | Designs MSA, baseline sigma | Collects data, runs capability studies | Provides access to process data | | **Analyze** | Reviews interim findings | Runs regression, hypothesis tests, root-cause analysis | Validates root causes on shop floor | Confirms analysis against process reality | | **Improve** | Approves pilot results | Designs and runs DOE, pilots solutions | Implements pilot solutions | Approves operational changes | | **Control** | Sign-off for project closure | Develops control plan, training materials | Updates SOPs, trains Yellow Belts | Owns controls, monitors KPIs, sustains gains | (Each row ≈ 1.4 marks; explicit linkage to roles and DMAIC is mandatory.) **(b)** *In a printed-circuit-board (PCB) assembly line, 850 defects are observed in 4,250 assembled boards. Each board has 6 soldering opportunities. Compute DPMO, DPO, and the process sigma level. Based on these metrics, justify which Six Sigma roles should be deployed and what their responsibilities would be.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute DPO.** $$N \times O = 4250 \times 6 = 25{,}500 \text{ opportunities}$$ $$\text{DPO} = \dfrac{D}{N \times O} = \dfrac{850}{25{,}500} = 0.03333$$ **Step 2 — Compute DPMO.** $$\text{DPMO} = 0.03333 \times 10^{6} = 33{,}333 \text{ defects per million opportunities}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Process Sigma.** $$\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{33{,}333}{10^{6}}\right) + 0.5\right) + 1.5$$ $$\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(0.5 \times 0.96667 + 0.5\right) + 1.5$$ $$\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(0.98333\right) + 1.5$$ $$\sigma \approx 2.13 + 1.5 = 3.63\sigma$$ (Each numerical step ≈ 1 mark = 3 marks total.) **Step 4 — Role Deployment Justification (4 marks):** | Metric | Value | Implication | |--------|-------|-------------| | DPMO | 33,333 | Severely poor quality (industry benchmark ~3.4 DPMO) | | Sigma | 3.63σ | Below world-class; requires immediate DMAIC intervention | **Role deployment:** - **Executive Leadership:** Must approve a Six Sigma initiative at the corporate level given the COPQ exposure. **(0.7 marks)** - **Champion:** Should sponsor a Black Belt-led project to reduce soldering defects; tie the project to PCB yield KPI. **(0.7 marks)** - **Master Black Belt:** Should mentor the Black Belt on DOE for reflow temperature profiling. **(0.7 marks)** - **Black Belt:** Lead the full DMAIC project; Measure → solder-paste inspection data; Analyze → regression on temperature/humidity; Improve → DOE on reflow profile; Control → SPC on solder-joint quality. **(0.7 marks)** - **Green Belts:** Line operators trained in DMAIC basics; collect hourly defect data. **(0.4 marks)** - **Yellow Belts:** Frontline assemblers flag anomalies during shift handover. **(0.4 marks)** - **Process Owner:** SMT cell manager — owns the post-project control plan and monitors Cp/Cpk monthly. **(0.4 marks)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Areas (KTU Board Pattern):** > 1. **Listing roles without DMAIC linkage** — merely writing "Black Belts lead projects" earns ≤ 2 marks; examiners want DMAIC-phase-specific statements. > 2. **Confusing Champion with Master Black Belt** — Champions are *sponsors/managers*, not statisticians. MBBs are *methodologists*, not decision-makers. > 3. **RACI errors** — assigning two "A"s to a task, or no "A" at all, loses full marks for that row. > 4. **Numerical errors** — wrong conversion between DPO, DPMO, and σ. Memorise: $\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}(\dots) + 1.5$ (1.5σ shift). > 5. **Skipping Process Owner** — students often omit this role. KTU examiners frequently award a dedicated 1 mark for correctly identifying the Process Owner's *Control* responsibility. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Six Sigma Roles Hierarchy (top → bottom):** Executive Leadership → Champions → Master Black Belts → Black Belts → Green Belts → Yellow Belts → Process Owners. - **Champion's charter:** Select, sponsor, sign-off, remove barriers. *Accountable* across DMAIC. - **Master Black Belt's charter:** Train, mentor, audit; expert-level statistician. - **Black Belt's charter:** Full-time project leader; *Responsible* for Measure→Improve. - **Green Belt's charter:** Part-time participant; bridges BB ↔ YB; leads smaller projects. - **Yellow Belt's charter:** Frontline awareness; supports Define + Control. - **Process Owner's charter:** Owns the process post-handover; *Accountable* for Control phase and sustainability. - **RACI:** Only **one Accountable** per task; multiple *Responsible*, *Consulted*, *Informed* allowed. - **DPMO formula:** $\dfrac{D}{N \times O} \times 10^{6}$ - **Sigma conversion:** $\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}(1 - \text{DPO}) + 0.5\right) + 1.5$ - **World-class target:** $\sigma \geq 6$ corresponds to **3.4 DPMO**. - **Project success criterion:** Sustained improvement owned by the **Process Owner**, not the Black Belt. - **Cultural note:** Yellow Belts and Green Belts ensure the *Kaizen mindset* spreads; without them, Six Sigma becomes a "Black Belt elite club" and dies out. - **KTU valuation tip:** Always end a 14-mark answer with a one-line **RACI table** or a **DMAIC-role matrix** to consolidate marks.

topics.title: DMAIC Process – Various Phases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** DMAIC Process – Various Phases # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of DMAIC **DMAIC** is an acronym for **Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, and Control**. It is a **data-driven quality improvement methodology** and the foundational problem-solving roadmap of **Six Sigma**, a disciplined, statistical-based philosophy introduced by Motorola in 1986 and later popularized by General Electric (GE) under Jack Welch. Each letter represents a sequential phase through which a cross-functional project team must pass when attempting to resolve a complex business, manufacturing, or service-oriented problem. The roadmap is governed by the principle that no permanent improvement can be made without first rigorously quantifying the existing process, isolating the root cause(s) of variation, and then statistically validating any new solution. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** DMAIC is the **core process improvement framework** for Six Sigma projects. Students must remember the exact sequence, deliverables, and key statistical tools associated with each of the **five phases**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the DMAIC process is taught as the practical application of statistical quality control (SQC) theory. It is the bridge that connects the **theoretical tools** (control charts, process capability indices, regression, hypothesis testing) covered in earlier modules to **real-world industrial problem-solving**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you take your car to a mechanic because the engine is making a strange noise. The mechanic does not just guess and start replacing parts. Instead, they follow a logical sequence very similar to DMAIC: | Mechanic's Step | DMAIC Equivalent | What Happens | |---|---|---| | **D**iagnose the complaint with the customer | **D**efine | Clearly state the problem and customer requirement | | **M**easure the sound level & run diagnostic tests | **M**easure | Collect data on current process performance | | **A**nalyze the test results to find the faulty part | **A**nalyze | Identify root causes using statistical tools | | **I**mplement the repair (replace part) | **I**mprove | Pilot and implement the verified solution | | **C**onfirm the fix and schedule future checkups | **C**ontrol | Sustain the gains through monitoring & SOPs | > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition:** DMAIC is essentially a **scientific experiment** (Observe → Hypothesize → Experiment → Conclude) applied to a business process. It forces teams to move from *opinion-based* decisions to *fact-based* decisions using verifiable data. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Six Sigma Metrics The following key metrics are **standardized** in any DMAIC project and must be memorized: - **3.4 DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities) → equivalent to **4.5 Sigma** performance - **1.5σ Shift** → the long-term mean shift correction factor assumed by Motorola - **Six Sigma Level** → **6σ** = 3.4 defects per million opportunities - **COPQ** (Cost of Poor Quality) → financial metric tying quality to profitability - **CTQ** (Critical-to-Quality) → the measurable characteristic that the customer cares about most > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Tip:** When asked "What is Six Sigma?" in a 3-mark question, a complete answer should always include the number **3.4 DPMO**. Forgetting this number is one of the most common reasons students lose marks. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Phases of DMAIC — Structured Logic The DMAIC roadmap is **sequential and iterative**. Skipping a phase or attempting to solve problems before completing the *Measure* phase is the single biggest reason Six Sigma projects fail in industry. ### Phase 1: **DEFINE** — "What is the problem?" - **Objective:** Clearly articulate the business problem, the scope of the project, the customer (internal or external), and the Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) requirements. - **Key Deliverables:** Project Charter, Voice of Customer (VOC) analysis, SIPOC diagram, Problem Statement. - **Why first?** Because without a clear, measurable problem statement, any "improvement" is merely a guess. - **How?** Conduct interviews, surveys, focus groups, and review complaint logs. ### Phase 2: **MEASURE** — "How is the process performing today?" - **Objective:** Quantify the current state of the process through reliable data collection and establish a baseline performance metric. - **Key Deliverables:** Data Collection Plan, baseline DPMO, baseline Sigma level, Process Map with value-added/non-value-added steps, Measurement System Analysis (MSA), initial capability indices (Cp, Cpk). - **Why second?** Because you cannot manage what you cannot measure (Peter Drucker's principle). - **How?** Stratified sampling, check sheets, run charts, Pareto charts, control charts (X-bar, R, p, c, u). ### Phase 3: **ANALYZE** — "What are the root causes of the problem?" - **Objective:** Use statistical tools to identify the vital few root causes (X's) that drive the critical-to-quality output (Y). - **Key Deliverables:** Hypothesis tests, Regression models, ANOVA, Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram, 5-Whys analysis, FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis). - **Why third?** Because treating symptoms rather than root causes leads to recurring problems. - **How?** Brainstorming, statistical inference, graphical analysis (scatter, box, multi-vari charts). ### Phase 4: **IMPROVE** — "What solutions will fix the root causes?" - **Objective:** Generate, pilot, and statistically validate solutions that address the validated root causes. - **Key Deliverables:** Pilot study results, Design of Experiments (DOE), mistake-proofing (Poka-Yoke), updated FMEA, revised capability indices. - **Why fourth?** Because the solution must be verified to actually reduce defects before full-scale deployment. - **How?** Pilot runs, simulation, Kaizen events, full factorial / fractional factorial experiments. ### Phase 5: **CONTROL** — "How do we sustain the gains?" - **Objective:** Lock in the improvements so the process does not drift back to its old state. Standardize and document the new process. - **Key Deliverables:** Updated Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts, Control Plans, Training plans, Response Plans for out-of-control conditions. - **Why last?** Because sustainable quality requires ongoing monitoring, not a one-time fix. - **How?** Control charts (X-bar, R, p, c), training, documentation, project hand-off to process owner. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" Behind DMAIC:** Each phase answers a specific question, and the sequence forms a closed loop. The "Control" phase feeds back to "Define" if new problems emerge, making DMAIC a **continuous improvement cycle** aligned with the philosophy of Edwards Deming's PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table summarizes every key formula, metric, and conversion you will need for DMAIC-related problems in the KTU examination. | Metric | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | **DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities) | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ | D = number of defects, U = number of units, O = opportunities per unit | defects per million | | **DPO** (Defects Per Opportunity) | $\text{DPO} = \dfrac{D}{U \times O}$ | Defects relative to total opportunity count | dimensionless | | **DPU** (Defects Per Unit) | $\text{DPU} = \dfrac{D}{U}$ | Average defects in one unit | defects/unit | | **Yield (Y)** | $Y = 1 - \text{DPO}$ | Probability that a unit passes a single step | fraction (0 to 1) | | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_{i}$ | Product of yields of all process steps | fraction (0 to 1) | | **Sigma Level (Z)** | $Z = \Phi^{-1}(Y) + 1.5$ | Inverse standard normal of yield, plus 1.5σ shift | σ | | **Process Capability (Cp)** | $C_{p} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}}$ | Process spread vs. specification spread | dimensionless | | **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\hat{\sigma}},\ \dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\hat{\sigma}}\right)$ | Centered capability (lower is worse) | dimensionless | | **COPQ** (Cost of Poor Quality) | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Prevention}$ | Total quality-related cost | currency | | **Pareto (80/20)** | Cumulative \% of defects vs. cumulative \% of causes | Identifies vital few causes | percentage | > [!WARNING] > **Markdown Table Rule:** When writing $\vert \bar{X} \vert$ in a row, always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the raw pipe character `\vert`, as the pipe breaks the Markdown table parser in many student notebooks and exam preparation PDFs. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In an engineering context, DMAIC is used across nearly every industry vertical: - **Manufacturing:** Reducing weld defects, improving assembly line yield, lowering scrap rate. - **Healthcare:** Reducing hospital-acquired infections, decreasing patient wait times in emergency rooms. - **Software/IT:** Reducing software bug counts, improving mean time between failures (MTBF). - **Finance/Service:** Reducing loan processing cycle time, lowering customer churn. - **Automotive (Toyota/BMW):** DMAIC is the *statistical cousin* of the Toyota Production System's A3 problem-solving, both relying on root-cause analysis and visual management. The combination of **DMAIC** + **Lean** (waste elimination) is called **Lean Six Sigma**, the dominant quality framework in global industry today. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Calculating Baseline Sigma Level > **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Module 4 Numerical):** > A semiconductor manufacturing line produces 5,000 microchips per day. Each chip is inspected at 3 test stations, giving 3 opportunities for a defect. On average, 250 defects are recorded per day. Calculate (a) DPMO, (b) DPU, (c) DPO, (d) Yield, and (e) the corresponding Sigma Level. ### Given Data - Number of units produced per day: $U = 5000$ - Number of opportunities per unit: $O = 3$ - Number of defects recorded per day: $D = 250$ --- ### Step 1: Calculate DPU (Defects Per Unit) The DPU is the average number of defects contained in a single unit.
